Full_Text:
CHAPTER 7 
Why Do the Mekranoti Trek? 
Trekking is a common practice among many South American Indian 
groups. The Siriono , the Bororo , 
the Nambikwara , the Kaingang , and the Kayapo  are all well known for the lengthy trips villagers undertake to gather 
products from the forests and savannas surrounding their villages. Various 
researchers have given treks an important role in explaining native social 
structures or adaptations to their environments. Gross  sees the seasonal breakups of village communities to go out on trek as responsible for 
the many nonkin sodalities (age-grades, ceremonial groups, men's societies, etc.) of Ge groups. Turner  views trekking as important for 
the exercise of power by male heads-of-households among the Kayapo. 
And Meggers  regards trekking as an adaptation to a tropical forest 
that limits population densities and political development. But despite this 
interest in trekking, we have few data on actual expeditions in specific 
groups, and we know little about why groups trek in the first place. 
Using information gathered among the Mekranoti-Kayapo Indians of 
Central Brazil (Posto Indigena Mekranoti), I attempt in this study to analyze some of the possible reasons for trekking in lowland South America. 
The Mekranoti are a group of 285 Kayapo-speaking Indians living in a 
single village in the southern part of the Brazilian state of Para. Originally 
part of a larger village, the Mekranoti split into two groups in 1953 shortly 
OF NATIVE AMAZONIANS All rights of reproduction in any form reserved. 226 DENNIS WERNER 
after their first contact with Claudio Villas Boas near the Xingu River. The 
group remaining at the Xingu River is now known as "Txukahamae." The 
other group is now called "Posto Indigena Mekranoti" or simply "Mekranoti." This latter group remained isolated in the tropical forest until 1966 
when a missionary moved into their village. 
At the time of my fieldwork in 1976-1977, the Mekranoti depended 
entirely on shifting agriculture, hunting, fishing, and gathering for their 
subsistence. Although their lands had not been officially demarcated, they 
lived in an otherwise seemingly uninhabited territory about the size of 
Belgium. Compared to other Indians in Brazil, they exploited a relatively 
fertile and productive environment . 
Working through FUNAI (the Brazilian Indian Foundation), the Mekranoti 
traded Brazil nuts and handicrafts in exchange for medicines, shotguns, 
metal pans, and other articles of Western manufacture. But only one Indian spoke any Portuguese, and none could deal well with money. 
The Mekranoti spend a great deal of time on trips away from their 
village, including lengthy journeys into the forest by the entire village 
travelling as a unit. Many of these trips would be called "treks" by anthropologists. But some of them would not be included under the "trekking" rubric. Presently, the Mekranoti, with the help of FUNAI or missionaries, sometimes travel to Belem or Brasilia to receive medical treatment, 
buy goods for the villagers, or participate in meetings of the Summer 
Institute of Linguistics. Young men also go off for part of the year to collect 
Brazil nuts to sell to FUNAI, and individual families sometimes go off for a 
few days to live near their gardens or to hunt in the forest. In the past, the 
Mekranoti sometimes deserted their homes entirely to escape enemies 
after raids or to relocate near new garden sites. While all of these different 
trips may be important to Mekranoti life, they do not constitute treks in the 
sense intended by most anthropologists. In this study I restrict trekking to 
overland trips into the forest or savanna, lasting several weeks, and involving large numbers of people with the intention of returning home to a base 
village. During my stay among the Mekranoti, the Indians spent about 22% 
of their time on treks. (They spent another 1% of their time on short family 
trips into the forest.) I accompanied the Indians for portions of three different treks for a total of about 8 weeks, during which time I carried out 
studies of time allocation, dietary intake, and hunting and fishing 
productivities. 
All Mekranoti treks are similar in certain respects. And the Mekranoti 
refer to them all with the same expression, me djy hot te. Usually treks 
involve daily or almost daily moves of campsites from one area of the forest 
to another. They also require that people carry around large quantities of 
garden produce to feed themselves. On trek, the young men generally 
spend most of their time hunting, while the women (and some elderly 
men) transport produce and occasionally do some gathering of wild foods. 
But there is a great deal of variation in the seasonality, duration, composition, and organization of treks. I have described some of these variations 
elsewhere . 
Some anthropologists see Kayapo trekking as exclusively a dry-season 
activity . But the Mekranoti also trek during the rainy 
months, albeit not as often. Table 7.1 shows that for the year of my fieldwork people spent a greater proportion of their time on trek during the dry 
season. Although my historical notes are unsystematic and far from complete, they also suggest a possible dry-season bias. Of the treks mentioned 
in my notes, 37% took place in the dry season that constitutes only 25% of 
the year. 
Trekking is undoubtedly more difficult during the rainy season, when 
the Mekranoti must cut paths through large stretches of flooded land and 
must construct shelters against the rain. Consequently, new campsites are 
generally set up only 30-45 minutes away from previous encampments. 
During the dry season, the Mekranoti can travel longer distances over solid 
land, and can sleep in the open air on leaves spread on the ground. But the 
distance between campsites is never more than a few hours walk. Part of 
the reason is that the Mekranoti must transport garden produce from one 
site to the next. This requires frequent return trips and could not be accomplished if distances were too great. As it is, Mekranoti adults on trek 
average about 2 hours a day simply transporting garden produce from one 
campsite to the next or from home and forest gardens to the trekking 
village. 
The duration and composition of treks also vary. Some treks last several 
months; others are completed in a few weeks. Sometimes the entire village 
travels together as a single unit; at other times only the men go out, or the 
village splits into two separate treks. During my stay among the Mekranoti, 
half of the village left for a long informal trek from June to September, and 
the other half made a shorter trek in June and July. In December, and again 
in May, the entire village participated in formal ceremonial treks. In October, the men trekked without women and children. 
The organization of treks depends to some extent upon the people who 
participate. If only the men go out, they are likely to divide themselves into 
their two men's societies and go off in different directions. If both men and 
women trek, organization depends on whether the trek is ceremonial or 
nonceremonial. Nonceremonial treks are informal and may involve only 
part of the village. Nuclear families locate themselves anywhere they wish 
within a campsite, often varying their relative placement from one day to 
the next. Ceremonial treks are much more rigidly organized. 
Most Kayapo ceremonies center on the bestowal of special names to 
children. These ceremonies (such as Bep, fakak, Nhäk, or Koko) last several 
months. In the early stages of the festivities, men (or women, depending 
on the ceremony) must sing songs every morning and evening in the 228 DENNIS WERNER 
central plaza. They are rewarded for their efforts by gifts of food provided 
by the parents of the children who will receive names. For the final days of 
a ceremony the Mekranoti need tortoises to feed the assembly, and they 
must trek for several weeks to obtain a large enough supply. 
If only the men do the trekking, then they divide themselves into their 
men's societies and go off in different directions. But when both men and 
women go on ceremonial treks people generally travel together as a single 
unit. Housing arrangements are decided on the days before departure. The 
fathers of the children receiving names {mekra rer mex), and their "helpers" 
{me kuka tyk) go to the men's house and "choose up teams" consisting of 
their real and fictive brothers with their respective wives and children. 
Virtually everyone is chosen at this point. During wet-season treks each 
team builds a long lean-to with sections set aside for its nuclear family 
units. Each team also constructs its own atykbe, a dormitory for boys and 
young men who do not sleep with their families. And in front of each team 
dwelling a clearing is prepared and covered with leaves to serve as a men's 
house {ngä) for nightly men's meetings. Most ceremonies require wetseason treks, but there are also ceremonial treks during the dry season. 
The organization is much the same, although shelters need not be 
prepared. 
Of the 1449 observations of people on trek that appeared in my time 
allocation studies, 816 (or 56%) were of nonceremonial treks, 608 (42%) of 
ceremonial treks, and 25 (2%) of male-only treks. 
Mekranoti treks vary widely, but they all require a great deal of work 
transporting garden produce. Why do the Mekranoti leave the comforts of 
their village to engage in such pursuits? Explanations for Trekking 
When I asked (informally and unsystematically) why the Mekranoti 
went on trek, they gave various answers. They may want to collect forest 
products such as game, fish, tortoises, palm oil for hair, tree resin for body 
painting, or wood for bow staves. One informant mentioned an incident in 
the past when the Mekranoti trekked to avoid enemy attacks. Others mentioned reasons such as escaping illness in an area, or avoiding intravillage 
conflicts. Indians elsewhere in Amazonia offer a similar variety of interpretations for trekking. Maybury-Lewis  even records a case 
when the Shavante went on a trek while their beans were growing in order 
to avoid the temptation to eat unripe green beans. All of these native 
explanations may be valid for particular treks. But they do not explain why 
trekking exists in the cultural repertoire of some Indian groups but not 
others. For example, many groups probably suffer from the temptation to 
eat unripe beans, but only some groups trek. To explain why treTcking 
exists in some cultures but not others, we need to turn to more comprehensive theories. 
One explanation for trekking regards the practice as an adaptation to 
scarce protein resources in areas distant from large rivers . I accepted this explanation in a previous article . The basic argument is that some South American Indians may be 
forced to go out on trek in order to meet minimum protein requirements 
because the areas in which they live are short in fish and game. 
Recently analyzed data do not support the protein scarcity view. First, a 
comparison of protein capture in four Central Brazilian societies—the 
Mekranoti, the Shavante, the Bororo, and the Kanela—shows that the 
groups that trek most are those with the highest, not the lowest protein 
availability . The Shavante and the Mekranoti trek a 
great deal and also have relatively high amounts of protein in their diets. 
The Kanela trek very little even though their protein supplies are very low. 
An analysis of the seasonality of Mekranoti trekking also casts doubt on 
the protein scarcity argument. Table 7.1 shows that the Mekranoti are most 
likely to trek during the dry and intermediate seasons. But Table 7.2 shows 
that these are the times when hunting and fishing returns are greatest. 
According to the Mekranoti, hunting is easier during the dry season bebecause animals must come together at this time around smaller streams and 
waterholes. Also, game is easier to chase over dry land than through 
flooded swamps. Thus, trekking seems to be most popular when game and 
fish are most plentiful, not when they are scarce.^ 
In arguing that resource scarcity cannot account for trekking among 
South American Indians, some anthropologists  suggest social, rather than economic, origins for the practice. Speaking about the Kayapo (including the Mekranoti), Turner 
 argues that trekking exists primarily to allow male heads-ofhouseholds to express their dominance in matrilocal societies. According 
to this argument, men cannot "test" their control over their daughters in 
normal matrilocal situations because the daughters always remain in their 
father's household, making a test of control impossible. But the organiza-230 DENNIS WERNER 

"Data come from FUNAl (Fundaςäo Nacional do Indio—National Indian Foundation) records taken daily with a rainfall 
gauge installed in the village by a hydroelectric company. 
<^No information. 
tion of treks around males allows male heads-of-households to demonstrate their control by requiring that their daughters and sons-in-law remain with the household head regardless of the collective group affiliation 
of the sons-in-law. Turner argues that this demonstration of control is 
important, not only to the individual household head, but also to the 
"consistency and integration of society at large" . Turner 
is not very clear about why this elaborate test of control is so important to 
social integration, but the Mekranoti data do not support the argument in 
any case. First, not all treks are organized around males. Second, even in 
those that are, heads-of-households do not necessarily attempt to remain 
with their sons-in-law on trek. When the men divide themselves into their 
respective men's societies to go on all-male treks, young men follow their 
men's society regardless of their household affiliation. When the entire 
village goes on a ceremonial trek, teams are chosen based on the choices of 
the ceremonial sponsors. My daily records of living arrangements on these 
treks showed that sons-in-law are often separated from their fathers-inlaw. 
Another social reason for trekking may be the avoidance of conflict. In 
her discussion of treks and village splits, Bamburger  argues that 
conflict can be handled either by "voice" or by "exit." That is, individuals 
can either discuss their problems and search for a solution, or they can 
simply leave the troublesome situation altogether by moving away. The 
Kayapo, according to Bamburger, have chosen the "exit" option. Bam-''Data come from time allocation studies based on randomly timed visits to random households . 

Hunting Success by Season'' "Data come from the random sample of hunting and fishing trips that appeared in the 
time allocation studies. When someone was reported out hunting or fishing, I checked 
back later in the day to see what was obtained. If possible 1 weighed the returns. Otherwise I used standard figures to determine the weights of reported kills. 
burger is not entirely clear about which kinds of society would choose 
which option. But one possibility is that the choice is related to the amount 
of territory people have to move into. Unfortunately, this argument does 
not account very well for the South American data on trekking. Some 
groups (such as the Kuikuru or the Kamayura in the Xingu) have large 
territories, but they do not trek. 
Another possibility is that trekking might be related to the amount of 
conflict in a society. Presumably, groups with more internal fighting might 
have a greater need for the "exit" option. But this argument also fails to 
account for the South American data on trekking. Some groups with a 
great deal of warfare  do not trek as much as more peaceful groups  which are known for their lengthy 
journeys. Finally, many Mekranoti treks do not result in village split-ups at 
all, and so could not be attributed to the need to avoid intravUlage conflict. 
Of course, these critiques do not mean that individuals cannot take advantage of some treks to deal with conflict. Indeed, the Mekranoti mentioned 
instances in their past when they went on trek specifically to avoid enemies 
or to solve internal disputes. But the reason trekking exists in the 
Mekranoti's cultural repertoire in the first place must be something other 
than internal conflict. After all, not all Mekranoti treks resolve conflict, and 
the evidence from other South American groups suggests that conflict 
alone does not necessarily lead to trekking practices. 
Although protein scarcity may not explain Mekranoti trekking, it is still 
possible that the practice may result from other dietary considerations. 
Although he did not actually accompany the Indians on forest trips, Posey 
 emphasizes the importance of wild foods to trekking Gorotire-Kayapo in years past. In the days before steel axes, gardening may have been 
more difficult, and gathering a more attractive way of obtaining food. For 
certain groups, the ease of gathering wild foods (such as pine nuts among 
the Kaingang) may, indeed, explain why these Indians trekked. But the 
explanation is unlikely among the Mekranoti. Table 7.3 shows that the 
amount of time spent gathering on trek is minimal—less than an hour a 
week. And in the food consumption studies, gathered foods like Brazil 
nuts, honey, and agai, formed a minuscule part of the diet, usually 
amounting to no more than a handful of nuts or fruits a day. Garden 
produce provided the overwhelming bulk of calories. 
Parenthetically, I also think a word of caution is in order about reconstructions of past Kayapo treks. In discussing past expeditions, the 
Mekranoti insisted that they, too, lived entirely off gathered foods. But on 
eliciting further details, I discovered that the Indians, in fact, consumed 
garden produce from outlying forest gardens of Kayapo and non-Kayapo 
cultivators. Thus, statements about past treks may not be entirely reliable 
among the Kayapo. 
If the Mekranoti trek for food, it is not wild fruits they are after. More 
likely, they want extra meat, even if they are not short of protein. Individual Mekranoti sometimes told me that the village was going on trek for 

"Includes manufacturing handicrafts for sale and the collecting of Brazil nuts. 
«»Indudes housekeeping, child care, food preparation, manufacture, gathering firewood. 
"^Includes eating, sleeping, ceremonial activities personal hygiene, conversation, recreation, sitting idly. 
some other reason—such as collecting tortoises—but they would add that 
they, personally, were going along because they wanted to eat lots of meat. 
Dietary studies of a few families (Table 7.4) showed a clear difference in the 
amount of meat consumed while on trek and while in the village. On trek, 
people averaged almost three times more meat or fish a day than at home. 
The extra meat is due primarily to an increase in the amount of hunting 
men do while on trek. Table 7.3 shows how much time Mekranoti adults 
spend on different activities while on trek and in the village. On trek men 
hunt on the average 29 hours per week, compared with only 8 hours per 
week at home. 
If the Mekranoti are not short of protein, then why do they bother with 
all of this hunting? As argued elsewhere , the Mekranoti 
could live with less meat than they do, but they would probably not live as 
well. Extra protein helps in conferring greater resistance to disease, in 
allowing optimal use of other nutrients, and in protecting against manioc 
poisoning . I think it is the relative ease of gardening and hunting in the 
Mekranoti environment that makes the pursuit of a higher-quality diet 
worthwhile. Elsewhere in Amazonia people may be forced to accept lower 
dietary standards. They may be too busy with gardening to allow time for 
hunting, or they may not have enough land for trekking. On the other 
hand, in some places (such as the Xingu Park) fishing may be so productive 
that hunting is not worth the trouble. In summary, I suggest that people 
will be willing to go on trek whenever trekking is an easy way to improve 
one's diet. That is, trekking is primarily a way to optimize diet. The 
Mekranoti obtain sufficient calories with relative ease from their gardens 
, leaving them plenty of time to improve their diet 
through hunting. I shall return to this argument later in order to suggest 
possible cross-cultural tests. But first, I need to answer a possible objection 
to the "trekking for protein" argument. 
Some anthropologists might argue that extra protein can be obtained 
more easily through vegetable than through animal products. This would 

Meat Consumption on Trek and at Home" "Data come from dietary studies of a few families during different seasons of the year. 
 
make extra hunting for protein a wasteful activity. Beckerman  argues that in some areas of Amazonia, Brazil nuts {Bertholetia excelsa) might 
be an especially rich source of protein. Since the Mekranoti environment 
contains large numbers of Brazil nuts, which the Indians export for sale, I 
decided to check whether Brazil-nut gathering would be a better way to 
obtain extra proteins than would hunting. Table 7.5 shows that the 
Mekranoti must spend more time gathering Brazil nuts than hunting to 
obtain an equivalent amount of protein. This suggests that the Mekranoti 
are wise to devote more time to obtaining meat than to Brazil-nut gathering. But fishing is less productive than either of these two other pursuits. 
Yet the Mekranoti spend more time fishing than gathering Brazil nuts. This 
latter finding does not seem to support the protein maximization argument. But on closer inspection the apparent problem disappears. 

are also high in calories. Wu Leung  reports that Brazil nuts contain 640 
kcal for every 100 g edible portion. For meat, the figures would be closer to 
150 kcal, and for fish, closer to 100 kcal per 100 g edible portion. Mekranoti 
individuals currently consume an average of 63 g of animal protein a day. 
For them to acquire an equivalent amount of protein from Brazil nuts, they 
would also need to consume 3055 kcal in Brazil nuts alone, compared to 
only 455 kcal from the animal proteins they now consume. This would not 
leave much room for any other kind of food, and is hardly the best way to 
balance a diet. During my stay in the field, one Indian contracted scurvy 
and needed to be taken to Belem for treatment. Thus, I am particularly Table 7.5 
Return Rates for Game, Fish, and Brazil Nuts^ 
"Data come from studies of hunting, fishing and gathering productivities.  
«»Estimates for protein content are based on Wu Leung and Flores : 
Protein/100 g edible portion game is 20 g. 
Protein/100 g edible portion fish is 20 g. 
Protein/100 g edible portion Brazil Nuts is 13.2. 
interested in the loss of vitamin C that might come about if the Indians 
were to depend more on Brazil nuts for their protein. As it turns out, Brazil 
nuts are particularly low in ascorbic acid; even bitter manioc has more 
. It seems the Mekranoti are better off if they depend on meat 
or fish for their protein supplies and consume more vegetables for other 
nutrients. In short, they must not simply maximize protein but also limit 
the number of calories in their diet in order to meet minimum requirements 
for other nutrients. In short, they must optimize their diet. Cross-Cultural Tests 
As I have suggested, the explanation for trekking in lowland South 
America may lie in the concept of dietary optimization. I expect Indians to 
go on trek whenever trekking is the best way for them to improve the 
quality of the food they eat. This hypothesis sounds rather vague, but I 
think it can be tested. First, we must show that native South Americans do, 
in fact, attempt to optimize their diets. And second, we must evaluate 
whether trekking would be the best way to do this. Let us look at each of 
these propositions in turn. 
Are people diet optimizers? Some anthropologists have protrayed humans in technologically simple societies as interested primarily in minimizing the work they do rather than in improving the quality of their diets or 
in acquiring additional goods. That is, they see humans as energy minimizers rather than as diet optimizers. A classic example is Sahlins's  
attribution of "Zen" affluence to primitive peoples. According to Sahlins, 
by simply desiring less, people can feel satisfied with what they have and 
enjoy abundant leisure. Allen Johnson  formalized this argument in 
his linear programming analysis of Machiguenga data. In this study, Johnson attempted to account for how the Machiguenga allocate their work 
activities by assuming that these Indians are trying to minimize the amount 
of work they do while still acquiring minimum basic nutrients and avoiding too many calories. Johnson's linear programming model suggested that 
the Machiguenga ought to devote 100% of their subsistence labor to gardening and none to hunting and gathering. In reality, however, the Machiguenga devoted about one-fourth of their working hours to hunting and 
gathering. I suggest that the discrepancy between the model and reality 
may be due to the incorrect assumption that the Machiguenga are energy 
minimizers rather than diet optimizers. The linear programming model 
ought to be conceived differently. Instead of attempting to find a solution 
that minimizes the amount of work people do, the linear program ought to 
find a solution that optimizes diet (e.g., maximizes protein) while holding 
constraints on the number of hours people are willing to work. That is, 
hours of work may be the constant, while quality of diet varies. 

the Mekranoti work at different village tasks in the different seasons. The 
last column shows the total amount of work people do between the hours 
of 6:00 A.M. and 8:00 P.M. Monthly figures vary somewhat, but the averages for the different seasons are remarkably similar. It seems people have 
decided to limit their work to about 50 hours/week regardless of the season. There may be brief spurts of work or leisure (chi-square tests of work 
versus nonwork observations showed significant variations from the 50hour mean for the months of June, July, August, and January). But the 
variations seem to even out in a relatively short space of time (chi-square 
tests show that work inputs do not differ significantly by season). Except 
for brief periods, it seems people have allowed themselves only a limited 
amount of work, or stated differently, they have allowed work to expand 
to fill the space available. 

Adult Mekranoti Village Activities, by Season'* 
"In percentage of time between 6:00 a.m. and 8:00 p.m. 
^Includes eating, ceremonial activities, personal hygiene, conversation, recreation, sitting idly, and sleeping. 
^Includes hunting, fishing, gathering, and domestic animal care. 
•^Includes market activities, child care, food preparation, housekeeping, manufacture, and gathering firewood. 
This figure of approximately 50 hours/week of work also appears in other 
studies of time allocation in lowland South America. Using the same techniques as for the Mekranoti, Flowers and Ritter calculated work at 50.9 
hours/week for the Xavante, and 50.4 hours/week for the Kanela, respectively . Using different 
protocols, but the same basic random sampling technique, A. Johnson 
 arrived at a figure of 54.6 hours/week of work for the Machiguenga. 
The Bororo, who suffer from a high rate of invalidism, work somewhat 
less—44 hours/week . Although these constitute only a 
small sample of South American societies, the figures are impressive. Possibly, people at vastly different technological levels may work very different numbers of hours (indeed the whole question of what constitutes work 
becomes difficult with such broad comparisons). But within a given technological level (e.g., the combination of gardening, hunting, and gathering 
practiced by most South American Indians), there may well exist a set 
amount of time people are comfortable working. The major variations between these groups may not be in the total number of hours they work, but 
rather in the allocation of work to different tasks. The economic question 
facing the Indians is not how to minimize the number of hours they work 
but, rather, how to allocate their labor to get the best results. (In a hierarchy of needs, basic nutrients would have to be met first, of course, 
regardless of labor costs. But if these needs can easily be met, as seems to 
be the case among lowland South American Indians, then people may 
begin to work to improve their diets.) 
The question of diet optimization in Native South American might be 
examined further by comparing time allocation studies for the total amount 
of work done in different societies, and by carrying out linear programming analyses. If the hypothesis is correct, we should expect most groups 
to work roughly the same number of hours, although the amount of time 
they devote to different tasks should vary. (The variance across groups in 
overall work should be lower than the variance for specific tasks.) Also, 
linear programs that assume people are optimizing diet should give results closer to reality than linear programs that assume people are minimizing labor. Testing the hypothesis is beyond the scope of this study, but is 
entirely possible given the data now available on these societies. 
If the "diet optimization" hypothesis is supported, we can begin to 
check whether trekking is a means of improving diet. For this, we need 
data from different societies on the activities people pursue while on trek 
and the productivities of subsistence tasks on trek and in the village. Such 
data may be difficult to find given the diminishing importance of trekking 
in South American societies. But the few groups that still trek might be 
examined and contrasted with groups that do not trek. Cross-cultural tests 
may be difficult, but not impossible. 238 DENNIS WERNER 
Conclusion In this review of trekking among the Mekranoti-Kayapo, I have argued 
that protein maximization accounts better for the Mekranoti data than do 
alternative explanations that stress absolute shortages of protein, conflict 
resolution, social control by elder males, or the ease of gathering foods. I 
have also suggested some possible cross-cultural tests of a broader, "dietary optimization" hypothesis for trekking. Probably I have raised more 
questions than answers. The problem of trekking is a complex one, involving basic assumptions about the economic motivations behind human actions. If the questions raised here dealt only with trekking, they would 
probably not be worth the trouble answering, but I think more important 
issues are involved. We need to arrive at a better understanding of why 
people work, not only to understand cross-cultural differences, but also to 
understand our own society. The problem of trekking is closely related to 
these basic questions. 
^One question that might arise concerning the seasonality data is whether the higher 
hunting yields in the dry season are due simply to the fact that the Mekranoti trek more at this 
time and bag more game while on trek. The data do not support this view. Hunting yields are 
the same on trek as in the village. The failure of trekkers to bag more game may be due to two 
reasons. First, because many more men go out hunting while out on trek, there is more 
competition over the day's individual hunting area than in the village. Second, younger and 
inexperienced men do more of the hunting while on trek, and their hunting productivity may 
be lower. Were it not for these drawbacks, hunting productivity would presumably be higher 
while on trek. Unfortunately, my data are too limited to control for all of these factors. In any 
case, the recording of higher hunting yields for the dry season is not due to data gathered 
while on trek.

and Cultural Adaptability: 
of Eastern Ecuador 
NORMAN E. WHITTEN, JR. 
University of Illinois. Urbana- Champaign 
Ecological imagery among the Upper Amazonian Canelos Quichua native peoples of 
eastern Ecuador provides a paradliqmatic, symbolic template organized by sets of 
cosmological premties. Thti template, anchored by traditional shamanism and ceramic 
manufacture, is invoked in emotionally charged ceremonial and juropolitical contexts to 
express resistance to nonnative culture bearers who have a potential or real impact on the 
ecosystem and indigenous political economy. The imagery is described as it exists within a 
system of radical change. Mechanism are discussed whereby ecosystem knowledge and 
social structure are systemically linked to cosmological premises within a dynamic system 
of indliqenous cultural adaptability. [Ecuador. symbolism of Canelos Quichua, Amazonian cosmology, cultural adaptability, ecological imagery] 
THE COLOMBIAN ANTHROPOLOGIST Gerard0 Reichel-Dolmatoff  has 
done more than anyone else, it seems, to deflate the intellectual notion embedded in both 
structuralist rationalism  and cultural materialist thempiricism  that cosmology and ideology may be most productively considered as superstructural masking devices. Turning to the tropical rain forest 
ecosystem of the Northwest Amazon for his exemplar, Reichel  equates 
cosmology with "real infrastructure," and establishes a cognitively oriented research 
strategy paving the way to what he calls "the true ecological perspective," which

Carolina . He haa been conducting field research with Afro-American 
and indigenow peoples of lowland South America sine hi fint trip to Ecuador in 1961 
and haa also done fieldwork in the YOU.S. and Canada, He pied the faculty of the 
University of Illinois. Urbana, in 1970, where he is profaor of anthropology and interdiriplinary fellow in tropical ecology. He received a Cugecnheim Fellowship for 
1976-77 and recently publiihed two books. Black Frontiersmen and kcha RUM. He is 
completing a second book on the Canelm Quichua and editing one entitled Culfural 
Tratuformafion and Elhnicily in Modem Eatador. Whitten and his wife, Sihby. a 
medical dologint. maintain an applied program of health delivery care in eastern 
Ecuador through the auspim of the Sacha Runa Research Foundation, Urbana. 

consists . . . in describing and analyzing the meaning of the native's intelligence du milieu, and this interpretation can only be accomplished by an extremely detailed study of the native's evaluation, categorization, and "use" of such aspects as geographical features, fauna and flora, meteorological phenomena, sounds, colors, movements. . . . These and other categories are culturally coded and constitute a means by which "reality" is handled and man is fitted into the biotope.
More recently, in the 1975 Huxley Memorial Lecture, Reichel  stated his perspective with persuasive force:
Aboriginal cosmologies and myth structures, together with the ritual behaviour derived from them, represent in all respects a set of ecological principles . . . [which] formulate a system of social and economic rules that have a highly adaptive value in the continuous endeavor to maintain a viable equilibrium between the resources of the environment and the demands of society [Reichel-Dolmatoff 1976:308].
Amazonian cultures, he argues, present us with abstract philosophies and with ecological theory. While many contemporary ethnographers agree with Reichel's general position, a severe limitation for continuing investigations of ecological and symbolic processes through ethnographic studies of lowland South American peoples is evident: the vast majority of indigenous peoples in the moist tropics of South America are currently being subjected to radical shifts in ecology brought about by national and international efforts at resource extraction, cash cropping, tourism, evangelism, and colonization. It is uncomfortably easy to draw the conclusion that fragmentation of ideological systems is inevitable under conditions of such radical change.
The thorough disruption of native lifeways, including ethnocide and even genocide in some areas  is often based upon previous disruption . The devastating effects of disease and displacement on indigenous peoples and their societies, together with the fragility of the neotropical ecosystem , raise the question of whether a careful analysis of indigenous cosmology can do more than turn up crystalline fragments of shattered lifeways.
The purpose of this paper is to present evidence of structural coherence and adaptability manifest in an Upper Amazonian culture—the Canelos Quichua of east-central Ecuador—undergoing rapid, perhaps cataclysmic, change. The general perspective set out by Reichel-Dolmatoff is carried to a system of radical change to demonstrate the continuing value inherent in considering ecological and symbolic processes within a unified framework. Symbolic processing of ecological imagery among the Canelos Quichua, I shall argue, promotes effective communication about ecosystem dynamics and generates a myriad of metaphoric predications  allowing for both cultural continuity and change within an environment of ecosystem transformation.
This paper presents a more condensed analysis and interpretation of data than does the book Sacha Runa: Ethnicity and Adaptation of Ecuadorian Jungle Quichua . It also incorporates new data from intensive field research totaling eight additional months interspersed over a four-year period during which time remarkable ecosystem alteration occurred, and equally remarkable adaptive processes were evident.1
The Canelos Quichua of eastern Ecuador live in a large, hilly, rain-forest area ranging from the 3,000-ft. elevation of Puyo eastward to the 800-ft. elevations near the border of Peru. The heartland of nucleated settlements is on the Bobonaza River, extending north-
east from Puyo and Canelos to Villano on the Villano River and Curaray Uesus Pitishca), 
on the Curaray River. From other Bobonaza sites of Sarayacu, Teresa Mama, and Montalvo, the area fans out north to the Corrientes, Conambo, and Pindo Yacu rivers. The 
population is somewhere between 9,000 and 12,000 indigenous people, with major concentrations near trade-administration sites such as Puyo (the largest town in eastern 
Ecuador with its 10,000 nonindigenous population), Canelos, Pacayacu, Sarayacu, 
Teresa Mama, and Montalvo, on the Bobonaza River, and Curaray on the Curaray 
River. East of the Llushin River, the Pastaza River defines the boundary between "Jivaro 
proper"  and Achuara. The area south of the Bobonaza 
from the Yatapi River eastward is inhabited by Achuara Jivaroans who are variously 
allied with groupings of Canelos Quichua. There are also Achuara groupings north and 
northeast of Canelos Quichua territory on the Corrientes and Conambo rivers; intermarriage and other forms of alliance between Achuara and the Canelos Quichua exist. There 
are bilingual, bicultural Achuara throughout the Canelos Quichua area. 
The subsistence economy is based on swidden slash-mulch and more rarely slash-burn 
cultivation of manioc, sweet potatoes, taro, plaintain, palm, maize, and, in some areas, 
peanuts. Root crops are strictly in the domain of female labor and female knowledge. Extensive use of chicha, a female-produced manioc gruel drink of very low alcoholic content, is made. Plaintain and maize cultivation is primarily a male activity. Men hunt and 
fish, exploiting widely the diurnal and nocturnal protein resources of their acknowledged 
territory, and of selected territories of other indigenous peoples, as well . Gathering of nuts and fruits, insects, insect larvae, land and water snails and crustaceans, and turtles and turtles eggs, 
and poultry raising supplement hunting, fishing, and horticulture. The economic system 
of the JIvaro proper  and Achuara is essentially the same, except horticulture is perhaps less intensive and less extensive than that of the Canelos Quichua. 
There is a current turn to some large-animal husbandry (cattle, horses) in the western 
area, together with some small-scale cash cropping as part of a polycultural swidden. 
The egalitarian  social system is composed of intermarried segments of kinship units, aytlu, centered around powerful shamans who duel with other powerful 
shamans by reciprocating killing spirit projectiles. A division of labor by sex, and a concept approximating that of parallel transmission of kin class status  are also characteristic. Indigenous territories ramify out of each of the main trade-administrative sites. Within these territories, 
allied, intermarried, segments of larger kinship units are grouped into named subterritorial units, llacta. Achuara Jivaroans and some Jivaro proper are intermarried within 
Canelos Quichua kin units, and marriage exchanges with Achuara of the Conambo, Corrientes, Capahuari, and Copotaza river areas take place. 
Sporadic evangelical efforts by Catholic friars have centered on Canelos Quichua 
culture from the 16th century to the present with various emphases in the different territories. At the sites of Catholic Dominican penetration -especially Canelos, Pacayacu, 
Sarayacu, Montalvo and Curaray- there has been an obvious effort to impose an ecclesiastical sense of order. Dominican friars consistently confronted a situation whereby 
they were accepted for a brief period by Canelos Quichua groupings, asked to provide 
trade goods, medicine, and adjudicative advice, and then asked to leave. When the friars 
did not leave, indigenous people did, dispersing to various second, often hidden, garden 
plots from six-hours to two-days trek to the west, north, or east. The church eventually 
developed a system of visits wherein they would call for afiesta (which became jdsta, in 
Quichua) to be held at the trade-administrative site. During the fiesta, sacraments would 
be administered, disputes adjudicated, goods passed out, marriages performed, and

Today, in the focal areas of the Catholic mission activity, ceremonial enactment draws on a paradigm of ecological imagery to express rejection 
of the church. 
Prominent secular events in recent history include the Amazonian rubber boom of the 
late 19th century which penetrated the fringes of Canelos Quichua territory and 
stimulated the development of Puyo as an Andean-Amazonian trade site; Royal Dutch 
She Will oil explorations in the 1930s; sugar plantation development and consequent colonization begun in the 1930s and continually expanding; Peruvian invasion of Ecuador 
and penetration into this area in the 1940s; development of national infrastructure of 
roads, towns, and schools.in the western area of Canelos Quichua territory from the 1950s 
to the present; establishment and expansion of tea plantations, with more colonization 
from the late 1950s on; and return of oil explorations during the 1960s and 1970s. Near 
Puyo, indigenous peoples were dislodged from earlier homesites and resettled in jungle 
territory now ringed by plantations. Today, in the Puyo area, indigenous juropolitical activity draws on the same paradigm of ecological imagery to express rejection of national 
bureaucratic control of the ecosystem . 
The "paradigm of ecological imagery," which the Canelos Quichua draw on to express 
rejection of the Catholic church and to protest national bureaucratic control of the 
ecosystem, must be understood by reference to certain organizing aspects of their 
cosmological system. The most convenient way to present these aspects is by reference to 
some anthropological constructs. It must be emphasized here that the concepts presented 
are apparent in the remote jungle regions of Canelos Quichua culture, in the administrative nuclei of the river settlements, and on the outskirts of urbanizing Puyo. 
Canelos Quichua cosmology is based on concepts of animism as discussed by Tylor 
 and animatism as originally set forth by Marret . Some things are inbued 
with particular strength which allows individuals with a special relationship to such 
things to have a degree of control over other people, over social events, and much more 
tenuously, over nature. Such relationships are usually discussed, and presumably thought 
of, in terms of a person's soul, aya, and the soul of the particular thing. Spirits, supui, are 
also postulated as ubiquitous, and each spirit also has a soul. Spirits, too, have special 
relationships with things and with humans. Relationships between a spirit and a person 
or thing are discussed in terms of postulated relationships of the spirit's soul to the 
person's soul or thing's soul. 
Souls and spirits are grouped by certain dominant "sign-images" .2 For example, Sacha Rum, jungle person, is a multivocalic, associationally 
complex, ambiguous, open-ended, highly condensed  signimage which may be abstracted as symbol  to refer to the Canelos 
Quichua themselves, to other jungle people (livaroans such as Shuara, Achuara, Maina; 
Zaparoans such as Andoa-Shimigae, Zgparo; Candoshi such as Shapra, Murato; and 
Huaorani "Auca"). to a postulated spirit master, to the spirit of the forest, to the spirit of 
Datura, to a master of animals, in either singular or plural form. The dominant signimages may be grouped as transformations from even more condensed masterimages- Amasanga for Sacha Runa -and such condensation invokes discussion of two 
other highly condensed master-images: Nunghui and Sungui. The latter two concepts are 
common in Jivaroan culture . 
Discussion of any one of the three master-images invariably results in transmission of 
concepts and observations pertaining to the relationships between phenomena relegated

to the contrastive domains delimited by the master-imagery. Because discussion of any one of these master-images promotes discussion of the other two images, I refer to relationships inherent in the imagery as "paradigmatic" . Because the information conveyed is set forth in terms of relationships between plants, animals, insects, fish, humans, souls, and spirits of forest, air, soil and water, I refer to it as "ecological" . Ecological imagery among the Canelos Quichua is readily apparent in the "traditional" activities of feminine ceramic manufacture and decoration and in masculine shamanism; we will draw upon these highly ritualized, stylized activities in discussing the relationships among vision, thought, and knowledge.
This imagery is also evoked repeatedly in emotionally charged ceremonial and juropolitical contexts to express resistance to nonnative culture bearers who have a potential or real impact on the ecosystem. As such, the paradigmatic imagery may be regarded as providing a cognitive basis, or symbolic template , for protest. It is through the analysis of the structural aspects of protest in both ritual and political arenas that I hope to contribute to the analysis of intercultural relationships in settings of contemporary radical change.

From the outset of this analysis it must be understood clearly that concepts such as closed system versus open system do not fare well when applied to the Canelos Quichua. These people actively accept some aspects of ecclesiastical and nationalist sponsored change within their political economy. For example, adjudication of some interpersonal disputes, modification of shelter and clothing, some national infrastructural development—airstrips, roads, schools—and modern medicine for ubiquitous diseases are generally regarded as acceptable and desirable modifications of their indigenous

system. What is steadfastly rejected is the destruction of dominant aspects of the tropical forest-swidden-riparian ecosystem, and the Western capitalist set of utility values  attached to acceptable modifications.
Examples of the former include strong resistance to any sort of pesticides or herbicides whatsoever; resistance to water pollution, whether by urban waste or increased silt as a result of road construction; resistance to permanent fixed-field horticulture; and resistance to pressures to permanently clear forest areas larger than six to ten acres. Such resistance is always discussed in terms of ramifying strings of effect within the food chain which terminate in diseases of plant, insects, animals, birds, fish, and humans.
The resistance to the ideological aspect of political economy alternation seems to stem from an implicit premise of Western culture most recently explicated in anthropological literature by Marshall Sahlins . Writing of what he calls "Bourgeoise Society," Sahlins  states:
reproduction of the whole of nature constitutes an objectification of the whole of culture. By the systematic arrangement of meaningful differences assigned the concrete, the cultural order is realized also as an order of goods. The goods stand as an object code for the signification and valuation of persons and occasions, functions, and situations. Operating on a specific logic of correspondence between material and social contrasts, production is thus the reproduction of the culture in a system of objects.
I do not mean to imply here that the Canelos Quichua have anticipated Sahlins' exposition of the utility value inherent in Western capitalism. What I am suggesting is that the Canelos Quichua react adversely to the myriad of cues presented by nonnative intruders which consistently signal an alien cultural order expressed in Western values placed on goods. Adverse reactions are expressed directly through simple statements, and allusively, through paradigmatic imagery, in various contexts. The Canelos Quichua seem to sense a clear stress on the reproduction of an alien culture in a system of Western objects in virtually every encounter with nationalist and nonnationalist administrators, planners, developers, colonists, tradesmen, or evangelists. They accept many objects, indeed are dependent on some such as machetes and axes, but act and speak so as to suggest repeatedly a rejection of the logic of Western utility value as basic to the transformation of their cultural system.
We find Canelos Quichua paradigmatic imagery to be as clearly discernible in contexts involving indigenous response to an alien cultural logic and praxis as in contexts of shamanistic performance and pottery manufacture. Although the semantic transformations and symbolic processing of the master-image paradigm is context specific, the paradigm itself transcends contextuality. Paradigmatic structures are presumably part of a semiotic system by which imagery is transmitted through a contrastive set of signs. Symbolic processing is based on positional arrangement. With regard to symbolism, Dan Sperber  argues that
cultural symbolism focuses the attention of the members of a single society in the same directions, determines parallel evocational fields that are structured in the same way, but leaves the individual free to effect an evocation in them as he likes. Cultural symbolism creates a commonality of interest, but not of opinions.
By "evocation" Sperber  means "a search in the memory." Evocation together with "focalization," which is a "displacement of attention," constitute the two aspects of what he refers to as "symbolic processing." Ecosystem knowledge provides one type of shared commonality of interest based on habitat exploitation. Ideology refers to another type of commonality of interest based on cultural coding. Paradigmatic structures presumably unite such commonality of interests, providing shared means for objectifying correspondence structures drawn from observation and from extant codes.

The structure of ideas to which we now turn involves the intersecting spheres of influence of the three master-images in Canelos Quichua culture which can be invoked in multiple ways to express metaphors signaling known ecological dynamics, to suggest flexible animal-human and plant-human relationships, and to move people to ceremonial and political action. The paradigm is bound up in the transformational, positional, associational, and exegetical properties of Amasanga, Nunghui (also pronounced Núnghui), and Sungui (also pronounced Tsungui), which Canelos Quichua exegesis relates initially to the dynamics of forest, soil, and water.
In a somewhat overly simplistic sense we can say that this paradigm and its various qualities is anchored in Canelos Quichua culture through this developmental sequence: children learn, youths practice and experiment, parents teach, shamans know and transmit, powerful shamans (male) and master potters (female) manipulate and preserve. The paradigm, teased out of field data from daily life and made explicit by some native exegetes, is not necessarily, of itself, "symbolic," in Sperber's sense ; rather, its sign-imagery provides a cognitive basis for encoding and organizing "encyclopedic knowledge" necessary for symbolic processing.4
Canelos Quichua paradigmatic imagery is particularly elaborate in sectors where the
postulated force fields with sentient spirit foci interpenetrate. Rain, for example, is of the
water domain, and nearly omniscient evidence of Sungui's power in the world. But forest
spirit Amasanga controls the weather—thunder and lightening belong to him. The conceptual interplay between Amasanga and Sungui during thunderstorms links the nondomesticated forest with the water domain. Nunghuí, a strictly female spirit, controls the
soil base for the roots of garden life, and the pottery clay. Pottery is the medium for storing and serving chicha, regarded as a merger of root food (basically manioc) and water.
The conceptual interplay between Nunghuí and Sungui imagery during the production
of chicha and production of ceramics links the domesticated earth domain (garden soilpottery clay) with the water domain. Finally, for another example of allegorical interplay
between sentient spirit force fields, Amasanga is regarded as a domesticator of animals
(or as a principle of game animal increase) and Nunghuí as a domesticator of pottery clay
and root crops. The Amasanga-Nunghui conceptual interplay as allied sets of malefemale oppositions expressed in terms of division of labor and affinity metaphors also
links forest life and garden life with human life. For the Canelos Quichua, playing flutes,
singing songs, and telling myths punctuate discussion of Amasanga, Nunghuí, and
Sungui. These activities are, among other things, mechanisms for associational, or
analogic, linking of ecosystem knowledge to social rules and breaches, and social
dynamics to cosmological premises.
We turn now to a brief illustrative description of common intracultural strings of analogic association within each of the master-images before returning to their paradigmatic nature, and the paradigmatic expression of protest and resistance in emotionally charged ceremonial and juropolitical contexts.
Amasanga: Forest Spirit
Amasanga is a convenient starting point for a portrayal of this paradigm. He is frequently called Sacha supai runa, or Sacha Runa. He is sometimes known as sacha apu, jungle head; from the standpoint of a postulated spirit animal or soul of a contemporary animal he is paiba curaga, its chief. He is conceived of as generally male, but may also be conceptualized as enchantress, Sacha huarmi, jungle [spirit] woman, or Sacha chagra mama, female spirit master of plants not domesticated by humans but which are analogous to them (e.g., "deer manioc," "deer papaya," "catfish taro," "quail yam," "jungle sweet potato"). Before turning to the third master-image, Sungui, in this tripartite paradigm, it is essential to consider some aspects of visionary imagery in Canelos Quichua culture.
The human portal for visionary imagery is conceptualized by the Canelos Quichua as
deriving from huanduj, Datura (Solanacea Brugmansia sp.). The concept of Datura
stresses a spirit order which is alien from, but coexistent with, the waking world of
humans. Canelos Quichua exegesis relates Amasanga and Nunghuí as "of" or "from"
Datura. They are huanduj supai, Datura spirits. To induce a prolonged, overwhelming
vision of an otherwise disguised spirit universe, datura is used. The taker drinks squeezings from the inner bark and central pulp of the woody stem and is propelled within a few
minutes directly into the spirit visionary world wherein a continuous series of visualauditory-tactile episodes is experienced for six to 12 hours. Amasanga, as spirit shaman,
mediates between the spirits and human souls in the spirit world, and between the spirit
world and other unknown realms. For women, Amasanga as Sacha huarmi, or huanduj
supai huarmi, does the same. The return to the waking world is facilitated by a dual i amage of Amasanga, for men, of Nunghuí and Amasanga (in feminine transformation as
Sacha huarmi), for women. This return is characterized by coterminous interaction
within normal surroundings and continuing hallucinations for one to three days. During
this period the seeker sees both waking world and the spirit world. The former endures as
the latter fades away. Master-images Amasanga, and Nunghui are not in and of
themselves merely "visions," defined by the root muscuna, as muscuyu or muscuí. They
are foci for the organization of overwhelming visionary experience, sought occasionally
by a man or a woman, sometimes only once in a lifetime, never very often.
Vision itself comes fundamentally from dreams, and the Canelos Quichua seek to understand their dream mechanisms. It is important to them to be able to awaken after dreaming, to sift the jumble of images into an analyzable system of signs and symbols through which past knowledge is related to observed behavior. Flashes of insight in which one realizes the relationships of dream content to previous knowledge, experiences, or sets of encoding devices are also regarded "visionary." Long before dawn, men and women converse with one another about the night's dreams, and they seek dynamic, shared, perceived relationships among dream content, personal and social situation, graphic design, and mythic event.

Sungui: Water Spirit 
Sungui is the spirit master of the water domain and its postulated inhabitants, the yacu 
supai Tuna, water spirit people. This sentient force field is regarded as the ultimate 
source of biosphere, lithosphere, and atmosphere power; all shamanistic power ultimately derives from Sungui's domain. A manifestation of Sungui (as Urcu mama, see below), 
is found in the pupation dynamics of a large helgramite, mayfly larvae, or water bug, collectively classed apahuaicu, and conceptualized as a "water spirit stone" in feminine 
form. Where Amasanga and Nunghui embody the concepts of domestication of animals 
and plants, respectively, I tentatively suggest that Sungui metaphors portray a hydraulic 
principle wherein hydrosphere dynamics are signaled and an environment out of which 
life originally emerged. 
Sungui, whether male or female manifestation, is dressed in a beautiful multicolor 
cloak representing the spectrum with predominant red. This spirit sits on a bancu which 
is either the cayman, or the large water turtle, charapa. The latter, as a Sungui bancu, is 
Yacu julas, the feminine water representation of the rapids. Sunguts corporeal 
manifestation is the great anaconda, yacu amazun, which is also seen as his canoe (on 
which he travels under the water). The concept of increase in Sunguts domain is bound 
up with the freshwater porpoise, biijiu (or biigiu) in feminine form; the porpoise caused 
fish to proliferate. Men use porpoise substances to attract women and fish, and women 
may call the feminine porpoise, through song, to "go after" a man, and make him feel 
both guilt over transgressions, and fondness and longing for the woman. All amamn 
manifestations- including anacondas, boas, and the rainbow -span the force fields of 
water, garden, and jungle. Human use of amarun substance and symbolism is extensive, 
penetrating virtually every otherwise discrete domain. 
Symbolism of Sunguts domain in terms of feminine form, Yacu mama, reveals three 
dimensions. The first is the world dimension manifest in rivers, swamps, bogs, fog, rain 
clouds, rain, and in mire following excessive rain with flood, or where the jungle has been 
stripped of its plants and churned up by human or peccary activity. The second is the sky 
domain, where long dark clouds represent "high rivers." And the third is the 
underground domain with underground river^.^ In (or just beyond) the latter, fire and 
water are conceived of as meeting and the merger is manifest during volcanic and earthquake activity. There is also an association with the enduring souls of the dead, 
huanushca aya, and tenuously contained cosmic power, utipana, derived from the 
merger of fire and water. This power source itself is beyond the domain of Sungui, but 
discussion of Sungui evokes these associations and focuses discussants' attention on such 
postulated relationships. 
When discussing Sungui, or an aspect of this water force field, it is common to elicit 
the statement that all spirits and souls are really the same, that they all derive from this 
domain. But under no circumstances in Canelos Quichua exegetical portrayal can 
Sungui be related to the Datura visionary world. Sungui exists as a basic source of power 
derived ultimately, it seems, from the earth splitting merger of fire and water. Through 
the Datura spirits, Amasanga and Nunghui, control is generated to channel this power 
source of Sungui. Datura spirit control, and human control-usually referred to as a

resources and swidden resources. These resources, in turn, are culturally focused through the Amasanga and Nunghuí master-imagery.
Mythologically, the sun emerged from the water to bring day-night oscillating order and east-west directionality to the world. Amasanga and Nunguí emerged as discrete Datura spirits with the discrete color manifestations after the sun brought order to emerging human life. Sungui, as a force field with nondiscrete, continuous, spectrum order, encompasses the very world to which it is contrasted. For this reason it seems to be a metaphor for the hydrosphere. Uterine imagery is also apparent, though implicit.
Women making pottery symbolically embed their work within both the domains of Nunghui, as Manga allpa mama, and Sungui, as Urcu mama . The discrete white, red, and black rock and clay dyes, and red and white slips, signal both the discrete colors of Nunghui and the spectrum of Sungui. Burnishing is done with a water-polished stone from Sungui's domain. The most prominent graphic designs are those of reptiles, especially frogs and toads, which underlie shaman power, manioc force, and allegorically embody the principle of transformation.
During the ceramic manufacturing process, women converse gently, quietly, always without conflict, about ecosystem dynamics, especially those having to do with feminine domestication of plants and the male bringing of animals to clearings, and about new events in an outer world. As they do this they portray a juxtaposition of that which is regarded as ancient and traditional knowledge, with that which they perceive as new, and potentially destructive.
When the concept of destruction becomes overwhelming, women talk of ultimate return of the indigenous people to the domain of Sungui, of death, and of one's soul living in the water, as a fish. Such discussion invariably inspires one or another woman to think of a deceased female relative, and to sing a song to her enduring soul. Sometimes the soul of a cherished dead relative is symbolically built into the wet clay, and then fired into the pot to give both soul and pot strength and endurance.
Shamans also deal with the domain of Sungui. About 20 minutes after taking
ayahuasca, an ancient waterfall is heard encompassing the house. Then, in about ten
minutes more, two giant anacondas appear, undulating in parallel form around the
shaman. As the shaman "sees" them as sinchi amarun, strong boa-anaconda image,
women helpers who have snuffed tobacco water "identify" the dominant image as Yacu
mama. As this feminine image is so identified, it examines the shaman's shungu,8
"stomach-heart" area where his spirit helpers reside; only after this symbolic examination
signaling inner strength of the shaman as validified by Yacu mama has taken place does
the shaman receive the Amasanga image in the form of two great black jaguars. The
jaguars represent the animal transformation, tucuna, of the shaman himself. Curing is
done with the help of Amasanga, but within the power domain of Sungui in feminine
form. When killing is performed through the transformation of Amasanga into the three
merged feminine forms-under-water, under-earth, under-hill (cave)-the shaman is
possessed by Juri juri (who allegorically domesticated the amarun, a Sungui image).
While serving as seat, bancu, for Juri juri, the shaman tenuously, and destructively,
channels this Sungui-derived power into a spirit lance, or other spirit projectile to kill an
adversary shaman, or a member of the adversary's ayllu .
Knowledge, Vision, and Imagery
Knowledge, from the verb root ricsina, derives from a combination of learning by instruction, as children learn from parents and peers, and from observation. Pivotal imagery for children revolves around Amasanga for males, Nunghuí and Amasanga as Sacha huarmi for females. This imagery does not serve to disguise, or mask, observa-

tional information. Rather it is employed to heighten the interpretative possibilities of 
discrete observations by providing multiple transformational, associational, allegorical, 
and indexical avenues. The integration of retrievable pieces of information with visionary 
and ideological aspects is regarded as personal. However, certain individuals, the male 
shamans and female master potters, serve as paradigm builders and manipulators. 
Embedded in both shamanistic procedure and the pottery tradition is the paradigmatic, 
semiotic base of master-imagery and sign-imagery upon which symbolism is generated. 
Symbolic processing, in turn, communicates, reshuffles, refocuses, and evokes a dynamic 
integration of ecosystem knowledge, personal experience, social structural coherence, 
and ideology. 
Where cosmological imagery is acquired first from parents, and then from shaman 
and ceramicist, knowledge must be acquired in an encyclopedic fashion from all 
available sources. For example, the fact that maize depletes soil more rapidly than 
manioc unless beans are planted with it, the fact that a venemous snake bite often kills, 
the fact that caterpillars pupate into moths, the fact that the commercial naranjilla 
(Solanurn quitoense) plants are attacked by plagues and pests if planted as a 
monocultural crop, and the fact that governmental officials seldom listen to Indians 
when discussing the Upper Amazonian ecosystem, are all grouped as retrievable pieces of 
useful information. Such "facts" are indexed within systems of classification which include botanical, zoological, icthyological hierarchies probably much like those described 
by Berlin  for the Aguaruna Jivaroans of the Peruvian Montaiia. 
But, especially in the Puyo area, such classification is also used to call forth concepts 
drawn from the Antasangu-Nunghui-Sunguz master-image paradigm to conceptualize 
and interpret contradictions expressed by the increasing numbers of outsiders who vent 
their judgments, values, and incompetencies upon the rain forest-swidden-riparian 
ecosystem. Knowledge grouped within a dynamic master-image/sign-image paradigm 
allows for symbolic processing of semantic and encyclopedic material. 
Basic to the ideology of the contemporary Canelos Quichua is a learning process, called 
yacha (from the verb yachanu), through which an individual's thought or reflection, 
yuyana, dynamically and continuously integrates knowledge and vision. Care, also 
signaled by the verb root yuyana, is part of the knowledge-vision integration, for physical 
and social survival depends upon the nature of the result. Ancient culture and 
knowledge, yachai, are transmitted through the intracultural learning process, always 
embodying the knowledge gained from the outside, as well as inside. One who learns to 
control the learning process within himself, and to unite this self process with ancient 
culture, becomes yachaj, pOSsessor of the learning process, and moves into shaman status. 
Let us turn now to illustrative ceremonial and juropolitical dimensions of this paradigm 
and the ways by which resistance and protest are expressed through it. 
The basis of male activity signaling strength and control based on knowledge and vision is expressed in a simple ritual, called yachajui, whereby a man walks in a circle, 
beating a snare drum with about four beatdstep. The sound of the drum represents the 
thunder of Arnasanga controlling the weather; the buzz of the snare evokes vision. 
Female activity signaling strength and control based on knowledge and vision is bound up 
in the preparation of chicha with a higher than normal alcoholic content, and possibly 
with a mild psychotropic effect . It is also embedded in the tradition of producing ceramics. Both chicha production and ceramic production are tied to 
Nunghui and to Sungui, The power channeled by Sungui into the world of the living is 
rechanneled into human subsistence and human vision, respectively, by the underlying

hair ball, and sometimes aquatic larvae analogs. When a man "walks like Amasanga," 
women force excessive amounts of chichu on him, ideally serving it in particularly fine, 
decorated, ceramic bowls. They sometimes douse him in it; this activity signals torrential 
rain, promoting flood. 
During Dominican church-sponsored and initiated annual or biannual ceremonies, 
chayuj, called jistu via-d-vis church officials and outsiders, the Canelos Quichua enact 
waste and excessive exploitation of rain forest-swidden-riparian resources. They call to 
them souls of animals, distant humans, and spirits, and play, puglhna, with them and 
with the new forms of putative, imposed order. Male hunts are organized to obtain excessive amounts of monkey meat, large game meat, and fish. Women make excessive 
amounts of chicha, fungus chicha, and a heady cider-like drink from the drippings of the 
latter. As the ceremony gets under way, men enact the opposite of their normal respect 
toward animal souls through actions and words signaling abuse of monkey meat (symbolic of foreign people's captured souls domesticated by Amasungu, asJurijuri). They 
continuously drum the thunder of Amusunga, and the snares buzz a continuous calling 
for visionary experience. Women enact downpour and flood by forcing excessive amounts 
of chicha on everyone. Men also dance with women while drumming; women dance with 
men, but to Nunghui. Men play the song of the puspunchu bird on their transverse flutes, 
singing, essentially, "evil is coming but I meet it calmly." Men and women both sing 
songs which they regard as coming to them from deceased relatives and from spirits. 
They dance and sing as spirits or souls as time goes on, while continuing the enactment of 
thunderstorm, rain, and flood. 
Women's enactment of escalating chaos stresses graphic ceramic representation of the 
ancient and the new. They bring forth ceramic figurines illustrative of the immediate 
penetration of their ecosystem. For example, a pottery friar's boot, chalice, or hat brimming with chicha might be forced on a strong drummer. Figurines representing the 
duality of Canelos Quichua soul substance-that they are embedded in a new politicaleconomic order and so Alli Runa, Good Indian, but that they are of the forest system, 
Suchu Runa- are also displayed with wreckless abandon as women continue to soak participants in a life-sustaining chicha deluge. Such figurines invariably symbolize 
something of the new, and much of Amasanga or Nunghui in one or another transformation. Women may also produce ceramic snakes, symbolizing shamanistic death and the 
encompassing power of Sungui. Still later, figurines directly representing Sungui, including ceramic fuh, water mammals, or (rarely) aquatic larvae, are used to serve chichu 
and as plastic art vehicles of metaphoric animation. 
This activity builds in intensity for about 48 hours until a climax occurs just before 
dark, During this climax male dancers, representing an Amasunga image, make and 
bear a bamboo representation of a spirit anaconda, a Sungui image, while women's song, 
dance, and ceramic figurines bring the ancient garden soil-pottery clay spirit, a Nunghui 
image, to dance with them in the increasing mire of the plaza (which represents the pending domination of Sungui). As the men circle the plaza, and the intensity of dancing by 
women increases, the women call the water spirit people to join them in the dance area 
(and, by association, the entire territory under the domination of the church). When this 
occurs the territory under tenuous de jure church control is metaphorically encompassed 
and flooded by the unleashed power channeled through the water domain. 
Ideally, and actually if not blocked by the clergy, the bamboo representation of Sungui 
is then borne by the men representing Amasangu through the church in serpentine 
fashion, where possible crashing through the church's bamboo walls. When this occurs, 
ceremonial participants voice the possibility that the continuous enactment of a 
thunderstorm (Amasanga image) and the female dance (Nunghui image) in Sunguts

mire will, taken together, generate the mythic water-fire merger producing earthquake, volcanic eruption, and flood which, they say in such contexts, swept the ancient people eastward toward the sea. Should a flood recur, the Canelos Quichua say in such settings, they will swept away with the church. But, they add, the indigenous people will become regenerated through woody fungus, ala , while the church will be destroyed. Such enactment of the Amasanga-Nunghui-Sungui master-image paradigm would appear, then, to represent the possibility of the sacrifice of the contemporary people for the sake of freedom from the church. It is to be taken as a "root paradigm," in Victor Turner's  and Firth's  terms. Turner  summarizes the concept of root paradigm in this way: ". . . consciously recognized (though only on occasions of raised consciousness) cultural models of an allusive, metaphorical kind, cognitively delimited, emotionally loaded and ethically impelled, so as to give form to action in publicly critical circumstances."
Juropolitics in the Puyo area of Canelos Quichua territory is based upon the socioeconomic clash of evolving moist tropical subsistence ecosystem exploitation with that of Western cash-crop market economy (cf., Whitten in press). The latter is causing population expansion at the expense of ecosystem maintenance. Shamans have access to both economies. Within the indigenous system they are stem kindred nodal figures of the kinship-subterritorial (ayllu and llacta) socioeconomy. Because they charge fees for curing, killing, and assistance in accumulation of shamanistic power for others, they have more stable access to cash than do most nonshamans. Also, because of their nodal position in the indigenous socioeconomy, they are often the recipients of payments for land sales as Western concepts of land ownership become pervasive.
The shamans provide the creative, adaptable set of transformable interpretations linking cosmology, social structure, and ecology within any subterritory, at any given time.
They serve as brokers, so to speak, between private symbolism and public symbolism . Since it is common for people seeking greater knowledge and
sharper vision to visit shamans in various territories, shamanistic interpretations are continuously disseminated. Such spread of interpretations occurs with news of developments
throughout eastern Ecuador. For example, a shaman in Pacayacu who resides in an area
experiencing petroleum exploration and the building of a new school may be visited in
the same week by a client from Puyo, where urban growth and development are continuous, and another from Sarayacu where Protestant evangelism is struggling with
Dominican quasi domination. Another shaman living near Puyo may be visited by a
Jivaro proper client from the Upano Valley who is a member of the Shuar Federation,9
and an Achuara from the lower Copotaza River who is an enemy of the Jivaro proper, but
considering affiliation with the Federation as a political act to reaffirm the generalized
Jivaroan (Shuar) ethos of resistance to pending colonization. Ecosystems vary in these
areas, and each is, from time to time, subject to very different kinds of intrusions.
Because of the steady pattern of movement to and from various shamans, there is an
evenness of spread of ideological interpretation throughout the Canelos Quichua culture
area, while private conceptualization and synthesis remain highly diverse.
Sons and sons-in-laws of powerful shamans who demonstrate language competence and social competence vis-à-vis outsiders are selected and/or elected (depending on the area) as representatives of indigenous groupings to governmental, military, and commercial-administrative-ecclesiastical personnel. The elder male (father, or father-in-law) shaman-younger male (son or son-in-law) spokesman union is very close, and severe competition with cognate dyads is normal.
