The social complexity of the Central Brazilian groups may be due to alternation between 
dispersed, mobile groups and large village aggregates. In particular, the circular village plans, 
crosscutting moieties, elaborate systems of name transmission, age grades, ceremonials, and 
sporting events for which these societies are known, may have served as cultural means for 
integrating semi-autonomous foraging units into unitary, annually reconstituted villages, 
providing for the control of conflict between groups, the distribution of garden produce 
throughout the village, and the mobilization of warriors for defense and raiding . 
(2) The three habitats distinguished may help to elucidate some anomalies of 
contemporary ethnography. As Lathrap  suggested, a number of societies known 
ethnographically may be former riverine groups driven into upland areas where they 
dispersed into smaller villages undergoing depopulation and deculturation. The Amahuaca 
, the Isconahua  and the Sirion6  may 
be such groups. The distinguishing characteristic, in the absence of some direct evidence, 
may be traces of former social complexity, e.g., inherited chieftainship, not ordinarily 
associated with such small, simple societies. 
Other upland groups may have moved recently to the edges of rivers, following the 
destruction of riverine peoples by warfare, introduced disease, and exploitation brought by 
national populations in recent times. This process was certainly hastened by the great rubber 
boom of 1882-1910 which sent hordes of rubber tappers into the forests near rivers to 
extract latex from Hevea brasiliensis . Some examples of such 
groups may be the Yanomamo , the Sharanahua , and the Tukuna . These societies, in contrast with 
those above, may exhibit characteristics less appropriate to complex sedentary life than to 
more migratory upland life. They may lack the ability to construct water craft and fishing 
techniques, indicating their recent arrival in riverine habitats. 
(3) The foregoing may also have some relevance for the study of warfare. Prior to 
European penetration, I suggest that two fairly different patterns of warfare existed in 
Amazonia. The first was a relatively organized form of military campaign, perhaps involving 
multilocal mobilization of warriors, and having as an outcome the occupation and control of 
territory. This pattern could have been beneficial to groups in the context of a circumscribed 
riverine habitat depending on fishing and farming of alluvia . There is some 
indication that such warfare patterns prevailed among the Omagua of the Central and upper 
Amazon  and the Tupinambi of Brazil's East Coast . 
The second pattern of warfare, characteristic of upland areas, was the organized raids 
and counter raids motivated by vengeance, sorcery, headhunting or the capture of women 
which was virtually ubiquitous in the Amazon basin. This pattern probably did not have the 
consequence of the taking and holding of territory for agricultural purposes . Given the shortage of game in upland areas, a more important outcome of such 
raiding might have been the dispersion of settlements over wider areas and a consequent 
relief of pressure on game resources.' 
CONCLUSION' O
I have suggested that the size, form, and permanence of settlements, social complexity, 
and warfare patterns may vary in the Amazon basin with differences in the availability of 
animal proteins in the diet. The forms observed were not the only forms which are possible. 
A number of processes could have yielded different outcomes. For example, greater reliance 
on a seed crop or domestication of a native animal species for food could have had striking

effects. The encroachment of powerful territorial states from outside can and did have striking effects on local populations in terms of the variables discussed here. The imposition of wages, contracts, rents or tributes can lead a population to exceed crucial tolerances of parameters like fallow periods, intensity of hunting, or land clearance, leading to such responses as malnutrition, migration, or depopulation. Cultural practices which had, as consequences, the prevention of such excesses may therefore be characterized as the unconscious "wisdom" of a people, even though the particular practice might appear "irrational" to the ecologically unsophisticated observer.
It has become fashionable in recent human ecological thought to downgrade the importance of the natural environment in favor of other factors taken as independent variables such as demographic change, social dynamics, or even the "ideological environment." These are taken as though they were somehow exogenous to the population under consideration. This is unfortunate, first of all because it tends to blur the concept of environment, making it less useful for ecological analysis. Secondly, this approach may prematurely deprive us of one of the few firm bases on which to begin analysis of a socio-cultural system. It is undeniable that the natural environment has often been dealt with simplistically and that specific kinds of population interactions with the environment are more important than the environment taken alone. But the environment, nevertheless, does provide a set of limits and possibilities that is external to the population itself. This is true, even when we deal with the application of modern industrial technology to the tropics .
In the case under discussion, I have suggested that the Amazonian environment imposes limitations on cultural development of a society dependent on a neolithic technology. That the indigenous populations of South America may have developed mechanisms which prevented their degrading their environments is a tribute not so much to their rationality as to their responsiveness to signals and cues in the environment comparable to that possessed by other animal populations. Recently, many of these populations have been compelled to abandon or modify these practices. The results have been, in many cases, malnutrition, disease, runaway population growth, etc. As the large, politically centralized societies extended their control over smaller, autonomous groups the latter have been forced to ignore the accumulated wisdom of their ancestors.
This section explains how the values for daily per capita protein intake in Table II were obtained. Although it is less desirable, a per capita value was calculated rather than one based on body weight or the actual distribution of age and sex in a population. This was done for the sake of uniformity since the latter data were not available for the cases used. Including infants below one year of age has the effect of lowering the overall average value most of them would not ingest protein of animal origin apart from mothers' milk. Nevertheless, I believe that there may be a systematic bias which inflates the estimates of animal protein in the diets of these groups. This is so because in all but three cases (Wayaná, Miskito, Shipibo) the measurements were made during periods of "normal" hunting activity and do not reflect the decline in hunting and fishing which usually occurs during the rainy season and during the clearing and burning of new swiddens. No attempt was made to correct for this probable bias in the measurements. Thus the data do not reflect seasonal or annual variation in protein capture rates.
As will be seen, the calculations depend upon a number of assumptions, e.g., the comparability of average size for species from one area to another. Obviously, it would be far more desirable to work with more and better data in arriving at these estimates. One hopes that such data will be collected by field workers in the near future. Nevertheless, in working with these scattered and imprecise data, the values for protein capture were remarkably consistent with each other and with the expectations discussed in the text.

Miskito: Nietschmann  kept daily records of game, meat, and fish brought into a

Kaingáng: Henry  describes a 5-week hunt by a party of 18 which left the base
camp. Henry states that meat from a hunt would last up to 3 days, so I added 3 days to the

(90.90 kg) each were killed. Ten monkeys and about 20 birds were also bagged but weights
are not given. From Nietschmann  and Eisenberg and Thorington  I took
average live weights of monkeys at about 5 kg each and birds at 1 kg each, summed these

solution was to assume that the entire Kaingáng population of 106, both those at the post
and those on the hunt, shared the game and that no other game was coming in at the post.

4Given the patchiness phenomenon and the rather good possibility of local extinction from over efficient predation, one might undertake a fresh look at the many taboos on the killing and eating of certain animal species among Amazonian tribes. These taboos, which sometimes are general, sometimes specific to particular statuses or life stages, may have the effect of averting the hunting out of certain animals.
5Two species of peccary (Tayassu spp.) are found throughout the Amazon Basin, the collared peccary (T. tajacu) and the larger white-lipped peccary (T. pecari). Gilmore asserts that
either species would seem to be potentially domesticable because the young tame easily, but unless castrated, the males soon become vicious and unmanageable [1948:382].
According to Crandall's authoritative work , the collared peccary breeds frequently in captivity (i.e., in zoos), the white-lipped species less often. But, he cautions,
while there is still some question concerning the reputed ferocity of peccaries in the wild, there can be no doubt that captive animals, especially males, may sometimes be aggressive...[p. 529].

introduced diseases. The introduction of plantains and steel tools may have set the expansion off, creating an imbalance between the ability to produce calories and protein availability. This expansion may have intensified a pre-existing raiding pattern. Differential infanticide favoring males provided a mechanism which motivated and sustained raiding as a prominent feature of Yanomamö life . Continuous and ruthless conflict within the boundaries of the society preadapted them to fierceness against other groups on their border, facilitating expansion. Chagnon  suggested that warfare—not soil exhaustion—was the proximate because of distant relocations (or "macro moves") of villages, but these moves could still have the function of preventing the depletion of game stemming from the overconcentration of this expanding society.
Chagnon has rejected this interpretation, and referred back to his circular chain of processes in which male fierceness generates a preference for boy babies, female infanticide, thus a shortage of women, raiding for women, more warfare, and consequently a status structure offering high rewards to the fiercest. Arguments over food, states Chagnon, sometimes precipitate mortal confrontations:
I could easily fight with Möawä 'over a banana,' and one of us might get gravely injured or killed. But the banana would have been only a very insignificant aspect of the fight. The real reasons for the fight, ... would have to do with the status system. It would be ... a gross misrepresentation to say that the because of fight X was 'bananas'.... Recent trends in ethnological theory are tending more and more to crystallize around the notion that warfare among swidden cultivators must always be explainable in terms of population density, scarcity of strategic resources such as territory or "proteins."... The Yanomanö are an important society for their warfare cannot be explained in this way [1974:194-195].
Chagnon seems to impute to some the notion that the Yanomamö are "fighting over" territory or proteins. No one, to my knowledge, has suggested this. The proposal made by myself and also by M. Harris  does not invalidate Chagnon's political and motivational analysis, but it does provide a plausible causal explanation. Improved opportunities to secure resources may be an unintended or "latent" consequence of Yanomamö warfare, and it does not depend on the actors' perception of this as a goal. To discredit this suggestion would require a demonstration that the settlement and migration patterns encouraged by Yanomamö have a negligible or negative effect on protein capture, and this Chagnon has not yet provided.
10The conclusions are presented as if it had been adequately demonstrated that proteins of animal origin are a limiting factor on native population densities and sizes in South America. In fact, it has not. One of the purposes of writing and publishing this paper has been to stimulate fieldworkers to attempt to measure such variables as dietary intake in the societies in which they conduct research. A number of people who have commented on this paper's findings have suggested that members of one or another society obviously do not suffer from protein scarcity since they have been observed gorging themselves on meat. One may easily gain this impression from Holmberg's eyewitness account of the Sirionó . But without careful measurement, even first-hand observation can be misleading as may be seen from the per capita animal protein intake value calculated from Holmberg's own data. The dialectic between theory and data has now brought us to the point where only more and better measurements will yield satisfactory results.

File_Name: American Anthropologist - September 1979 - Beckerman - The Abundance of Protein in Amazonia  A Reply to Gross.txt
Full_Text:
The Abundance of Protein in Amazonia:
University of California, Berkeley
A critical examination of Gross's hypothesis that aboriginal Amazonian populations were limited to low levels by lack of adequate protein resources concludes that (1) evidence either for or against the hypothesis is still in short supply; (2) the role of vegetable protein in aboriginal diets needs much more attention and may ultimately overthrow the protein-limitation hypothesis; (3) the abundance of animal protein in the tropical forest has likely also been underestimated; (4) the abundance of people in precontact Amazonia may well have been underestimated as well. [Amazonia, protein, cultural ecology, limiting factors, population].
GROSS  HAS ARGUED that the size, permanence, and density of aboriginal settlements in the Amazon basin were and are limited to low levels—for the most part the levels manifested today by the surviving indigenous peoples—by insufficient protein resources. This paper takes issue with that claim and submits that the evidence to support Gross's conclusion is fragmentary and ambiguous enough to permit a contrary claim: that, currently at least, protein sources may be underexploited in the Hylea. (Gross has cast the argument in the best possible way by directing attention not only to the broad category "protein" but, more specifically, to the crucial matter of the relative proportions of the essential amino acids, which determine the quality of any protein in human nutrition. That vital information is utterly lacking for many of the protein foods discussed below, but if the issue is raised in anthropological circles, we may eventually gain the missing data.)
I want to stress at the outset that I am not hostile to the idea of protein limitation of human populations per se. In fact, I have argued  that precisely such a mechanism as Gross suggests did indeed limit the size of the populations inhabiting some of the Polynesian islands. The difference is that in the Polynesian case we have (1) a situation in which the flora and fauna are notably impoverished, for good biogeographical reaons; (2) a situation that therefore provides us with a good a priori reason to suspect that a limitation of resource alternatives may have been an important factor in the population dynamics of the people involved; (3) recent firm estimates of the size of the
STEPHEN BECKERMAN received his Ph.D. in 1975 from the University of New Mexico. His dissertation, based on three years of fieldwork in Colombia, was an examination of energy flow parameters among the Bari, a tropical forest tribe of the Maracaibo basin. He was a postdoctoral fellow at Berkeley for two years and is now an assistant professor at Southern Methodist University. Most of his publications have dealt with either energy or the cultural ecology of the humid tropics. He is the owner of a large dog.

sizes with a reasonable measure of what are reliably believed to have been the major precontact protein sources. In the Amazonian case, as I shall show below, all of those 
elements are missing. 
The organization of this paper follows that of Gross at the beginning, examining first 
his evidence for the size of the precontact Amazonian human population, then his conclusions as to the abundance of fish and terrestrial vertebrates. Next the issue of aquatic 
mammals and reptiles is addressed. The following section considers invertebrate protein 
resources, largely ignored in Gross's treatment. Then the real nub of the question, the 
presence and abundance of vegetable protein, is considered in three subsections: wild, 
cultivated, and fermented varieties. Thr conclusion returns to the problem of the size of 
the precontact human population and addresses itself to the question of why, if there is 
all that protein, there are not more people eating it. 
Gross  cites Steward's and Faron's  estimate of the precontact 
population of Amazonia. The latter authors consider an average density of 0.6 persons/ 
sq. mi. as likely for the South America tropical forests. Gross transforms that figure into a 
density of l/sq. km., although the correct conversion of the English into metric units is 
0.23/sq. km. In fact, the most recent evaluation of Amazonian populations  suggests an overall density of 0.7/sq. km. (1.81/sq. mi.), with a range from 
O.l/sq. km. (for superhumid upland forest) to 14.6/sq. km. (for flood plain) . Although Denevan is admirably conscientious in the attention he gives to the 
magnitude of depopulation caused by epidemic disease, I shall argue in the final section 
of this article that there is good reason to suspect that even his "revisionist" figures may be 
unduly cautious. The major point at issue here, however, is not precisely whether there 
were a great many people in the precontact Amazon, but, rather, whether there was 
enough protein to sustain a great many people. It is this second question that is addressed 
in the following sections on the various kinds of resource options in the rainforest. 
Taking the Kuikuru as his case in point, Gross  concentrates on Carneiro's 
 assertion that this single village of 145 people could have raised its agricultural production sufficiently to support a population of 2,000 while remaining completely sedentary. Noting that manioc, which forms the agricultural base of the Kuikuru diet, 
is nearly worthless as a protein source and that the Kuikuru depend mostly on fish for 
protein, Gross attempts to calculate the size of the territory that would be necessary to 
supply enough fish for the protein requirements of 2,000 people. 
He begins by assuming a need for 50 gr. of protein/person per day. or a total, for a 
population of 2,000, of 182.5 t. per year. Let us accept that assumption. He then speculates, citing Cable  and Hickling , that the upper Xingfi rivers of the 
Kuikuru habitat can produce about 5 t. of fish/sq. km. per )ear. Since Cable confines 
her article to the fisheries of the United States, and Hickling's book is a discussion of fish 
culture in artificially stocked lakes, tanks, and cages, it is difficult to see how the figure is 
obtained.  
Unfortunately, we have very little idca of the secondary production of thc Amazon

river-black, white, or clear . There are certainly cases in which it depends 
as much on allocthonous matter as on autocthonous primary productivity, but hard data 
are nearly nonexistent. 
For the upper Catatumbo system of the Maracaibo basin, a region completely unconnected to Amazonia although climatically similar, I calculated figures  that can be manipulated to suggest that the indigenous inhabitants 
of the area actually harvest about 1.2 t. of fish/sq. km. per year, and that the Indians 
and the homesteaders together take something like 2.5 t./sq. km. per year. Nevertheless, 
I would neither place great confidence in the basic data, nor would I claim that actual 
harvest nearly equals potential harvest in this case, nor that this case is strikingly informative about the Amazonian situation. I have mentioned the datum to indicate the 
possibility that Gross's figure may be seriously undergenerous, for my figure probably errs 
in dramatically underestimating homesteader fishing, while it deals with an area much 
smaller and faunistically much poorer than Amazonia, and is a measure of actual and 
not potential maximum catch-all errors that would tend to make it fall short of the 
overall potential of the Amazonian rivers. Nevertheless, there really are no good data. It 
is quite likely that, given the variability that surely exists among the various regions of the 
Hylea, there is some river system that does have a potential yield of 5 t./sq. km. per year. 
Let us assume that this river system is the XingG. 
Given the dietary requirements and fluvial productivity figures just developed, it is 
straightforward to calculate a need for 36.5 sq. km. of water to provide fish for 2,000 
Kuikuru. Gross  limns that as 365 linear km. of 100-m.-wide streams and calls 
it a "vast extent of river." But is it? Returning to the Catatumbo basin for comparative 
figures is somewhat safer here, since there is no reason to think that the land-water ratio 
there is much different from that of any other tropical rainforest. For that region I 
calculated, from measurements based on aerial photographs and ground reconnaissance 
 that flowing water equaled about 2% of all surface area. 
Geisler et a].  state that water bodies of all kinds occupy between 1% and 2% 
of the land area of the Amazonian lowlands, depending on season. Let us take the 
smaller figure. Transferring that ratio to the water surface required by Gross indicates 
that 2,000 Kuikuru would have to fish over an area of 3,600 or 3,700 sq. km. In the form 
of a square, such a territory would have a side of about 60 km. 
Gross argues  that "the distances involved would probably make it impossible, given the Kuikuru technology, to exploit and defend this vast extent of river." Yet, a 
village in the middle of a square of the dimensions just noted would be only 42 km. (25 
mi.) from its corners. That distance is hardly more than a day's journey for the superbly 
athletic inhabitants of the Hylea and scarcely seems incapable of defense. A more 
realistic deployment of the people over the land would have a main village located at the 
confluence of the major river and its main tributary, thus ensuring rapid riverine access 
to the largest concentration of water within the territory. A small camp or two could easily put the rest of the territory within a few hours' walking distance. 
Finally, and most important, the discussion to this point has assumed that fish are not 
mobile and that one must cover 36.5 sq. km. in order to reap the harvest of 36.5 sq. km. 
Clearly, such is not the case; in fact, fish is an excellent example of a resource that, at certain times of the year, comes to the consumer. 
In sum, the evidence currently available does not proscribe the possibility that the 
Kuikuru might well have fed themselves adequately on fish as well as manioc at the upper 
population bound of 2,000 individuals.

Turning to animal species, which he takes to be the only important protein alternative 
for peoples without access to a major stream, Gross  begins by quoting Fittkau 
and Klinge  to the effect that the animal biomass comprises only 0.02% of the 
total biomass of the section of central Amazonian rainforest they studied. There are two 
points to be made in response to that citation; the first has to do with the difference between a percentage and an absolute quantity and the second with the character of the 
particular patch of forest where those investigators did their work. 
The second point is disposed of more easily and can be considered first. The patch of 
forest in question is not, as Gross  states, "about 100 km. south of Manaus;" it 
is, rather, as Fittkau and Klinge  state, "at km. 64 of the Manaus-Itacoatiara 
road," that is to say, almost due east of Manaus on the north bank of the Amazon, and no 
more than 32 or 40 km. from the main course of the great river itself. Now, the field 
study that Fittkau and Klinge report in the article under discussion was made in 1970, 
several years after the road had been opened (Hilgard O'R. Sternberg: personal communication). There has been hunting pressure from homesteaders and professional 
hunters and sportsmen as they gained access to the area while traveling up and down the 
Amazon in addition to the pressures of similar groups leaving from the city of Manaus 
and subsistence hunting by the road-building crews who had passed through the area only a few years before. The area is now directly accessible from the road itself. It is hardly 
surprising that "hunting for human food is never effective (even by experienced people)" 
. The area has been hunted-out. 
Even at that, when we return to the first point raised, the difference between a percentage and an absolute amount, we find that Fittkau and Klinge  estimate an 
animal biomass of 200 kg./hectare, of which 7% or 8% belongs to the vertebrata, with 
over half of this figure coming from mammals . These figures 
give us about 1,400 kg. of vertebrate biomadsq. km. If that is the rock-bottom figure at 
which hunting with firearms is no longer effective, it is likely that we are dealing with an 
area that, before it was hunted out, was not in any sense impoverished. Smith  
presents Amazonian hunting data that accord well with that view. 
Quite different comments are in order for the next reference that Gross cites. This is 
the famous study  of El Verde Forest in Puerto Rico. It is quite 
true, as Gross  reports, that only a very small fraction of the animal crop found 
at El Verde consisted of birds and mammals; however, once again, we are dealing with 
an area subject to hunting pressure (even stronger and of longer duration there than near 
Manaus). Furthermore, this location is on an island, which from biogeographical considerations would be expected to have a depauperate fauna. Finally, introduced species 
have played havoc with the native varieties, as the following quotation from Odum, 
Drewry, and McMahan  makes clear: "The few native ground mammals are 
extinct, and the niches are occupied by the ordinary cat, roof rat, house mouse, and 
mongoose. 
It comes as no surprise that El Verde Forest is less than a bountiful habitat for human 
hunters. 
However, rather than rest with the conclusion that human activity invariably leads to 
diminished game resources, we must recall that both of the study plots considered above 
have been the object of a particular, and nonindigenous, exploitive regime. Linares 
 and Nations and Nigh  point out that in the tropical forests of Central 
America, game animals thrive on abandoned swiddens, which are considered to be 
managed wildlife areas. Thus, the centrifugal force of hunting, in trimming and driving 
away animal populations, must be balanced against the centripetal force of agricultural

modification of the vegetation, in attracting and augmenting game-animal populations, 
in a well-integrated aboriginal subsistence strategy. 
Nevertheless, it will not do to belabor the terrestrial vertebrate productivity of the 
tropical forest overmuch. Although it is probably a good bit higher than is suggested in 
Gross's study , there is little question 
that there are more bountiful protein resources than game. Fish is clearly one of these. 
Other likely candidates can now be considered. 
Although Gross  concedes the possible importance of caymans and 
tortoises as well as turtle eggs, he characterizes the last as having a brief season of availability and an uncertain significance in the diet. The topic is not considered further. 
An excellent article by Smith  supplies additional information concerning the 
Amazonian river turtle, Podocnemis expansa: 
The aboriginal population of Amazonia, recognizing the food value of these 150 pound reptiles, 
penned them for year round cropping. Orellana's hunger-plagued expedition down the Amazon 
in 1542 was relieved to find a village with more than a thousand turtles in flooded enclosures and 
wells . A century later along the same river, Acuiia  reported that 
there was hardly a village containing fewer than a hundred corraled turtles and that the natives 
were thus ignorant of hunger. 
Smith  continues with a list of 29 tribes and groups of tribes reported to 
eat turtles or their eggs and/or to have the turtle as an important figure in art or 
mythology. The tribes occupy the bulk of the Amazon basin, the only missing area being 
that between the Nap0 and the Uaupes. They also occupy the upper and mid course of 
the Orinoco. 
The abundance of turtles seems to have been truly remarkable. Smith quotes Matthews 
 to the following effect regarding the Rio Madeira: 
For miles as far as the eye can see, which hereabouts runs straight for some 6 or 7 miles, were continuous rows of turtles at the water's edge: the rows being eight or ten deep, many thousands must 
have collected together. 
Apparently, the occasion was the egg-laying season. It seems from additional information provided  that the turtle population was able to tolerate an annual harvest of over 12 million eggs, the product of 100,000 to 150,000 adult females, for 
nearly a century before the population entered a serious decline. In fact, the harvest was 
four times that figure in the 1860s. Those figures concern only eggs. Smith also notes 
 that over 50,000 turtles were taken in state turtle corrals at Barcelos on the Rio 
Negro between 1780 and 1785. 
Clearly, aboriginal exploitation was less orgiastic than those figures for commercial exploitation. Nevertheless, the case seems quite strong for a real abundance of turtles and 
for year-round availability of their meat along the courses of major rivers. Eggs are, of 
course, a seasonal resource although the Otomac at least did smoke them in baskets to 
preserve them . 
Although alligators and caimans are carnivorous and thus cannot be expected to provide nearly as abundant a source of meat as the herbivorous turtles, it is germane to mention that many riverine peoples of the Amazon and Orinoco basins are recorded to have 
eaten those toothy reptiles and their eggs in addition to turtles and their products. Given 
the abundance- at least the past abundance-of the fish on which the crocodilians feed, 
their numbers may have been considerable enough to class them as an important secondary source of animal protein.

In my 215 day game sampling period Ye'kuana hunters at Toki (population 76) killed 98 caimans

A topic surely worthy of further research is the importance of the manatee, a slowmoving large herbivore, in the diet of people along the courses of major rivers. AS

Entomophagy is widely recorded throughout the Amazon basin; Harner  
speaks as follows of the Jivaro:

With those references we have encircled the Amazon basin. It is unfortunately true 
that we have only in Lizot's work a true quantitative measure of the contribution of entomophagy to the diet, and, even there, no figures for the completeness (= amino acid 
distribution) of the protein thereby ingested. It does seem that insects may have played a 
rather important role in maintaining animal-protein intake during periods when 
vertebrates were less available than at peak hunting and fishing seasons, since insects in 
general contain a relatively high percentage of protein. 
That point is underlined by the several pages in Bodenheimer  that 
discuss the eating of insects in South America. Most of the examples are drawn from 
Amazonia and Orinoquia, and the bibliography is quite extensive. One favored genus, 
mentioned almost continually, is Atta, the ubiquitious leaf-cutting ant. Although it 
swarms seasonally, at the time of swarming it is apparently quite abundant. 
It must not be thought that arthropods are the only likely source of invertebrateanimal protein. Gastropods probably deserve an entire section to themselves although 
they are seldom given more than passing mention, if that, by ethnographers. Archaeologists, for obvious reasons, are liable to grant them more attention, as witnessed in the 
observation that: 
humid areas of shaded vegetation are favorable for the development of important colonies of 
gastropods, whose importance as ordinary food in the diet of the indigenous communities of the 
Orinoco as well as other areas of Venezuela is attested by the constant, numerically important 
presence of shells of terrestrial snails in the archaeological material of many prehispanic 
Venezuelan habitation sites . 
I suspect that other observations of this sort will shortly be coming from Amazonia 
proper, as archaeologists move into that region in force.

An idea of the importance of this next topic can be gauged from the fact that even the 
Yanomami, who are widely seen as an example of people overwhelmingly dependent on 
game for their protein supplies , derive 15% to 30% of their protein 
from plants, depending on the season . I shall argue in the final section 
that the average precontact percentage of vegetable protein eaten by Amazonian tribes 
was likely much higher. 
Therefore, in this connection it is significant to observe that the following discussion of 
vegetable protein, like the preceding discussion of insect protein, largely moves away 
from the rivers and enters the forest itself- an important point in view of the now-wellaccepted distinction  between riverine and upland tribes. Many vegetableprotein sources override this distinction to some degree, so the distinction between plants 
that can grow only on river flood plains and plants that can grow in the uplands as well, 
may turn out to be one of the most significant distributional characteristics. 
The following discussion is somewhat complicated by the division of vegetable-protein 
sources into wild and cultivated varieties. Some tree species occur both wild and under 
cultivation. These cases will be mentioned under both headings although nutritional 
data will be discussed under one heading only. 
This section is limited to the fruits of trees although I suspect that other products and 
other life forms could be added. 
It is probably no exaggeration to say that the great majority of serious ethnographies of 
Amazonian tribes mention the consumption of palm fruits, although almost always in an 
offhand manner. Among the genera most frequently mentioned are (in no particular 
order) Guililema, Mauritia, Bactris, Oenocarpus, Jessenza, Euterpe, and Scheelie.  
The most illustrious names connected with the subject at hand here are Richard 
Spruce and Alfred Russell Wallace. The latter published a small (and until recently very 
rare) book dealing with the palms of Amazonia and their uses. From that work , we can extract a series of citations dealing with the uses and distribution of 
the closely related palms that Wallace referred to as the genera Euterpe and Oenocarpus. 
Eutei-pe oleracea . . . The Assai of Par2 is a tall and slender tree, from sixty to eighty feet high, 
and about four inches in diameter. . . . It grows in swamps flooded by the high tides-never on 
dry land. A very favourite drink is made from the ripe fruit, and daily vended in the streets of 
Par%. . . . [It is] a thick creamy liquid, of a fine plum color. . . . It is generally taken with 
farinha, the substitute for bread prepared from the mandiocca root, with or without sugar, according to the taste of the consumer. 
During our walks in the suburbs of Par% we had frequently opportunities of seeing the preparation of this favourite beverage, Two or three large bunches of fruit are brought in from the forest. 
The women of the house seize upon them, shake and strip them into a large earthen vessel, and 
pour on them warm water, not too hot to bear the hand in. The water soon becomes tinged with 
purple, and in about an hour the outer pulp has become soft enough to rub off. The water is now 
most of it poured away, a little cold added, and a damsel , , , plunges both hands into the vessel, 
Beckerman]

and rubs and kneads with great perserverance, adding fresh water as it is required, till the whole
of the purple covering has been rubbed off and the greenish stones left bare. The liquid is now
poured through a wicker sieve into another vessel and is then ready for use.
The inhabitants of Pará are excessively attached to this beverage, and many never pass a day of their lives without it. They are particularly favoured too, in being able to get it at all seasons, for though in most places the trees only bear for a few months once in the year, yet in the neighborhood of Pará there is so much variety of soil and aspect, that within a day or two's journey, there is always some ripe Assaí to supply the market. From the great island of Marajó, its igaripes and marshes, from the rivers Guamá and Majú, from the thousand islands in the river, and from the vast palm swamps in the depth of the forest, baskets of the fruit are brought every morning to the city, where half the population look to the Assaí to supply a daily meal, and hundreds are said to make it, with farinha, almost their main subsistence.
The trees of this genus also furnish another article of food. The undeveloped leaves in the centre of the column form a white sweetish mass, which when boiled somewhat resembles artichoke or parsnip, and is a very good and wholesome vegetable. It may also be eaten raw, cut up and dressed as a salad with oil and vinegar. As however, to obtain it the tree must be destroyed, it is not much used in Pará, except by travellers in the forest who have no particular interest in the preservation of the trees for fruit [Wallace 1971:23-26].
It is true, that lengthy quotation makes no mention of the use of Euterpe fruits outside the city of Pará. If we follow his subsequent descriptions, however, we see that this urban preparation is ony one variant of a very widespread indigenous food:
Euterpe catinga... grows to forty or fifty feet high. The spadices are fewer and much smaller. The fruit also is smaller, and has more pulpy matter, so that a small quantity of it makes more of the "vinho d'Assaí" (the Assaí wine) than the same quantity of fruit of the larger kind... It inhabits the forests on a dry sandy soil, of the Upper Rio Negro.
The preparation of the fruit of this species is sweeter and more finely flavoured than that of any other, and is therefore much sought after, but it takes the produce of four or five trees to yield as much as a single spadix of the larger kind will often produce. I found the fruit ripe in the month of April on the river Uaupes, a branch of the Rio Negro above the Falls [Wallace 1971:27].
Continuing to a closely related genus, we find that:
Oenocarpus baccába... is a smooth thick-stemmed handsome tree, faintly ringed, and reaching fifty or sixty feet in height.... The fruits are of a violet or black colour when ripe, but are covered with a dense whitish bloom. They are prepared in the same way as the Assaí, but the pulp is of a pinkish cream-colour instead of purple, and the liquid is more oily, and of delicious flavour, somewhat resembling filberts and cream. It is said however, not to be so wholesome as the Assaí, and in districts where intermittent fevers are prevalent, to bring them on, and to be particularly hurtful to persons recovering from that disease.
This species inhabits the dry virgin forests of the Rio Negro and Upper Amazon. In the lower parts of that river and in the neighbourhood of Pará, it is replaced by another species, the Oenocarpus distichus [Wallace 1971:29-30].
In the same genus we also have O. batawá (called Patawa in lingoa geral), whose fruit "is very similar to that of the Baccába, and is said to be of even superior flavor" . It "is found in the whole of the Amazon and Rio Negro in the virgin forest, though apparently nowhere very abundant" . Nigel Smith (personal communication) observes that this species grows "mostly along terra firma streams."
Finally, Wallace mentions O. minor, which "is a small species common on the upper Rio Negro. The stem is not half so thick as in the O. baccába, and the leaves are in the proportion. The fruit is also very small, but is very fleshy and fine-flavoured, and ripens at a different time of the year from the larger kind. It grows in the dry virgin forest" .

To complement those data from Wallace, we may add similar passages from Spruce 
 that were written from August 1853 to November 1854 during the eminent botanist's stay at the village of San Carlos on the Rio Negro. 
On the Patau5 called Uardma by the Barre Indians, and by Spanish settlers Seje, which is a general name for all palms whose fruit is used for mixing with jueuta. 
[Extract from Journal] 
There are two species at San Carlos. One, which is the same as the Barra Oenocarpus , . . a 
tall, noble species with large oblong fruit; the other (which I have not seen) has much smaller 
subovate fruit (not globose as in Bacsba), and the drink prepared from it has a distinct reddish 
tinge almost like that of the Bacaba, while in the larger Pantau%, it is white-very slightly fleshcoloured. 
That with the smaller fruit is probably the smaller species spoken of in another place, distinct 
from Oe. Minor, Mart., by the fastigiate pinnae. Patau5-yukise, L. G. (yukise is the general 
name for extracts produced by cooking either vegetables or meats, and is equally applied to gravy 
of flesh or fish, and the juice extracted from fruits, roots, etc.), jukdta de seje (Venezuela), is one 
of the most wholesome and delicious drinks in nature. The taste is exceedingly rich, resembling 
more that of new milk than of anything else. It is prepared in the same way as assai, either by 
scalding the ripe fruit, or still better, by slightly boiling it, then breaking it up by hand in water, 
when the thin light-coloured pulp mingles with the water and the brittle purple skins fall with the 
stones in the bottom. The liquid is either poured off or the whole is passed through a sieve which 
retains all the grosser parts. A small quantity of mandiocca is added, as in mixing xibe, and when 
it has softened, the whole is ready for drinking 
There was a small quantity of Pantau5 ripe when I left the Uaupes in March, and we had had 
it at San Carlos all through the months of April, June, August, and September. The trees are very 
abundant in dense forests on the west side of the river from the pueblito of San Felipe (by the fort 
of San Carlos) towards the Guasie. 
The Indians in their sitios grow exceeding fat during the season of Pataud, and there can be no 
doubt of its being very nourishing. 
The various species of Oenocarpus abound on the Amazon and Orinoco, and on their 
tributaries. I have lately seen the Patau5 in the greatest plenty throughout the Casiquiari, Alto 
Orinoco, and Cuncundma. Near the Barra it is frequent, but less so than the Bac5ba. The 
Forests opposite San Carlos, extending from the Rio Negro to the Xie, are literally sown with 
Patauk. The fruit is in season nearly all the year round. We are just now beginning to make use of 
it , and we shall have it (in unlimited quantity if there were always Indians to 
climb the trees) all along until November [Spruce 1970:477-4801. 
To round out this discussion of the products of Euterpe and Oenocarpus palms, we 
have recourse to another eminent name, that of Henry Walter Bates. In his The 
Naturalist on the River Amazons, he speaks of a journey he made with Wallace in the fall 
of 1848 up the Tocantins, the (probably mixed-blood) inhabitants of whose islands "live 
chiefly on fish, shellfish, . . . the never ending farinha, and the fruits of the forest. 
Amongst the latter the fruits of palm trees occupied the chief place. The Assai is the most 
in use, but this forms a universal article of diet in all parts of the country [Bates 1892:62; 
emphasis added]. 
It seems quite clear that one or more of the edible-fruit-producing species of either 
Euterpe or Oenocarpus were present and abundant in almost all parts of the Hylea, and 
that the fruits were both available and used. Moreover, it is likely that in most parts of 
forested Amazonia there were several edible species and that their fruiting times were 
somewhat distinct. The protein value of the fruits is a matter on which surprisingly little 
work has been done, considering the continuing popularity of assai among modern 
Brasilians. I have been able to locate only two studies that give information on the protein content of assai, and both report only on the overall amount of protein, ignoring the 
amino acid balance.

pericarp of the fruits of Euterpe precatoria and E. oleracea (the most popular sources for the contemporary beverage) as 3.38\% of fresh weight (= 5.73\% of dry weight). The beverage itself is analyzed as having 1.25\% protein on a fresh-weight basis (= 8.33\% dry weight). The difference in the dry protein percentages of the two products is apparently due largely to the conversion of sugars by fermentation during preparation and storage. Sugar is 12\% of the pericarp (which contains 41\% water) but only 1\% of the assaí drink (which contains 85\% water).
The second study  pertains only to the assaí prepared from Euterpe oleracea. It was found that 10 samples of pericarp averaged 2.52% protein, fresh-weight basis (=6.25% dry), while 6 samples of the beverage prepared from the pericarp averaged 2.37% protein, fresh-weight basis (=18.37% dry). In these experiments the average water content of the pericarp was 59.7% and that of the beverage was 87.1%.
For comparison, whole fresh cow's milk, which also contains about 87% water, has a protein content of about 3.69%. The bulk protein content of assaí is in the range where it must be seriously considered as a protein source, despite its high water content. (The human body can, of course, take on and excrete very large quantities of water in the process of obtaining other nutrients. For this reason, all discussions of the protein content of foods should allow the computation of the really significant index, the amount of protein per unit dry weight.) What is needed to complete this discussion is information on the amino acid balance of assaí—and that information is lacking.
We can now return to Wallace and his discussion  of the edible species of the genus Mauritia; he begins with M. flexuosa, whose common name is mirití:
The fruit is spherical, the size of a small apple and covered with rather small, smooth, brown reticulated scales, beneath which is a thin coating of pulp. A spadix loaded with fruit is of immense weight, often more than two men could carry between them.
The leaves, fruit and stem of this tree are all useful to the natives of the interior.
From the fruits a favourite Indian beverage is produced. They are soaked in water till they begin to ferment, and the scales and pulpy matter soften and can be easily rubbed off in water. When strained through a sieve it is ready for use, and has a slight acid taste and a peculiar flavour of the fruit at first rather disagreeable to European palates.
The Mirití is a social palm, covering large tracts of tide-flooded lands on the Lower Amazon. Besides this species which is mentioned by Martius as occurring at Pará, my friend Mr. Spruce ascertained that another closely allied palm, the Mauritia vinifera, also occurs there. On the Upper Amazon and Rio Negro a palm is found supposed to be the M. flexuosa, but it is not so lofty a tree, which may perhaps be accounted for by its growing on annually instead of diurnally flooded lands. It is believed to be the same species which Humboldt observed on the Serra Duida. The Itá palm growing on the delta of the Orinoco is also thought to be the same species. On the river Uaupés, a branch of the Upper Rio Negro, I observed an allied species called by the natives "Caraná assu" [Wallace 1971:47-51].
As with the previous genera, Spruce  both seconds and amplifies the discussion:
The most universally distributed palm throughout the basins of the Amazon and Orinoco, or, say, from the Andes of Peru and New Granada to the shores of the Atlantic, is undoubtedly Mauritia flexuosa (L.). . . . The earliest American voyagers and missionaries noted its abundance in the delta of the Orinoco, and how, in the season of inundations, the natives dwelt on stages supported by the growing trunks of the Mauritia, whose fruit afforded their chief food; so that to them it was truly the "Arbol de la Vida," or Tree of Life.
At the mouths of all the rivers between the Orinoco and Amazon the Mauritia abounds, but does not seem to reach much further southward along the coast of Brazil. . . . It extends westward right across to the first undulations of the Andes, where it fails at from 2000 to 3000 feet, and . . . is equally common in the Subandine and in the Submaritime regions, as well as along the whole course of the Amazon and Orinoco.

At the mouth of the Amazon, the Maurztzu abounds most on the shores of low flat islands, and 
about swampy lakes. It is common all the way up the river on low shores, where it stretches in 
long avenues-and at the debouchures of the tributary rivers and creeks, where it forms groves. 
At the opposite extremity of the Amazon valley, on the river Pastasa . . . we find long strips of 
the same Maurztza stretching parallel to the river, and occupying low land inundated in the rainy 
season to a slight depth. 
Far away northward of the Amazon, at the head of each of the "caiios," or rivulets, that run into the Upper Rio Negro and Orinoco, there is a swamp where the predominant vegetation is 
Maurztzaflexuosa, if the soil be good: but if it be thin and sandy, then probably the curious M. 
Caranri takes its place, or grows along with it. 
Near the cataracts of the Orinoco, the savannahs are adorned with small groves of Maurztza 
flexuosa . . . and here and there with a long winding double line, which marks the course of a 
rivulet. 
In the Lower Oriental Andes, it is fond of growing near springs, where it finds the necessary 
moisture, and aids in maintaining it by protecting the springs from evaporation. The inhabitants 
take advantage of this property to plant Mauritius near their wells of water. . . . 
The prevalent opinion, or rather superstition, throughout Amazonia and Guayana is, that the 
Maurztzu has the power of attracting water to itself wherever planted. This is what Velasco says of 
it in his "Historia Natural de Quito," p. 73: -"The Palm AguPshi (or AchuP1) has the property of 
drawing water to it, from whatever distance; so that this Palm is nowhere seen without a spring of 
water at its foot, or some rivulet close by. The reason of this is not that it will not grow except 
where there is water, but because water can never be wanting where it grows. With the certainty 
of this, when any spring has dried up, the Indians of Maynas plant one of these Palms, and the 
water soon wells up again. In whatever part of the forest, high or low. they descry a Palm of this 
kind, they go up to it, assured of finding delicious water at its foot. 
Humboldt heard the same thing at Esmeralda, where in 1853, I saw the Maurztzu still growing 
as abundantly as he had it half a century before me, although the human inhabitants had 
almost disappeared. 
The only edible part of the fruit of the Maurztza is the rather thin orange pulp, which easily 
separates from the endocarp when ripe, but is clad with cartilaginous scales that it requires practice to get rid of. The Indians of Venezuela are fond of it, eating it with or without cassava, and 
find it quite sufficient to sustain life for a considerable time without other food. 
At Maypures and elsewhere on the Orinoco, when the fruit of the Maurztza is ripe enough to 
fall of itself, it is gathered up, the pulpy covering is rubbed off and kneaded into a mass, which is 
wrapped up in fresh leaves of "Platanillo" (Tranzde sp.?), and enveloped in a framework of slips 
of Blowing-cane Palm (hzartea setzgera), made first into a cylinder, and then the ends brought 
together and tied tightly, so as to bring it to a spindle-shape. In this way, the pulp is kept for 
weeks, until it becomes intensely acid. When used, it is mixed with water and passed through a 
sieve, which retains all the scales of the fruit; and a little sugar or molasses being added, it makes 
a pleasant cooling drink. . . . 
Bundles of this "Moriche curtido" (as it is called) are sent for sale up to San Fernando de 
Atabapo, the capital of the canton, where it is much esteemed. 
We must note here that in Spruce's description some fermentation is involved in 
preparing the Maurztiu fruit, a matter taken up again in the later discussion of fermented 
protein sources. At this point, we can return briefly to Wallace for a description of M. 
gracilis: 
This beautiful little palm is first met with about Barcellos on the Rio Negro, more than 3000 
miles up the river, and is thence common as far as the blackwater tributaries of the Orinoco. It 
always grows close to the water's edge in clumps of thirty or forty individuals. . . . The fruit is 
eaten, after being softened by soaking some time in water [Wallace 1971:57]. 
Wallace also notes  without comment that M. aculeata is used in the manner of M. flexuosa to make a drink.

Although the protein is a rather small fraction of the dry weight—only 5%—it cannot be rejected as insignificant in a diet in which (a) large quantities of the food are consumed; (b) the food actually consumed may be considerably higher in protein content than the raw fruit because of removal of fiber and other nonnutrients; and (c) fermentation may actually increase the absolute amount of protein contained in the processed food.
Further, it appears that the "whole pulp" analyzed also included some fiber and scales, which are never eaten. A shorter independent analysis of the "edible part" of Mauritia, presented in Table I, gives a substantially higher protein content.
Under this general heading, we can also mention Bactris maraja, of which Wallace  says:
It produces large clusters of fruit resembling small black grapes, and having a thin pulp of an agreeable subacid flavour—a peculiarity not found in the fruit of any other American palm I am acquainted with.
We defer to the section on cultivated plants our consideration of Bactris gasipaes (which is still generally known under the names of Guilielmia speciosa or G. gasipaes although the genus Guilielma was rejected by Spruce over 100 years ago).
A few more palms that Wallace  mentions as having edible fruits are Astrocaryum tucuma, A. humile, and Maximiliana regia (= M. martinana). Of the last, he further states :
The fruits are often eaten by the Indians, and are particularly attractive to monkeys and to some fruit-eating birds.
This magnificent palm is abundant from Pará to the Upper Amazon and the sources of the Rio Negro. It grows only in dry virgin forest.
It seems that the case for the abundance of Mauritia and Maximiliana is, if anything, stronger than that for Euterpe-Oenocarpus.
With this observation we can conclude the recitals from these classics of natural history and turn to more modern anthropological sources. An exhaustive list of references to palms in modern ethnographies would be quite unnecessarily tedious, but a few succinct citations from a standard compilation are in order.
Of good service in this connection is The Handbook of South American Indians, whose third volume , devoted to tropical forest tribes, contains a good deal of information on the consumption of palm products. A few of the more significant references are cited here in as brief a form as possible.
Lipkind  reports that the Carajá of the area around Bananal Island gather "a large number of vegetable products . . . for use as food, medicine, and raw material for manufacture, but only a few are of great importance. The babassú and the burití palms, used for food and textile materials, are among the most valuable." Babassú is the palm Orbignya speciosa and burití is Mauritia spp.
Métraux , in speaking of peoples around the Madre de Dios river, says "The Araona—and probably all the other Tacanan-speaking groups—depend greatly on wild foods, such as the fruits of several palm trees (Euterpe oleracea, Jessenia bataua, Attalea humboldtiana, Attalea spectabilis, Bactris maraja) and Brazil nuts."

Having argued that the plants were present and were eaten, we still have not fully 
demonstrated that they provided a significant supply of protein. On that issue, unfortunately, the data are quite scarce. Table I provides the only additional information I 
have found in print, culled from Wu Leung . In general, these data show that 
palm fruits (the "edible part" that was analyzed is not specified) contain a significant 
amount of protein although hardly spectacular. 
Nevertheless, the figures suffice to make the point. Table I expresses the raw amounts 
of protein also as percentages of dry weight, an elementary step in standardizing for differing moisture contents. The resulting figures are compared with those for maize and 
rice. It is clear that the palm fruits are in the vicinity of those grains in terms of protein 
content-in fact, Mauritia even surpasses them, according to Wu Leung. It is well known 
that many whole populations survive and even prosper with one or the other of these two 
grass seeds as their major source of protein, with only minor supplements from richer 
sources, such as meat. 
We might add parenthetically that the amount of protein in heart of palm (see Table 
I) is quite surprising, and that this product is probably underreported as an aboriginal 
foodstuff.  
It is unfortunately the case that there have been no quantitative analyses of the various 
essential amino acids in the protein of wild palm fruits; the real value to human nutrition 
of these forest products must remain an open question. 
Although the space devoted to making the case for palms as an important Hylean food 
source indicates the emphasis I believe should be placed on this family, there are other 
wild tree fruits with even more substantial protein contributions per unit weight. 
The Brazil nut, for instance, contains 13.2 gm. of protein for every 100 gm. of the edible meat, fresh weight . For its distribution we refer to Wallace 
again, in his Narrative of Travels on the Amazon and Rio Negro : 
The Brazil-nuts, from the Bertholletiu excelsa, are brought chiefly from the interior; the 
greater part from the country around the junction of the Rio Negro and Madeira with the 
Amazon rivers. 
The fruits are all procured as they fall from the tree. They are collected together in small

to the canoes in baskets. Other trees of the same family (Lecythzdeae) are very abundant, and 
remarkable for their curious fruits. . . .

Bates , however, supplies another datum from his excursion to the lower 
Tocantins: 
Joaquim pointed out to us grove after grove of Brazil-nut trees (Bertholletza excelsa) on the 
mainland. This is one of the chief collecting grounds for this nut. The tree is one of the loftiest in 
the forest, towering far above its fellows; we could see the woody fruits, large and round as cannon balls, dotted over the branches. 
In fact, if we add to the observations of Wallace and Bates those of Spruce, it becomes 
clear that those explorers found Brazil-nut trees distributed both widely and abundantly 
throughout the Amazon basin. The amino acid distribution of the protein of Brazil-nuts 
is given in Figure 1, along with the distributions of the amino acids of a number of other 
foods considered here. For comparison, there is also presented the relevant information 
for whole chicken eggs, which are probably the best naturally occurring source of protein 
in terms of the relative percentages of the essential amino acids. Tryptophan and lysine 
appear to be the limiting amino acids for Brazil nuts, and even those are by no means absent. 
Another tree familiar to readers of Amazonian ethnographies is the piqui, Caryocar 
spp. Fifteen species are known , and it is 
thought that the plant may sometimes be cultivated , especially in the upper Xingij region. Nevertheless, it is apparently 
better known in the wild form and is treated under that heading in this article. The fruit, 
like most of the palm fruits mentioned earlier, is better known as a source of oil than of 
protein. However, given Quain's description of the roasted fruits as tasting "like egg yolk, 
or, vaguely, like deviled eggs" , one suspects that a full 
analysis of the fruit would show a significant proportion of protein in its oily meat. 
Cavalcante  indicates that it is "encontrado por t6da AmazBnia, them matas de 
terra firme." Among the Trumai, this item was a very important foodstuff, consumed in 
large quantity in fresh and stored form from October until April . 
Although the avocado (Persia americana) does grow wild in the Amazon-Orinoco 
region and is exploited in its wild state by some tribes, it is mentioned most commonly as 
a domesticate or semidomesticate. For that reason, this fruit is considered in the section 
on cultivated plants. 
The final source of vegetable protein discussed in this section is the wild cashew, 
Anacardium spp. Again, we are dealing with a nut known to have been widely consumed 
, in both wild and cultivated varieties. The major wild variety, according to Cavalcante , is A. giganteum, which is "largamente 
distribuida pela Hileia inteira (Amazonia e Guianas) e alcanca ao Sudeste, a zona de 
transiciio das floras, no MaranhZo e them Mato Grosso." The protein content and amino 
acid distribution are deferred until discussion of the cultivated variety A. occidentale, 
noting here only that if the wild species resembles the cultivated species in protein content, we are dealing with a food of extremely high quality. 
Although the wild fruits and nuts mentioned so far give only the scantiest hint at the 
enormous floristic richness of the Hylea, and although I have been able to provide adequate nutritional information for only one or two species, it is one of the aims of this article to stimulate further investigation of the distribution, availability, consumption, and, 
especially, the dietary value of the products mentioned above.

A palm much cultivated in the Barra [the old name for the Manaus region] and said to grow 
wild up the Rio Negro is the Pupunha, which I suppose to be the same as the Piryao (Guilielma 
speciosa, Mart [ = Bactris gasipaes]) mentioned by Humboldt as growing on the upper Orinoco. 
The fruit of this is perhaps more valuable as an edible than any other palm fruit: the sarocarp 
contains a large quantity of starch, and is sometimes developed to such a degree that the nucleus 
is quite obliterated. Eaten with salt, the boiled or roasted fruit resembles a potato. . . . 
A similar statement can be found in Bates : 
The Pupunha grows wild nowhere in the Amazons. It is one of those few vegetable productions . . . which the Indians have cultivated from time immemorial. . , . It is, however, only the 
more advanced tribes who have kept up the cultivation. The superiority of the fruit on the 
Solimijes to that grown on the Lower Amazons, and in the neighborhood of ParP, is very striking. 
At Ega it is generally as large as a full sized peach, and when boiled almost as mealy as a potato: 
whilst at Par5 it is no bigger than a walnut and the pulp is fibrous. Bunches of sterile or seedless 
fruits sometimes occur in both districts. It is one of the principal articles of food at Ega when in 
season, and is boiled and eaten with treacle or salt. A dozen of the seedless fruits made a good 
nourishing meal for a grown-up person. It is the general belief that there is more nutrient in 
Pupunha than in a fish or Vacca marina. 
Finally, Wallace  echoes those descriptions: 
the tree is not found wild in the Amazon district, but is invariably planted near the Indians' houses. In their villages many hundreds of these trees may often be seen . . . supplying the 
inhabitants with an abundance of wholesome food. In fact, it here takes the place of the cocoanut in the east, and is almost as much esteemed. 
The fruits are eaten either boiled or roasted, when they resemble Spanish chestnuts, but have a 
peculiar oily flavor. They are also ground up into a kind of flour, and made into cakes which are 
roasted like cassava bread; or the meal is fermented in water and forms a subacid creamy liquid. 
(Discussion of that fermentation is deferred to the following section.) 
Sauer  suggests that the "location of its cultivation may identify (the 
papunha) with the proto A.rawak and proto Chibcha areas as a quite old cultigen." 
Information on the nutritional value of the papunha (also known as the pejibaye) palm 
is given in Johannessen , Hunter , and Zapata . The first of those 
authors reports on the composition of 26 samples of fruit, all taken from trees growing in 
Costa Rica and Honduras, near the northernmost extension of the range of this tree. He 
found Uohannessen 1967:374) that the average protein content of the fresh fruit was 
2.12%, with a maximum of 2.85% and a minimum of 1.37% . That source also summarizes a number of earlier analyses 
of pupunha fruits, all of them showing somewhat more protein than determined in his 
work, and points out that methodological questions exist for some of the earlier analyses. 
The second author cites still other sources in presenting the protein content of 
pupunha fruits  and presents additional information, to which we 
shall return, on the productivity of this palm. Although Hunter's data show only a 
moderately higher protein content than Johannessen's, here again, we are dealing with 
fruits taken from near the northern limit of growth of the tree, in Trinidad and in Costa 
Rica. 
The last author used fruits from the Pacific coast of Colombia, one of the wettest 
places in the world and possessed of an extraordinarily equable climate. This region is 
near the center of the latitudinal range of the pupunha, although on its western border of 
longitude. Zapata  found that one of the two varieties he tested contained 
5.1% protein and the other contained 6.3% (both fresh-weight figures). Converted to 
dry weight, the corresponding figures are 9.9% and 12.8%. Zapata  further 
presents a preliminary amino acid analysis showing that seven of the eight essential acids 
are present in pupunha fruits. Results of that analysis are presented in Figure 1.

Production per tree is not mentioned, but for pure stands an average annual production of 5,791 kg. of fresh fruit/hectare is reported (range = 2,063-12,427). If that fresh 
weight is reduced to a 12% moisture-equivalent basis (for comparison with dry maize), 
the yearly average production is about 3,375 kg./hectare. Maize at the same moisture 
content was reported to reach about that level of productivity only for selected conditions; the general yield reported in agricultural censuses was only a third of the perhectare productivity of the pupunha. 
Although Hunter  complains that pupunha fruits begin to rot in three or 
four days after the ripe raceme is cut from the tree, Zapata observed that 
ground pulp of the fruit that was air-dried at room temperature in Cali, Colombia, and 
kept in amber-colored jars, was unafflicted with fungus after 15 months of storage. I have 
already quoted Wallace's observation that Amazonian Indians not only ground the 
pupunha into flour but also baked the flour into cakes as though it were manioc. 
Concerning the cultivated cashew Annacardzum occidentale, for which nutritional information is presented in Figure 1 and Table I, we can cite Sauer  to the effect 
that its "association is probably with the tribes of the Tropical Forests, especially the 
Amazonian area." In addition to the high protein content shown in the table and figure, 
another potentially important aspect of this crop is indicated by Cavalcante's  
chart showing that cashews of one kind or another-either wild or cultivated-are available in Belem in every month except April, and that the cultivated variety alone is available from May through the following January. 
The avocado (Persea americana) is generally accepted as a very old American cultigen. 
Patiiio  notes that both the Orellana and the Ursua-Aguirre expeditions down 
the Amazon encountered this fruit above the confluence of the Rio Negro, but feels that 
it was introduced to the lower Amazon after the conquest. A good deal of work remains 
to be done on the remaining Hylean distribution of the avocado. Robinson  indicates that about 2% of the fresh weight of avocado pulp ( = 7% dry) is protein. 
Turning from trees to some humbler plants, though probably even more important 
ones, we can attend to the sadly neglected peanut, one of the most valuable of the native 
American crops. Although it seems to be part of the lore of South Americanists that the 
peanut was everywhere a minor crop, a reading of the relevant sources indicates that that 
position may be misleading. Sauer  is forthright that "the peanut was generally 
important in Tufii economy," and Lowie  notes that the crop is "aboriginal 
throughout Tropical Forests." LCvi-Strauss  reports that "the tribes of the upper Guapore River, especially those upstream, rely for food mainly upon maize and 
peanuts" (emphasis added). It seems likely that the common conviction of the unimportance of the peanut results from contemporary Western unconscious ethnobotanical 
preconceptions, which class this underground bean as a starchy appetizer, like popcorn. 
As a starch, a source of carbohydrates, peanuts were undoubtedly much less important 
than manioc, corn, yams, and other calorie staples. As a protezn source, a relatively small 
quantity of peanuts, as compared with the starchy staple, would nevertheless be a major 
item in the diet. It is apparently in this sense that we should take Sauer's later  
statement that the peanut "was never more than a subordinate crop anywhere, and being 
a protein food, would not be expected to be one of the first domesticates in a planting 
culture. " 
In accounts of the Panoan tribes of the Ucayali River, we read that the "Chama and

In dealing with the aboveground beans, it is again appropriate to begin with a summary statement from the Handbook of South American Indians. In charting the 
cultivated foodstuffs of the tropical forests, Lowie  says of the kidney bean 
(Phaseolis vulgaris) that it is "probably widely distributed but rarely identified with certainty in the Tropical forests"; of the lima bean (P. lunatus) that it is "aboriginal among 
Tupinamba, Maut, Apici, and probably many other tribes"; and of the jack bean 
(Canavaliu ensformis) that it is "rarely identified but probably of wide native distribution in Brazil." Information is even more lacking for aboveground beans than for 
peanuts; one can say with some assurance that the majority of Hylean tribes was acquainted with beans at the time of the conquest and that many, if not most, had one or 
more varieties under cultivation. As to the percentage of their contribution to the diet, 
we remain in ignorance. The relevant protein data for the aboveground beans are given 
in Table I and Figure 1. 
Although there is no solid quantitative evidence as to the de facto contribution of 
cultivated protein foods to the diet of any single tribe, it is difficult to imagine that 
peoples having in their crop inventories the peanut, the cashew, and the pupunha (and 
quite likely the kidney bean as well) would have found themselves strictly limited by the 
amount of fish and game they could procure. 
A final section is still necessary on aboriginally-available protein sources. Under this 
heading we will examine briefly what has come to be called in recent food-engineering 
literature, single cell protein, or SCP. The reference is to yeasts, fungi, and other microorganisms, the workhorses of fermentation, which have been discovered to be surprisingly 
rich and easily obtainable sources of protein (sometimes of very high quality). 
It is probably premature to make more than the most tentative assertions on the subject of fermented protein sources among the tropical-forest peoples. Many of the basic 
data are simply missing. Nevertheless, because one of the aims of this paper is to 
stimulate the collection of the necessary data, a few broad suggestions are inevitable. 
The fermentation of manioc is well known from a great number of locations in indigenous Amazonia . In addition to manioc beer (chicha), there is also the widespread consumption of 
products made of flour produced from fermented manioc tubers . The 
fermentation of Mauritia and pupunha palm fruits was mentioned above. Neither do we 
know what microorganisms are responsible for the fermentation of those products, nor do 
we know the postfennentation protein content, or amino acid balance, of the food that is

ultimately consumed. It is fairly safe to say that both the overall protein content and the amino acid balance of a food product must be changed by fermentation. It is quite plausable that both are enhanced, perhaps quite significantly in some cases. Should such turn out to be the case, the protein limitation theory of tropical-forest populations would fall even in the absence of all the data furnished in the foregoing sections.
If there are so many widely available plant, insect, and even protozoan sources of protein in the tropical forest, why do the tropical-forest peoples we know today concentrate so on fish and game in their diets as well as in their subsistence activities?
An immediate and not entirely facetious answer is: There is enough meat to go around. A more serious expansion of that answer covers the following points:
(1) Meat (or the "proto-meat" of embryos such as eggs) has protein of the highest quality of any food. It contains just what we need for our own tissues in just the right proportions. Furthermore—perhaps for precisely those reasons—it tastes good. As long as there is enough meat to feed a human population, it makes a great deal of biological sense to concentrate on it.
(On this point, Gross and I are probably in agreement. We differ in our deductions from it. He appears to hold that since it is now the case that Amazonian populations concentrate on meat, they always did so, and therefore their populations have never been greater than what could be supported by meat protein sources. I hold the opposite view.)
(2) In the absence of meat, the amino acid combination necessary for adequate human protein intake can be mimicked by vegetable proteins, sometimes alone, although more usually in combination (as in the famous Meso-American corn-beans combination, in which the beans make up the lysine and trypotophan that are in short supply in the corn). However, vegetable proteins are, in general, less concentrated and less well balanced than animal proteins. They frequently need to be combined with other vegetable proteins to be really effective, and often require considerable preparation time and effort, particularly in obtaining the proper conjunction of foods to assure correct amino acid ratios. (This last point in particular can be verified by talking to a modern American vegetarian.) It therefore makes a good deal of housekeeping and economic as well as biological sense to switch from vegetable to animal protein sources if possible. (Again, hunting and fishing are usually recreation, and gardening is work.)
(3) Vegetables are a step (sometimes more) down the food chain from animals. They can therefore support a considerably larger population per unit area. If we take the general figure of ten percent for energetic ecological efficiency , we shall, as a first approximation, predict that a population living on vegetable protein will be ten times as dense as one living on animal protein in the same food chain, all other things being equal.
(4) One concludes from the first three points that biologically sensible people will concentrate on vegetable protein when their population is large and dense and will switch to animal protein when it is small and sparse.
Gross holds that the Amazonian populations have always been small and sparse. I hold that they have become so in the last 400 years. My argument is essentially that contemporary Amazonian populations can tell us rather little about the economic and demographic parameters of preconquest Amazonia because a spectacular demographic disaster has intervened. The disaster is, of course, the introduction of Old World diseases, often combined with predation by whites.
There is no space here to recite all the evidence as to the magnitude of the shock that
