File_Name: Health and Demography of Native Amazonians- Historical Perspective and Current Status.txt
Full_Text:
ANÁLISE
Amazonians: Historical Perspective
and Current Status
Warren M. Hern* University of Colorado,
Boulder, Colorado, 80309-0233
Os nativos da Amazônia foram vítimas de dois grandes
ataques históricos: um na época da Conquista e outro
durante o século XX. Devido a doenças epidêmicas e à
destruição ambiental, inúmeras tribos passaram a
vivenciar problemas de deslocamentos, dizimação e
extinção them uma única geração. A aculturação e a
construção de grandes projetos desenvolvimentistas
tiveram efeitos catastróficos sobre as populações
indígenas. Them diversos aspectos, os nativos da
Amazônia sofreram uma "Transição Epidemiológica".
Paradoxalmente, um dos efeitos da dilaceração cultural
para alguns dos nativos da Amazônia foi a perda de
controles culturais sobre a fecundidade, fazendo com
que a elevada fecundidade se tornasse um importante
problema de saúde. Com o rápido crescimento de
populações amazônicas não indígenas, o desmatamento
e a urbanização, os nativos da Amazônia enfrentam
sérios obstáculos para a sua sobrevivência a longo
prazo.
On a recent excursion to Chapada dos Guimarães to
photograph Brazilian wildlife, I had the good fortune to
encounter a young Bororó man who was now living near
the town of that name. He offered to show me a special
waterfall near his home. We descended through the brush
to a beautiful scene. The falls cascaded some ten meters to
a shallow pool. The young man slipped off his shorts and
plunged under the waterfall. He whooped and called me to
Cadernos de Saúde Pública, RJ, 7(4): 451-480, out/dez, 1991join him, so I did. I imitated his joyful Boróro whoop and
we both roared with laughter. The water in that pristine
place was cool and wonderful. It was a precious moment. My new friend is only one generation from a tribal life
in the bush. He now lives a marginal existence as an artist
and day laborer with his pleasant wife, a "branca", who
was pregnant and about to deliver their first child. The
waterfall he loves and shared with me is in a pathetically
small patch of undisturbed forest surrounded by the
devastation on an advancing "civilization". The natural
forest and wildlife is being replaced by cultivated crops.
Pantanal, the greatest wetland wildlife refuge in the
Americas. We sat my friend's house after a dinner of vegetables
and sipped mate as we watched the moon rise. The
evening air was filled with the smoke of great fires on the
plains below. "Estão matando o pulmão do mundo", he
said. "First they kill us and then they kill the earth." The
government would not let him return to his village, he
said. He had lived away too long. His family was gone,
but he wanted to be with his friends, to speak Boróro, to
take part in rituals. His tribe, he said, is dying. They will
soon be gone, he said.
This was a moving personal experience for me, but
more than that, it seems to capture a little bit of the fate of
the Amazonian Indians.
Amazonians, one must first see that they are victims of
two massive historical assaults, one at the time of Conquest and the other, an even more inexorable twentieth
century. Many of them have been in highly isolated
enclaves until the past few decades, but these have gone
from contact to displacement and decimation in a generation. The ultimate enemies of the native Amazonians are
the loss of their pristine environment and the loss of their
unique cultures.
tropical lowlands probably arrived around 10,000 years
ago, according to Martin . Carneiro  agrees
with this estimate. Lathrap  estimates that intensive
cultivation of bitter manioc in alluvial flood plains began6000 to 7000 years ago, but Roosevelt 
suggests a date of 4000—5000 BP. Lathrap  especially thinks that riverine populations were relatively dense
by the time of conquest. Both Lathrap and Roosevelt agree
with other authors  that
indegenous populations collapsed following the Conquest.
Dobyns  estimated a 20:1 loss of population
among indigenous groups following the conquest due to
the introduction of European disease, slave trading, and
intertribal warfare, although Denevan  states
that a 35:1 ratio may be more accurate. It is not unlikely
that depopulation ratios reached 50:1 on the basis of
reports cited by Dobyns. Meggers  states that a
smallpox epidemic occurred in the lower Amazon in 1621
and reached the upper Amazon in 1651. Meggers also
states that yellow fever and malaria were introduced to the
slaves from Africa. Steward and Metraux  note that
smallpox epidemics also occurred in 1670-80 following
upper Amazon Basin. Myers  estimates a reduction
of as much as 99.5% of the Omagua, Cocama, and
indigenous populations of the lower Huallaga in the 150
years following European contact.
have described the catastrophic effects of depopulation on
native tribes including the loss of many cultural traditions.
In studying two Brazilian tribes, the Tenetehára and
Tapirapé, Wagley  noted that they had two quite
different approaches to controlling fertility and natality.
The Tapirapé, valuing small families with no more than
three children and no more than two of the same sex,
sanctioned infanticide to maintain this norm. The Tapirapé
also practiced postpartum sexual abstinence. Among the
Tenetehára, infanticide was permitted only occasionally.
many children as possible. Their success in dealing with
the loss of population as the result of Western contact was
quite different. Wagley reported that the Tenetehára were
growing in numbers and that the Tapirapé culture was
disintegrating. Among other things, the Tapirapé socialorganization is much more vulnerable to disruption by
population loss.
European contact, native Amazonians have been subjected
to unprecedented destruction of their environment
since World War II with the construction of numerous
highways across the Andes and across the Brazilian
Amazon . This
has been accompanied by widespread colonization and
deforestation . Various countries,
including Brazil, Bolivia, Ecuador, and Peru, have sponsored colonization of the Amazon as the partial result
of population growth in other parts of those countries
. In 1987 alone, 47
million acres were burned in Brazil, of which 40% was
primary rainforest . Other
development projects such as hydroelectric dams have
destroyed or threaten to obliterate colossal areas of native
Amazonian environments .
have had catastrophic effects for native populations
, the long-term implications for
biodiversity and for regional and global climate change are
extremely ominous .
Denevan  estimates that 8,500,000 indigenous people lived in Amazonia at the time of Conquest,
although Myers  estimates that there may have been
as many as 10,000,000 in parts of the upper Peruvian
Amazon. Denevan estimates that, following a reduction
of the Indian population to approximately 250,000, it
has increased now to around 500,000. Population nadirs
probably occurred at around the end of the 19th
 century.
Gunter Tessman, for example, estimated that the Shipibo
numbered approximately 3,000 in 1920, whereas the
Shipibo-Conibo population now numbers between 25,000and 40,000 . Many tribes
have become extinct; others have flourished or have
absorbed others. Thorough contemporary demographic
studies of Amazon Indian societies are uncommon.
Studies of the Xavánte , Kaingang
, Yanomamo , and Shipibo
, appear to be among the main
demographic reports available until recently, but they are
often not comparable. Black et al  studied eight
unacculturated tribes in northeast Amazonia. Flowers
 gives a report of repeated observations of the
post-contact demography of the Xavánte. An exceptionally
thorough report of the Mucajai Yanomámá by Early and
demographic studies of a single group. There are no
integrating studies that give overall demographic information about all Amazonian tribes.
phic information, of course, is that Amazon tribes have not
had written languages until recently, and in any case,
systems of counting are often lacking. Categories of time
are general and do not allow precise interpretations. The
Shipibo word for "yesterday", for example (vakisli), is the
same as the word for "tomorrow". Moatian means "a long
time ago" and serves for most reckonings of over a year.
Even having birth "documents" are not certain guides to
birth dates. Birth certificates are sometimes not sought
immediately after a birth. Reports of dates to recording
officials are approximate, and they may be misunderstood
or recorded improperly. Owners may cheerfully change
documented birth dates in order to attain certain objectives
such as military induction. Stillbirths may not be counted
as term births, thereby complicating the calculations of
birth interval length or reproductive span. Children who
have died or been killed may be forgotten or ignored.
interpreted as entertaining opportunities for the creative
imagination, not to be taken seriously. As a result, the only
way it is possible to obtain some accurate demographic
data is to live permanently with a group and observe every
vital event. This, obviously, is not practical for most
observers.Data collected by Maybury-Lewis, Salzano, Chagnon,
Neel, and Peters in connection with ethnographic and
genetic studies are among the most detailed demographic
reports.
Xavánte had a Mean Completed Fertility of 5.7 and
speculated that the Xavánte practiced postpartum sexual
abstinence in order to control fertility. Neel and Chagnon
 found an overall Mean Completed Fertility for the
Xavánte of 3.6 and 3.2 for the Yanomámá. They described
these numbers as "unbelievably low" and suspected that
births resulting in infanticide are not reported as live
births. The result is one live birth every 4.4 years for the
Xavánte and one every 6.6 years for the Yanomamo. Both
the Xavánte and Yanomamo  practice
sororal polygyny, with a 50% polygyny rate among
Yanomamo males. Mean age for the Xavánte is 18; mean
age for the Yanomamo is 22. Infant mortality is 18% for
the Yanomamo. Neel and Chagnon reported that the
proportions of the populations under the age of fifteen
for the Xavánte and Yanomámá was 39% and 32%, respectively. The authors judged that both birth rates and infant-
-child mortality rates were lower for the Yanomámá than
for the Xavánte. Salzano, Neel, and Maybury-Lewis 
also found that 39% of the Xavánte population was under
15 years of age. Flowers  found that a comparable
Xavánte group was younger in 1977, with 48.6% under the
age of 15. Mean birth intervals were 20.9 months, although
for women whose babies had died, intervals were only
16.6 months. In a 1988 follow-up study, Flowers 
showed that, after an initial post-contact decline, fertility
increased markedly among the São Domingos Xavánte. I
have found exceedingly high fertility rates among the
partially the result of disruption of traditional cultural
patterns such as polygyny, which dampened fertility.
rate of .250 for the Yanomámá with a Net Reproduction
Rate of 1.25. Yanomámá women complete a pregnancy
every 3-4 years, with 85% of the births resulting in a live
birth. The authors estimated annual population growthrates at between 0.5 and 1.0%. 50% of the women who
reach age 15 die before menopause. Childhood mortality
rates have dropped considerably, but overall combined
infant and child mortality, including infanticide, stood at
about 50% at the time of the report.
mortality among the Mucajai Yanomámá in the two years
immediately preceding contact compared with lower
mortality in the two years before that, with general
diminution of death rates and population increase in post-
-contact years. Even with data from 28 years of observation by Peters, the authors could not reach firm conclusions concerning changes in longevity following contact
.
In a study of the health status of Kayapó Indians,
Ayres and Salzano  found that 48.4% of the population was under 15 years of age. In an earlier study of the
Kaingang, Salzano  found that 35.7% of the population was under 15, whereas 54.5% of a mestizo population
was under 15.
Neel  reports a 15-20% infanticide rate for
primitive tribes in the Amazon, with weaning at 3 years
for Yanomamo. Polygyny provides an important device for
natural selection, according to Neel. Polygyny is common
throughout South American tropical forest societies
, although its effects on fertility are not
well documented.
Goldman  observed among Kubéyou that women
are not eager to bear children. Kubéyou women had
knowledge of abortifacients and acknowledge performing
infanticide. An unwanted child is buried alive on the spot
where it is born. Childbirth is considered a dangerous
period. Sexual abstinence is practiced by both parents for.
one year.
Infanticide is practiced among the Mundurukú in the
cases of twins and congenital birth defects .
and stillbirth among the Waorani of Ecuador, who otherwise appear to be quite healthy.Devereaux  reported use of "imi rau" among
"Kashinaia" [Kaxináwa] as a contraceptive and as an
abortifacient. The Jivaro of Ecuador used "sacha mangue".
The words "imi rau" as reported by Devereaux are virtually identical to the Shipibo words jimi "blood" and rao
"medicine". Johnston and Kensiger  found a
27% polygyny rate among the Kaxináwa, a Panoan tribe,
and noted that the medicine man proscribed the use of
either modern or herbal contraceptives. A conscious
decision to cease using abortifacients has contributed to a
high fertility rate. Also, the practice of infanticide is not
observed to the same degree as before. This tribal "population policy" was reported to have been a response to a
declining population seen as the result of Western contact
and an epidemic in 1951. The effective fertility ratio
for the Kaxináwa was 113.5 compared to 96.3 for the
Hutterites .
Siskind  observes that the Sharanahua, also a
Panoan group, have abortions in order to have three-year
birth intervals. The Sharanahua have three ways of making
women scarce: through limited sexual access, polygyny,
and female infanticide.
Ayoreo of southwestern Bolivia, who practice infanticide.
The Ayoreo total fertility rate is 6.185; infanticide lowered
it to 4.02. The strength of the authors' conclusions are
diminished by the fact that women who did not practice
infanticide were excluded from the sample.
abortion and infanticide are practiced among the Mucajai
Yanomamo, and infanticide may account for as much as
44% of all infan t deaths.

 AMONG THE YANOMAMI*
 J. LIZOT
 Agcole Pratique des Hautes Atudes, Paris
 The possibilities available in the Amazonian forests to the human groups which live there
 are still poorly known. It has been wrongly supposed, and without taking into account
 regional variations, that the game resources are limited and the protein supply restricted.
 The present article demonstrates, with reference to the Yanomami, that the diet is balanced
 and that the growth in population has never broken the equilibrium between man and the
 environment. Variations in the level of consumption between different communities depend
 more on the attitude towards work than on the supply of game. Food requirements are
 easily satisfied by a very moderate amount of work.
 The author rejects the interpretation of Yanomami behaviour given by Marvin Harris,
 and he presents facts and figures which invalidate the thesis that the aim of warfare is the
 expropriation of hunting territories.
 When ecology is given undue emphasis, social anthropology tends to lose a
 sense of proportion and forgets that various factors combine dialectically to make
 a society what it is: the natural environment and resources, to be sure, but also
 the history, the internal dynamics specific to each civilisation-technological
 innovation or borrowing, etc.... One runs the risk of mistaking effect for because,
 of deliberately or unknowingly distorting the social facts while collecting them
 and when interpreting them in order to fit them into pre-established and simplistic
 frameworks. Some of these bad habits are quite apparent in recent publications
 where Marvin Harris and Daniel Gross offer an explanation of warfare among the
 Yanomami; this is the case to an extreme degree in the former's work, in a more
 refined and cautious manner in the latter's.'
 In spite of Gross's (I975: S44) denial of the fact Harris's basic argument is as
 follows: the reason why warfare and violence are so intensive and cruel among the
 Yanomami is that their diet is deficient in protein and different communities
 are competing for the hunting territories.2 In order to establish this hypothesis
 Harris finds himself misinterpreting the ethnographic facts: he exaggerates the
 intensity of warfare and violence, he overestimates the actual population density;
 he misjudges the role of women, depicting them as the eager slaves of men, over-
 come by brutality, subservient and passive in everything; he completely ignores
 the possibility of a causal connexion between violence and ideology and insists on
 mistaking effect for because. I do not really know what has motivated such an
 attitude and approach, but on the whole I suspect something of a primary mat-
 erialism not lacking a certain subtlety: it is a case of ideological prejudice, which is
 distorting and simplistic.
 * Translated from the French by Suzanne Hoelgaard.

In this article I intend to take up some of the questions broached by Harris in order to place the problem in its proper perspective; I wish to reassess the recent history of the Yanomami and certain aspects of their population, the importance of violence and warfare, animal protein resources and hunting.
For Harris, the history of the Yanomami, about which he seems better informed than the specialists themselves, can be summarised as follows:
Their ancestors were nomadic hunters and gatherers living away from the main rivers in small scattered bands that relied on wild forest products for their chief source of subsistence. We can be certain that only in more or less recent times did they begin to depend on bananas and plantains as their staple foods, since these plants were brought to the New World by Portuguese and Spanish settlers .
He even goes as far as to specify:
Let me give a somewhat speculative account of recent Yanomamo history. The nomadic Yanomamo who lived in the remote mountains between Venezuela and Brazil began to experiment with banana and plantain gardens. These crops yielded a great increase in the amount of food calories per capita. As a result the population of the Yanomamo also began to increase . . . .
Speculating on the possible reasons for the Yanomami population explosion, Harris asserts that it results from a change in the mode of production, in particular the introduction of agriculture and intensive banana and plantain cultivation. According to him the introduction of metal axes and machetes may have led to the adoption of agriculture, or at least to the development thereof; and Harris quotes a text by D. de Basandiaran according to whom it would have taken several days to cut down a single tree before metal axes were introduced.3 Then he adds:
Another factor promoting the intensification of banana and plaintain gardens may have been the pacification and/or the extinction of the Arawak and Carib groups who previously dominated all the navigable rivers in yanomamoland (n.d.: 14).
All this is disputable and very ill-informed, not to mention the distortion in interpretation, and it is essential to reconsider each of the questions raised.
As there are no existing records whatsoever, the distant past of the Yanomami
remains something of a mystery; their linguistic affiliation has not yet been determined, and we have to admit we are left with mere hypotheses. Still, there are a
few scraps of evidence: about one hundred years ago, when the central Yanomami
occupied the Sierra de Parima—which was the geographical centre of their subsequent expansion—they were already horticulturists and there is no reason
whatever to believe that they had not been so for a very long time; quite
the contrary, their knowledge, their practices and traditions point to its early
origin; finally, it is not irrelevant to recall here that agriculture appeared in the
Amazon basin several thousand years ago, probably with manioc cultivation. On
several occasions we have disputed the theory that the Yanomami were hunters
and gatherers, who have recently been converted to agriculture4; an interpretation
devoid of empirical foundations. The travellers who saw the Yanomami for the
first time at the beginning of this century in Roraima territory (Brazil) seem to
have encountered them in a nomadic phase or while they were on hunting expeditions, far from their usual settlements and gardens, and their testimony is not
This content downloaded from 132.174.254.116 on Sat, 14 Mar 2026 22:11:11 UTC
All use subject to https://about.jstor.org/terms
J. LIZOT 499
decisive; perhaps that is where the myth of the hunting and gathering Yanomami comes from, which survives in spite of the collected data which entirely refute this interpretation. As to the problem of the origin of the banana, let us humbly confess that it has not been resolved with any certainty and its South-American origin, or pre-Columbian diffusion cannot be excluded a priori. Whatever the case may be, whether or not the banana has recently been introduced into South America (i.e. in post-Columbian times), it tells us nothing about the age and origin of Yanomami agriculture; intensive banana cultivation may have appeared late among a people, already practising agriculture, who adopted it as the main cultigen in place of other plants (manioc or maize).
The intermittent nomadism practised by the Yanomami has to be placed in complementary relation within the overall economic system, including agriculture and hunting (fishing). There is no reason not to consider subsistence economics as homogeneous wholes, rather than isolating one factor of production from the rest to discover whether or not it is prior to these. The low protein content of bananas and manioc is well known . By exploiting the surrounding forest, and by gathering wild fruits many of which have a high protein content, the Yanomami improve the quality of their diet.
II
Having clarified these points, it is difficult to retrace the history of the Yanomami
further back than a century and a half without indulging in more or less risky
speculations. However, in order to reply to Marvin Harris, I shall offer my version
of their history, as it seems to me that the ethnographic context has been a more
faithful guide to me than to my New York colleague. Incidentally, the linguist
Migliazza has made an interesting discovery which will help me; in his thesis
, he has pointed out that the toponymy of the lower Rio Branco was, or
might have been, of Yanomami origin. This fact remains to be considered and
verified scrupulously, but it is significant; a rapid and necessarily superficial
investigation of this toponymy on ordinary maps would appear to confirm
Migliazza's findings. Formerly the Yanomami occupied a territory bordering on
the main tributaries of the Upper Amazon, where the Rio Negro and Rio Branco
meet. They may have clashed with Arawak warriors inhabiting the region and
been driven out and they may also have retreated before slave hunters who were
operating in the area after the European conquest. Whatever the case may have
been, the Yanomami found refuge in the mountainous region called Sierra de
Parima, which marks the border between Brazil and Venezuela. It is inconceivable
that they should not already have been engaged in agriculture following their
contact with the civilisations along the upper reaches of the Amazon or in the
course of previous peregrinations. They did know how to make pottery5 and they
used stone axes. The choice of sites for making their gardens must have been more
important than later when they became equipped with metal tools: at any rate the
introduction of axes and machetes took place at a later date ,
occurring a good while after the demographic and territorial expansion had begun.
and it was in no way decisive. Although a certain number of large trees were
acceptable in the garden, too many were not, and fire was used to eliminate those
This content downloaded from 132.174.254.116 on Sat, 14 Mar 2026 22:11:11 UTC
All use subject to https://about.jstor.org/terms
500 J. LIZOT
that were too thick to be cut down with stone axes alone. It is certain that the practice of agriculture was more laborious then and that the plots were smaller, but these were difficulties which the Yanomami shared with all the South American peoples before the advent of steel tools, so that is not as unusual as we are led to believe.
Isolated in mountains inaccessible at the time, the Yanomami were able to adapt to their new environment, perhaps by developing banana cultivation, perhaps also by emphasising somewhat the bent towards nomadism characteristic of many South American peoples. In the healthy mountain region they found a new demographic balance, protected as they were from epidemics which decimated the Indians elsewhere; this balance was followed by a strong population increase and concomitant territorial expansion. This territorial expansion was facilitated, perhaps even prompted, by the presence of vast uninhabited areas, where the population had long since vanished.6
It is not inconceivable that the Yanomami were skilled navigators in the past, but that after settling in the Parima mountains they necessarily lost this skill; there the valleys are narrow and deep, the rivers are torrents congested with rocks battered by the foaming waters and there are many falls: any navigation is practically impossible. Around the year 1950, when the Yanomami occupied—or reoccupied—flat, open country with tranquil rivers they had lost all knowledge of navigation and were only able to make foot bridges for crossing the rivers, or let themselves float along the water on rafts. It is not possible to learn overnight how to carve monoxyle canoes. Marvin Harris is mistaken when he says:
They do not know how to construct or paddle canoes, although their settlements are now on the banks of or close by the Orinoco and Mavaca rivers. They do little fishing, although such waters are usually rich in fish and aquatic animals .
Today, less than one tenth of the total Yanomami population dwells along the banks of navigable rivers; the communities which have settled there have adapted in varying degrees to their new habitat, but the Indians on the whole know how to handle boats, some carve paddles, and others are already able to make boats. Some Yanomami along the rio Padamo have become as skilled in canoe building as their Ye'kwana teachers. As for fishing, the Yanomami have been doing it since time began; they have a good knowledge of fish poisons; formerly they knew how to make fish-hooks from bone, and when the water was low, they shot the fish with arrows. They went into the swamps and caught fish in nets made from plantfibre or in baskets. Today they have adopted nylon lines and metal hooks, practise fishing with floats and are just as skilled as others in exploiting the rivers.
A number of specialists have established a hypothetical account of Amerindian peoples' history which entirely corroborates my own speculative reconstruction. Thus, Donald W. Lathrap, in his description of the past history of the inhabitants of the tropical forest uplands and areas lying between the main waterways, recalls two theories: one considers Indians there to be backward representatives of a remote and primitive stage of the tropical forest culture; the other suggests that in most cases it is a matter of simpler, less sedentary groups than those who occupy the banks of the main rivers, the degraded representatives of peoples who at a certain time had a more advanced culture .

Rejecting these two interpretations, Lathrap writes:
The more astute students of tropical forest culture ... have all observed that tropical forest culture is less an adaptation to the forest as such, than to the riverine environment within the tropical forest .
He then posits this remarkable conclusion:
Most of the primitive groups inhabiting the tropical forest uplands away from the major flood plains can be interpreted as the wreckage of evolved agricultural societies forced into an environment unsuitable to the basic economic pattern. Deprived of the riverine resources, such groups had to rely on the hunting of forest game to provide the protein and fat essential to the diet. A more intense orientation to hunting the relatively scarce game led to more nomadism, a decline in agriculture productivity, and a still greater dependence on wild food .
Leaving aside the kind of implicit value judgement in these lines let us only consider the plausible historical outline, and thus: the type of agriculture practised by the Yanomami, their relative nomadism, their dependency on wild fruits, these are the best adaptations to their present environment. Lathrap's contribution raises other problems which will be discussed in the following pages: it is not certain that game is always all that scarce in the forest and unless formal proof can be adduced, it seems unjustified to say that agricultural productivity decreases when one moves away from the river (bank). To put it briefly, let us say that the opposition between the riparian areas of the main waterways (propitious for agriculture, rich in animal life) and the intermediate zones of the uplands (less favourable for human living) is perhaps not as pronounced as is generally believed: the pedological analysis of the soil, the estimation of garden productivity, the observation of hunting, and the study of diet do not evidence any marked differences.
Ш
Let us now examine the demographic aspect of Harris's various contributions which relate war to an alleged scarcity of protein resources. The central Yanomami9 are composed of two population blocks, 10 distributed in thirty-five distinct local groups derived from two original communities: husirawë and irabraobë. There are strong indications that these two communities may have once represented one single group (about 120–150 years ago). In 1974, complete demographic statistics were compiled for twenty-five of the local groups and a population count made of the remaining communities. The figures for the total population of the central Yanomami are given in table 1.

DISTRIBUTION OF THE POPULATION OF THE CENTRAL YANOMAMI BY SEX AND AGE

On the basis of these data we can make a number of observations concerning the demographic structure of the central Yanomami, but let us confine ourselves to the following: the marked imbalance in the sex ratio in favour of men for all age brackets ranging from birth to 20 years of age, the balance being resolved between 30 and 35 years of age. The sex ratio for the whole sample is 1:19, and this concurs with the data which N. A. Chagnon and I have published previously and does not present any major regional variations.
Harris points to a close correlation between warfare and the sex ratio imbalance in favour of men  or, in other words, that warfare seems to be connected with the practice of female infanticide. After making this correct, but commonplace observation, Harris exaggerates the extent of female infanticide and the sex ratio imbalance by submitting that the Yanomami kill 50 per cent. of their girls and that in this society there are, globally speaking (i.e. for all age-groups) 154 men to 100 women. Let the reader for a moment check Table 2 and he will note that from birth to 5 years of age there are 143 boys to 114 girls, which is far from the alleged 50 per cent. of girls killed. In the age levels between 0 and 15 years the ratio is 139:100 (139 men to 100 women) and the balance is redressed to 96:100 for all age groups over 25 years of age.
Harris notes a direct relation between the local and temporary intensity of warfare, and the sex ratio variations, as though a sudden outbreak of violence, of short duration, would have immediate repercussions on the sex ratio equilibrium. This argument prompts him to use in a distorted form the figures I published in 1971, which reflected the short-lived and unstable situation of two localities with dissimilar demographic profiles, although both were equally at war with some of of their neighbours .
What was the Yanomami population growth rate during the last century before the devastating effects of contact? A tentative assessment of the initial population in the two original communities yields the following results: around 1900, husirawë and irabraobë must have had 150 and 250 inhabitants respectively; in 74
This content downloaded from 132.174.254.116 on Sat, 14 Mar 2026 22:11:11 UTC
All use subject to https://about.jstor.org/terms
J. LIZOT 503
years the population of these two groups rose from 400 inhabitants to 2068, a more than five-fold increase.
The rate of population growth was probably even higher, as the Yanomami ceased to increase in numbers after contact with the white and creole world  and Falciparum malaria epidemics  and measles  decimated them. If one wishes to estimate what the maximum number of these Indians was in the past, at least 20 per cent. has to be added to the present population figure. Table 3 shows the central Yanomami population representing the most important stages of their evolution since 1900.

In 1950 there were 2482 central Yanomami (0.34 inhabitant per sq. k), and between 1900 and 1950 the population in the specified zone had grown six times, which represents a doubling every twenty years and this despite war, infanticide and the late weaning of children (accompanied by the prohibition on sexual relations). During the same time span the number of communities had increased from two to thirty-five subsequent to a fissioning process and highly intensive territorial expansion. One cannot therefore evade the following problem. How sharply can a population rise without threatening the ecological balance and without requiring a change in the economic and political organisation? We are trying to answer the second question here: the first one is too complex and will be dealt with in a work on Yanomami economy currently under preparation, when complete, organised and analysed demographic data are available.
Harris notes that under certain conditions war may have a stabilising effect on the population and help prevent excessive exploitation of critical resources . It has just been demonstrated that war, which is endemic among the Yanomami, has not achieved this two-fold result, but that the precise relationship between population and natural environment remains to be assessed. Our author commits another error when he remarks:
Deaths due to warfare among the Yanomamo, for example, have no long-run effect on the size of the Yanomamo population because like almost all warmaking preindustrial societies, the Yanomamo are polygynous (i.e. each man has several wives) .
The word 'preindustrial' presupposes an evolutionary scheme of societies which I would be inclined to reject, but that is not the issue at hand. The Yanomami do, in fact, practise polygyny, just as they do polyandry which to a certain extent compensates for it and makes it tolerable. But how widespread is it? A brief survey of a few communities indicates that 13·23 per cent. of alliances are polygynous and that only 1·4 per cent. of married men have more than two wives at the same time, which invalidates another of Harris's assertions that 25 per cent. of the men have at
This content downloaded from 132.174.254.116 on Sat, 14 Mar 2026 22:11:11 UTC
All use subject to https://about.jstor.org/terms
J. LIZOT
least two wives . In his presentation of the position of women among the Yanomami Harris starts out by quoting a partially distorted myth11; he calls the separate partition where young girls are isolated during their first menses a 'cage' 12 according to him men are apparently as tyrannical with their spouses as oriental monarchs are with their slaves  and the economic function of women is supposedly neither more nor less than that of an exploited class;  a good deal of the violence which they are subject to is committed for no good reason or motive; men could prostitute their women with the first visitor passing through . All this is untrue.
I cannot help feeling that female infanticide has been greatly exaggerated and that the imbalance of births slanted towards men, real enough though it is, is not a valid indicator. First and foremost a close investigation of the demographic data is indispensable, and this investigation is under way. I suspect that a natural deficit of births of female children makes the disproportion between the two sexes more pronounced, and recent research carried out by a number of biologists on the population of primitive peoples could validate this hypothesis. I observed few cases of infanticide among the Yanomami, but I know that male infants are also killed.
The decision to kill a child is often made before birth, when the sex of the child is still unknown. In many families it is a girl who is the eldest and it is not true, as Harris writes, that the first children are killed systematically until one of the male sex arrives. Once they have been accepted, girls are just as well fed as boys and although they may be disciplined more frequently, one can by no means assert that this relative severity is the because of a higher mortality.
In the link he establishes between the intensity of warfare, population density
and exploitation of natural resources, Harris contrasts a central area where war is
deemed to be intensive and population relatively dense, and a peripheral area where
warfare is less pronounced, as it were, and population scattered. Perhaps the
source of confusion here is to be found in N. A. Chagnon's early writings13 where
this distinction appears. What exactly is being referred to in this opposition between 'centre' and 'periphery'? It is a matter of relative geographical positions for
the Yanomami whom I call central are actually settled in the heart of the territory
occupied by the ethnic group, and the so-called shamathari live on the periphery:
this is exactly the inverse of the alleged situation. If, on the other hand the reference
applies to the 'Social circumscription' concept used by Chagnon to describe the
situation in certain communities lacking unoccupied territories around them for
migratory purposes, then the concept only applies, strictly speaking, to the communities in the Sierra de Parima. That is where Yanomami expansion really began
and the groups living there are small, contiguous communities; it is in the Sierra de
Parima that population density is probably highest; but contrary to what Harris's
theory implies, war and violence there have a rather low profile: the alleged
relation between the different variables listed (population density, level of exploitation of natural resources and degree of violence) is not relevant.
One may indeed mention differences which obtain not between a 'centre' and a 'periphery' but between what Chagnon, in a more felicitous if not more elegant formula than the preceding, refers to as 'blocks of population', that is to say collections of communities linked together by a common origin, but separated as a result of fissions and migrations tending to move them further and further apart.

Furthermore we have only a fragmentary and superficial knowledge of the
forest, its resources and ecological balance, and thus we must be all the more
modest and cautious in our judgements. We know little or nothing of plant
associations, nothing about the plants and their relation to the composition of the
soil, nothing or next to nothing about the habits of animals, their supply and the
distribution of their habitats, nothing about the complex set of relations established
between animal life and the plant cover which serves as auxiliary food. Just to
mention a few studies and a few cases: the hand book Plantas comunes de Venezuela
by L. Schnee , based on the Pittier files, omits a large number of plants
commonly used by the Indians for nutritional and technological purposes; the
leguminous plants of the Inga species which grow in the Upper Orinoco are not
listed; an inventory of the Carvocaceae was made only because of my own
ethnographic researches. Napier & Napier's Handbook of living primates 
documents well African monkeys but fails to mention the weight of the Saki satan,
although this is one of the most common Cebidae. In order to carry out my research on Yanomami economy I have therefore had to take the initiative in collaborating with botanists, zoologists and pedologists, and to study problems not
directly related to social anthropology.
If we on the other hand turn to Amerindian economics, we cannot but realise how little has been done up to now. No complete work has been published to date and in order to give an opinion one is forced to regurgitate and misuse some meagre facts. The human geographer K. Ruddle's worthwhile thesis  deals especially with agriculture among the Yukpa, necessarily leaving a number of important problems aside; as for Lapointe's thesis  on the Wayana often mentioned as a reference source, it is distressingly threadbare and one can never be sure when the author is measuring and when he is evaluating. Thus Gross and Harris are reduced to supporting their argument with mediocre data; one need only consider what dangerous extrapolations they are forced into to realise this.
For example, Gross's general table of the game and fish supply in Amazonia , completely disregards the regional variations the importance of which ichthyologists and zoologists are just beginning to discover. Harris and Gross also endeavour to demonstrate that there is game shortage in the forest and that hunting is a gamble with a highly unpredictable output, but how are we to reconcile the impressionistic image they construe of it on the basis of accounts of unequal value, with their apparent concern to conduct a rigorous analysis substantiated by figures and precise calculation methods. The discrepancy between facts, methods and objectives of analysis is obvious. To gather an approximate idea of the animal population in a given area, one must first know it intimately, travel with a chronometer in one's hand, and take note of all animals encountered on one's way, list and weigh game killed by hunters and mark the boundaries of the hunting territories.
Having joined in countless hunting expeditions both as observer and participant, I am in a position to claim and to prove that game is not so scarce in this area as we are led to believe: in some areas game abounds, one rarely comes back empty-handed from a hunt, in unfrequented places there are numerous hordes of wild pigs, an abundance of tapirs, Cracidae and Tinamidae in profusion, the trumpeters
This content downloaded from 132.174.254.116 on Sat, 14 Mar 2026 22:11:11 UTC
All use subject to https://about.jstor.org/terms
J. LIZOT 507
are not very wild, monkeys live in large bands; these are the animals most frequently eaten by the Indians.
To ensure their daily meal requirements, the hunters roam through the surrounding forest for five to seven hours and return to the dwelling when the sun has passed its zenith. The women gather and slaughter the smaller animals. When a large quantity of meat is required for a major feast to which neighbours have been invited, or for a funerary rite, the hunters go into areas not normally visited. They set up camp there where they remain from four to six days. During these expeditions food is abundant and the amount of meat which is smoked is often impressive; as an example, here is the yield from three hunts performed with traditional weapons (bow and arrow).

V
Little is known about the idea Amazonian groups have of their territory, or about their economic practices; we have more information about the areas occupied by gibbons or gorillas; to ascertain this one has only to compare the two pages of generalities, which Leroi-Gourham  in the absence of appropriate documentation, devotes to the matter, with the condensed and precise facts contained in Jolly's Evolution of primate behaviour . Nevertheless, Harris peremptorily writes the following with respect to the Yanomami:
extended expeditions inevitably cross and recross hunting territories that are used by villages other than one's own .
In fact each Yanomami community has its own hunting territories. These territories are exclusive and determined by use alone, and they are never the object of dispute. Since neither game nor space is scarce, there is no competition for resources and the community is sovereign.
To assess the protein and energy content of the diet, we have chosen two local groups for purposes of comparison. The first is karohi-theri, on the banks of the River Manaviche occupying the peneplain formed by the Orinoco and its main affluents. The second, kakashiwë-theri which is more representative of the groups of the interior, deploys its activities in hilly terrain, has a garden in the mountains and enjoys access to a small river. The information we offer here represents a mere fraction of what is available to us, but as it will be seen, does show useful variations and adequately reflects the phenomena evoked here. The facts and figures are presented in a series of tables; the first indicates the composition of population in the two communities; the second specifies the sources of animal protein, and the

All use subject to https://about.jstor.org/terms

third the sources of vegetal protein; with a distinction drawn between men's and women's respective contributions; the fourth, finally, is an evaluation of the diet in terms of each product.

Without undertaking a detailed analysis of the results obtained let us simply calculate the daily calorie protein ration per person in each of the two communities. Observations in karohi were carried out for a 42-day period, there are 47.5 consumers, which equals 1995 days of food-intake; observations were made in kakashiwë for 28 days and there are 2.88 consumers, that is 806.4 days of food-intake.
When one attempts to explain the differences in protein value between the
groups one discovers that these are not due to a greater or lesser abundance of game
in the areas under consideration, but rather to the attitude to work of members in
these communities. To come to the point, kakashiwë and karohi are engaged in
equally intensive and murderous war. The forest is more abundant in game at
kakashiwë than at karohi, but hunting is more intensive at karohi than at kakashiwë.
In the vicinity of kakashiwë one encounters the first animal, along well-used paths,
after 29 minutes walk and after that an animal is driven out from cover whenever
one has walked for 22 minutes, on an average. The first camp for long duration
hunts is located at a distance of I hour and 22 minutes from the shabono, and the
most remote is 2 hours 14 minutes away. At karohi, one comes across the first
animal after 38 minutes walk and after that encounters one after every 37 minutes
of territory covered. The first camp for long hunting expeditions is 2 hours and
13 minutes away from the shabono, the furthest 3 hours and 25 minutes away. Why
then, if the forest around kakashiwë is more abundant in game, do they eat less
meat there than in karohi? For the simple reason that people are lazier in kakashiwë
than in karohi: their gardens are smaller, they hunt less frequently and for shorter
periods, prefer to eat less well and take it easy instead. When karohi hunters take

off on a hunt of long duration, they are gone for five or six days and do not return until they are honourably laden with smoked meat—in kakashiwë such expeditions do not last more than three or four days and the men come back no matter how much game they have killed. During one of the weeks I spent in kakashiwë obtaining the nutritional information presented here, the men did not go hunting once, they had just collected their favourite hallucinogen (Anadenanthera peregrina) and spent entire days taking drugs; the women complained there was no meat, but the men turned a deaf ear, and that week the amount of protein consumed had fallen to 11 grams per person per day (which lowered the general average considerably). In other words it is not because the forest is short of game that the diet is less rich in kakashiwë, but because the inhabitants there are more idle; and warfare is not the more intensive for it either. Here is an unexpected variable which Harris's model had not provided for: the attitude to work.
In estimating the protein content of the Indians' food, Gross  uses
documentation which is neither consistently reliable nor precise; lacking the necessary information, he entirely disregards plant protein which, according to the
season, represents 15 to 30 per cent. of the total food intake. Gross does not base
his evaluation of requirements on the weight per person, but on general standards
which when applied to the Indians, who are usually of small stature, are probably
excessive; finally, the energy expenditure of the activities is not taken into account.
With regard to the standards themselves, there is by no means any agreement
among the specialists in human dietetics. Without specifying to which populations
these standards apply, the author indicates that F.A.O. considers a ratio of 0.300.35 grs of protein per kg of weight to be the minimum daily requirement; according to some specialists these standards may reach 0.65-0.80 gr. per kg. The
average weight of an adult Yanomami male is 48 kg, so according to the first ratio
the adequate protein ration would be 16.8 gr per day, and according to the second
38.4 gr; the average weight of an adult female being 40 kg, her requirements in
each case would be 14·10 gr and 32·24 gr respectively. Finally Gross considers a
daily protein ration of 40 to 50 gr to be indispensable for Amazon Indians; these
figures overrate the requirements defined by our previous calculation. To avoid
such a fluctuation it is essential to base any estimate on the weight of the Indians
of each age group, and to assess as precisely as possible the energy expenditure of
work. The present study does not adequately meet these criteria, but our future
publications will.
Whatever the case may be, with 74 gr of protein per person per day, the Yanomami come far above the minimum requirement. If one compares this

gross result with the data presented by Gross for nine Amazonian societies  one notices that the Yanomami hold second place in terms of the quality of their nutrition, just after the Sharanahua of Peru. This is a serious matter for if there is a relation between the intensity of warfare within a given society and the degree of exploitation of natural resources, then why are the Yanomami, who enjoy a rich and varied diet and work so little, by far the most bellicose of all the peoples singled out by Gross? Infinitely more warlike than the Bayana Cuna of Panama with their modest 18 gr of protein per person per day? Harris of course opts for a comfortable retreat when he in the end concedes that war breaks out to regularise the situation long before the upper threshold of the exploitation of resources has been reached. But whether this is a rule or a law the mechanism must work at all times and in all places in order to exist. This is simply not the case for the very obvious reason that the postulated, but undemonstrated, relation is absent and the explanation of warfare has to be sought elsewhere.
It remains to be determined to what extent agriculture, hunting, fishing, and gathering each contribute to the Yanomami diet, how much time is devoted to the various subsistence activities and how much women participate.


In order to eat quite adequately, it is sufficient for the Yanomami to work between 2 hours and 26 minutes (women) and 2 hours and 50 minutes (men) a day, a minimal effort to achieve this excellent result. Would the same be true if nature were stingy with its products and the environment had reached an extreme stage of degradation? These are Indians who are supposed to be on the brink of starvation and material want who work so little and prefer to indulge in cruel warfare rather than working a bit harder. Such are the absurd conclusions established by prejudices and the theoretical preconceptions of vulgar materialism.
A great selectivity in the choice of their foods (in the forest, the Indians do not gather everything available), the localisation of those forest areas which are really exploited, abundant and varied food, unexacting work, all these are signs of abundance. It is voluntarily that the Yanomami neglect work requiring too great exertion and limit themselves to satisfing the daily subsistence with as little effort as possible. The Indians never work more than is strictly necessary. Rather than

All use subject to https://about.jstor.org/terms
J. LIZOT

repair a hammock cord which is defective they prefer to wait for it to break and
fall apart. Rather than replacing the broken and rotten roof of the shabono, they
let the water drip until the situation becomes unbearable. Sometimes the yield
from a garden turns out to be totally inadequate due to insufficient work; the
Indians then resort to resources ordinarily disdained: they eat the plants and fruits
which they did not previously condescend to collect, they work harder, nomadise
more, and receive the hospitality of allied neighbouring communities.
No, the Indians have not 'eaten the forest' nor destroyed the environment. On
the contrary, their economy is in harmony with the possibilities of the natural
environment and is perfectly integrated with it. The reasonable limits of population growth have never been reached and territorial expansion could still be
carried out into uninhabited areas.
Who can deny that in particular cases war may have served the purpose of
conquest among certain primitive tribal peoples; the Tsembaga example has become a classic in ethnographic literature and Rappaport's demonstration is conclusive. But should we on the basis of one proven example establish an automatic
link between warfare and the appropriation of neighbouring territories? This
causal relation has never been demonstrated. The Yanomami do not make war to
conquer hunting territories, nor to capture marriageable women either, and even
though they themselves offer us the latter rationalisation, there is no reason for us
to go along with them: the ideology of a people is one thing, the deeper, underlying motivations for action another. The Yanomami make war to punish insults,
avenge deaths, and fulfil kinship obligations.
That one of the consequences of warfare and internal violence in the communities happens to have led to a better adaptation to the environment is an effect and
not a because. Vayda  and Gross are not wrong when they stick to such general
observations and claim that war has profound economic and demographic repercussions. But have they explained the nature of the phenomenon of war? They
have not. I, in this article, am only saying what war is not.
If ecological anthropologists are to lead us into such misconceptions it would be
better to turn away from them, give up their gadgets and refrain from using their
distorting mirror. A certain caution compels us to shun fads like the plague.

Department of Anthropology, University of California, Berkeley, California 94720, YOU.S.A.
Recent research shows that lowland forests of the Amazon Basin differ in numerous ways including
features of climate and soils, faunal composition and forest structure, composition and phenology. Such
differences strongly suggest that single-factor models used to explain features of human ecology in
Amazonia may be too limited. A comparative study of the dietary ecology of four forest-living indigenous
groups in Brazil (Arara, Parakana, Arawete, Mayoruna) revealed a number of differences. Primary crops,
as well as animal types most: utilized as prey, were found to differ markedly between groups. Although
some differences can be accounted for by general environmental factors, no compelling single
environmental factor can explain why any one group could not behave dietarily in ways more similar to
another. Many of these intergroup dietary differences appear to represent a type of cultural character
displacement that aids in distinguishing the members of one group from another. As all human groups,
through the medium of culture, are actual or potential occupants of the same dietary niche, each group
may distance itself from potential dietary rivals through cultural conventions. This behaviour may be
justified, as the lack of overlap between forest-living groups in combination with generally intense
intergroup hostility suggests that the biomass and distribution patterns of critical dietary resources in this
environment may set limits to viable population size for particular areas.
1. INTRODUCTION
invoked to explain numerous aspects of human ecology
in tropical forests of the Amazon Basin, including
population and settlement size, patterns of residence
and nomadic and semi-nomadic behaviours . Limited
dietary resources have been suggested to influence
behavioural traits such as aggression, warfare and
infanticide . It has been hypothesized that tropical
rain-forest environments are so nutrient-poor that
human populations could not survive in them without
access to crop foods . Tropical forest dwellers are often denigrated by
terms such as 'marginal' peoples or 'refugees', the
implication being that no one would choose to live in
such an environment if other alternatives were possible
. All of this creates a largely negative
and greatly oversimplified picture of tropical-forest
peoples and their environment.
been the fact that most have been advanced without
detailed knowledge of the potential of local dietary
resources or actual food habits of the associated
indigenous inhabitants . Often
present-day environmental or dietary conditions are
ascribed to the past without good historical or
archaeological evidence. I suggest that all of the singlefactor dietary models used to explain features of human
ecology in forests of the Amazon Basin may have been
pre-destined to failure by their attempts to provide a
single generic explanation for what is, in effect, a vast
and complex mosaic of different dietary possibilities
calling for a variety of different solutions by human
foragers . These differences include: (i)
wide regional and seasonal variation in rainfall patterns
and hours of solar insolation; (ii) a range of soil
conditions as diverse as in temperate zones ; (iii) a number of forest types with
structure, composition and phenological production
patterns which vary within, as well as between,
geographical regions ; and (iv) variation in the composition,
distribution patterns and biomass of the associated
faunal communities .
Given all of these differences, it seems logical to assume
that the human inhabitants of such forests might face
a wide range of different possibilities with respect to
dietary potential.
2. THE MONKEY MODEL
forest dwellers was stimulated by earlier work on the
diets of non-human primates in this same environment
. In the neotropics, non-human
primates are confined to the forest canopy where the

largest biomass of digestible plant matter occurs. By
specializing on different subsets of the available plant
resources, generally supplemented by second trophic
level foods, a large number of primate species are able
to coexist sympatrically .
In non-human primates, strong intraspecific aggression between groups is the norm; as members of the
same species, they occupy the same dietary niche, and
intense dietary competition is expected to prevail. In
contrast, interspecific aggression between sympatric
groups of non-human primates is relatively rare
because each monkey species has its own dietary niche.
In contrast to non-human primates, humans live on
the forest floor where the biomass of edible plant
matter is generally low. Similar to conspecific monkey
groups, human groups in the Amazon Basin show a
strong tendency to repel or discourage other human
groups from utilizing their supplying area; this
antipathy generally holds whether non-residents speak
the same or a different language than residents. In
ecological terms, it would appear that the establishment of one human group in a given area has a
decidedly negative effect on the probability of establishment of another human group within the same
area. Given all of the disturbances to indigenous
populations in the Amazon Basin since contact, this
spacing pattern may now be less obvious than in the
past. However, the large number of different language
groups known to have occupied forests of the Amazon
Basin pre-contact , in combination
with the present-day locales of remaining groups
, strongly suggests that in the past
(and, in many areas, even today) Amazonian lowland
forests were well saturated with indigenous groups,
each of which strived to maintain exclusive use of its
particular supplying area .
human ecology or behaviour in this environment
prohibit or minimize the potential for overlap. By
analogy with non-human primates, dietary factors
would appear to be implicated. To understand better
what such factors might be, I here examine the
respective forest environments and diets of some forestliving groups.
3. STUDY GROUPS
successive generations within the vast expanses of
upland terra firme forest and should not be confused
with other indigenous inhabitants of the Amazon
Basin, such as riparian fisher-gardeners (e.g. the
Tukanoans) or savannah-transition woodland peoples
(e.g. the Kayapo). The inhabitants of these environments differ from one another in a large number of
traits, including physical traits , and
typically reside in quite different ecological zones.
Although they were forest-dwellers, all the groups I
worked with not only hunted and gathered but also
practised slash-and-burn horticulture and are best
regarded as hunter-gatherer-horticulturalists (HGH).
torical, static isolates . Rather, all such groups have obviously
been affected over centuries by contact both with
other, generally hostile, indigenous groups and by
outside influences, including trade goods, new cultivars
and new diseases . None the less, it is still possible
to find little-acculturated groups who are long-term
inlhabitants of forests of the Amazon Basin, who still
hunt almost exclusively with their traditional weapons,
and whose food (possibly excluding salt) is still
obtained through their own efforts and interactions
with their forest environment. The four groups
discussed in this paper conform to this description.
Three of the four study groups - the Arara, Parakana
and Arawete - live in central Para state, Brazil (figure
1). What is known of their history suggests considerable
transitional movement throughout this region, such
that in the past, one group may actually have lived in,
or very near, an area now occupied by another. Actual
hostilities are known to have taken place between at
least two and possibly all three of these groups, in one
case (between the Arawete and Parakana) as recently
as 1983 . The fourth group, the Mayoruna,
lives to the west of the other three in the state of
Amazonas (figure 1).

centuries, presumably having migrated south from the
speaking groups still occur . Once estimated to consist of more than 300
individuals, the total group today consists of around 90
people. My work was done in Curambe, a small village
on the Iriri River inhabited by 20 Arara, ten of whom
were adults. During my study, the residents of this
village were moved by the Indian Bureau (FUNAI) to
a new site called Ikopty, closer to the junction of the
Laranjal.
The Parakana, a group of approximately 350 individuals, live in three villages in the region between the
Tocantins and Xingu Rivers. They speak a language of
the Tupi-Guarani linguistic family. The village I
worked with was located approximately 30 km east of
the Xingu River on a small tributary called Bom
Jardim (figure 1). This village is unusual in that until
two years before my study its inhabitants had lived for
20 or more years as nomadic hunter-gatherers in the
forest in an effort to escape attacks from hostile G&-
speakers and outsiders. In 1983, after being contacted
by the Indian Bureau, 105 Parakana agreed to settle in
the BomJardim area, and in 1984 they were joined by
another 31 nomadic Parakana .

The Mayoruna, Panoan speakers, live east and west
of the Javari River in Peru and Brazil. This group is
noted for its extremely aggressive behaviour; its raids
on settlers and river boats, and abduction of women,
made the Javari River region unsafe for outsiders until
the 1970s. The Mayoruna group I studied consisted of
117 individuals living in the state of Amazonas on the
Lobo River, a small tributary of the Javari River
(figure 1).

4. RESULTS
(a) Variation in environmental factors
patterns presented in figure 2 confirm regional and
seasonal differences in these parameters for the two
main study regions. Similar differences were noted for
my on-site rainfall data. No general statements can be
made with respect to soil analyses other than to note
confirmation of the well appreciated fact that clearing
and burning forest cover greatly enriches soil fertility
for periods lasting longer than two years. These results
were consistent regardless of locale. However, in the
total data set, some in-site differences in soil parameters
were as profound as between-site differences. Space
does not permit discussion of these often complex
results, which are presented in a separate publication
(K. Milton, in preparation). Information on forest
composition is presented in table 1. At the family level,
forest composition did not differ notably between sites;
there were, however, striking local differences in the
number of genera and species per family, as well as the
number of stems. For example, forest plots of the
Mayoruna contained no arborescent palms; in contrast, palms were one of the best represented families
in forest plots of the Arara and Parakana in Para
(table 1).

For each group, data were compiled on the size and
condition of the residents' teeth. The Arawete had
extremely poor teeth relative to other groups, who
generally had excellent teeth. Individual Arawete as
young as 19-24 years of age had molar teeth missing,
as well as some premolars, and conditions only
worsened with age. The poor condition of Arawete
teeth may result from abrasive action of corn powder
on dental enamel and the continuous decay of corn
starch at the gum line.
The Arawete drank, and cooked corn only in fresh
water from water holes which they dug in a clay-based
substrate. They also ate clay from the bottom and sides
of these water holes. Chemical attributes of this water
may enhance nutrients found in corn, or aid in
detoxification of adverse chemicals in the Arawete diet
. A non-dietary possibility is that, by drinking only ground-filtered water,
the Arawete may avoid water-transmitted parasites.

particularly plantains. In summary, each group utilized

monkeys accounted for 20-29\% of the total prev

In striking contrast to the Arara, the Parakana are
specialists on terrestrial game, particularly land tortoises, tapir and wild pigs, but also armadillo and paca
(table 2). They hunt with dogs, which is effective for

The Parakana also eat insects, particularly palm
larvae which are a rich source of fat. Analyses show fat

the forest, animal fat may have been particularly

actively cultivated palm larvae at sites throughout the

available to them throughout the year.

(table 2). The Arawete village was densely populated

As shown in table 2, the Arawete also ate a variety
of other prey items including numerous land tortoises
as well as agoutis and monkeys. De Castro 
regards tortoises, particularly tortoise liver, as the most
preferred animal food of the Arawete. He also notes

Although the Mayoruna ate a range of prey species,
hunting returns show a focus on wild pigs (Tayassu
spp.) and certain of the larger monkey species (table
2). Wild pigs were taken more frequently and

(T. pecari). Although only one tapir was killed during
my study, the Mayoruna stated that tapir was an
important and preferred prey species.

6. DISCUSSION
(a) Factors related to dietary differences
ferences between regions appear to affect the dietary
pattern of their indigenous inhabitants. The Mayoruna,
for example, live in an area lying outside the ancient
and heavily weathered Guianan and Brazilian shields
. Climatic data confirm high and
relatively uniform rainfall, and forest samples confirm
a high and relatively even diversity of tree types per
unit area. High primary productivity and less dominance by particular plant groups should result in a
diverse and high faunal biomass which, in turn, should
be reflected in the dietary pattern of the Mayoruna.
Among the Mayoruna, prey choice was more
specialized than in other groups as hunters generally
were able to secure large and highly preferred prey
species. Conversely, in Para the return on large game
was low and less even than the return on small game
(table 2). Prey selection by Mayoruna appeared similar
to that of the Waorani, who live in a productive
lowland forest region in eastern Ecuador. Large
monkeys, peccaries and large birds make up the
highest percentage (seven species compose > 53 %0) of
Waorani kills . Further, in
striking contrast to the three Para groups, the
Mayoruna had an extensive list of avoided prey.
for calories, the Mayoruna also routinely consumed
large quantities of bananas, so much so that, in effect,
they came close to having two primary carbohydrates.
In contrast, the Para groups were more narrowly
focused on a single carbohydrate crop. Overall, the
broader crop focus than other groups considered in this
study, a pattern that appears related to their generally
more productive environment.
vironmental differences, none of the environmental
factors I examined appeared sufficiently distinct to
explain most of the dietary differences observed
(although, certainly, future research on this question
may provide new data to alter this view). A priori,
based on present data, in terms of the physical
environment, it seems probable that any one group
could successfully cultivate the primary carbohydrate
crop of another. In contrast to this view, Balee 
has suggested that intensive corn cultivation by the
fertile terra preta soils in their geographical region. In
my view the success of the Arawete as corn cultivators
does not stem from the patchy presence of terra preta
soils in this region, but rather from the Arawete habit
of clearing large new fields for corn cultivation each
year, a labour-intensive endeavour, particularly before

. For example, I
can find no environmental reason why the Parakana
do not eat monkeys. They have lived in Parai for
centuries, a state in which monkeys are abundant. It is
difficult to argue that the heavy, broad arrows of the
monkeys, as the arrows of the Arawete seemed almost
identical to those of the Parakana, yet the Arawete
focus much of their hunting on birds and also eat some
monkeys. Deer and tortoises occur in the supplying

whereas tortoises were eaten. The Parakana and
Mayoruna strongly desire tapir meat, whereas the
Arawete and Arara find tapir meat barely edible. The

could be obtained through censuses to determine the

origins, history and experience in combination with
present-day ecological constraints. Although recognizing that explanations of foodways are generally
neither simple nor clear-cut , I can find no compelling environmental reason why any one group could
not behave dietarily in ways more similar to another if it
so desired. These different dietary practices appear to
reflect, as do the distinctive facial perforations and
body decorations of each group, a type of cultural
character displacement in which the members of group
group B or C by means that are distinctive but do not
pose any actual economic disadvantage. As Rozin
 has noted, dietary customs help to define
cultural and social categories and draw distinctions
within and between groups. Indeed the Indian name
possessed by these groups typically translates into the
'true' or 'real' people, with outsiders described as not
quite human . Similarly, Bahuchet
et al. , in discussing the different agricultural
strategies found among four non-Pygmy groups in the
Lobaye forest, comment ' by investing with cultural

values one or more plant species within their food
system ... each of these contiguous groups affirms its
own ethnic identity' .
(b) Returning to the monkey model
in the Amazon Basin have many dietary differences.
Despite these differences, human groups in these forests
do not live sympatrically; rather, allopatry is the
decided norm. Sympatric monkey species often show
notable differences in details of their dental and
digestive morphology and physiology .
monkey species can dominate a particular subset of the
available dietary resources . Data suggest that, not infrequently, these
interspecific differences are sufficiently pronounced
such that successful invasion of one monkey species into
the dietary niche of another may not be possible
.
This is not the case for human groups. Basically, all
humans have the same digestive morphology and
physiology, as well as the same need for high-quality
dietary resources . Humans in tropical
forests meet their dietary needs with a wide range of
food items, but food choices tend to follow a common
theme, that of using plant foods to meet most energy
demands and animal foods to satisfy most protein
demands. Humans may form into discrete social units
with particular dietary customs and term themselves
' the people', they may decorate their faces and bodies
with specific group symbols but, unlike members of
different monkey species, no human group is immune
to dietary invasion by members of any other group and
the immediate adaptation of any and all aspects of its
subsistence behaviour that seem desirable to the
invaders. Through the adaptive mechanism of culture,
all human groups are actual or potential occupants of
the same dietary niche.
However, this fact alone does not appear sufficient to
explain the allopatry and general xenophobia characteristic of these forest-based groups. Rather, such
behaviour suggests that some essential foods in the
tropical forest may be distributed in patterns that set
limits to viable population size (greater or lesser,
depending on features of forest productivity at particular sites). An increase in human biomass in a given
supplying area might therefore overtax local dietary
resources or make their acquisition prohibitively costly
(central place foraging, for example, sets an upper limit
on viable day range).
could serve as mechanisms to facilitate spatial overlap
by different forest-based human groups, and that in
the past more intergroup overlap might have prevailed.
There seems little evidence, either historical or recent,
to support this assumption. Not only do present-day
groups show a high degree of intolerance for individuals
of other linguistic backgrounds, but there is frequently
considerable hostility between groups speaking the
same language . Roosevelt
 has remarked specifically on the
possibility of pre- and post-contact local and regional
population pressures on subsistence resources. As she
notes, the frequent association of female infanticide,
polygyny and warfare suggests the existence of a selfreinforcing system of population control. Mechanisms
of population control suggest that particular supplying
areas may at times approach the limits of their carrying
capacity .
Before horticulture, forest-living groups presumably
relied on wild plant foods for much of their caloric
intake . Advantages for
human populations utilizing plant foods, rather than
meat, as an energetic substrate are discussed in Milton
1984 (pp. 19-20). In a remarkable recent paper,
Dwyer & Minnegal  suggest that the presentday hunting patterns of the Kubo in lowland rain
forests of New Guinea relate not to present-day
horticultural practices but rather to distribution
patterns of their pre-horticultural carbohydrate staple,
sago palms (Metroxylon sp.). Similarly, the Guaja
as nomadic hunter-gatherers (FUNAI archives, unpublished report). An informant who lived with one
Guaja group for 14 months after first contact stated
that their travel patterns revolved around stands of
babacu palms, the mesocarps and kernels of which
provided the bulk of their intake with respect to
calories. Spacing patterns of human groups in the
tropical forest might, therefore, have evolved initially
to protect access to limited and highly desirable caloric
supplies of wild plant foods. Once horticultural
practices were adopted, protection of wild carbohydrate foods should not be critical. However, with the
adaptation of horticultural practices, population size of
hunter-gatherer groups presumably increased, as a
result of improved and more reliable caloric returns
. Pressures on prey
resources may then have intensified to provide protein
and animal fat for this greater human biomass. Thus,
unless these present-day spacing patterns and avoidance behaviours are historical artifacts, it seems
likely that the efficient utilization of patchily distributed game resources may be involved. The addition
of extra-group hunters into a given supplying area
could reduce the resident group's hunting returns,
either through disruption of traditional patterns of ingroup land use (which could lower prey capture rates
or increase temporal or energetic expenditures involved
in hunting) or by actually lowering the amount of prey
available to in-group hunters. Greater knowledge of
relative prey abundances and renewal rates as well as
details of hunter movements over their total supplying
area for one or more annual cycles would help to test
these possibilities. Local group size, prey sharing
networks and day range are critical factors that must
be kept in balance with the carrying capacity of each
supplying area .
make it clear that single factor hypotheses are unlikely
to suffice in explaining the dietary and other ecological
practices of Amazonian forest dwellers. Rather, in the
