It is useful, at this juncture, to review some of the scattered
observations by ethnographers and naturalists on the problems
entailed in hunting the large mammals, particularly since the
Achuarä can provide no direct data of this kind. The tapir is
perhaps the rarest large species in the tropical forest . Wallace  observed that "it is nowhere
very abundant. . . . It is a very shy and timid animal, wandering
about principally at night." On a number of occasions, he
joined Indians hunting tapir, lying in wait for hours at a feeding
place, "but always without success." On his journey into the
Mato Grosso with Rondón in 1914, Theodore Roosevelt
observed that the tapir was solitary and nocturnal, and took
readily to water when pursued . Such behavior is more likely to be prevented by a
coordinated group than by a solitary hunter .
Because of the special ecological features of the Llanos de Mojos
of eastern Bolivia-seasonally inundated savanna with nearby
forest-there seem to be more than the usual number of tapir,
but Holmberg  noted there that "a tapir is hard to
kill and, when discovered and shot, frequently escapes into a
swamp where the pursuer dares not venture" . The Achuarä region, I might
note, is characterized by numerous and extensive swamp
Almost everywhere in the world, the deer is a furtive creature; in the tropical forests it has a marked "ability to conceal itself and to escape on land and in the water" . Roosevelt  observed of the Brazilian bush-deer that they are solitary animals which "when chased . . . were very apt to take to the water" . In hunting deer, says Holmberg  "the Sirionó recognize the difficulty of killing one by emphasizing that the hunter must aim for the heart."
The capybara is chiefly nocturnal. Even more than the tapir and deer, it "has been specialized for a semi-aquatic life" ; it is very timid and quiet and, when alarmed, seeks the water and plunges in, "swimming and diving with great mastery" .
The sloth, 18 too, tends to adhere to river courses; peculiarly enough for such an arboreal and lethargic creature, it is a fine
swimmer. In addition, its solitariness and nocturnal habits combine with an unusual camouflage to afford it a high degree of elusiveness. After only a few weeks of life, a sloth acquires a covering of green algae which adds to its existent disguise of "coarse, moss-like hair . . . strikingly similar to the surrounding masses of epiphytic vegetation" .
In contrast to these species, smaller vertebrates unquestionably provide a more secure basis for obtaining game within a
certain range of settlement size. Beebe  commented.
for example, that "the most abundant mammal in the study
area is the agouti . . . my Indian hunter and I have killed one
hundred and thirty-five for eating and yet we hardly ever take
a walk through the jungle without seeing or hearing one of these
red-rumped rodents." The agouti, indeed, may be the most
resilient of game animals. Nimuendajú  reports that, among the Tukuna who inhabit
the banks and islands of the Middle Amazon, game was "tolerably abundant" until around the turn of the century, when
firearms and sales of skins depleted most of the major animals;
after this occurred, the most common game animal in the
jungle surrounding settlements was the agouti, followed by the
paca and coatimundi. Denevan  notes that, while
"most of the large game is seriously depleted in the central
Gran Pajonal," the Campa rely chiefly upon small animals,
especially rodents and birds. Wagley and Galvão 
observed that, in the neighborhood of relatively long-established
villages of the Tenetehara, deer and tapir (and, in fact, peccary
and paca as well) were hard to obtain and, after 20 years
occupying one site, villagers "killed only agoutis, tortoises, and
occasionally a paca." Among the westernmost Jívaro, in the
region of the Bomboiza River (a tributary of the Zamora),
larger rodents such as paca and agouti were the Indians' chief
source of meat.
Evidence such as this has a tremendous consistency from region to region, and what is notable is how well it coincides with an insider's own impressions. According to a Desana Indian informant of Reichel-Dolmatoff , "a man who goes to hunt for two or three days per week obtains approximately three catches; for example, a small rodent, an armadillo, and a few birds. In a month, he can get three or four wild guinea pigs, two cavies, and a monkey, a deer or peccary every two months, and a tapir once a year."

While the mature tropical forest is generally poor in food for
most mammals, as well as for humans, modifications of the
forest create significantly different microhabitats for a variety
of fauna. Pfeffer , speaking of humid tropical forest
in Asia, for example, observes that, while soil types, altitude,
and similar factors alter forest composition,
the animal population seems to be little affected by these subtle
variations in plant associations. The fauna is, on the other hand, far
more sensitive to the transition from primary or "virgin" forest to
secondary forest, which replaces the former when this has been
destroyed either naturally or artificially.... In the secondary
forest . .. the trees are generally smaller, with more branches, and
the soil is always covered with varied and often dense vegetation.
This is equally true for the Amazon. Secondary forest, however,
is rarely created by natural forces on a significant scale, since
"the tropical rain forest is perhaps the only major vegetation
type in which fire is not a factor" . Two
alternative mechanisms are seasonal inundations by alluvial
rivers and periodic and relatively random windfalls. By any
measure, however, the process of swidden cultivation must be
considered an important means by which the forest canopy is
opened up and light permitted to reach the ground, where it can

. I would merely recommend a modification of
terminology here, since encounters are not synonymous with
hunting, a point nicely brought out by Nelson  in his
discussion of moose hunting among the Alaskan Kutchin: "A
novice could fortuitously kill one now and then, but he would
not be a real moose hunter. . . . There is a great difference between being able to kill an animal and being able to hunt it, one
involving a large measure of luck and the other being principally based on skill and knowledge." The chance killing of a
deer in no way alters the general probability of success in
actively hunting one; and, indeed, a significant long-term value
of the taboo may well lie in dampening the seductive power of
such statistically anomalous events.
One other aspect of the attraction to former habitation sites of terrestrial herbivores such as deer and tapir should be noted here. Abandoned swiddens have a tendency to exist in areas of somewhat tenuous security, places concurrently exploited by enemies. Site abandonment frequently results from a mortal exchange between settlements and creates in its wake an uninhabited buffer zone where such game animals are prone to take refuge after the release of predation pressure . Large communities with communal hunting can reduce the dangers involved in pursuing large animals in or into such regions, but for a solitary hunter to follow an erratic or wounded deer or tapir for any great distance presents unacceptable risks.

An estimated one-quarter of the game killed by rural mestizo peasants is traded away .
Since the organization and demands of mestizo life, with its
dependence on rivers for trade and livelihood, do not permit the
mobility that is integral to backwoods Amerindian communities, a marginal level of protein consumption and the exchange
of dried meat for nonfood items necessitate the hunting of
large game mammals. The Achuarä, without exploiting such
animals, have a satisfactory diet which is embedded in a total
behavioral pattern involving sorcery and homicide, which
disperse population and adjust it to changes in the resource
base ; smaller vertebrates comprise the bulk of
their diet most of the time, supplemented by good supplies of
fish throughout the year. Among the mestizos, adhering over
long stretches of time to the same watercourses, game is often
so seriously depleted that few families regard meat as a dietary
staple . Fishing productivity, at the same time, is likely to be
seasonal.
According to Pierret and Dourojeanni , such species as birds, monkeys, rodents (excluding capybara), and turtles represent about 51% of total fresh weight harvested by the mestizo population they surveyed; peccaries constitute an additional 20%. Deer, tapir, and capybara embody an important 30%—an indication of how impractical it would be for them not to hunt these particular animals.
Yet, a comparison with the Achuarä reveals an important point: neither their respective needs nor the presence or absence of taboos seriously affect the availability of the diverse faunal species; 87% of all the animals killed in the mestizo sample are the same small and moderate-sized vertebrates constituting virtually the identical percentage in Achuarä hunting production . The larger animals, which the Achuarä do not hunt or eat, represent only 5% of the total number of individual animals killed by mestizo hunters; sloths are not brought in at all .
An important part of the adaptive process of hunters is the coevolution of hunting technology and general procurement
strategies. In this regard, the displacement of the traditional blowgun by the 16-gauge shotgun has had an impact on Achuarä productivity which is germane to the present discussion. Today, comparatively little serious hunting is carried out among the Achuarä by means of the palm-wood blowgun. One factor in its decline in use is the scarcity and high cost of what is said to be the most potent dart-poison, that made in the Cocama region of the lower Huallaga River and sold commercially on an irregular basis. Another is the difficulty of adherence to the blowgun in a context of endemic revenge killing, in which, once firearms had been introduced in the late 19th century, technological parity with enemies was of critical importance.20

The difficulties presented by shotguns insofar as deer, tapir,
or capybara are concerned, in addition to other obstacles posed
by such species, become more significant when one considers
that the utilization of firearms created dependence on outside
traders as a source of ammunition and altered the entire
nature of the risk and economic investment which hunting
entailed. Blowguns, darts, and poison are made from palm-wood,
cafia brava, and strychnine-containing plants, respectively; the
fiber that is wrapped around one end of the dart to make it fit
the bore of the blowgun derives from the kapok tree, while the
dart container is manufactured from a section of bamboo. All
of these materials, like those utilized to manufacture arrows or
bows among other Amerindian groups, are found in the forest.
The adoption of guns, however, created a perpetual need for
shells or powder, in spite of their high prices (now approximately
23 cents per she will).
In view of the fact that the shotgun has little utility against
the large, solitary land mammals, particularly where hunting
is not a cooperative enterprise, ammunition has represented a
new cost which has further shaped a strategy of harvesting less
problematic species. Though little examined, the difficulty of
obtaining ammunition is common wherever Amerindians have
acquired guns and, thus, affects hunting behavior in many
instances. In the 1940s, for example, Wagley and Galvao
 observed that the Tenetehara were using Brazilianmade muzzle-loaders; as elsewhere in Amazonia, such trade
guns were "flimsy affairs made from inferior metal [and] a
hunter must be careful just how much gunpowder and shot he
uses or he may burst the barrel." Among the Kalapalo of the
Upper Xing(you, where guns are beginning to be used, the owners
"must depend upon the generosity of Post officials and casual
visitors to the -[Xingui] Park for ammunition, and thus frequently find themselves unable to use their firearms" . Denevan  observes that, among the Campa,
"while many . . . men have shotguns, shells are less available,
and most hunting is done with bow and arrow."
Most noteworthy is Goldman's  statement about

A hunter is not disposed to take risks with his small store of shot,

more likely to be harvested successfully. Thus, along the canals

The Achuara, however, cannot fall back on bow and arrow
to conserve ammunition and reduce risk in hunting deer and
tapir; the watercourses they exploit for fish do not approach the
productivity (albeit seasonal) of the Ucayali or the streams and
rivers of the Cubeo region, which permit seasonal deployment
of guns or the reduction of hunting to a relatively minor role.
Hence, the effect of shotguns among the Achuara was most apt
to be reflected in a perennial selectivity in regard to animal
targets.
Because fishing under aboriginal conditions is generally a more
stable and productive source of protein than hunting, it appears
that the latter is apt to decline in direct proportion to the
productive potential of the former. This is especially true where
the adoption of shotguns has added to the costs and risks of
hunting; but, in any case, species such as deer, tapir, capybara,
and sloth are certainly likely to be among the first to drop out
of the hunter's repertoire, particularly as fishing or some other
compelling factor increases the degree of sedentarization.
Among the Munduruc'you, where there has been a population
movement from a traditional savanna habitat to the Curur(you,
an alluvial river , "residence by
a large stream rich in fish has resulted in a changeover to fish as
the basic protein resource" (p. 189). Where the aquatic fauna
is especially abundant, as in the Upper Xing(you Basin, an area
"characterized by numerous swamps, oxbow lakes, and large
expanses of relatively low rain forest which are flooded during
the rainy season" , we find that "things that
live in the water" (kaya) are highly prized as food, while "land
creatures"-not simply tapir or deer, but rodents, armadillos,
and many birds-are considered inedible. The Kalapalo, for
example, "regard virtually all land animals ... as disgusting
and refuse to eat them" . Coatimundi, some
birds, and monkeys are the rare exceptions, but even then "an
expedition undertaken specifically for hunting game is rare"
and what game is harvested is generally acquired in the course
of fishing .
Basso asserts (p. 16) that it is "because of the value placed
on kaija [that] fish alone account for nearly the total amount of
protein consumed by the Kalapalo," but I would argue that
the process has been the opposite: that it is the greater efficiency
and security of fishing and turtle collection relative to hunting
which has led to the respective values conferred by Xingiu
groups upon these two productive spheres. The virtually unique
productive situation of the Upper Xingiui thus provides a
cogent explanation of the extreme position of these societies on
a continuum of degrees of food avoidance, to the point of nearly
total exclusion of land species .

only to place such taboos "within the proper context of the

today represent the extreme case, in which fishing has such

Among such backwoods groups as the Achuara, fishing is

(This is not that low, of course; the Waika subgroup has been charfood taboos as part of the total adaptational pattern of the
Amerindian populations of this region. It is no longer plausible
to maintain that differences in dietary behavior are simply the

As observed earlier, biotic interactions constitute the preeminent limiting factor in Amazonia. For most Amerindian
populations, this is primarily an issue of protein procurement,
but the acquisition of high-quality protein involves multiple
relationships: that of fishing to hunting, large animals to small,
warfare to settlement pattern, etc. In the broadest sense, the
explanation of the presence or absence of taboos lies in the
faunal productivity of the habitat of a given population. This
productivity is not absolute, however, but is defined by a set
of specific conditions: available environmental resources,
population distribution, technology, biobehavioral characteristics of animal species exploited, etc. While it may depend, in an
absolute sense, upon total existing biomass, it is a function of
the conditions, natural and cultural, which govern the accessible
proportion of biomass in a given habitat.
Accessibility is not a static condition, as I have shown,
particularly where the larger animals are concerned. If fishing,
as it assumes a more significant role in the diet, encourages
greater sedentarization , the hunting of such
species as tapir or deer is bound to decline; the less important
fishing is, the more dependent a community is apt to be upon
hunting large animals and the greater the likelihood of its
moving with such game. Thus, of all the variables considered,
the productivity of fishing must be viewed as probably the
major determinant of the scope of hunting.
The influence of fishing, by increasing sedentarization, profoundly emphasizes the distinctions between smaller and larger
animals; it limits accessibility of the latter and determines the
progressive exclusion of terrestrial fauna in an order which
approximates their vulnerability to local predation pressure.

A number of other factors have been seen to have bearing on hunting behavior, but they largely reinforce already established tendencies. Shotguns, for example, influence a number of aspects of hunting strategy where hunting is already problematic; these consequences are diagrammed in figure 1. Conversely, it has been shown that a traditional weapon such as the bow and arrow provides the kind of flexibility and power requisite for a highly generalized hunting pattern. Such a pattern is nevertheless not primarily a function of weaponry. If increased sedentarization based upon a greater reliance on fishing downgrades the role of hunting, as more and more species are locally exhausted the advantages conferred by the bow and arrow will become progressively less important.
There remains little doubt that dietary selectivity is a function of infrastructural characteristics, but the extent to which
views as to the acceptability of various animals are formally
stated is not readily susceptible to formulaic presentation. If,
as it appears, dietary breadth is inversely related to fishing
productivity and, hence, to the degree of sedentarization,
it is likely to be the case that the extent of formalization
of attitudes toward animals not part of the diet will vary in
direct relation to the level of sedentarization. A community
such as the Kalapalo, inhabiting a single locality virtually in
perpetuity, can probably be expected to disqualify nearly all
of the terrestrial fauna in emphatic terms, with, perhaps,
notable metaphysical support. With relatively less commitment
to sedentary village life, however, progressively more flexibility
in conceptualization of the comestibility of animals may be
required in accord with more flexible procurement strategies
(see fig. 2). A nonriverine community which is relatively mobile
or large will generally not express ideological distaste for large
animals; but the species which the Achuarä proscribe may, for
the very same characteristics, be objects of mythic or magical
attention.
While the proportional contribution of the macrofauna typically declines from initial settlement, it just as characteristically rises again with relocation. Indeed, the combination of the emic appeal and etic decline of the major game animals constitutes a forceful mechanism to trigger resettlement. In contrast, long-term occupation of a region and a relatively high population density probably tend to select for more stable settlement. They may also lead to smaller communities, since these can more effectively rely on smaller, more stable species and, therefore, fit more people into a given area. This is particularly important where, as among the Jivaroan groups, the large species may have suffered overpredation in the past and are most prevalent today in high-risk "reserve" areas  which have been vacated in the wake of feuding/warfare and left uninhabited because of the sustained sorcery fears and periodic silent ambushes which maintain the pattern of small, dispersed communities.
For such communities, the likelihood of successfully hunting a deer or tapir, for example, is quite low, but the active hunting of such species would threaten such a settlement and subsistence pattern anyway. In fact, the "inedibility" of such species both expresses and preserves the Achuarä mode of production—which, to a large extent, manages to come to terms with the long-term nature of human/faunal relations in the Amazonian biome. In this context, emic inedibility also represents an accurate probabilistic statement of etic hunting realities.

This paper by Ross is the latest entry in the widening controversy over the consequences of hunting in the Amazon Basin.
Ross begins by considering conflicting types of explanations of
food taboos and concludes that "mentalist" explanations are
unsatisfactory. I quite agree. To account for a taboo by calling
it "a reflection of the cognitive map of a society" is ignorance

In interpreting Amazonian culture, I align myself squarely
with the "materialist" school. Ecological explanations have always struck me as more compelling than any others. However,
favoring ecological interpretations in general does not commit
me to accept any one of them in particular. Ecological explanations can be false as well as true, and I think Ross's
explanation of why the Achuara prohibit the eating of large

Finally, let me attempt to generalize my views on some wider ecological issues.
Attitudes are not self-generating, but derivative. They arise as responses to events in the external world. Human behavior changes, and then rationalizations are invented to justify and "explain" the changes. In this way, for example, practical reasons for not eating tapir come to be overlaid and reinforced with taboos.
Since a taboo is often a cognitive reflection of a behavioral necessity, it may at first be useful to a society. Once in existence, however, it develops an inertia of its own, and may end up helping to perpetuate a form of behavior that is no longer adaptive.
I do not believe, though, that every taboo starts out as the
cognitive counterpart of adaptive behavior. Indeed, we must
guard against the tendency to push ecological explanations too
far. We should beware of the Pangloss in our midst who would
claim that every belief and practice, no matter how obscure
or perverse, is at bottom an adaptive response to ecological
conditions. Amazonian societies, for example, survive by a wide
enough margin to be able to tolerate certain maladaptive practices without imperiling their existence. (The common Indian
practice of plunging into a river to relieve a high fever and then
contracting pneumonia is an example.) Thus there is always a
residue of culture that may never be explainable ecologically.
The size of this residue may be debated, but its existence cannot be denied. As cultural ecologists we should content ourselves with a preponderance of triumphs and not demand total
subjugation.

Unlike North Americans , particularly the bisonhunters of the Plains, South American Indians barely utilized
the techniques of communal hunting. In his stimulating paper,
Ross makes it abundantly clear why this was so in Amazonia.
Only peccaries were suitable prey; ferocious, they were dangerous and difficult to hunt. Other large game animals, such as
deer and tapirs, tended to be nocturnal and solitary; one gets
the impression that they were also marginal, on the verge of
extinction.
Scarcity of game, as Ross evidently sees it, is a central concern. Certain species are said once to have lived abundantly
in much of Amazonia; they were thinned out, particularly near
human settlements. As they lost economic importance among
some groups, taboos developed about them. So far, so good.
But if these taboos were at all effective, why did the animals
not regain their numbers to the point where they were again
worth hunting, particularly by groups living along rivers in
settings especially favorable to animal life? Were they killed
by predators or foreigners? Does fishing preclude hunting? I
am even more puzzled when Ross apparently contradicts himself at one point in suggesting that increasing game scarcity
led to abandoning of taboos on the hunting and eating of deer
and tapir. Perhaps I have misunderstood him. In any case, one
is left to wonder what might have happened if some species
actually had become extinct. Very little, I would imagine, if
taboos were still respected. Would they have disappeared without replacement by or expansion of other species that may have
been better adapted to the environment? Biologically improbable as that may be, perhaps the Amazonians thought it possible. If they did, I wonder if they were really cognizant of
the concept of game conservation with which Ross seems to
credit them.

If Ross set out to prove that dietary restrictions in Amazonia are the product of the sum of productive forces, then he has at least presented a challenging argument. As he notes, "simplistic" explanations will not suffice, but he has introduced "extrinsic" factors which, to my mind, are not well integrated into the scheme. His explanation may go a long way toward accounting for the rise and persistence of a hunting taboo, but why a restriction of hunting should extend to a restriction on eating animals yielding high-quality protein would appear to be more a psychological than an economic question.
If taboos are essentially practical, why did the Amazonians fail to taboo the shotgun? Its blasts may have impressed the ladies and kids; otherwise it comes out of the discussion as dangerous, unreliable, limiting, expensive, ineffectual, and inefficient, far less adaptive to local conditions than the native blowgun and bow and arrow.

Ross argues that indigenous populations such as the Yanomami which live in the interfluvial regions of the Amazon and emphasize large-game hunting do so because exclusive hunting of small game would give insufficient yields. He adds that large-game hunting requires the exploitation of larger territories and that under these conditions "sorcery and varieties of armed conflict or agonistic display" act as a spacing mechanism to enable access to these areas. I would like to focus my comments on his references to the Yanomami, since I have recently completed a two-year field study of hunting and protein consumption among these people.
While no Yanomami settlement exclusively harvests small game, neither do these groups hunt with a "focus," "emphasis," or "specialization" on large game. Meat from large animals is not the singular predominant source in Yanomami protein consumption. The communal multi-day hunts do not occur with great frequency. Although these hunts may last up to five days, total distance travelled generally does not exceed a five-hour walk from the village, and they are rarely located anywhere near areas hunted by friendly or enemy villages.

Ross's discussion provides a much more realistic knowledge of
protein availability in Amazonia, and its consequences for human adaptation, than we had before. It certainly raises the
level of discussion of Amazon cultural ecology. It also raises
some old problems, placing them in new perspective. Three
seem especially worth noting.
First, what is the actual degree of protein scarcity in Amazonia? Ross naturally concentrates on protein as a limiting
resource influencing population density and settlement size,
but many different resources limit human population under
traditional technoeconomic conditions: wild foods of all kinds,
construction materials for houses and other indispensable manufactures, firewood with suitable burning properties, and even
high-quality soils (despite low population density) are all
quickly exhausted by human settlements. Population scattering
in Amazonia is not due mainly to protein shortage, any more
than it is due mainly to land shortage-the larger problem remains one of the total human use of the environment.
The second point is related: many Amazon groups appear
to have per capita protein supplies far in excess of the bare
subsistence minimum. The Sharanahua  and
other Panoan groups in the Montafna, for example, appear to
experience "meat hunger" at levels many times greater than
the standards set by nutritionists as "adequate." Other Montafia groups, like the Machiguenga , eat very
small amounts of meat and even claim that "too much" meat
(i.e., more than 100-200 g) makes them sick. We are not, therefore, dealing with a single "protein need," but with varying
population-specific expectations regarding "proper diet."
Third, the evidence is not in yet concerning the degree to
which food taboos are obeyed in practice. I have seen Machiguenga eating every tabooed meat except for a core of "inedible" meats considered poisonous or disgusting (e.g., snake,
vulture)-even jaguar meat was eaten by a few. And Wagley
 reports from the Tapirape that meat taboos tend
to apply selectively in a community, so that hunters hunt game
freely knowing that, even if they or their immediate families
cannot eat the meat owing to a taboo, it may still be given
away to others who are permitted to eat it. To the extent that
people do not obey their own food taboos, then, the function
Ross attributes to taboos will not be fulfilled. This issue i amplies the need for more research maintaining a clear distinction
between norms (taboo) and behavior (eating).
These comments do not detract from Ross's argument, since
he has effectively structured it in a more specific direction; they
are, nonetheless, part of the larger picture.
I applaud Ross's message to antiecological structuralists:
food taboos, being part of belief systems, are surely amenable
to structural analysis-but why this insistence that they are
independent of ecological constraints? Nothing compels us to
make such an assumption, and Ross gives convincing evidence
that Amerindian meat taboos are subject to the test of daily
experience in the environment and are quite likely adjusted to
both ecological and structural necessities. Vol. 19 * No. 1 * March 1978 21

This article consists of three items: a short treatise on the ecology of Amazonian animals; a study of the interrelationships between the environment and such cultural variables as hunting strategy, settlement size, degree of mobility, and intensity of feuding; and a discussion of food taboos. The first two constitute a piece of good writing on human ecology; the third is, in my opinion, less informative. It gives us some data about the dietary habits of a dozen Amazonian peoples and some general remarks on the inadequacy of structuralism in studying these habits and similar phenomena. In its present form, this criticism greatly misses the point.
I agree that some structuralists see cultures as nearly closed systems, but this emphasis on systemic explanation is, in a way, a counterreaction to atomism, diffusionism, and the tendency to explain cultural idiosyncrasies in terms of environmental influences. Against this background, the structuralists have established the view of culture as a system. This view in itself is not wrong; we can only say that the system is not complete—it cannot explain some important things unless a dimension is added. This addition will perhaps be made by ecological anthropology. It would be wiser, however, to introduce ecological anthropology, not as the antithesis of previous work, but as a synthesis of old and new, the latest step in the evolution of the Western idea of man from the unidimensionally individualist Robinson Crusoe to the society- and nature-bound being in his multidimensional space.
Culture, like a living organism, is a self-reproducing system, and environmental factors have little direct influence on it. Their role is selective: the environment decides whether a culture is viable or not. Certainly, a culture that destroys its material basis, like a parasite that kills its host, is doomed. Therefore every culture (even our own) must have some mechanisms restricting its possible overeffectiveness. Such mechanisms, as well as other cultural traits, however, are not directly created by the environment. To state the contrary would be bad teleology. The source of variations and modifications of culture is the cultural system itself.
In studying such phenomena as dietary regulations, we should try to maintain a distinction between random variations, the raw material for selection, and culture traits that have already proved their adaptive value. The time lag between the emergence of a culture trait and its acceptance or rejection through selection may be rather long, as in contemporary cultures. It is (was) certainly shorter in Amazonia, but the author's data offer us little possibility of distinguishing between adaptive and preadaptive phenomena. Perhaps we need a statistical analysis of a body of data on animals hunted, killed, and eaten to correlate with certain characteristics of the environment.
The well-known circumpolar bear cult shows that, for primitive man, animals were not always primarily a source of protein. Here we find a compromise between two attitudes towards a big animal that was both to be revered as a mysterious quasihuman being and killed as a lot of good meat and fat. It seems to me that animals, especially the big ones, such as apes, bears, and tapirs, were always an intellectual and ethical problem for man. If this is so, it is not at all surprising that there were restrictions, taboos, awe, and hesitation connected with using them as food. This is one of the recurrent themes in Lévi-Strauss's writings. We should remember that man is a creature whose world is and has always been paradoxical, and he has to spend much of his adaptive energy attempting to create himself a(n) (eco)logical niche in a world full of irreconcilable contradictions.

My comments on this paper will have to be limited to selected aspects of the theoretical contribution that it hopes to make by juxtaposing mentalist and materialist explanations and to Ross's "vote" in favor of material (read ecological) strategic resources for explaining food taboos. I call it a "vote" because the pursuit of this theoretical issue (namely, the superior explanatory power of one stance over the other) is unsystematic and unconvincing. Probably this was too heavy a burden for the paper to carry, for without it the exercise remains successful as a straightforward study of certain "human ecological adjustments" in obtaining food.
As the argument is presented, however, there are several
problems. For example, the essay does not tell us how, and to
what extent, the numbers and distribution of animals in a
locality can prescribe and perpetuate food taboos in such a way
as unequivocally to exclude the priority of cognitive categories
and processes. If "hunting selectivity" (is it a single variable
or a group of unspecified ones?) leads to "formalization of
proscription" (which is also shown to have a feedback connection with selectivity) and "generalized hunting" to "ideological
flexibility" (under the same arrangement as before), we are
asked to accept too much too uncritically as a "causal" proposition. How does hunting selectivity actually work to produce
or alter food taboos in the region under discussion? (The people
studied and their explanations of what they see [or do not see]
and do [or do not do] are mostly hidden from the reader, except
when convenient to the author's purposes.) How does the
proposition work when hunting is replaced by agriculture or
other competing modes of food acquisition in other parts of
the world? Alternatively, what is the translatability of the
proposition to other world regions?
It is because of such unanswered questions that Ross's exercise must remain largely "verificatory" in its theoretical accomplishments. Its "ecological" explanation for supposedly
"all" food taboos remains approximate and indeterminate. The
problem is aggravated by the facts that there is no precise,
manageable enunciation of the paper's analytical aims and
that the categories most crucial to its argument are not rigorously defined or discussed. For example, Ross initially defines
"taboo" as "avoidance" à la Radcliffe-Brown (n. 2) and then
never takes up the matter again. One wonders about possible
connections between availability and adaptability, adaptability
and avoidance, and availability and avoidance (to mention just
the obvious ones). How is the socially conceived "avoidance"
translated into an "ecological process" or a "selection pressure"? How socially deep or statistically pervasive does a social
avoidance have to be in order to become an ecosystemic pressure? Does the ecosystem offer degrees of freedom of action in
such avoidances? Do the people under study most often recognize ecological selection pressures on foods as food taboos, or
are other modes of expression available to them? In fact, aspects
of these questions could be examined through the data already
presented.
My own work  on foods in India, though illustrating the other approach (namely, that of cultural ideology and social practices), implies different (but related) issues. How does one explain the case in which people do not submit to the "selection pressures" (or the logic) of the ecosystem? Obviously, if people resist the pressures in a certain instance and get away with it generation after generation, we may conclude that the ecosystem provides leeway for human manipulation and alteration. There is, then, sufficient opportunity for the human mind to produce diet selections based on complex considerations of symbols and ideologies.
For example, a study of the consumption of "coarse" versus "fine" grains in India suggests that people do not always eat what they grow (or gather) in their surroundings. A group may

conform to the behavior patterns of other predators. There are
two things, however, which trouble me about Ross's article.
First, I think that Ross does not give Levi-Strauss due credit
for his contributions to the understanding of man. Second, I am
uneasy with any explanation of human behavior which leaves
out what Ortega y Gasset  calls the "commentary." It
will discuss each of these in turn, but first I will point to what
I consider to be the importance of Ross's article for understanding human behavior.

If what Ross is doing is important, it is still incomplete, for

Leach  has identified Levi-Strauss's main concern as

Ross's paper raises several important issues that have been actively debated by anthropologists in recent years. One of these is the utility of the materialist approach vis-à-vis the mentalist or structuralist approaches in the study of tropical-forest peoples. Another is the question of protein availability in the neotropics and its implications for cultural development. A third is the question of the degree to which a materialist theory, or any theory, can predict actual cultural patterns.
Ross's assertion that a materialist approach can increase our
understanding of the cultures of Neotropica has merit. Unfortunately, few studies conducted in this part of the world have
been based on a rigorous quantitative methodology, and descriptions of game availability, hunting activities, and diet are
frequently impressionistic and reflect the particular bias of the
observer. Ross's own call for empiricism is muted by the necessity
of employing comparative data which are often based on casual
observations. Some of the behavioral characteristics attributed
to the tapir and white-lipped peccary make them seem so
scarce, fierce, or difficult to kill that one might wonder why
any of the indigenous groups would bother to hunt them at all.
Contrary to Ross's statement, a census of tapir has been made
 and indicates a density of
0.53 animals/km² (on Barro Colorado Island, Panama) rather
than the 0.006 animals/km² he proposes. My own observations
and records of hunting yields made during 18 months of fieldwork among the Siona-Secoya Indians of eastern Ecuador indicate that individual hunters possess sufficient expertise to kill
tapir and white-lipped peccaries without undue difficulty . This point is raised not
merely to provide a negative example, but to underline the need
for a better empirical foundation upon which to build a theory
of tropical-forest culture.
Ross treats the decision to hunt large versus small game as if
these options were mutually exclusive and implies that once a
choice has been made certain cultural effects follow automatically. A corollary of this is his view that when men go hunting
they seek a specific species. The latter is true in certain circumstances, but my observations convince me that most Sionanation is made as to the species sought. Records of Siona-Secoya
hunting yields indicate a high incidence of kills composed of
mixed species of all sizes, including both "nocturnal" and "diurnal" types. (Animals such as the tapir, white-lipped peccary,
and armadillo, although presumed to be nocturnal, are often
killed during the day.) Consequently, the inability of a hunter
to locate a tapir or other large game on a specific day does not
necessarily dictate a hunting failure; hence the "cost" of hunting a particular species like the tapir is not so clear-cut and
exorbitant as Ross would have it.
With regard to the protein question, Ross's own data show an adequate or better than necessary intake for the Achuarä Jívaro, despite their reliance on the smaller game species. The "average" figure of 107.7 g/day of total protein intake cited by Ross is consistent with the 80.7 g/day mean for all age groups and 112.6 g/day for adult males indicated by dietary surveys among the Siona-Secoya. Although the question of protein availability has other significant parameters, the current tendency to interpret tropical-forest culture in terms of protein deficiency alone appears to be based on inadequate primary data and undoubtedly ignores a whole series of important human-ecological interactions.
In the end, Ross's model remains conjectural because it fails
to predict actual patterns of food avoidance, including even
those of the Achuarä Jívaro. Instead, historical particularisms and "flexible aspects" are invoked to explain phenomena that resist "formulaic presentation."
by Wilma E. Wetterstrom
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, Mass. 02139, YOU.S.A. 15 VIII 77
Ross has made a valuable contribution by demonstrating that there are some extremely interesting relationships among diet, animal behavior, hunting patterns, and social organization. His data on animal ecology and hunting she would far more light on food taboos than most of the explanations which have been offered before.
However, I would caution against assigning all first causes to ecological and biological factors. In particular, I question Ross's discussion of protein. He seems to imply that a biological need for protein is the driving force behind hunting patterns. If I understand him correctly, it is the need for "marginal protein intake" that sets a lower limit on the amount of meat which people consider sufficient. Thus a village relocates when the men can no longer find enough game in their territory to satisfy the community's total protein requirement.
While it is indisputable that protein is an essential nutrient
and that nearly all people eat some animal protein, I would
suggest that these needs are culturally defined and that the
cultural requirements are more important than the biological
ones. It would be extremely difficult for a community accurately to assess each individual's protein needs and calculate
a collective requirement. Nutritionists and physiologists still
cannot do this in spite of sophisticated equipment and many
years of research . If people do understand
biological needs, it is not apparent from their behavior. In
many societies young children and pregnant and lactating women receive the smallest portions of meat, although they need
high-quality protein more than any other group. Adult males,
on the other hand, often eat the largest shares of meat, although
their need for protein is relatively small and could be satisfied
with plant proteins .
Even if every individual's needs were determined, it is not clear what would be an adequate quantity of protein for the whole community. One definition of "adequate" might be enough protein to assure that everyone is reasonably healthy. However, that would be more than adequate, since a community could survive even if not everyone were healthy. For example, if the children were undernourished, the community could still perpetuate itself as long as some children survived and eventually bore their own children. Such a strategy might even be adaptive, since undernourished children would grow up to be small adults with relatively modest food needs .
Clearly, communities cannot and do not make decisions about hunting on the basis of biological needs. Cultural factors set the boundaries and define when people are to be satisfied or discontented. For example, a "meat-and-potatoes" North American is not satisfied with his dinner unless he has had steak, roast, or pork chops, although a vegetarian casserole would provide for his biological needs.
Although Ross has presented a very stimulating discussion of taboos, I would like to have seen this analysis extended to the entire dietary pattern. I would have been especially interested to learn how ecological factors influence the foods which Amazon communities do eat. For example, does the definition of a meal vary among societies according to the abundance and availability of game? Is fish considered essential for a meal among the groups that fish and have little access to game? Are the prestige foods those animals which are most difficult to bunt?

Beckerman seems to me entirely too optimistic in his reference

which could support vastly larger human populations than
fish and game." This is a hard claim to substantiate, especially
for the precontact period. The harvesting of wild plants for
protein has major drawbacks; above all, it is energetically more
expensive for a human to gather the diversity of plant foods
needed than to eat "an animal that is close to his biochemical
needs after the animal has gone about achieving the necessary
diversity through its food chains" . Beckerman
notes that the Yanomamo "are mighty collectors of palm
fruits and Brazil nuts," but Chagnon  makes a more
telling point:
Despite the amazing variety of food products to be found in the jungle
. . . the Yanomamo could not exist for any substantial period of time
on collected foods only.... The tropical forest is much less productive and reliable than one would imagine. The most abundant foods,
palm nuts, are seasonal-a group of people could only rely on them
for a few months of the year, during which time some 80 group members would have to forage over a wide area to obtain sufficient palm
fruits to keep everybody well fed.
While it is true, as Beckerman writes, that various legumes
(peanuts, beans) are found in Amerindian garden repertoires,
he is somewhat oversanguine about the prospects for their
elaboration into major sources of dietary protein. Production in
tropical soils of seed crops is expensive in terms of requisite
work energy, except in limited alluvial areas (or where there is
volcanic soil), and rapidly induces soil exhaustion . Furthermore, all of the available cultigens have
deficiencies in one or another critical amino acid: peanuts, for
example, lack methionine and lysine; manioc leaves also lack
methionine; corn is deficient in tryptophane and lysine
. In depending upon a
wide variety of plant foods to complement one another, there
is still the problem of a potential imbalance in the proportions
of essential amino acids, which would reduce their utility
.  In the case of peanuts, there is also
the problem that, in a humid and warm environment, storage
could lead to mold and contamination by aflatoxin, a liver
poison .

strategy. Basso, for example, urges the materialist to seek a

philosophy and a probabilistic theory of knowledge . On the other hand, the present paper is analytic
in method and introduces into the discussion of the problem
it addresses two critical and neglected features: the logicalempirical dimension which permits the articulation of the study
of contemporary Amerindian food preferences with analogous
material relations elsewhere in time and space (cf. comments

to verification. Neither is present in previous discussion of
Amazon food taboos; and, while they necessarily invite the
kinds of criticism encountered here, no others have the same
potential for systematic demystification.
Title: Food Taboos, Diet, and Hunting Strategy: The Adaptation to Animals in Amazon Cultural Ecology
Author: Eric Barry Ross
Sentiment_Summary:
['The text employs a behavioral and ecological lens that views Amazonian cultural practices as adaptations to their environment, with a focus on the practical and strategic aspects of food taboos and hunting strategies.'
 'The text employs a critical anthropological lens that challenges metaphysical and symbolist perspectives on dietary taboos, instead emphasizing the importance of ecological and environmental factors in shaping food procurement strategies among indigenous groups.'
 'The text employs a sociobiological and ecological lens that reduces social and cultural practices to environmental adaptations and availability of resources.'
 'The text employs a sociobiological and ecological lens that reduces the discussion of Indigenous people to their environmental adaptations and resource management, without explicit evaluative or socio-political framing.'
 'The text employs a descriptive and observational lens, focusing on the cultural and environmental practices of the Achuarä people without explicit evaluative or socio-political framing.'
 'The text employs a sociobiological and ecological lens that analyzes the hunting strategies of Indigenous populations, particularly the Achuarä and Jibaros, in relation to their environment and resource management.'
 'The text employs a sociobiological and anthropological lens that describes the hunting patterns and cultural adaptations of various Indigenous groups in the Amazonia region.'
 'The text employs a sociobiological lens that reduces social conflict to environmental pressures and resource competition, with a focus on the hunting strategies and settlement patterns of Indigenous groups.'
 'The text employs a sociobiological and anthropological lens that describes the cultural and subsistence practices of Indigenous groups, particularly the Yanoama and Mundurucú, with a focus on their hunting and warfare practices.'
 'The text employs an anthropological lens that describes the cultural and environmental dynamics of Indigenous groups, such as the Jivaroan, Mundurucú, and Sharanahua Indians, in a relatively neutral and descriptive manner.'
 'The text employs a descriptive and observational lens, focusing on the ecological and hunting practices of indigenous groups without explicit socio-political framing.'
 'The text employs a descriptive and observational lens, focusing on the ecological interactions between indigenous communities and their environment, without explicit evaluative or socio-political framing.'
 'The text employs an anthropological lens that recognizes the cultural complexity and ecological agency of Indigenous groups in the Amazon region.'
 'The text employs a comparative anthropological lens, examining the hunting practices and ecological adaptations of Indigenous groups, such as the Achuarä, in relation to non-Indigenous populations, like the mestizos, without overtly reductionist or objectifying language.'

All use subject to https://about.jstor.org/termsHUNTING EFFICIENCY IN ACCULTURATED AND UNACCULTURATED YANOMAMA VILLAGES
Department of Anthropology, University of Pittsburgh, Pittsburgh, PA 15260
This paper examines hunting efficiency in two groups of Yanomama villages in the Catrimani River region of northern Brazil. One group, which has relocated along the Northern Perimeter Highway, has experienced resource degradation, reflected in lowered efficiency of traditional, predominantly individual hunting techniques. However, in developing a new overall subsistence strategy, this group has modified traditional hunting techniques, resulting in higher hunting yield and increased overall hunting efficiency. Three forest villages are examined as controls.
HUNTING EFFICIENCY is in part the result of a choice between alternate economic strategies. This paper examines such a choice in a group of Yanomama villages of the Catrimani River region of northern Brazil. Using data from both acculturated and unacculturated Yanomama villages, we will test the proposition of Gross et al.  that:
native groups undergo circumscription and become sedentary as national frontiers encroach on their lands. This causes intensified use of local resources. A degraded habitat results in lower yields per unit area and per unit labor. Under these conditions, people turn to new techniques, tools, and activities to meet subsistence needs . . . With low yields people must work more to meet their food needs.
Our hypothesis is that two villages which relocated along the Northern Perimeter Highway (BR 210) have experienced resource degradation, reflected in lowered hunting efficiency and increased utilization of new techniques. Three traditional forest villages were examined as controls.
The Yanomama comprise one of the largest indigenous populations of the Amazon. Early research was carried out by Barker , Becher , Zerries  and de Barandiaran , but little was known about Yanomama culture until the work of Chagnon  in the mid-1960s and early 1970s. Shortly thereafter work on several aspects of Yanomama culture appeared , followed by studies of Yanomama agriculture, food production, protein consumption, and hunting efficiency . These investigations have focused on the Yanomama of southeastern Venezuela.
The present study concentrates on five of the nine Yanomama villages in the Catrimani River basin in the territory of Roraima, Brazil (Figure 1). The nine villages comprise some three hundred people; they are located between 1°40′ and 2°10′ North and between 62°20′ and 62°50′ West. Descended from groups living in the northwest Parima mountains, this population has occupied the Catrimani basin for at least one hundred years. Since the late 1920s they have had sporadic contacts with Westerners, including members of scientific expeditions, boundary commission teams, hunters, rubber collectors, and Brazil-nut gatherers. In October 1965 a Catholic mission was established in a Yanomama village in the middle Catrimani River. During the following years, villagers of the upper and lower Catrimani

visited the mission and bartered with missionaries. In January 1974 highway BR 210 reached the middle Catrimani River. Hundreds of lumber workers, settlers, and farmers entered Yanomama territory and occupied the first fifty kilometers of the highway. Since 1977 two Yanomama villages have built their houses on the borders of the highway and have had steady and relatively friendly contacts with these laborers and settlers.
Construction of the highway has had significant effects on the social organization and behavior of these "highway" villagers. Previously these groups had strong internal cohesion and weak or nonexistent ties with outsiders. The large influx of settlers (presently outnumbering the Yanomama) has resulted in a desire for, and indeed a dependence upon, goods, wages, and food from mainstream Brazilian society. The coincident weakening of social organization in these villages is detailed in Saffirio .
Yanomama hunting consists of two basic patterns: rama (carried out by individuals and very small groups of patrilineal kinsmen) and heni (collective hunting in preparation for a feast). In rama hunting typically an individual leaves in the morning for subsistence hunting; at least some men from a village will go out every day. Occasionally men will start out in late morning, kill a few birds nearby, and return to the village soon afterwards, but the more common pattern is to leave in the early morning and return in the late afternoon. Yanomama do not hunt at night.
A collective hunt (heni) is mounted by most of the men of a village, who are usually also kinsmen. They take along most of their possessions, since such hunts usually last a week or more and take place in relatively unexploited areas far from the village. At the end of the hunt, smoked game, vines, and fruits are carried back to the village in baskets.
Hunting is accomplished mainly with the bow and arrow. Each hunter's equipment consists of a bow, three or four arrows, and a quiver containing extra arrow points and tools to repair broken shafts and points. The Yanomama make their bows of young wood from peach (Guilielma gasipaes), açai (Euterpe precatoria), paxiuba (Socratea durissima), or bacaba palm (Oenocarpus bacaba). The bow for a young adult ranges from six to seven feet long, while the bow for a boy is three feet long. Bows are shaped with machetes and shaved with mandibulary teeth from a collared or white-lipped peccary. In cross section the bow is a semicircle about one and one-half inches thick, with the flat surface facing away from the hunter. The bowstring is a strongly twisted cord of curawa (Annas comargo paraguanense).
Yanomama arrows are about seven feet long. The arrow cane (Gynerium sagittatum) is dried in the sun and over a fire, after which split wing feathers from the curassow or bush turkey (family Cracidae) are attached. Feathers and arrowpoint are attached with a cord from the cultivated curawa, which is then coated with resin. Three types of arrowpoints are commonly used: poisoned, pencil-like palm-wood arrowpoints, eleven to twelve inches long, used for hunting monkeys and sloths; lanceolate bamboo arrowpoints, eight to twelve inches long, used for shooting terrestrial quadrupeds such as tapir, peccary, and deer; and bone-tipped, barbed arrowpoints, ten to twelve inches long, used for shooting birds and fish. In addition, points consisting of a knot of several small branches, five to six inches long, used to stun small birds without damaging their feathers, are made in the field, when needed.

 Yanomama hunters carry up to a dozen arrowpoints of the first three types
 inside a quiver. The quiver is constructed from a segment of bamboo (Guasdua
 latifolia), approximately two inches in diameter, naturally closed on the bottom and
 covered with a piece of animal skin on the top. It is suspended around the hunter's
 neck and rests on his shoulders.
 The Yanomama first received shotguns from Brazilian hunters and missionaries
 in the late 1960s. However, ammunition is difficult to obtain and only a few Yano-
 mama hunters own shotguns (two of the fifty-one hunters in this study). These
 Brazilian shotguns are 16 or 20 gauge, breech-loading, single-shot, and full-choke,
 often old, rusted, and worn. Cartridges, shot, and powder are obtained from other
 Yanomama living along the Tootobi and Mucajai Rivers. Cartridges are used as many
 times as possible. Shotguns are generally reserved for large game-mainly tapir, deer,
 and peccary.
 The Yanomama kill more than 90 percent of their game with bow and arrows.
 In addition to bows and shotguns, they also use axes, machetes, knives, makeshift
 clubs, and smoke. Some Yanomama use dogs when hunting, but few raise hunting
 dogs; they are employed as companions more than as hunting assets. The predomi-
 nant, traditional hunting pattern is that of one or a very few hunters, possibly
 accompanied by dogs, armed with bows and a variety of arrows, going out in the
 morning and returning the same day.
 Background research was carried out between 1968 and 1977 by Saffirio, who
 lived as a Catholic missionary on the middle Catrimani River. Research specific to
 this study was conducted during a five-month field investigation by Saffirio between
 October 1979 and February 1980. This period was chosen to coincide with the dry
 season, the best months for hunting, fishing, and gathering.
 Five of the nine Catrimani River Basin Yanomama villages were selected for this
 study. Two, Opiktheri 132 and Opiktheri 135, chose locations beside the highway in
 order to exploit the new possibilites. These villages, in close contact with Brazilians
 since 1974, are the most acculturated of the Catrimani Yanomama. In contrast,
 three villages that were farthest from the highway were selected as being the least
 acculturated. Uxiutheri and Iropitheri were temporarily living together during most
 of the fieldwork period, because members of the latter were planting a new garden
 near the mouth of the Lobo de Almada River. The hunting area being used by these
 groups had not been previously exploited by them. The other traditional village,
 Maxikopiutheri, was exploiting an area which had been hunted twenty years pre-
 viously.
 The first period of this research, 23 October to 12 December 1979, was spent
 in the traditional villages. One month was spent with Uxiutheri and Iropitheri, and
 three weeks with Maxikopiutheri. On 13 December, most of the Uxiutheri and
 Maxikopiutheri men left their villages for a feast with the Hewenahipitheri and were
 accompanied by the researcher. Such temporary absences from the village for pur-
 poses of visiting and feasting are common in traditional Yanomama villages. After
 a period of time observing other villages, forty-five days between 14 January and
 28 February 1980 was spent with the acculturated villagers. During most of this

time, thirty-four adults from Opiktheri 135 and four from Opiktheri 132 were working at the FUNAI (National Indian Foundation) farm. Nine others, eight from Opiktheri 132, were absent, working with settlers for two weeks; such absences occur fairly regularly throughout the year. Table 1 summarizes basic data concerning the sample villages.
Data from hunting interviews were collected with a tape recorder and transferred to index cards. Hunting instances were the units of analysis. For each instance, variables such as times of departure and return, type and weight of game killed, hunting zone exploited, types of weapons used, and disposition of game were recorded. Data were later transferred to punch cards and analyzed using SPSS .
Yanomama hunters exploit a wide variety of animals (Table 2). Hunters from
the highway villages, however, concentrate on fewer types of animals, including
mainly the white-lipped peccary, tortoise, long-haired spider monkey, and several
species of birds. Traditional villagers hunt a greater variety of game, with only the
macaw hunted in large numbers. Table 3 groups these game animals into broad
categories and indicates mean and total butchered or field-dressed weights of meat
obtained for each category. It is perhaps surprising that the more acculturated
Yanomama hunters from the highway villages obtained some 989 kilograms of meat,
compared with only 522 kilograms for the traditional Yanomama, living in what
we have assumed to be an ecologically richer area. Since the total populations are
similar, this would seem contradictory, but it should be realized that the traditional
villagers rely more on fishing and gathering than do the acculturated villagers. Traditional villagers obtained 124.05 kilograms of fish and 480.5 kilograms of gathered
food resources (fruits, honey, nuts, crustaceans, insects, etc.), compared with 87.75
kilograms of fish and 192.50 kilograms of gathered food resources for the acculturated villages. The degraded nature of the environment surrounding the acculturated
highway villages is further underlined by their relatively low total efficiency in fishing
and gathering as measured in kilograms of food obtained per hour of fishing or
gathering: 0.18 kg/hr. fishing and 1.27 kg/hr. gathering, compared with 0.37 kg/hr.
fishing and 2.60 kg/hr. gathering for the traditional villages. Thus increased reliance
on hunting by acculturated villagers is due in part to the relative lack of fishing and
gathering resources .
The most significant difference between the hunting patterns of the two groups lies in their techniques for utilizing manpower. Traditional villagers generally hunt alone, or occasionally in small groups. In only one instance did a traditional villager engage in communal hunting, when he joined in heni hunting with another village. Game is plentiful in the traditional village areas, and single hunters have a high success rate. Acculturated villagers are less likely to hunt alone, for game appears to be scarce and restless in the area of the highway, so that two or more hunters are better able to track animals. Acculturated villagers are also much more likely to engage in collective hunting. Collective heni hunting was traditionally undertaken in preparation for a feast to which guests from other villages were invited in order to

All use subject to https://about.jstor.org/terms



(Instances)
Traditional villagers use a wide variety of weapons, whereas more acculturated hunters use the bow and arrow exclusively . The only shotguns used belonged to two of the more traditional villagers. This would seem to contradict the proposal of Gross et al.  that in more acculturated contexts hunters will turn to increased utilization of new techniques due to resource degradation. However, as we shall argue below, the new techniques employed by more acculturated hunters involve different hunting strategies rather than different weapons. Lack of the shotguns in acculturated villages is due mainly to pressure from National Indian Foundation agents, who have prohibited the sale of guns and ammunition to Yanomama, and from missionaries, who discourage their use.
Acculturated village hunters went hunting almost three times as often as did hunters from the more traditional villages, averaging 8.8 instances per hunter compared with only 3.0 for traditional hunters, even though the latter were observed for a slightly longer period of time. Traditional villagers averaged only 1.7 hunting instances per day, whereas acculturated villagers averaged 4.3. Controlling for length of observation, number of hunters, and total average population for which food was being provided, there is still a dramatic difference between the two groups of villages in frequency of hunting (\chi^2 = 52.69, p < .001; Z = 7.25, p < .001).
Acculturated village hunters also averaged more time per hunt. Traditional village hunters spent about six hours and ten minutes for each hunting instance, compared with about seven hours and twenty-seven minutes per instance for acculturated village hunters. A two-tailed t-test for the difference between these means is significant at the .001 level. Because the acculturated villagers took part in many more instances of heni hunting, a comparison was made using only instances of rama hunting. Traditional hunters spent an average of six hours and ten minutes per hunting instance compared with seven hours and forty-two minutes per instance for acculturated hunters; this is also significant at the .001 level.
In order to examine the comparative hunting efficiency of the various techniques and groups, it was necessary to construct a standardized efficiency ratio. A common method for constructing such ratios is to divide the total kilograms of meat obtained by the total hours expended hunting. Calculating total efficiency ratios in this manner, it is obvious that the traditional villagers' overall ratio (.97 kg/hr.) is much higher than that of the acculturated villagers (.66 kg/hr.).

All use subject to https://about.jstor.org/terms

When hunt-by-hunt efficiencies are compared, interesting patterns emerge. For this analysis, we constructed a different measure of efficiency, defined as kilograms of meat obtained by each hunter per hour of hunting. This efficiency ratio was calculated for each hunting instance, and mean efficiency ratios for traditional and acculturated groups were compared. The mean ratios were virtually identical: 1.06 kg/hunter/hr. for the traditional hunters and 1.07 kg/hunter/hr. for the acculturated hunters. The apparent rise in the efficiency ratio of the acculturated hunters is due to the relatively large number of instances of highly efficient heni hunting.
When the rama and heni techniques were examined individually, significant differences were noticed. The acculturated villagers' mean efficiency in rama hunting was only 0.50, significantly lower (t = 2.54, p < .01, two-tailed) than that of the traditional villagers (1.06). In contrast, heni hunting by acculturated villagers is extremely efficient. The mean kg/hunter/hr. is 3.77, a significant difference (t = 8.89, p < .001) from rama hunting. Hames (personal communication) found similar differences in total efficiencies of rama and heni hunting by a different group of Yanomama: 0.50 and 0.82 kg/hr., respectively. Thus it appears that acculturated villagers' traditional rama hunting is relatively unproductive, due to their depleted environment, but their more productive subsistence heni hunts raise their overall hunting efficiency.
On a superficial level these data would seem to contradict the contention that as "native groups undergo circumscription and become sedentary," they will intensify local resource use, which will result in lower yield per unit labor and ultimately in new "techniques, tools and activities" . Despite permanent residence along the highway, acculturated villagers tend to use more traditional hunting implements and have a greater total yield of meat than forest-living villagers. However, we would argue that the acculturated villagers have already reacted to environmental depletion by modifying their hunting techniques to develop a new subsistence strategy which has produced a higher total hunting yield.
It is obvious that the highway villagers have depleted their local resources. Gathering yields are small, fishing is less productive, and the pattern of individual or small group hunting is less productive. Game is scarce in the vicinity of the highway villages. Thus these villagers are forced to intensify their utilization of the traditional but infrequently practiced collective hunting technique. They must go deep into the forest in order to find game; during these hunts they also gather wild food. This strategy has resulted in higher total yields from hunting, yet the overall effects of such a modified subsistence strategy must also be considered.
For the present, villagers are able to supplement normal rama hunting with increased heni hunting, at a cost of additional time and labor. Whether the environment is permanently capable of supporting such a strategy is arguable. If it is, the Yanomama will be able to continue their traditional subsistence pattern, supplemented by occasional cash labor for buying Western goods. If it is not, surrounding forest resources will be depleted to the point where even heni hunting efficiency will decrease below acceptable levels. Additional time spent hunting would mean decreased time

available for horticulture, and overall productivity would be inadequate for their needs. At that point the highway villagers would face a choice: either move on to a new location as they have done in the past, or adopt an entirely new economic strategy, such as working permanently as salaried laborers.
A complex set of factors makes predictions difficult. Sedentary residence patterns create more than just game depletion problems. For example, diseases caused
by intestinal parasites spread by the Yanomama practice of unrestricted defecation
in the village area are intensified by permanent residence, and have become a serious
health hazard for acculturated villagers. Changing social patterns are acting to
strengthen the nuclear family, decreasing the importance of village residence and
threatening the fabric of traditional Yanomama village life. In addition, the Brazilian
minister of the interior on 9 March 1982 signed a decree of interdiction of an area
of 29,700 square miles for the protection of the Yanomama. The southern border
of this region is highway BR 210; the traditional villages described in this study are
located in the protected area while the acculturated villages are just outside. FUNAI
agents have asked the residents of Opiktheri 132 and 135 to relocate north of the
highway borders, so that it is likely that the acculturated villagers will move to a
new location. If they combine this move with a shift either east or west, they can
take advantage of a less degraded environment and still live along the highway. However, as the southern highway border area opens up, the land available for hunting,
fishing, and gathering will decrease, and the inevitable problems of environmental
degradation will intensify.

ABSTRACT. The occurrence of numerous areas of black soil associated with
potsherds in Amazonia has stirred controversy on the origins of the soil type and
its significance in terms of precontact aboriginal population densities in the region.
The theories on the origin of black earth are reviewed and it is argued that it is
anthropogenic. An analysis of the physical and chemical properties of the soil
type, based on widespread sampling, supports the anthropogenic argument. The
abundance and depth of black earth sites indicates that Indian populations were
dense and in many cases sedentary before the arrival of Europeans, even in
interfluve areas.
Amazonia, with only I inhabitant per sq.
km, has long puzzled geographers, anthropologists, sociologists, and economists. Why
does such a huge area, roughly 7 million sq.
km, which contains one of the world's richest
biomes, contain so few people? According to
one viewpoint, the rain forest is not conducive
to the development of higher cultures.1 The
meager protein sources and the generally poor
soils of the uplands (terra firme), it is argued,
mitigate against sedentary settlement by aborigines. Even in floodplain areas, the pervading influence of the rain forest supposedly arrests cultural development.
Dr. Smith is Senior Researcher at Worldwatch Institute, 1776 Massachusetts Ave., N.W., Washington, DC
20036.
ing capacity of Amazonia is greater than has
been generally accepted. A mosaic of black
earth patches, scattered throughout the Amazon basin, provides evidence that precontact
native populations were in many cases large
and sedentary. Black earth, ceramic material,
and stone tools are the only vestiges of ancient
civilizations in Amazonia; the remainder, except for artificial mounds, has perished under
the hot and humid conditions. To examine the
case for the anthropogenic origin of black
earth, theories on its genesis are examined,
the physical and chemical properties of the
soil type are analyzed, and the site characteristics are described. Finally, the controversy
on the scarcity of subsistence resources in the
region is explored.
In the Brazilian Amazon, black earth with
potsherds is referred to as terra preta do indio
(Indian black earth) and is recognized as a soil
type.2 Terra preta is an anthrosol, characterized by a distinctive anthropogenic epipedon.3

rarely occur without the influence of man in

The dark color of terra preta is the most striking feature of the soil; it stands out among the predominant yellow and red soils of the basin. Unlike in temperate areas, peaty soils are very rare in Amazonia. The color ranges from jet black to dark gray-brown and is probably related to the time the site was occupied; the darker the soil, the longer the locality was inhabited. The stability of the dark color, even after prolonged exposure to the hot sun and torrential rains, is remarkable. The inky color is most likely due to a complex formation of organic matter and calcium ions that form a coating on the soil particles. 16 It is not clear,
though, why the dark color is so persistent. Organic compounds, such as phenols, are probably binding carbon to soil colloids.

Generally, upland Amazonian soils are poor
in nutrients because of prolonged leaching and
because the weathering front of the geological
substrate is too deep to provide nutrients for
plants. Fires would liberate nutrients into the
soil and the break down of kitchen middens
would also contribute potash, phosphorus,
calcium and nitrogen. Whereas in archeological sites in California, human urine and excrement are considered important sources of nutrients, they are probably only a minor source
in black earth sites of Amazonia.22 Adult Indians generally retire into the bush or forest
to eliminate metabolic wastes; small children
would contribute a few nutrients to the soil by
defecating and urinating promiscuously within
the village.

of the most significant elements indicating

soils. Whereas in oxisols and ultisols, which account for the majority of Amazonian soils, the
available phosphorus content is usually under

rapids, such as at Altamira on the Xingu (Fig. 2). Along the Rio Negro, black earth almost invariably occurs wherever a slab of granitic rock juts into the water. Such sites would provide convenient work benches for grinding foodstuffs and stone tools. The ready availability of potable water, then, is one of the most important characteristics of terra preta sites.
Many black earth sites are occupied by neo-Brazilian communities. The advantages of the sites persist. According to the Mapa da Bacia Amazonica published on a scale of 1:1,500,000, four communities in the Brazilian Amazon are called Terra Preta. Three are in Amazonas state along the Abacaxis, Canuma, and Aripuanã rivers, and one is in Pará state along the Tapajós River. In Manacapuru, an Amazon River town 80 km west of Manaus, a suburb called Terra Preta rests on one of the most extensive black earth sites so far discovered in Amazonia.
