In interfluve areas, the twelve sampled terra preta sites were all under 6 ha and averaged 1.4 ha (s = 1.5). Upland sites are roughly circular or lenticular in shape. Such sites were occupied by either a large central communal house (maloca), or several houses in one or more circles. The shape and area of black earth sites encountered in the Brazilian Amazon by other investigators are close to the range that I have found (Table 3). Although interfluvial black earth sites are generally small, in some areas they are numerous. Terra preta is estimated to cover some 50,000 ha

a Falesi, op. cit., footnote 25
between the Tapajós and Curuá-Una rivers alone.41
Most anthropogenic black earth sites are less than 1.5 m deep, though 2 m has been recorded (Table 4). The seventeen black earth sites sampled in riverine areas are an average 0.73 m (s=0.38) deep, whereas the twelve sites sampled in interfluvial areas averaged 0.36 m deep (s=0.25, Table 2). Both terra firme and floodplain environments, then, were inhabited by sedentary aboriginal groups in precontact times.
It is difficult to estimate how large a population inhabited each black earth locality. Native buildings were, and still are, made from wood and palm fronds that soon disintegrate. Another problem is that aboriginal cultures in the region use different village plans, ranging from a large communal house to a circle of dwellings. The large riverine terra preta sites may have been occupied for various periods by different groups, each time deepening and possibly extending the area of stained earth. But the entire area of even the largest sites may have been settled at one time; the 80 ha

Although upland black earth sites generally are smaller than riverine ones, they nevertheless could have been occupied by large groups. A native long-house measuring 34 by 24 meters can shelter at least 100 people; Witoto malocas that measured 100 by 50 meters sheltered a community of 400.43 Terra preta mounds on Marajó Island, once occupied by Indians of the Ananatuba phase, ranged from 0.03 to 0.07 ha and were the sites of communal houses each sheltering from 100 to 150 individuals.44 The interfluvial black earth sites I sampled range from 0.3 to 5 ha. They were once occupied by villages containing from 100 to 2,000 inhabitants.
The depth of black earth is undoubtedly correlated with the length of time the site was inhabited, but it is not known how fast ths oil was formed. In the case of Marajó Island, 1 m depth of terra preta has been assumed to represent approximately 100 years of occupation. 45 It seems unlikely that black earth would have formed that quickly considering the rapid rates of organic matter decomposition. A rate of 1 centimeter of terra preta per 10 years of settlement is more likely.
If 1 centimeter of black earth was created every 10 years, then 2 meters of the soil would represent some 2,000 years of settlement. A study of sherds from black earth sites 5 and 6 along the Transamazon highway reveals the presence of at least two, and possibly three, wares at each locality. 46 Several different wares at a site does not necessarily mean that the locality was abandoned and occupied by different groups; the wares could be transitional, or trade items. If a 2 meter black earth site was not continuously settled, it could have been first occupied tens of thousands of years ago.

The earliest ceramic date for the Ucayali River in the Peruvian Amazon is 650 BECAUSE \pm 100 years. Some archeological sites along the Ucayali have been occupied as many as eighteen times with an average stay of 100 years. There have been relatively dense settlements along the Ucayali and other rivers of Amazonia for at least 4,000 years.
In many terra preta sites, sherds do not always extend to the bottom of the profile; it is possible that preagricultural folk once inhabited some of the sites. Leaching of carbon might account for some, but not all, of the black earth in the lower profile without ceramics. Man has been in South America for at least 22,000 years, and in the Brazilian states of Goias, Pernambuco, Minas Gerais, and Mato Grosso for a minimum of 10,000 years.50

File_Name: Time Allocation in a Machinguenga Community.txt
Full_Text:
TIME ALLOCATION IN A MACHIGUENGA COMMUNITY Johnson, Allen Ethnology; Jul 1, 1975; 14, 3; ProQuest pg. 301
Community1
The manner in which individuals spend their time is a basic dimension of ethnographic description. Under such headings as "the daily round," "the annual cycle," or "the division of labor by sex," most ethnographies eventually describe the broad outlines of time allocation in the community. This information is then used by theorists to construct comparative generalizations.
In general, however, ethnographic estimates of time inputs or product outputs are rarely quantified. Sahlins , for example, has recently brought together some of the best quantitative production figures available to anthropologists. Employing studies such as those of Lee  among the Bushmen and McArthur  among Australian Aborigines, Sahlins  argues that hunters and gatherers typically work relatively few hours per day in obtaining ample food returns, a conclusion of particular interest for culture-evolution theory.
Despite the achievement of a relatively high level of quantification in a
few studies, some serious measurement problems remain. The most serious
concerns the representativeness of the data. Detailed study of time expenditure has usually been restricted to a narrow time period, such as four days
, or four weeks , during which the
researcher attempts to observe, or to elicit through intensive interviews conducted once of twice daily, the ways in which a small number of individuals spend all their time throughout the period. But conclusions reached
by this method are weakened by the limitations on the sample: first, in
most subsistence economies seasonal variation in production is so great that
a brief sample of one part of the year is rarely a reliable estimate for the
entire year; and second, conclusions drawn from an intensive study of a few
individuals cannot automatically be extended to the remainder of the community. A researcher's belief that the time period and the individuals in
question are "average" (that is, intuitively representative) is not very much
to the point; a representative sample is strictly speaking a random sample,
and any departure from randomness is a cost which should be offset by
clear research benefits before it is accepted.
A second measurement problem is that full-time studies of human activities are both time consuming and exhausting, because direct observation
of activities is the only reliable technique for most purposes.2

Copyright (c) 2000 Bell & Howell Information and Learning Company
Copyright (c) Ethnology / University of Pittsburgh
A third problem is the absence of uniform means of reporting data. Sahlins , for example, cites labor figures for hunter-gatherers and swidden agriculturalists which are not strictly comparable because of differences in reporting the data by the ethnographers. Thus, data on New Guinea horticulturalists refer only to labor in gardens, whereas data on the Bushmen refer to all food-acquiring activities, and the figures for Australian Aborigines extend even further to include cooking and the manufacture of implements. The data are perhaps adequate to demonstrate Sahlins' general point, but more precise analysis would increasingly find these data-incomparabilities unacceptable.
A solution to these problems could be sought in devoting more field time to the study of time allocation. Experience shows, however, that this strategy rapidly exceeds the point of diminishing returns. For although time-expenditure data are often crucial in anthropological explanations, they are seldom the only matters at issue; rather, they constitute one data set in far more extensive arguments. Devotion of major amounts of field time to collecting time-allocation data results in degrees of accuracy too detailed to interest most anthropologists, while simultaneously sacrificing such other basic information as exchange relations or kinship structure, which anthropologists also consider essential.
The data reported here, collected with the above considerations in mind, represent the tabulated results of a large number of random spot-checks on activities of members of a community of Machiguenga Indians in the Upper Amazon of Southeastern Peru. The technique was designed to make use of sampling procedures and computer processing  to increase the efficiency of a fieldwork project in which accurate estimates of
time expenditure were needed.
The data were collected by Orna Johnson and myself as part of a more
general investigation into Machiguenga cultural ecology, family organization, and sex roles, conducted between June 1972 and August 1973. Ten
months of that period were spent in the community of Shimaa, along a
tributary (Kompiroshiato) of the Upper Urubamba River in the Department of Cuzco, Peru. The Machiguenga of the Kompiroshiato typically
live in small groups, either as single families, or as clusters of closely related
families, varying from less than ten to as many as 30 individuals in a
settlement. They derive most of their food from slash-and-burn gardens,
cultivated largely by the men, supplemented by smaller quantities of food
from the tropical forest and rivers, in the form of fish, grubs, wild fruits,
and occasional large game such as monkeys or peccary. Small family
groups are almost completely self-sufficient, except for occasional needs for
trade goods such as aluminum pots and machetes. Machiguenga hunt with
bow and arrow, manufacture their own cotton fabrics, and in the past
have preferred to do without trade goods rather than become closely bound
to traders or missionaries.
Through an increasing number of bilingual public schools, run by
Copyright (c) 2000 Bell & Howell Information and Learning Company
Copyright (c) Ethnology / University of Pittsburgh
MACHIGUENGA TIME ALLOCATION 303
Machiguenga school teachers, accessibility to trade goods such as shotguns,
manufactured cloth, and medicines is beginning to grow. The community
of Shimaa has a bilingual school. Some households in the vicinity (within
about two hours walking distance of the school) have been associated with
the school for as long as eight years, others for much less than that, and a
few have only arrived within recent months. In the meantime, others have
left, preferring the self-sufficient forest existence to which they are accustomed. Those in the school communities are not yet strongly affected by
the school, in the material sense at least. Only a few men or women own
manufactured articles or clothing, and on the average less than 2 per cent of
their time is spent in labor for wages or trade goods. The Machiguenga
school teacher is the only member of the community who owns a shotgun.
The population sampled included all households within reasonable walking distance (up to 45 minutes) from our house, so that they could be visited
regularly. Under the scattered settlement of the Machiguenga this resulted
in thirteen regularly visited households, with a total of 105 members.
The sample included all members of the thirteen households. Visiting
all households at the same time proved impossible as walking distances were
so great. Therefore, the community was divided into two parts, upstream
and downstream, which were visited alternately at random hours.
Households were visited only during daylight (6 a. m. to 7 p. m.),
both because travel after dark is hazardous, and because visiting at night
is not encouraged by the Machiguenga. Hours were selected in advance:
with a table of random numbers, and housholds were visited during the
hour specified. Visits were not made every day because special events occasionally interfered; in any week, however, many visits were always made.
Tabulations show that visits were evenly apportioned by hours of the day
and by season. Visits were made on 134 different days, resulting in 3,495
cases (observations of individuals).
Visits were generally brief, which is well suited to Machiguenga visiting
patterns. Visits could be as short as five minutes or as long as 45 minutes
if other information were desired. The ideal was to describe the activities
of housemembers at the instant before they became aware of the ethnographers' presence, but this was not often realistic, so sensitive are the
Machiguenga to their surroundings. Activities of all members were described in longhand in notebooks, and the whereabouts of absent members
inquired into. When feasible, such information was immediately verified
by personal observation, but often individuals were fishing or hunting and
such checks were impossible.
Copyright (c) 2000 Bell & Howell Information and Learning Company
Copyright (c) Ethnology / University of Pittsburgh
After the first month of visits, coding procedures were devised. Longhand notes were transferred after each visit onto coding sheets for later key-punching. The codes included time, place, person and activity. Activity codes were three letters arranged in order of generality, so that the first letter of the code specified a main activity, the second letter specified a subsidiary activity within the main heading, and a third letter, if necessary, specified a still more detailed subdivision. The main activity codes were:
1. Eating, including meals, snacking, or drinking beer, specifying which foods (maize, manioc) and how prepared (boiled, roasted).
2. Food preparation, including what food and how prepared.
3. Child rearing, whether nursing, holding, patting, etc.
4. Manufacture, specifying item and stage of manufacture.
5. Wild foods, including hunting, fishing, collecting.
6. Garden labor.
7. Idleness, including sleeping, awake doing nothing, awake chatting, recreation at beer party, etc.
8. Hygiene, including laundry, bathing, defecation, etc.
9. Visiting, including who visited and what exchanged.
10. School (applies only to ages nine to eighteen).
11. Wage labor (mainly for Machiguenga school teacher in exchange for tools).
The coding sheets are based on the 80-column IBM card, and are easily punched by professional keypunchers at low cost and low error. The data for this paper were processed using the Cross-Tabs II program for the distributions in Tables 1-3, and the SPSS T-TEST program for the significance tests in Table 4. Midway in the research I returned briefly from the field to have preliminary data processed. These materials were most helpful in developing research questions for the latter phase of the research.
By randomizing the observations, problems of representativeness of the data were eliminated. By including whatever people were doing at the moment they were observed, the data constitute an essentially unbiased description of all activities, not just those, such as productive labor, which were of particular interest at the time of research. The brief time spent in recording activities took only a small fraction of the total field time. In fact, the visits brought us into frequent contact with community members who could then be interviewed for other purposes. Finally, observations were coded in the field and processed by computer during the fieldwork, so that results of the investigation could be used in guiding research as an integral part of fieldwork. The technique seems well suited to many anthropological purposes, by providing essential, well-quantified data on particular subjects without inhibiting the wide-ranging data collection commonly sought in holistic anthropological research.
Tables 1 and 2 exemplify the data obtained by the method outlined above. The time period covers the thirteen daylight hours from 6:00 a. m. to 7:00 p. m. The tables clearly reveal the transformation of time allocation from infants to toddlers, occupied with idleness and eating, to the diversity characteristic of adults, who reach a low of 18-19 per cent of idleness. This low figure for idleness is quite surprising; can it be that primitive horticulturalist/hunter-gatherers, living under low population density, are engaged in productive labor more than 80 per cent of their daylight time?
To be sure, the answer depends on how the notion "productive" is construed. We might follow Sahlins  in considering only the amount of time spent in gardens as productive labor. Apparently once a group has

1. N. = number of observations
Due to the small number of observations, the data in this column
are weak and presented only for the sake of completeness.

No = number of observations
been classified as slash-and-burn horticulturalists, Sahlins takes their garden time as an estimate of total productive labor. This definition, applied to adult married Machiguenga, finds the men productively engaged only 18.5 per cent and the women only 6.6 per cent of the time.
But I think this approach is overly typological. Simply because we might classify the Machiguenga as slash-and-burn horticulturalists does not mean that garden labor provides all their subsistence. Consideration of all foodgetting activities reveals the men active in production about 35 per cent of the time, and the women 13 per cent. Furthermore, from here it is a small step to include as productive activities manufacture and food preparation. Some might also want to include childrearing and time spent in eating, hygiene, and even certain kinds of visiting. In that case, men appear to
be productively employed 65.7 per cent of the time, and women 73.3 per cent
(Table 3).
Depending, therefore, on which measure of productive labor we wish to use, we could conclude that Machiguenga men spend less than two and one-half hours or more than eight hours per day in essential subsistence activities, and that women spend less than one hour or more than nine hours per day. The advantage of providing a complete record of the activities performed by different categories of individuals should be clear: comparative theorists are then free to define variables in accordance with their theoretical aims rather than having to accept the incommensurable figures each idiosyncratic fieldworker may choose to publish.

A further breakdown of time allocation appears in Table 4, giving a more complete view of the division between the sexes. For example, differences between men and women in the gross category, "manufacture," are not statistically significant; but subheadings within that category reveal that men do nearly all the work involving woodworking, whereas women are predominantly concerned with making conton cloth, and these differences are statistically significant. In obtaining wild foods, men are the only hunters, but men and women both fish and collect fruits, grubs, etc. In horticulture, women's only significant contribution is in the harvest. In sum, the Machiguenga division of labor by sex is extreme in terms of observed behavior. Yet each sex puts in a relatively long working day, if that term is broadly defined.

*Differences are significant at p < .01 level (t-test).
A large number of special-interest tables could be generated from the data collected in the random visits. Tables which have already been of use include the distribution of tasks by time of day, the distribution of tasks by season, and the frequencies of visiting between different households. Presentation of these and other tables will be reserved for appropriate publications,

but a number of general points have emerged which bear on the value of such data and techniques for collecting them.
The principal advantages of describing time allocation in this manner are:
1. The resulting data are quantified at a high level of measurement, and can be compared with exactness across cultures.
2. Erroneous conclusions reached intuitively can be corrected. For example, after months of being with the Machiguenga in a variety of settings, I came to believe that the women were idle much more of the time than the men. Table 4 reveals that there is no significant difference in idleness between adult males and females. Women's work is more often conducted in a sitting position and at a slower pace than men's work3, and that probably led to my incorrect impression. Whenever anthropologists estimate time allocation without some objective research method, errors like this are bound to arise.
The randomness of the visits introduces an element of unpredictability into the anthropologist's routine, counteracting the natural tendency to be predictably in certain places at certain times. It thereby provides the anthropologist with a wider range of experiences than he or she might otherwise encounter.
A few rules of procedure should be observed in conducting random activity surveys:
1. Visits should be random (or occasionally stratified) samples of:
(a) the population.
(b) the time of day.
(c) the days of the year. Random visits scheduled in advance should not be missed except for good reason, such as special events or situations of personal danger; and a minor inconvenience, such as a rainy day, should not interfere, since this would lead to just the kind of bias to be avoided (in this case, a bias against rainy-day activities).
2. All activities should be recorded, not just those of special interest, since the overall configuration of activities is the theoretically most useful form of the data.
3. All activities should be coded unambiguously according to person, time, location, and activity. Activity codes may be as detailed as necessary. As long as codes have unambiguous meanings, those assigned in the field may be punched directly onto IBM cards without time-consuming recoding later.
The data reported in this paper represent a random sample of daily activities of members of an Indian community of the Upper Amazon. Estimates of time expenditure are basic data in ethnographic reporting, but to be useful for cross-cultural theory construction they should reach a high level of measurement and constitute a truly representative sample of the activities of the community being described. Owing to a natural tendency to find patterns through implicit mental processes, the memory of both the ethnographer and the informant in such matters is liable to be faulty. Since these mental processes are culturally determined in part, reliance on memory
Copyright (c) 2000 Bell & Howell Information and Learning Company
Copyright (c) Ethnology / University of Pittsburgh
is bound to distort descriptions of time allocation in some unpredictable direction determined by cultural notions of what constitutes "spending time." The ready availability of computer-processing of masses of quantitative data has opened the door to descriptive techniques which avoid such biases, yet at relatively little increase in field-time costs.
1. I am grateful to Conrad Arensberg, Robert Carneiro, Gertrude Dole, and Marvin Harris for their valuable comments on an earlier draft of this paper. Orna Johnson shared in the data collecting and coding phases, and her personal commitment resulted in a more thorough sample than I would have obtained alone. My research assistant, Paul Heikkila, managed the bulk of the computer processing. The National Science Foundation supported the research under grant no. NSF-GS-33012.
2. Informant interviewing may be a useful supplement, to estimate time spent in locations where the researcher was unable to follow, but this cannot stand alone as a
reliable technique.
3. Measures of calorie energy expenditures by task show that Machiguenga women consistently expend fewer calories at work than men.

As the world rapidly changes, humanity's biocultural heritage encoded in indigenous languages, natural resource
management practices, technologies, and artistic expressions is increasingly threatened by globalization, territorial displacement and encroachment, urbanization, climate
change and other drivers of development . Given the pace of change in many areas,
some scholars question whether indigenous knowledge
(IK) will survive the present millennium .
digenous communities around the world documents diverse social mechanisms that support sustainable natural
resource use and management, as well as the dynamic,
fluid and mobile nature of local knowledge systems in
contemporary contexts as indigenous people adapt to
changing conditions .
Berkes et al. , highlight the adaptive nature of IK
systems through social mechanisms that support sustainable
natural resource use and management, which may include: (a)
adaptations in the generation, accumulation, and transmission
of ecological knowledge through reinterpreting signals for
learning, revival of local knowledge, as well as knowledge
integration and geographical diffusion; (b) local institutions
as rules for social regulation; (c) mechanisms for cultural internalization of traditional practices (such as cultural frameworks for resource management as well as rituals, ceremonies
and other traditions); and (d) worldviews and cultural values
reinforcing environmental ethics and stewardship. Placebased knowledge systems work as reservoirs of adaptive, experiential knowledge, and may provide insights useful for the
design of adaptation and mitigation strategies to cope with
global environmental change . However, the question remains as to
how indigenous knowledge is retained, transmitted, and
adapted over time.

a given culture's knowledge traits, behavior patterns, ideas,
practices, values and cosmological beliefs are communicated
and acquired across generations . This process is embedded in social structure (e.g., gender, age, and kinship), institutions (marriage, religion, community associations, school) and place. Hewlett and
mission in human societies, used in various combinations
through one person's lifetime: vertical or parent-to-child; horizontal (between any individuals); oblique (across generations);
one-to-many (predominant today: schools, media, books); and
many-to-one modes (elders teaching younger members). The
multi-faceted nature of cultural transmission, embedded in diverse social relationships, constitutes a source of adaptive capacity as knowledge transmission changes over time.
In part because of this complexity in forms of transmission,
knowledge - including ecological and agricultural knowledge
- is differentially distributed among members of a given group
(intra-cultural) or across ethnic groups (inter-cultural), according to factors such as age, gender, occupation, economic status, level of formal schooling, place of residence, kinship relations, among others . Proficiency in the native
language has been reported to be an important indicator of
ecological knowledge retention . Other studies found that IK is negatively
correlated with formal schooling, and that market integration
might have an ambiguous effect on knowledge retention and
erosion . Mechanisms of indigenous
knowledge transmission and distribution may also differ according to gender, related to the different roles men and women play in the society . These sources of diversity influence the
extent to which indigenous knowledge persists and adapts.
Authors have documented a combination of kinship and non-kinship (horizontal, vertical, oblique) modes for ethnobotanical and medical knowledge transmission among Amerindian peoples . Cultural transmission of craft skills among indigenous Xinguano societies¹ over the past millennium shows conservatism in ceramic, housing, and land-use technologies through both vertical and generational transmission (direct kinship modes). As new indigenous groups entered the region, sharing or horizontal transmission became salient with the adoption of local technologies, which were incorporated into regional socio-cultural systems in the Upper Xingu . Association has been found between horizontal (non-direct kinship modes)
Indigenous groups who currently inhabit southern portions of the Xingu Indigenous Park (PIX, Terra Indigena do Xingu), in the Xingu River headwater basin in the southern Brazilian Amazon.
transmission and innovative patterns in craft skills among pre-Columbian societies . Mixed transmission modes combining both vertical, oblique, and horizontal mechanisms (such as borrowing and experimentation, for instance) have also led to innovation in designs, techniques, patterns, and decorative materials . Assessment of the importance of different modes or mechanisms of knowledge transmission informs our understanding of the stability or loss of cultural traits across time and space, as well as of the human capacity to innovate .
Around the world, indigenous peoples have engaged in cultural revitalization initiatives and community-based projects using media, workshops, film-making, and other tools, allowing them to recreate traditional knowledge in different ways . Such initiatives address various knowledge domains, and are typically developed in collaboration with external actors and institutions. Despite these efforts, the longer term effects or trends of these processes on mechanisms of indigenous knowledge retention and transmission are still largely unknown . The lack of quantitative and longitudinal studies that apply comparable methods across different sites hinders robust understanding of how processes and drivers of change (including contemporary initiatives) affect interrelated knowledge domains across spatial-temporal scales.
cultural distribution and transmission of weaving knowledge
of basketry and textile graphic designs among 214 men and
women in four villages across three Kaiabi groups in the
Amazon, geographically set apart by the Brazilian government since the 1960s.2 Factors involved in the retention, erosion, and innovation of a technical-artistic knowledge domain
are identified and compared to results described in previous
research for other knowledge domains. Evidence of innovation in mechanisms for knowledge creation and transmission
is presented, documented through a long-term communitybased cultural revitalization project. The implication of these
mechanisms and innovations for the future are discussed.
The Kaiabi are a Tupi-Guarani speaking people who originally occupied several tributaries of the Tapajós River in the southern Brazilian Amazon. Between 1950 and 1966, the

All use subject to https://about.jstor.org/terms
2 We adopted the designation Kaiabi in this paper, which is broadly used across the indigenous lands occupied by this ethnic group in the Amazon. The self-designation Kawaiwete is recent (about two years ago, the Xingu group asked to be recognized and named as such). Kawaiwete, in the native language means "true people" .
Hum Ecol  45:533–544 535
Brazilian federal government forced the relocation of the majority of the group to what is now the Xingu Indigenous Park (PIX) . While most people relocated to Xingu, two smaller groups resisted, and live close to their ancestral region, one on the Dos Peixes River and the other along the Teles Pires River (Fig. 1). The resettled group have developed strong leadership skills, maintaining and recreating traditional practices, while the two smaller groups have experienced loss of traditional knowledge .
The Kaiabi have a sophisticated agriculture and cuisine, as well as an elaborated material culture in which the most technically and artistically developed items are designed and painted twill-plaited baskets . These baskets were traditionally produced by Kaiabi men to represent different social meanings and for economic purposes, which have shifted with the changes faced by the group after their transfer to the PIX. Basketry designs have become strong symbols of Kaiabi identity, and have been applied in other artifacts such as painted benches, woven club handles, and sculpted gourds, as well as body painting. While in Xingu basketry knowledge has been at the same time eroded, perpetuated, and innovated, it seems that in the other two areas knowledge of this cultural domain is eroding at a faster pace.
Contradictions and paradoxes are more of a rule than an exception when it comes to cultural persistence and change among indigenous peoples in the modern world. Whereas in Xingu the Kaiabi have managed to perpetuate their basketry knowledge, they have been dealing with a critical lack of the main natural fiber used in weaving work (Ischnosiphon gracilis, Maranthaceae, arumã in Portuguese) . By contrast, this fiber is abundant in the other two areas, where weaving knowledge has been lost.
Aiming to revitalize weaving knowledge across geographically separated groups, a group of men and women from Tuiararé village in Xingu and Kururuzinho village in Teles Pires developed a community-based project that lasted seven years (named Kaiabi Araa, "Design of the Kaiabi"). It included the organization of weaving workshops for men and women involving "teachers" or experts and students from several age groups in villages in Xingu and Teles Pires rivers. However, it was not implemented in the Capivara and Rio dos Peixes communities. It was funded by the Indigenous Peoples Demonstrative Projects (PDPI) of the Pilot Program to Conserve the Brazilian Rain Forest (PPG7)3 and implemented by the indigenous communities in partnership with the Brazilian NGO Instituto Socioambiental (ISA) and the
local indigenous organization Associação Terra Indígena Xingu (ATIX). A video-documentary was produced documenting the workshops and activities of the Kaiabi Araa project, which was one of the winners of the "Indigenous Cultures" award from the Ministry of Culture in Brazil in 2007.

The research hypotheses tested in this study (summarized in Table 1) address statements on indigenous knowledge distribution and transmission from the literature. To assess the relationships between the various factors and drivers involved in IK retention and erosion, weaving ability was the dependent variable (not weaving = 0; weaving = 1). The independent variables were as follows: (a) gender (females = 0; males = 1); (b) language proficiency measured as not proficient (0) or
proficient (1) (since intermediary proficiency levels were difficult to assess and were not considered); (c) no schooling (0) or any level of schooling (1); and (d) stability of income, used as a proxy for market integration. Irregular or temporary workers, handicraft income, and eventual sale of indigenous agricultural or forestry products were considered "unstable (0)," whereas salaried officers, health agents, recipients of government pensions, and teachers were considered as having "stable income" (1).

During workshops of the Kaiabi Araa cultural revitalization project, 21 different items that compose the rich Kaiabi basketry repertoire were identified. Some of these objects are used solely by men (23.8%), others are mostly used by women (28.6%), but the majority may be used by both (47.6%). Some are also used by the shamans and in rituals. The most basic type of basket, and the easiest to learn, is the myayta, made of vine fiber, used by everyone to transport food from the garden plot to the village.

Every married man should be able to make a myayta. Another basket of relatively easy manufacture is the simple fan (tapekwap) made of young tucumã (Astrocaryum aculeatum) palm leaves. It is used mainly to cover food and to make fire. There is a more complex fan (tapekwajowai) with two handles, which only a few men know how to make. Men also produce beautiful woven handles for wooden clubs, which are nowadays used in political events and during festivals. In all villages studied, men produce clubs for sale.
The different types of basketry strainers, used mainly by women to prepare food, reflect the sophistication of Kaiabi agriculture and cuisine. Women use a small sieve strainer for fine grain flour (yrũpemeaii), as well as a large sieve strainer (yrũpemeauu). A specific basket, made of inajá (Attalea maripa) palm leaf petiole, is used exclusively to store peanuts (juyp munuwi yrũ). Its name reflects both the material used in its manufacture (juyp = grass or fiber) and its use (munuwi= peanut). Both fan and strainer baskets can also be used by shamans. Other items in the basketry repertoire are woven supports for headfeathers, and woven adornments for arrows and fish traps, both used by men.

In contrast to basketry, the textiles repertoire comprises only six items, three of them developed after the transfer of the group to Xingu: strap for carrying babies; three types of hammocks; woven bags and belts. Bags and belts are innovations developed in Xingu for use in festivals, trade and sale.
We documented evidence of innovation in modes of creation and transmission of indigenous knowledge among three Kaiabi groups, including: (a) transmission of techniques across genders; (b) borrowing and modifying techniques from neighboring cultures; (c) using substitute plant species and industrial cotton for weaving baskets and textiles; (d) connecting with museums for cultural repatriation; (e) using media (books, films and photos) to revitalize traditional knowledge; and (f) enhancing modes of learning and transmission through community-based cultural revitalization projects (Fig. 3).
In the Xingu Indigenous Park, aiming to protect the valued
knowledge and the diversity of basket weaving designs, men
taught women how to weave them in textiles (hammocks,
straps for carrying babies, bags) using a technique borrowed

from the Yudja indigenous people . Before the transfer to Xingu Park, women used to weave hammocks (taity) and straps for carrying babies (tupai) using a 'twined' technique and a ground loom commonly employed by other Tupi groups. Through contact and inter-marriage with Yudja people in Xingu, Kaiabi women learned the 'twilled' technique, which allows weaving of complex geometric designs.
In addition to learning a new weaving technique, women have also begun to incorporate designs previously used only in baskets. Initially, men taught the women to copy the basketry designs into textile pieces. Some women subsequently began to copy other designs from baskets or, more recently, from printed photographs. At least five distinctive new graphic motifs have been incorporated this way into women's textiles. The Kaiabi have been copying their basketry designs into other objects as well, including bags, clubs, and benches, all of which are made for sale.
Comparing basketry and textile weaving among men and women, we can conclude that while less innovation was developed in basketry, where no new technique was learned, in textile weaving there was a significant development of techniques and incorporation of items and designs into the traditional repertoire. It is also interesting to note that, in spite of learning a new technique from another indigenous group (the Yudja), women learned the designs represented in the twill-plaited baskets from the men, and have used them in the new items they began to produce (baby straps, hammocks, bags and belts). The innovation developed by the men was the application of graphic designs over a range of other objects, such as wooden-carved benches and beaded bracelets, and in body painting.
According to Athayde et al. , men have used at least six plant substitutes for the arumā plant to cope with the scarcity of raw materials used in basketry weaving due to environmental changes resulting from geographical displacement: (1) taquarinha or kwasingevi (non-identified species, Poaceae family); (2) buriti or myricipe'yp (Mauritia flexuosa); (3) jacitara or panakuwa (Desmoncus sp); (4) banana-brava or pokop (Heliconia sp); (5) taquara or takwasing (non-identified species, Poaceae family); and (6) cana-brava or wywa (Gynerium sagittatum).
Another innovative mechanism used by the Kaiabi to develop different basketry designs is copying from photographs, drawings, and book illustrations. In 1997, we noted that some men in the villages had copies of an article published by Berta Ribeiro in 1986, which contained drawings of basketry designs further developed from Georg Grünberg's previous work , which they were using to learn new designs. We photographed and documented every basket in the village that contained a new design. At the same time, photographs archived in national and international museums and libraries were collected and returned to the communities in printed and digital formats. During the Kaiabi Araa workshops, planned and executed
by the indigenous communities, a mixed collaborative mode of
transmission was the main learning model used by participants:
many teachers, elders and others, independently of age or kinship, taught many students and also would leam from each

techniques of indigenous material culture may contribute to maintenance of the integrity of the broader socioecological system, as noted in long-term histories of indigenous groups in the Xingu region .
Based on our qualitative and quantitative analyses carried out across the three Kaiabi groups, we suggest that the maintenance of cultural diversity and traditional knowledge might increase the capacity of human systems to adapt and cope with change, and develop innovations. The greater their flexibility and capacity to adapt – while still ensuring the transmission of indigenous knowledge and traditions through generations, and thus their resilience - the greater is the power and potential of indigenous peoples to achieve self-determination and control over their land and resources . According to Folke et al. , adaptability in social-ecological systems can be interpreted as the capacity of actors in a system to influence resilience or maintenance of desired tracts over time.
The combination of qualitative and quantitative data sheds light on contemporary changes in patterns of knowledge transmission informed by new institutions and social roles such as community-based projects, collaborative research with academics, inter-cultural schools, indigenous associations, and the role of NGOs. Longitudinal and quantitative studies on mechanisms of transmission of cultural traits may be applied to predict intra-cultural distribution, variability, and the stability of cultural traits over time .
Weaving knowledge has been both maintained and eroded among the Kaiabi as a group through an interplay between forces of continuity and change. It is, indeed, the same, but different. While in Xingu this knowledge has been maintained and at the same time re-created, in Teles Pires and Rio dos Peixes it has been eroded and can be considered threatened with extinction, along with native language.
Patterns of knowledge distribution and transmission evidenced through this study corroborate previous scholarly findings about intra-cultural distribution of knowledge in indigenous societies. Our main findings are the positive relationship between native language proficiency and weaving knowledge retention, and the negative role of formal schooling, particularly if not in the local language, in leading to weaving knowledge erosion. The social dimensions of knowledge acquisition, distribution, and transmission should be considered in research designs aiming to assess the dynamics of indigenous knowledge systems within and across domains, including, when applicable, the role of gender. Additionally, research addressing cultural change among contemporary indigenous
societies, should consider the effect of policies and of non-indigenous institutions on the dynamics of IK through time.
Indigenous people's agency in adapting to socialenvironmental transformations informs our understanding of
humanity's cultural evolutionary paths. Documenting and
sharing indigenous creative responses to crises and transformational events (e.g., displacement) can also inform policies
and practices towards greater cultural resilience across cultural
and geographical borders.
Based on the present case, the question of how indigenous knowledge systems will survive the millennium is first and foremost a question of how indigenous peoples themselves, through their capacity to conserve, recreate, and innovate traditional knowledge, are able and empowered to interact with non-indigenous institutions and development policies.

understanding the common property institutions of indigenous
groups in the Amazon. Using the example of the Pueblo
Kichwa de Rukullakta, an ethnic Kichwa group in the Ecua
dorian Amazon, we analyze land tenure arrangements at the
household, community, and territory levels using a common
property framework. The specific bundle of rights identified
by the framework is held at the household level but house
holds rely on community and territory level arrangements for
their enforcement. Land claims at the community and territory
level also serve to define the pool of legitimate rights holders
at the next lower level. Due to the importance of scale in
understanding indigenous land tenure generally, we suggest
an adaptation of the common property framework to explicitly
recognize the role of scale. This adapted framework identifies
the function, characteristics, and means of enforcement for
land claims at each scale of analysis.

the importance of tropical forests, significant gaps remain in our
understanding of the diversity of governance arrangements that
influence forest management and result in different social and

towards decentralization in developing countries have put in
creasing control of forests in the hands of local people and
evidence suggests that participation by users in the governance
of forest resources enhances the probability of more sustainable
outcomes . There is a need, however, to look
deeper at specific forest governance arrangements to under
stand how rights and responsibilities are distributed among
users. A better understanding of specific arrangements
governing tropical forests is critical for assessing their influence
on outcomes and can inform the development of policies to
promote sustainable management .
Property institutions in particular are critical components of
sustainable governance arrangements and differences in these
institutions may shape management outcomes. For example,
in some cases communal forms of land tenure have been
shown to result in less deforestation than adjacent private
tenure . Security of land
tenure has also been shown to improve human development
indicators  and both reduce  and increase  deforestation rates in
different contexts. Gould  demonstrates, however, that
formal titling itself does not necessarily lead to land tenure
security, which is dependent upon functioning legal and po
litical systems to enforce property rights. Secure land tenure
can even exist in the absence of formal government titling
through local norms and social controls that enforce rights
. This suggests that
the role of property in promoting sustainable use of resources
is complex and complicated by numerous mediating variables.
Further complicating interpretations of outcomes is often a lack
of understanding of specific underlying property institutions,

In this paper, we examine the common property
arrangements in an ethnic Kichwa community in the
framework developed by Schlager and Ostrom  and
Ostrom and Schlager .' In our analysis, we find scale
to be an important factor in the allocation and enforcement of
property rights that was not accounted for in the framework.
Due to the importance of scale in understanding how rights are
distributed and enforced, we suggest an adaptation of the
common property framework to explicitly recognize the role
of scale. Multi-scale analyses may provide a more complete
understanding of common property institutions as critical
components of governance arrangements for tropical forests.
The Amazon, the world's largest tropical forest region, is
home to over 370 indigenous groups whose ancestors have
long inhabited and modified its forests . Preliminary estimates of current indigenous
land claims amount to 1,970,699 km2 of the Amazon basin in
2,200 distinct territories officially recognized or in the process
of being recognized by national governments in the basin
. This represents 25.3 % of the Amazon basin,
but the total is likely to grow as new lands are recognized or
claimed by increasingly organized indigenous communities.
In Brazil, the largest Amazonian country, indigenous terri
tories constitute 21.7 % of the country's portion of the Ama
zon. Ecuador has the highest percentage of all Amazonian
countries with approximately 65.0 % of its area of the basin
classified as indigenous lands .
Despite the magnitude of indigenous lands in the Amazon,
little is known about prevailing land tenure regimes in these
areas. According to Bremner and Lu  "common
property institutions in the Amazon are still poorly understood."
Based on a review of the Digital Library of the Commons2,
of common property regimes in the Amazon focused on the
establishment of institutions by non-indigenous communities in
order to create or manage extractive reserves, such as the
Extractive Reserve Chico Mendes, or to manage fisheries in
the floodplain lakes of Peru and Brazil . Notably, only a handful of studies have
discussed the common property regimes of indigenous peoples
. Bremner and Lu  also
point out that the impetus for the formalization of indigenous
common property institutions resembles that of many non
indigenous communities in that it often occurs in response to
outside challenges to land or resource claims .
tions in the Amazon were shaped by challenges to traditional
resource claims highlights the fact that land tenure systems
can be dynamic and adapt to changing circumstances. This
pattern holds for the Ecuadorian Amazon, where indigenous
federations and cooperatives were formed during the 1960s
and 1970s with a primary objective of protecting ancestral
land claims from colonists arriving in the region following the
passage of the 1964 Agrarian Reform and Colonization Law
. One strategy employed by these newly
created federations was to push for the establishment of gov
ernment recognized "territories"3. The push for extensive
territories, which often encompassed multiple communities
of the same ethnic group, challenged the "Ecuadorian state's
claims that these are 'open lands' and thus state property"
. Small communities or hamlets, typically
constituted of people related to one another and led by elder men
or shamans, were previously the primary social unit organizing
land tenure in the region . The grouping of
multiple communities into a single territory was a novel practice
adopted out of necessity to secure land claims and receive legal
land titles from the Ecuadorian government. Territories remain as
central entities organizing land tenure in the region today.
Since the initial challenges to their traditional land claims,
even indigenous groups that received official territorial recog
nition from the government continue to live in landscapes
highly influenced by external forces including oil and mining
companies and encroachment by colonists and other indige
nous groups. The extent to which these forces continue to
influence tenure practices is unknown and the contemporary
common property institutions of many indigenous groups
remain under-examined. A study by Bremner and Lu 
is a notable exception. Using survey and ethnographic
methods, they describe the land tenure characteristics of 36
communities representing five prominent indigenous groups,
the Kichwa, Shuar, Huaorani, Cofan, and Secoya, located in
the provinces of Orellana and Sucumbios in the northeastern
Ecuadorian Amazon. Their analysis provides three main con
clusions. The first is that the common property regimes in the
communities studied can be grouped into either individual or
communal arrangements. In communities with individual ar
rangements, tracts of land are divided among member house
holds and households hold rights to these tracts regardless of 1 Kichwa is an alternative spelling ofQuichua.

Agrawal and Gibson  describe institutions as "sets
of formal and informal rules and norms that shape interactions
of humans with others and nature." The importance of insti
tutions in the management of natural resources emerged as a
counter to Hardin's  famous article, "The Tragedy of
the Commons," which warned that resource degradation was a
probable outcome on commonly held property and a
simple
solution was privatization. Numerous scholars have since
challenged Hardin's basic premise and pointed out that his
hypothetical commons is not in fact common property, but
instead represents a situation of open access in which no
defined rights holders exist . Common
property, on the other hand, is characterized by a specific
group of individuals who hold defined rights to a resource
and are able to exclude non-rights holders from the use or
extraction of the resource. Rights holders also establish rules
governing how a resource is managed and enforce those rules
among themselves and non-rights holders . Them
pirical research has demonstrated a wide diversity of
longstanding common property regimes around the world that
undermines the logic of Hardin's argument when applied to
common property situations .
put forth a common property framework that characterizes the
rights commonly associated with different common property
regimes and views operational rights and collective choice
decisions as the basis ofproperty rights. Operational rights can
be further divided into access and withdrawal rights. Access
refers to the right to enter a physically defined space while
withdrawal refers to the right to extract resources from that
space. Rights of access and withdrawal to property owned by
another are known as usufruct and a holder of these rights is
referred to as an authorized user . Management, exclusion, and alienation rights
differentiate collective choice decisions and are distinct from
operational rights in that they allow holders to participate in
governance over a resource. Management refers to the right to
regulate use and improve resources. Exclusion rights allow
holders to determine who has rights of access and withdrawal
and ifthose rights may be transferred. Alienation is the right to
sell or lease either or both management and exclusion rights
.

to holders of the different property rights described above

rights originate both formally and informally. Rights may
originate from the state with official recognition through for
mal, legal instruments such as titles; such rights are termed de
jure rights and holders can reasonably expect to have their
rights enforced through formal legal processes, such as in
courts. In the Amazon and other tropical forest areas, enforce
able rights typically originate informally or outside official
settings, often among the holders themselves, who also coop
erate to establish social controls for enforcement , and these are termed de facto rights . Although holders may assume that their rights
are secure and enforceable through social means, they remain
de facto rights as long as they are not formally recognized by
the state. We note that in the Amazon, many indigenous land
rights typically began as de facto rights with groups claiming
and defending traditional territories in the absence of recogni
tion by national governments. Many of these claims became
de jure rights as governments granted legal titles and formally
recognized communally held lands. Although many groups

lous and widespread indigenous group in the Ecuadorian
guistic groups present in the Napo region prior to the

epidemics contributed to the cultural simplification and

tenure descriptions , maintained individual land tenure
arrangements similar to those described by Bremner and
Lu . The specific origin and timing of these arrange
ments, however, is uncertain. Many households maintained
primary residences in more concentrated communities as
well as secondary settlements in more remote and widely
dispersed areas where more abundant game meant the hunt
ing was better. The arrangement of primary and secondary
settlement areas is still practiced in Rukullakta, although
several primary communities have been founded in histor
ically dispersed secondary areas over the last 20 years (e.g.,
Lupino, Mushullakta). Due to this settlement pattern, these
newer communities do not have associated secondary areas
of their own.
Rukullakta is located approximately 100 km southeast of
Ecuadorian Amazon. The southwestern portion of the
Rukullakta territory and its administrative center is situated
2 km outside the town of Archidona and 12 km from the
provincial capital of Tena (Fig. 1). The 44,836 ha territory
comprises 17 communities and is home to an estimated 7,000
people, almost all of which are ethnically Kichwa .4 Residents of Rukullakta democratically elect leaders
at the community and territory level. At the community level,
each of the 17 communities elects a president, vice-president,
treasurer, and secretary. These leaders regularly facilitate com
munity forums to discuss and debate local issues. Each pres
ident also represents its community as a member of the
territory's general assembly. In addition to the 17 community
presidents, the general assembly consists of leadership posi
tions elected by adult members of the territory, including a
president and vice-president.
Local Kichwa founded the Rukullakta Cooperative in 1970
as a strategy to protect ancestral Kichwa lands from the
growing threat posed by colonists. National policy at this time
required communities to be organized into agricultural coop
eratives in order to be officially recognized by the government
and begin the process of acquiring legal land titles . The cooperative received official title
to their territory in 1977 . The word Rukullakta
itself means the "the old homeland" and indicates the long
presence of the Kichwa in the area, especially those located
near Archidona whose ancestors are believed to have settled in
the region before the sixteenth century . In 2007, the territory officially changed its name
from the "Cooperativa Agropecuaria San Pedro de
Rukullakta" to the "Pueblo Kichwa de Rukullakta".
Beginning in the 1970s and continuing through the 1990s,
the rapid frontier expansion of the Ecuadorian Amazon sig
nificantly changed the region. In the north, where Rukullakta
is located, this expansion was driven by the development of
road infrastructure for oil production and government policies
that promoted immigration to the region . The promotion of
colonization led to an increase in the total population of the
Ecuadorian Amazon from approximately 49,500 in 1974 to
82,500 in 1982 to 103,000 in 1990. This represents an annual
growth rate between 6 and 8 % . Rates of growth
dropped considerably after this period but continued above
3 % up to 2010. Although there were no oil developments in
or around Rukullakta during this period, much of the popula
tion growth occurred at the foot of the Andes in areas tradi
tionally claimed by the Kichwa since these were the first

forces, such as urbanization and exposure to neighbors oper

tenure systems of indigenous groups and moved them closer
to non-indigenous property arrangements. This explanation
assumes that communal arrangements are more "traditional"
or "indigenous" while individual or private arrangements are
more "western" or "modern". This assumption, however,
overlooks evidence of a diversity of different indigenous
property arrangements, including longstanding private ar
rangements, and suggests that these generalized characteriza

In the case of Rukullakta, it is difficult to assess the specific
role outside influences have had in shaping the current
common property system. Macdonald's  ethno
graphic accounts from the 1970s suggest that the Kichwa
around Tena utilized individual arrangements. Local shamans
enforced household land claims and resolved disputes. With
the influx of colonists beginning in the 1970s that threatened
indigenous land claims, most Kichwa households preferred to
receive a legal land title to their household land claims but
were unable to afford the cost associated with the process
. As an alternative, the Ecuadorian gov
ernment allowed indigenous groups to form cooperatives and
apply for a collective title or organize into a legally recognized
community (comma). These two options required communi
ties to elect representatives and make land available for com
munal use. According to Erazo , "Both require
ments were sharp departures from previous understandings of
the household as a relatively autonomous political unit that
actively claimed and defended access to particular territories."
Additionally, the processes for enforcing land claims and
resolving disputes described in this paper may not be absolute
or long standing. Based on research conducted between 1987
and 1993, Rogers  describes multiple
instances in which residents of Rukullakta went to municipal
authorities instead of community or territory leaders to resolve
land disputes. These accounts highlight the role multiple
actors and institutions, such as the state at various
administrative levels, played in shaping property
arrangements in Rukullakta and demonstrate that common
property systems are not static but can adapt to changing
conditions and circumstances.
The descriptions of land tenure by Macdonald , Erazo  and Rogers  suggest that the
individual arrangements in Rukullakta more closely re
semble the "traditional" practices of the area. What con
stitutes "traditional" however, is highly dependent on the
time period in question. Since the founding of the Span
ish mission in Archidona in 1559, indigenous residents
in and around the current territory of Rukullakta experi
enced varying levels of contact with outsiders of Euro
pean ancestry . How these experi
ences shaped land tenure practices over the past 400 years
is uncertain. It is therefore difficult to classify particular
aspects of Rukullakta's land tenure system as "tradition
al" or "modern". Such broad classifications of land ten
ure systems in Rukullakta and elsewhere may represent
oversimplified or forced dichotomies. Indeed, existing
studies on indigenous land tenure systems in the Amazon
and beyond demonstrate the diversity of these systems
and highlight the problems associated with generalized
assumptions of their origins and how they work . As

region of Guatemala, "sometimes land use differences

regimes as well. The common perception of many westerners
of "traditional" indigenous land tenure as communal is more a
western projection of an idealized indigenous community than
it is a reflection of existing land tenure practices. Just as
notions of "community" in the literature and instead direct
scholars towards the importance of institutions in managing
resources, we believe an institutional perspective at multiple
scales will also help scholars move beyond coarse "commu
nal" assumptions of indigenous property regimes. We suggest
that a more productive approach is to apply the common
property framework to understand the spectrum of contempo
rary indigenous land tenure systems and to analyze the con
textual factors that have shaped these systems over time. Such
analyses may provide a better understanding of how current
land tenure systems function, how they emerged, and how
they might adapt to changing circumstances in the future. The
advancement of a generalizable common property framework
will assist our understandings of indigenous land tenure
systems and the explicit recognition of the importance of scale
will help advance this framework.
Conclusion: Adapting the Common Property Framework
Indigenous property rights can be grouped in up to four levels
based on scale. Land tenure is arranged at the household,
community, and territory levels (for multi-community territories
like Rukullakta) and is influenced by and interacts with a
broader external setting. These arrangements are not mutually
exclusive but occur simultaneously and reinforce each other by
providing means ofenforcement. Due to the importance of scale
in understanding indigenous land tenure, based on the above, we
suggest an adaptation of the Schlager and Ostrom  and
explicitly recognize different scales of land claims and their
characteristics that can be expected in indigenous lands. This
framework: (1) identifies the relevant scales of land claims; (2)
distinguishes the function and characteristics of the land claim at
each scale and; (3) recognizes the source of the claims and their
means of enforcement at each scale. Additionally, the frame
work also considers the broader external setting and how the
various actors and institutions in this setting interact with the
common property system operating within the territory. This
framework can be applied to understand indigenous land tenure
throughout the Amazon and possibly in other locations.

Applying this modified framework to Rukullakta, we iden
tified the territorial land claim as functioning to exclude non
member communities or other outsiders and secure land for
the 17 member communities. The source of the claim comes
from a legal title recognized by the Ecuadorian government
making it a de jure land claim. The territorial land claim can
theoretically be enforced through formal means although
territorial leaders may utilize local processes similar to those
used to enforce de facto rights. The community land claims
function to exclude other communities and secure and defend
land for member households. The source of these claims are
informal and are thus de facto land claims that are enforced
through internal processes carried out by elected territorial
leaders. Land claims at the household level function to
allocate the specific bundle of rights among individual
households in the community. Households in Rukullakta are
classified as owners according to the Ostrom and Schlager
 framework since they hold all five of the outlined
property rights. These rights are informal making them de
facto rights that are enforced by elected community leaders
through internal processes. The broader institutional setting in
which Rukullakta is embedded also plays an important role in
the land tenure system. The formal legal title at the territory
level comes from the state and holds authority in helping to
resolve land disputes even when pursued through de facto
channels. The GTZ also played an important role in resolving
a dispute between Rukullakta and a neighboring Kichwa
territory by providing the technical capacity necessary to
situate the disputed area within the official legal descriptions
provided by the state titles. These external actors and institu
tions interact with processes internal to the territory to create a
functioning land tenure system (Table 5).
Ultimately, scale plays an important role in understanding
indigenous land tenure in the Amazon. Explicitly recognizing
the multi-scale nature of indigenous land tenure through the
adapted framework will enable scholars to better understand
how common property systems function. In turn, these insights
will advance our understanding of the diversity the governance
arrangements prevailing in these lands and can inform the
development of policies to promote sustainable management
. In our example, the four scales of
analysis collectively provide a full picture of a coherent and
functional land tenure system. Similar multi-scale analyses may
provide a more complete understanding of indigenous land
tenure in other contexts in the Amazon and beyond.

 Cooperatives and socially responsible corporations are being hailed as possible correc
 tives to the socioeconomic and ecological exploitation of transnational capitalism.
 AmazonCoop?a cooperative linking indigenous Brazil nut harvesters and the multina
 tional firm The Body Shop through trade and development projects?capitalized on indig
 enous symbolism to generate significant material benefits for both parties. At the same
 time, however, it made indigenous people more vulnerable and dependent Jailed to promote
 participatory development, masked the effects of unfavorable state policies, and perpetuated
 discriminatory distinctions among indigenous people. Furthermore, the cooperative did not

 tity politics or transform symbolic capital into enduring political-economic change. This

 organizations and economic activities oriented around the interests of tradi
 tionally marginalized people? How do cooperatives promote a form of eco
 nomic integration that respects cultural identity and autonomy?
 In June of 2005, my research partner and I traveled to Altamira, in the lower
 Brazilian Amazon, to study a cooperative that was reportedly harnessing the
 political and economic potential of globalization and responsible business to
 benefit its indigenous members. AmazonCoop, founded in 1998 by representa
 tives of the Fundagao Nacional do Indio (Brazilian National Indian Agency?
 FUNAI) and the UK-based cosmetics company The Body Shop, formalized
 trade links between indigenous Brazil nut harvesters and the multinational
 firm. The cooperative was intended to supply The Body Shop with the raw
 materials and ecologically-sustainable/socially-just symbolism needed for its
 best-selling products, to help FUNAI protect and serve indigenous people
 despite neoliberal reforms that decreased funding and restricted their respon
 sibilities, to allow indigenous people to finance their own community develop
 ment, and to protect the environment by offering tribes an income-generating
 alternative to alliances with mining and logging companies.
 A nonindigenous FUNAI administrator and cofounder of the cooperative
 shared the following perspective on how AmazonCoop contributed to the
 broader project of empowering indigenous people while conserving their
 identity:
 Our society no longer allows Indians to stay in their culture. The Indians need to
 be prepared to compete in our society, while respecting their own culture.
 FUNAI needs to prepare them to compete in our society, to walk their own
 paths. The idea is not that they change to become part of our culture, but that
 they learn to defend themselves in the city. To learn how to do business.
 AmazonCoop was thus a key element in the "correct training" of indigenous
 people . By participating in the cooperative, indigenous peo
 ple were meant to develop "the capacity to develop themselves" and to
 decrease their dependence on outsiders through a "modernized" indigeneity
 that permits intercultural economic integration.
 With its heady blend of justice, environmentalism, indigenous empower
 ment, and the exotic, the Body Shop-AmazonCoop alliance seemed like a
 perfect partnership. AmazonCoop therefore provides an ideal case for study
 ing claims that economically, socially, and politically marginalized groups can
 benefit from economic globalization and that corporate-cooperative partner
 ships promote fair globalization. The FUNAI administrator's statements,
 however, illustrate the many tensions within the cooperative. AmazonCoop
 incorporates not only tensions between social and economic goals, dependency
 and self-sufficiency, and democratic self-governance and external control but
 also tensions related to sociocultural change and identity. As Vasquez-Leon
 suggests in her introduction to this set of articles, the way in which a coopera
 tive balances these tensions significantly shapes whether it contributes to fair
 globalization, becomes an agent of change, or reinscribes historical inequali
 ties and patterns of exploitation.
 In this paper, I discuss the history of AmazonCoop, its economic and social
 activities, and our research among the Asurini, one of the cooperative's eight

All use subject to https://about.jstor.org/terms 32 LATIN AMERICAN PERSPECTIVES
 member tribes, as a basis for evaluating the impacts of this alliance. While the
 AmazonCoop-Body Shop partnership provided significant material benefits
 to indigenous Brazil nut harvesters, it also made indigenous people more
 vulnerable and more dependent on outsiders, failed to include indigenous
 people as informed participants in their own development, masked the
 effects of unfavorable state policies, and perpetuated discriminatory distinc
 tions among indigenous people. One of the unique features of this coopera
 tive was its use of romanticized indigenous symbolism to generate income
 and its connections to identity politics. I therefore discuss the dangers inher
 ent in international indigenous identity politics and possible strategies for
 ameliorating these dangers and converting momentary political openings
 into enduring alliances or achievements. I conclude by considering what
 AmazonCoop teaches us about corporate-cooperative partnerships and fair
 globalization.
 CORPORATIONS, ALTERNATIVE TRADE, AND COOPERATIVES
 Many commentators describe economic globalization as a corporate-driven
 process valuing profits over the livelihoods of increasingly vulnerable workers
 and farmers and as a threat to environments, public health, democracy, and cul
 tural diversity . While some critics reject global
 ization as inherently unjust, others hope for a reformed "just world economy"
 . The Fair Trade and corporate social responsibility
 movements exemplify efforts to make global markets work for socioeconomi
 cally marginalized groups  and underscore the
 power of consumers seeking products with more transparent and ethical "social
 lives" .
 explains her vision of socially responsible trade:
 "Trade Not Aid" is a way of trading honorably with indigenous communities in
 disadvantaged areas?not changing the environment or the culture. Instead, we
 listen to what these people need and try to help them with it. What we bring
 back with us are stories?how they do things, the connections; the essential wis
 dom of indigenous groups. Stories are the soul of The Body Shop. Customers
 come into The Body Shop to buy hair conditioner and find a story about the
 Xingu reserve and the Kayapo Indians who collect Brazil nuts for us. We showed
 them a simple process for extracting oil from the nut, which consequently raises
 the value of the raw ingredients we use. The result is we pay them more for it,
 and that gives them an alternative to their logging income, which in turn pro
 tects the rain forest. That is what we mean by helping through "Trade Not Aid."
 While some corporate programs have successfully addressed environmen
 tal and social issues, skepticism is reasonable. Corporations' control over
 information?the "stories" that Roddick describes?hides actual socio-ecological
 practices, particularly in programs that are not independently certified. Kaplan
  describes how The Body Shop's alternative trade program only appears
 to reveal the true social lives of commodities. Bryant and Goodman

 elaborate that, often, the commodity fetish is merely reworked to create more
 palatable narratives rather than fundamentally different commodities. As
 a result, consumers' pocketbook activism is self-limiting; they "'tune in but
 drop out' of both conventional global economies and more demanding forms
 of resistance to social injustice and environmental degradation" .
 Alongside the movement for corporate responsibility, cooperatives have
 also been hailed as an antidote to the abuses of transnational capitalism, as
 suggested by the theme of International Co-operative Day 2004: "Co-operatives
 for Fair Globalisation: Creating Opportunities for AH" . Cooperatives enjoy broad support because they have
 generated modest development gains, accommodate diverse ideological per
 spectives, and ideally avoid the charges that other nongovernmental organi
 zations (NGOs) have faced of being undemocratic. This grants them a special
 position in a context in which a participatory and inclusive development pro
 cess is valued as much as measurable development outcomes. For these rea
 sons, Brazilian Agriculture Minister Roberto Rodrigues celebrated cooperatives
 in a 2004 speech as "the third way that combines all of the advantages of
 capitalism and socialism."
 What does the third way offer? Hilde Frafjord Johnson , Norway's
 Minister of International Development, describes the panacea:
 Cooperatives can play an important role in our common efforts to fight poverty
 and promote democracy. In an increasingly globalized world . . . cooperative
 organizations are more needed than ever, as a balance to corporate power and as
 an anchor to the grassroots level of society. Cooperatives hold the potential of
 being a driving force ... in the developing world.. . . Cooperatives can provide
 a much needed opportunity for self-determination and empowerment. And we
 need opportunities like this in order to reach our goal of lifting millions out of
 poverty.
 The cooperatives that the ICA, Rodrigues, Johnson, and others promote are
 far from Fals Borda's  ideal of cooperatives as "active peasant
 organizations that. . . challenge the status quo." Through public-private part
 nerships, Fair Trade, and niche market production, they typically further
 integrate marginalized people into global economic systems and regimes of
 governance rather than confronting those systems or creating protected
 spaces apart from them. Much of the recent trade literature on cooperatives
 takes for granted their reformist role, providing guidance for professionalized
 managers negotiating international markets . As with Fair Trade, the social goals of cooperatives are valued
 primarily because they fetch a higher market price.
 Our research on AmazonCoop sought to delve beyond the familiar narra
 tives of socially responsible businesses, alternative trade, and cooperatives.
 We selected AmazonCoop as a case study because its partnership with The
 Body Shop could provide a model for other cooperatives. Furthermore, as an
 indigenous cooperative, it promised to include some of Brazil's most margin
 alized people , a feat that cooperatives have
 often failed to accomplish.

 AmazonCoop is located in Altamira, Para, in the lower Amazon basin, an
 area that government scout Curt Nimuendaju  once called
 "[possibly] the most inaccessible area in all of Brazil" (Figure l).1 At the time
 of our research, the cooperative served approximately 2,000 indigenous
 people living in 14 villages and representing eight tribes: the Arara, Arawete,
 Asurini, Kayapo, Parakana, Xikrin, Curuaia, and Xypaia.2 Member villages
 are widely dispersed; river travel between the two most distant ones
 requires up to five days. Cooperative membership is organized at the village
 level, with one or two representatives from each village granted the mem
 bers' right to vote.
