 including protection of the country's diverse protected area system,
 support for renewable energy generation, and social programs for
 Amazonian communities . Although such certificates
 would not be recognized under the Kyoto Protocol, Ecuador hopes
 they will be recognized under the new climate agreement currently
 being negotiated within the United Nations Framework Conven
 tion on Climate Change. Currently, only actions to reduce emis
 sions from deforestation and degradation stand a good chance of
 being recognized by a new climate agreement . Ecuador is pushing the novel concept that not
 developing fossil fuels should also be recognized as a valid tool to
 address climate change.
 Ecuadorian proposal. Spain has also contributed to the effort,
 and other countries such as Italy, France, and Hungary have voiced
 support, as have the Organization of American States, IUCN,
 and OPEC.
 The Yasuni-ITT Initiative has not, however, won universal
 praise. The fact that Ecuador is offering to protect a national park
 that should already be immune to oil development projects has
 caused critics to question Ecuador's commitment to environmental
 protection. It is important to keep in mind, however, that since
 discovery of these oil fields in the early 1990s, successive Ecuador
 ian Administrations have been planning for their development.
 President Correa's offer, therefore, should be viewed as a crucial
 turning point in the long-standing drive to drill in Yasuni. The i am
 portance of the initiative is also highlighted by the fact that,
 although the new Constitution made strides toward actually pro
 tecting protected areas, the strategic importance of the ITT oil fields
 would likely be enough to invoke the national interest loophole
 required to drill in Yasuni. Oil revenues contribute over one-third
 of Ecuador's annual federal budget and the ITT oil fields account
 for 20 percent of the nation's total crude oil reserves. President Co
 rrea has repeatedly stated that drilling plans will move forward if the
 initiative is not successful. Making the protection of the park
 economically attractive by recovering a portion of the foregone oil
 revenue would increase the chances of protecting Yasuni in the long
 term.
 The justification of such a costly initiative for a relatively small
 land area has also been questioned. One must consider, however,
 that the ITT block is adjacent to the northeast section of the Zona
 Intangible. Thus, if successful, this initiative would actually be part
 of a much larger area permanently off-limits to oil development in
 the heart of the Ecuadorian Amazon (Fig. IB). Smaller oil reserves
 just to the west of ITT (Block 31) would likely not be profitable
 enough to exploit on their own, therefore preventing ITT drilling
 could protect an even larger area.
 Several technical concerns regarding the Yasuni-ITT Initiative
 remain as well, particularly in relation to the new strategy of fi
 nancing this effort with carbon credits. While this approach offers
 financial prospects of a greater scale than other alternatives, it is also
 the most challenging from the point of view of environmental in
 tegrity. First, global CO2 emissions may not actually decrease since
 oil supply could simply be displaced to another location, a phe
 nomenon known as 'leakage.' Ecuador has responded by arguing
 that although there may be short-term leakage, oil resources are fi
 nite and nonrenewable. Therefore, the initiative in effect reduces
 the amount of oil available in the long-term?and by extension, the
 amount of C02 emitted . In addition, an offset is a
 flexible and cost-effective mechanism for a regulated emitter to
 meet its emissions obligations. Offsets do not result in absolute
 global C02 reductions. Instead, they allow regulated entities to
 'purchase' reductions instead of reducing emissions themselves. In
 this case, buyers of the Yasuni certificates would be entitled to emit
 the equivalent amount of C02 instead of lowering their own emis
 sions. Ecuador responds that only governments?not companies?
 will be allowed to purchase the certificates, enabling these govern
 ments to decide whether and how to sell the credits to companies.
 Governments could decide not to put all Yasuni certificates on the
 market, thereby generating real reductions since no entity would
 emit carbon in exchange for the certificates withheld. Alternatively,
 supporters could contribute financially to the initiative in the form
 of a donation without claiming carbon credits in return. This is the
 path that the German government seems to be taking. Such volun
 tary contributions?which could come from national governments,
 multilateral institutions, civil society organizations, companies,
 and citizens?by not generating credits pose a decisively lower risk
 in terms of environmental integrity, but are not as promising
 financially. Another technical concern is that if other major oil
 and coal producing nations replicate this strategy, there may be a
 dramatic increase in the amount of carbon credits available
 internationally. Ecuador's proposed solution is to restrict this
 type of initiative to mega-diverse, developing countries .

 are proposing an international Trust Fund managed by an inter

 uador, civil society, and representatives of major contributors

 The total financial ask has oscillated between three and seven bil
 lion dollars, based on estimates of the economic value of the oil or

 Like all bold new concepts, the Yasuni-ITT Initiative has both its
 enthusiastic supporters and ardent skeptics. We conclude that the
 Yasuni-ITT Initiative, with its focus on generating alternative sources
 of revenue, is a potentially precedent-setting advance towards avoid
 ing oil and gas development in sensitive areas of mega-diverse devel
 oping countries. For example, several large oil fields have been
 discovered in a remote section of the northern Peruvian Amazon
 and major hydrocarbon reserves are expected to lie under Bolivia's
 renowned Madidi National Park . This initiative
 also represents an innovative way to address climate change. Protect
 ing Yasuni National Park would serve a triple climate-related func
 tion: it could lock a substantial amount of C02 underground, avoid
 deforestation, and maintain a potentially important refuge for Am
 azonian species negatively affected by increased drought conditions
 in the eastern Amazon .
 Other innovative policy instruments, such as debt-for-nature
 swaps, cap-and-trade systems, and compensation for avoided defor
 estation, have been proposed, vetted, adjusted, and then imple
 mented. For its precedent-setting potential to both combat climate
 change and protect biodiversity and indigenous peoples, Ecuador's
 proposal deserves our attention and our resolve.

 College of William & Mary
 ABSTRACT: Successful titling of indigenous lands in the Brazilian
 Amazon is a result of governmental reforms in response to political
 pressures during the 1970s and 1980s. Under the neoliberal regime
 of environmental governance that has emerged since the mid
 1990s, the politics of opposition that led to reforms has morphed
 into a policy aimed at producing "sustainable development"
 through the spatial segregation of peoples and forms of produc
 tion. Although spatial reorganization is part of a national strategy,
 within the conservation sector this strategy is based on a model
 of autarkic production within territorial enclaves by indigenous
 managers of natural resources whose activity as "workers" and
 producers has largely been downplayed. This model is at odds
 with an understanding of pre-Columbian and frontier patterns
 of indigenous social reproduction. A burgeoning NGO sector has
 sprung up dedicated to fomenting short-term projects, and some
 conservationists seek to convert existing forest ecosystems into
 financial investments through REDD programs. It is arguable that
 indigenous communities will rely on government cash transfers,
 welfare benefits, and NGO or government-mediated projects while

 . Today the great expansion of in

 the past, hold the promise of making the reservation situation

 . Van Cott  has regarded new forms

 and Hecht  have regarded new forms of territoriality and

 He argues, correctly in my view, that analyses of territorial

 The Brazilian constitution of 1988 guaranteed unprecedent
 ed recognition of indigenous peoples' rights to their culture,
 languages, land, and resources . These rights
 are understood to belong collectively to the group and derive
 from occupation of Brazilian territory prior to the founding of
 the state. ILs are clearly defined: lands traditionally occupied
 by Indians are those on which they live on a permanent basis,
 those used for productive activities, those indispensible to the
 preservation of the environmental resources necessary for their

 according to their uses, customs and traditions" .
 Rights of original occupants do not rely on state recognition, but
 the constitution obligated the state to promote their tenancy and
 demarcate all "traditionally indigenous lands" prior to a 1993
 deadline . Although this deadline has not been
 met, over the past two and a half decades the Brazilian Ama
 zon has seen a tremendous expansion of conservation areas,
 including ILs. Almost 47% of this region, encompassing 59%
 of Brazil's surface area, enjoys protected status, including ILs,
 which constitute about 21.3% of the Legal Amazon . In some cases ILs overlap areas devoted to
 natural conservation. Although the right to exploit resources
 within ILs, including the soil, is a collective right held exclu
 sively by the group, it is also the case that the Brazilian state
 has exclusive power to authorize or license rights to use water
 and mineral resources. The constitution indicates conditioning
 mechanisms so that the legal exploitation of mineral resources
 may occur if Indian rights to consultation are observed and if
 the community receives benefits from the mining on their lands.
 Paragraph 3 of Article 231 specifies that "Hydric resources,
 including energetic potentials, may only be exploited, and
 mineral riches in indigenous lands may only be prospected
 and mined with the authorization of the National Congress,
 after hearing the communities involved, and the participation

 During the 1980s a combination of factors, including a de
 cade-long debt crisis and economic slow-down, an extended
 painful transition to democratic rule, the less than impressive
 economic results of many Amazonian initiatives and the eco
 logical and social catastrophes left in the wake of deforestation
 forced authorities to reconsider their objectives in the Amazon.
 With an opening of political space, organizations of indigenous
 peoples and their allies became more forceful in national and
 international human rights efforts. The debt crisis, in its turn,
 compelled Brazil and many other nations to subject themselves
 to externally imposed structural adjustment policies in return
 for International Monetary Fund and World Bank financing.
 Eventually, the impact of IMF and World Bank-funded projects
 on poor and indigenous peoples became an effective focus of
 political pressure. Advocates of indigenous rights and inter
 nal procedures of the institutions themselves linked project
 funding to the existence of plans to mitigate impacts on the
 environment and local peoples, although the results were and
 continue to be mixed .

 "The text employs a nuanced anthropological lens that acknowledges the complexities of indigenous communities' interactions with their environments and external actors."
 'The text employs a nuanced and descriptive approach to discussing Indigenous peoples and cultures, recognizing their agency and complexity within the context of environmental management, development, and social movements.'
 'The text employs a nuanced and empowering lens that recognizes the agency and complexity of Indigenous cultures in the Amazonian region.'

 'The text employs a nuanced lens that recognizes the complexity of indigenous cultures and their relationship with the environment, while also highlighting the tensions between market-driven conservation efforts and indigenous autonomy.'
 'The text employs a critical sociological lens that examines the complex relationships between indigenous peoples, territorial governance, and external factors such as NGOs and government policies.'
 'The text employs a critical sociopolitical lens that examines the complex relationships between indigenous communities, environmental conservation, and economic development in Brazil.'

Fonseca-Cepeda, V., C. J. Idrobo, and S. Restrepo. 2019. The changing chagras: traditional ecological knowledge transformations in
the Colombian Amazon. Ecology and Society 24(1):8. https://doi.org/10.5751/ES-10416-240108
Research, part of a Special Feature on Seeking sustainable pathways for land use in Latin America
The changing chagras: traditional ecological knowledge transformations in
the Colombian Amazon
Valentina Fonseca-Cepeda1
, C. Julián Idrobo2 and Sebastián Restrepo3
ABSTRACT. Shifting agriculture systems in the Colombian Amazon, locally known as chagras, have been traditionally managed by
indigenous peoples following their traditional ecological knowledge (TEK). However, different socioeconomic drivers of change are
affecting indigenous chagra TEK, resulting in changes in practices and land-use patterns. This study examines TEK transformations
from 1970 to 2016 and their relation to rainforest management in the Ticuna indigenous resguardo of El Vergel (Leticia Municipality,
Amazonas Department). It employs an ethnographic case study design that articulates quantitative data on land-use variables related
to chagras and qualitative ethnographic data describing dimensions of TEK and its perceived transformations, including knowledge
of the environment, practices and management systems, social institutions, and worldviews. Our findings reveal that TEK
transformations entail changes in land-use, including size of production area, temporality of land-use, and cultivated diversity. This
study contributes to a reinterpretation of TEK transformations and emphasizes the importance of the chagra as an adaptive system.
The TEK transformations related to chagras imply a constant reattunement of relations that bind people and their environments.
Rather than being frozen in an ethnographic past, people have responded to social and economic drivers to meet their current needs
and aspirations. Likewise, understanding TEK transformations and their relation to changes in land-use practices provides relevant
insights about social-ecological dynamics in the Amazon rainforest to navigate change and provide the basis for a discussion of how
to enrich management decisions to move toward sustainability in tropical forests.
Key Words: Amazon rainforest; chagra; Colombia; indigenous people; land use; traditional ecological knowledge

In the last sixty years, we have experienced ecological
transformations of unprecedented scale, rate, and intensity
around the globe . Land-use changes have altered the
structure and functioning of ecosystems, including their ability
to provide key services for human well-being . In the
tropics, the expansion of the capitalist agricultural frontier, road
development, and timber extraction have led to widespread
deforestation, and habitat and biodiversity loss . During the 1990s, natural forests
decreased by 16.1 million hectares per year at the global level and
by 15.2 million hectares in tropical regions .
In turn, these changes are associated with the loss of natural
resource-based livelihoods, affecting local peoples' social and
cultural capital , in which indigenous
peoples have been particularly affected because of their close
relationship with their local environment.

This study deals with TEK from a constructivist perspective that

Within this framing, TEK is a body of knowledge composed of
four levels that articulate a knowledge-practice-belief complex
that sustains forest management. First, there is the local
knowledge of biodiversity, soils, and landscape. In the context of
the chagra, this level includes knowledge of ecological and
biophysical features' taxonomy, life cycles of plants and animals,
and nutritional and medicinal properties of cultivated diversity.
At the second level, there is a resource management system that
includes a set of productive practices and techniques that require
an understanding of ecological processes, such as forest
succession patterns, flood pulses, and moon phases. The third
level includes sets of social institutions and rules-in-use that
determine gender roles, taboos, and otherwise regulate resources
use and access. Finally, the fourth level encompasses worldview
and environmental perception, as well as the multiple ways in
which human's position themselves in relation to the environment
and other beings. For the Ticuna, the chagra represents the
management of the territory, the relations between spiritual and
material dimensions, and the place where the memories of their
ancestors are expressed.

We conducted this study at El Vergel Ticuna Indigenous Territory
(Resguardo El Vergel in Spanish) located on the bank of the
Amazon River, 54 km northwest of Leticia, the capital of the
Amazonas Department, Colombia (Fig. 2). El Vergel has an area
of 2325 hectares and a population of 396, grouped in 40
households. With an average temperature of 26 ºC and an average
annual precipitation of 3193 mm, El Vergel is a lowland tropical
humid forest . In 1970, a few families that migrated
from Brazil to the north side of the Amazon River in the Colombia
territory, settled at El Vergel. The Vergel Resguardo is currently
held under a common property title granted in 1983 as part of a
national political process aimed at recognizing indigenous
peoples' rights over their ancestral lands .

qualitative data related to TEK changes in chagra use over time,
and key historical events research that participants associated
with those changes. After consulting with indigenous authorities
and owners of each chagra, Fonseca collected data during August
and September 2016 (Table 1). We analyzed TEK transformations
related to structural and functional attributes of the chagra at

In the first phase, we assessed the social and ecological attributes
of six chagras belonging to six households. Although families can
own more than one chagra at a time, we sampled one chagra per
family to include more households in our study. We gathered data
on variables related to land use, such as cultivation area, time of
production, fallow length, and cultivated diversity. To see changes
in the role of the chagra in household livelihoods, we considered
the uses and destinations of cultivated species and landraces.
Direct observations and semistructured in-depth interviews with
household members (male and female household heads, for a total
of 12 interviewees) provided the bulk of the data. We collected
corresponding information during visits to plots and drew
freehand maps of each cultivated space .
Based on the preliminary data, we asked interviewees about
changes in land use during their living memory. In this way, we
established differences in the size of the households' cultivation
areas and the changes in composition of the chagras between 1970
and 2016. 
The second phase focused on TEK associated with chagras,
including local knowledge of ecosystems, land and resource
management systems, social institutions, and worldviews . Participant observation on daily journeys with research
participants to their chagras was a fundamental tool for the
collection of qualitative data . Semistructured
interviews with elders addressing each level of TEK were used to
triangulate and enrich data gathered through participant
observation. 
In the third phase, we compiled local perceptions of recent change
related to chagras and complemented local narratives through a
review of national archives and reports from local villagers
. Then, we verified this information in a
timeline workshop , in which a focus group
of six people identified critical moments in the history of the
community associated with SE drivers of change between 1970
and 2016. Finally, we assessed the implications of those moments
on land-use change, considering the social-ecological land-use

As many indigenous settlements in Amazon region, El Vergel tells
a dynamic history of indigenous movements and migrations.
From the 18th century until the 1970s, slavery, evangelization,

owned the area. Those who arrived had employed shifting

and opened new chagras as soon as they arrived at El Vergel. The
newly arrived Ticuna were required to provide labor on the

The first decade of the 21st century saw the implementation of
cash transfer payments and subsidy programs (i.e., Familias
Guardabosque, Familias en Acción, Modalidad Familiar). Besides
medicine and education, they introduced a basic basket of
imported foodstuffs that weakened the relationship between the
chagra and the dietary needs of the community. After these
interventions, people spent less time producing their food and the
cultivated diversity reduced. Tobacco (Nicotiana sp.), huito
(Genipa americana), achiote (Bixa orellana), and some manioc
landraces (Manihot esculenta) are not planted anymore (TEK
level 1; see Appendix 1). Along with these changes, production
lost cultural significance, became less frequent, and the cultivation
areas became smaller and their composition became more
homogeneous (TEK level 2; Table 3). 
Since the beginning of the 21st century, the growth of the tourism
economy and the integration of local economies with regional
markets transformed the chagra's purpose and outcomes from
subsistence to commercial trade (see Appendix 1). Around that
time, the products from the chagra began to be sold to local
restaurants for tourists' consumption and to the families or
neighboring communities that had already stopped farming to
work in tourism-related jobs. The inauguration of the Natura
Park Tourist Reserve in 2012 on the property adjacent to the
Resguardo meant a direct integration with the tourism economy.
This proximity enabled a close working relationship between the
reserve and the Resguardo, which provided local employment but
also affected time spent tending chagras (TEK level 2). To adjust
to the reduced time available for agricultural activities, people
began to accelerate the productive cycle of crops by omitting the
burning phase and reducing fallow length, allowing faster
harvests and so reducing the chagra's production time (Table 3). 
The chagra had a drastic reduction in available labor from other
fronts as well, including formal education programs geared
toward younger Resguardo members and out-migration to urban
centers, such as Leticia. Access to formal education introduced
young people to other knowledge systems and at the same time,
they reduced their participation in the chagras and they became
less familiar with the knowledge associated with agricultural
production (TEK levels 1, 2, and 3; Table 2). However, this did
not provoke the total replacement of the new generations'
participation in productive activities, as affirmed by a farmer: 
because of their homework, but on the weekends they
come help me . 
reconfigure their use of cultivated diversity, productive practices,
and social institutions associated with the chagra (TEK levels 1,
2, and 3), they also had effects on the way that people positioned
themselves in the territory (TEK level 4). Integration with regional
markets changed the way in which people defined their livelihoods
at the local level, for instance, envisioning work outside their
territory, providing tourism services, and conceiving chagras as a
monetized productive space. Thus, the chagra has transitioned
from a reason for living to a means of subsistence. As a farmer
reflected:

consumption, because other labors no longer give time to

Labor-mediated interactions with the landowner, government
cash transfers and subsidy programs, formal education, and
tourism are SE drivers of change that have had direct effects on
Ticuna's TEK, changing land-use patterns in El Vergel. In
response to other time demands and decreased dependence on
locally produced crops, farmers changed their chagras through
land-use intensification strategies that include crop area
reduction, omission of the burning phase before sowing, a
decrease of fallow periods, and less time spent tending the chagra
(Table 3). These are technical solutions developed by the Ticuna
that have allowed them to adapt the chagra to a new regional
socioeconomic reality. Although structure, function, and cultural
significance of the chagra have changed in El Vergel, people
continue to rely on it as the main land-use system in which the
Ticuna still find much of their food and income, where there are
still spaces for family gatherings, and in which TEK finds
opportunities for its creation, recreation, and adaptation.
In El Vergel, SE drivers of change have led to TEK
transformations including changes in land-use patterns through
increased intensification of the productive dimension of the
chagra. In shifting agriculture systems around the globe, we find
similar patterns in which local decision-making processes result
in reductions of cultivated areas and agrobiodiversity, more
intensive productive systems, increases in rural-urban migration,
and livelihood diversification toward non-natural resource-based
activities .

systems are bound to disappear, but other studies have found that
these systems persist in different contexts and continue to be an
important part of local livelihoods, even when they have lost
cultural significance .
cultivable land, the global pattern has been that these productive
systems persist. Thus, despite TEK transformations, the chagra
remains an important component of rural livelihoods. 
Our analysis, however, not only describes how SE drivers are
transforming chagra TEK at multiple levels and the consequences
for land-use systems, but also suggests that inside these processes
of change lies a key component of how local communities adapt
to new realities. Traditional ecological knowledge transformations
are important to understand trajectories of change and contribute
to crafting sustainable land-use pathways in the Amazon region.
Given that chagras are a constantly evolving social-ecological
system, to focus solely on the loss of specific units of knowledge
obscures and underestimates the processes by which human and
environment relations are shaped and reshaped through time and
space. The chagra constitutes a setting in which TEK emerges
from processes through which Ticuna indigenous communities
have learned to live in and from forest ecosystems. Adaptation
and transformation define the way in which social-ecological
memory is related to ecosystem change . Chagra transformations are then
concrete expressions of this dynamic adaptation process to SE
drivers of change. 
Despite the integration of local communities to regional markets,
there is not always a direct relationship between participation in
the market economy and reduction of cultivated diversity. Chagra
transformations represent a chain of events in which drivers shape
actors' autonomy and motivations, and the actions that result in
land-use change . In El Vergel, historical
SE drivers changed local livelihoods, affecting everyday decisions
people made over their lands. Thus, current market integration
has meant a change in chagra composition. Although more
species of commercial use are cultivated, fewer species with
medicinal or ritual purposes are employed. The composition of
the chagra, however, has always changed in response to the
nutritional needs of indigenous peoples and the ecological and
economic contexts in which they live. Chagra diversity has been
known to fluctuate according to the booms and bust of
commercial crops and, in other cases, successful market
participation has been known to subsidize chagra production
. Likewise, the fact that some species stop being
used at a point in time does not preclude their potential future
use . 
In terms of land and resource management systems, our results
contribute to understanding the relation between changes in TEK
and land-use variables of chagras. Working as wage labor either
in commercial agriculture farms or providing services in the
tourism sector has meant less time available to work in the chagra.
Although this is a recurrent pattern in shifting agriculture systems
worldwide and is often linked to people reducing reliance on
locally harvested and products resources , people in El Vergel have not
abandoned this productive activity. Instead, they have developed
strategies to continue producing in the chagra, while diversifying
their livelihoods. These strategies, which imply producing new
knowledge about their social-ecological environments, consist of
changes in land-use patterns such as reduction in cultivated area,
shortening the cultivation cycles by learning to cultivate without
burning, and shorter fallow periods. The intensification of chagra
could be associated with the minimization of land-use changes in
the Amazon rainforest; however, it also could be related to
reduced productivity and soil degradation . More research is needed to
develop a clearer understanding of the long-term effects of these
changes over the productivity of this agricultural system as well
as its conservation implications. 
In terms of institutions and the productive roles related to shifting
agriculture, we found that the new generations move between the
chagra and school, playing important roles in both spaces. Rather
than knowledge loss, we found that children and young people
have been able to integrate academic knowledge as well as
agricultural wisdom from their elders in their everyday lives, even
as the spaces to participate in the chagra are limited. New
generations organize their time and activities to be able to go to
school, but continue to learn agricultural knowledge and skills
from their parents. The information gained through formal
education and the combination of different knowledge systems
increase exposure to drivers of change, but also promote the
ability of people to adapt to them . As long as there
are spaces to participate in shifting agriculture, children and youth
will continue producing new knowledge as they attune their minds
and bodies to the changing chagra . 
At the worldview level, the chagra has changed its meaning as a
way to connect with the territory. Although the symbolic and
spiritual connection has lost relevance, the chagra continues to
bind people and land together through productive and economic
relations. Traditional ecological knowledge transformations
registered in El Vergel's chagras imply a constant reattunement
of relations between people and their environments .
Such relations have not been frozen in an ethnographic past, but
rather have been influenced by an array of economic and political
forces, as well as local desires and aspirations that shape what
people do in their everyday lives and how their livelihoods are
constituted. Moreover, TEK includes processes of creativity and
innovation as individuals navigate the contemporary
environments of their lives, drawing upon their histories and
cultural memories linking the past with possible futures
. 
structure, function, and meaning, chagras continue to be part of
Ticuna everyday life in El Vergel. The transformative capacity of
TEK offers multiple possibilities that allow the chagra system to
persist. The chagra remains not only a repository but also a
ground for knowledge production, both of which are key for
developing sustainable trajectories needed for the resilience of
indigenous peoples and their land-use systems .
Thus, as a land-use system and social-ecological unit, the chagra
is an important vehicle for innovation and the creation of
knowledge. It also offers an empirical lens to see how indigenous
peoples' knowledge systems and ways of life evolve in changing

All use subject to https://about.jstor.org/termsEcology and Society 24(1): 8
https://www.ecologyandsociety.org/vol24/iss1/art8/
contexts. Changes in the chagra and related TEK should be seen
as expressions of knowledge creation and transformation instead
of as a loss of ecological knowledge .
Our case study contributes to policy design around sustainable
pathways for indigenous territories in the Amazon region and
brings in key elements of how local transformations allow
sustainable land-uses in changing environments. Strategies to
accelerate production cycles have been developed in El Vergel to
balance the time demands of wage labor and other activities,
without letting the chagra disappear. Innovation and learning
have been key for chagras to remain active and as an important
part of the local land-use system. Thus, policies for sustainability
transformations in the Amazon forest must promote TEK's
transformative capability, as well as create and maintain the
adaptive capability of local communities . 
Governmental agencies, NGOs, environmental authorities, and
other stakeholders must give TEK the chance to change, generate,
and regenerate, as well as sustain innovation and the ability of
local communities to adapt their knowledge to navigate change
and to maintain their culture and livelihoods. Territorial planning
and conservation initiatives for a more sustainable future of the
Amazon, in which social-ecological relations between indigenous
peoples and the rainforest are sustained, requires the development
of opportunities and spaces for TEK to be conserved,
transformed, and created. Such scenarios must retain valuable
land-use systems, like the chagra, that support the Ticuna of El
Vergel and other indigenous peoples' capacity to navigate change
while sustaining themselves and retaining control over their
territories.

Ecotourism in Amazonian Peru: uniting tourism, conservation and community development
Jennifer L. Hill and Ross A. Hill
ABSTRACT: With reference to two ecotourism
enterprises that operate within Tambopata, Peru,
this article evaluates key principles necessary to
enable the successful achievement of ecotourism in
a little-developed tropical forest region. In so doing,
it highlights the intricacies of the relationship
between ecotourism, environmental conservation
and local community development. Principles are
identified as i) empowering communities by
integrating them in an ecotourism venture; ii)
exchanging knowledge between a community and
tour operator; iii) managing forest resources jointly
between a community and tour operator; iv)
minimising local economic leakage; v) educating
tourists through interpretive programmes; and vi)
minimising environmental and wildlife disturbance.
tourism enterprises are consciously helping to
protect the rainforest of Tambopata, while meeting
the socio-economic needs of the local communities.
Ecotourism has been defined as 'environmentally
responsible travel to natural areas which
conserves the environment and improves the wellbeing of local people' . Ecotourism should
involve local people, feed economic profit into local
environmental protection, and contribute to the
maintenance of local species diversity by
minimising visitor impact and promoting tourist
education. The challenge is to accommodate
increasing numbers of visitors seeking an
intrinsically environmental tourism experience,
while minimising the costs and enhancing the
benefits associated with natural area tourism . As such,
ecotourism is being promoted by governments and
the tourism industry alike as a sustainable
alternative to mass tourism, despite criticisms that
it can be just as damaging to the natural
environment and local cultures .
Peru is the third largest country in South America, comprising three distinct physical regions: the western desert coast, the central mountainous inter-Andean region, and the eastern lowland tropical forest which occupies the upper reaches of the Amazon River . Here, we investigate two ecotourism enterprises operating within the Department of Madre de Dios in south-eastern Amazonian Peru. We evaluate key principles necessary to enable successful achievement of ecotourism in a little-developed tropical forest region and thus highlight the intricacies of the relationship between ecotourism, environmental conservation and local community development.
South-eastern Peru is a hotspot of biological diversity and this is reflected in its status as one of the most protected regions in Amazonia . This article makes reference to the
This content downloaded from 132.174.254.116 on Tue, 17 Mar 2026 02:34:38 UTC All use subject to https://about.jstor.org/terms
© Geography 2011
Geography Vol 96 Part 2 Summer 2011

Tambopata National Reserve (TNR), created in 2000 with an area of 274,690ha, and the Bahuaja Sonene National Park (BSNP), first created in 1996 and subsequently extended in 2000 to an area of 1.1 million ha (Figure 1). Unlike National Park status, the National Reserve designation officially permits sustainable use of forest resources into the future . The TNR and BSNP together support 1300 bird species, 200 mammal species and approximately 10,000 plant species (INRENA, undated). The key attractions for tourists include relatively abundant populations of monkeys, macaws, giant river otters and harpy eagles.
In 2006 over 40,000 visitors passed through Puerto Maldonado on their way to the Tambopata rainforest . While the key motive for visiting the area is to experience an
exotic location relatively close to Cusco, tourists have also expressed an interest in learning about the forest ecosystem and its conservation . Increasing numbers of visitors to the region have prompted a rise in the number of eco-lodges along the Madre de Dios and Tambopata rivers: from 14 in 1998 to 37 by 2007  (Figure 1).
Two ecotourism enterprises are examined here: Inkaterra, a Peruvian ecotourism company that has offered ecotourism experiences since the mid-1970s; and Rainforest Expeditions, a private ecotourism company founded in 1992 by two Peruvian conservationists. Inkaterra's mission is to generate profit while simultaneously helping to research and preserve the local ecology as well as aiding the sustainable development of local communities. The company has established a

parallel non-governmental organisation, the INKA
TERRA Association (ITA-NGO), which is funded by
profits gained from ecotourism and which invests in
research, conservation, social development and
education. The company owns and operates
Reserva Amazonica Lodge (formerly known as
Cuzco-Amazonico), which opened in 1976 as the
first tourist installation along the Madre de Dios
River. Rainforest Expeditions combines tourism
with environmental education, research and local
sustainable development to support the
conservation of the areas in which they operate.
The company manages three rainforest lodges: the
Tambopata Research Centre .
Refugio Amazonas Lodge . For the
purpose of this article, only Posada Amazonas
Amazonica Lodge of Inkaterra are examined.
The two enterprises were selected because of their long history in the region as well as their focus on environmental conservation and community development through tourism. There is a notable difference between the enterprises, however, with respect to the extent of community participation. The Posada Amazonas venture is highly participatory, displaying many characteristics of community-based ecotourism . The lodge is owned by the Ese'eja Native Community of Infierno (a mix of native Indians and immigrant peoples) and is operated jointly with
Rainforest Expeditions. Reserva Amazonica, by
contrast, is owned and managed by Inkaterra and
involves local community members as employees
and service providers. This difference is primarily a
result of the geographical locations of the lodges
and their relative accessibility to Puerto Maldonado
(Figure 1). The Reserva Amazonica Lodge is
located on the Madre de Dios River, approximately
13km east-north-east of Puerto Maldonado and
less than 5km north of the TNR. The Posada
River, approximately 25km south-south-west of
Puerto Maldonado. It falls just outside the
protected area of TNR and within the native
community land of Infierno. These differences in
location and participation help to draw out some
distinct issues for consideration with respect to the
achievement of ecotourism.
A case study approach was adopted to investigate ecotourism in context and to provide a detailed source of reference material . Primary data were obtained by participant activity in the ecotour products of the two companies. Field research was carried out for two weeks in April 2006, when the authors undertook a three-day ecotour at Reserva Amazonica Lodge, followed by a five-day ecotour at Posada Amazonas Lodge and the Tambopata Research Centre. The Posada Amazonas Lodge (PAL) is constructed from local materials purchased from the Ese'eja Community and is relatively compact in size to minimise its ecological footprint (c. 1.5ha) (MG personal communication). The 30-room lodge combines traditional indigenous architecture with low-impact modern technology. The 'walls' of rooms facing the forest are open verandahs to allow contact with nature, while those separating rooms incorporate clay to regulate heat naturally (personal observation). The operation of the lodge follows the same principles of resource sustainability as described for RAL above (MG personal communication).

project aimed at conserving macaw populations on the community's lands, assuming that this will be beneficial not only to the breeding success of the birds but also to the long-term success of the lodge (Figure 7). Over the short term, the project provides employment in the form of project assistants and offers small cash rewards for families that agree to host a macaw nest box on their land (roughly US$25 initially plus a further US$25 if one or more chicks fledge). Over the long-term, the project aims to increase the quality and quantity of tourist macaw sightings by increasing macaw reproduction rates .
A number of community projects have been established to promote capacity building and profitable business opportunities (KH personal communication). A computer house has been constructed for primary and secondary students, financed by families who work at PAL and who have children in the schools that will be serviced by the centre. In 2000 a US$50,000 World Bank grant helped to initiate an artisans committee with 25 community residents using local materials to create tourism products .
Income generated from the lodge is invested in the community. In 2000, net profits paid from the lodge to the community were approximately US$15,000 – three-quarters of which was divided among community members and the remainder used for investment in education . By 2007 the figure had risen to US$148,000, again distributed between private improvements in living standards and communal projects (KH personal communication). Positively, most families continue to engage in a variety of economic activities including farming and livestock-tending, thus avoiding becoming totally reliant on a single source of income .

Training in managerial skills is necessary if communities are to accept increasing responsibility for ecotourism ventures in the future . To overcome the challenge of instilling these skills, Rainforest Expeditions employs an adaptive management strategy (learning by doing) in the operation and management of its lodges. Likewise, Inkaterra trains local community members to manage sectors of its activities in order to improve community managerial capacity.
Joint management of natural resources between a community and tour operator can offer a means of utilising resources sustainably. Ceding of authority to a local community and allowing its members to decide how local resources are used, can be a powerful incentive to alter behaviour towards conservation and thereby protection of natural resources. This is witnessed in the Ese'eja Community commitment not to hunt wildlife considered a tourism resource, nor to log forest in areas designated for ecotourism. As one community member noted 'we do not have many

Economically, income generated by tourists to a region should be maintained in place and not lost to outside companies who transmit their wealth to headquarters in distant cities . By offering a full range of tourist services through the companies directly (including employment of local people and using local products) both Rainforest Expeditions and Inkterra reduce such economic leakage. Integration of the communities into lodge operations ensures that the local people gain direct financial investment.

Conscientious ecotour operators adopt four broad codes of socio-ecological and political conduct: indigenous community participation/development (employing and consuming locally, imparting management skills); visitor education (provision of pre-departure guidelines, in situ interpretation); environmental conservation (operating in small groups, minimising visitor environmental impact, avoiding wasteful practices); and minimising economic leakage (employing local people, consuming local products).

Flora Lu, Néstor L. Silva, Krysta Villeda, and Mark Sorensen
Throughout Amazonia, indigenous communities contend with colonization, urbanization, extractive industries, agribusiness, and the environmental degradation that results, including widespread contamination, loss of biodiversity, changed systems of land tenure, and increasing levels of integration with the market. However, the perspectives of most Native Amazonians—the disparate ways that people perceive both risks and resources—have not been interpreted as demonstrative of sociocultural, economic, and demographic diversity. Drawing upon two datasets in the Northern Ecuadorian Amazon, we first present a cross-cultural analysis of how seven different Amazonian communities of four ethnic groups perceive risk. Second, we make the methodological point that risk mapping should be paired with a method we call tenables mapping in order to provide a much more comprehensive understanding of the above-mentioned diversity. Finally, we describe a pilot tenables mapping study conducted among Waorani and Cofán youth in four communities of varying degrees of integration into the market. Taken together, these two methods convey the commonalities and differences of hardship and risk as well as indicators of cultural resilience.
Key words: risk mapping, tenables mapping, Ecuador, Amazon
n January 19, 2012, The Guardian  provocatively asked, "Could Ecuador be the most radical and exciting place on Earth?" Ecuador's President Rafael Correa has denounced capitalism "as a vulgar instrument" whose very essence is "exacerbated individualism, deregulation, competition, and so on," an argument that undergirds his leftist macroeconomic policies . The Correa administration's leftist discourse and plan for social investment—La Revolución Ciudadana (The
Flora Lu is an Associate Professor in Environmental Studies at University of California, Santa Cruz (UCSC). Néstor L. Silva is a doctoral student in the Department of Anthropology at Stanford University. Krysta Villeda is working towards her Master's degree in Latin American Studies at Georgetown University. Mark Sorensen is an Associate Professor in Anthropology at the University of North Carolina, Chapel Hill. The authors gratefully acknowledge support from the National Science Foundation , the UCSC Committee on Research, and the Joel Frankel Memorial Scholarship through the Department of Latin American and Latino Studies at UCSC. They thank their Ecuadorian collaborators (Dayuma Albán, Kati Álvarez, David Hidrobo, Citlali Doljanin, and Ana Oña) and express their deep appreciation to the indigenous communities and federations who participated in the research.
Citizens' Revolution)—are among many shifts in governance that underlie laudatory evaluations of the contemporary Ecuadorian government. These also include the 2008 Constitution (recognizing human rights, rights of nature, plurinationality, and cultural diversity); renegotiations with multinational oil companies (increasing the Ecuadorian government's share from 13 to 87 percent of gross oil revenues); increased direct tax revenues; increased social spending and construction of infrastructure (e.g., health care, education, public housing, wage increases, more public employment); institution of the now-defunct Yasuní-ITT Initiative ; and President Correa's penchant for dressing in indigenous clothing, speaking broken Kichwa, or emphasizing the right of Ecuadorians to sumak kawsay (the good life).
However, Correa government policies have clearly disproportionate effects, "significantly" alleviating urban poverty while negligibly affecting rural communities, especially those predominantly inhabited by Afro-Ecuadorians and indigenous people . In 2001, after more than three decades of oil exploitation, the poverty rate in Ecuador stood at 45 percent, a figure that dropped to 25 percent by 2006. But, provinces in Ecuadorian Amazonia, commonly referred to as the Oriente—where most of the country's oil exploitation occurs—demonstrate a higher poverty rate (66.8 percent) than highland and coastal provinces (43.6 percent and 52.4 percent, respectively). Cancer rates in oil-producing regions are almost triple the national average .

It has become increasingly clear that "[c]ommunities
and ecosystems [in Ecuador] continue to be threatened,
endangered, and ultimately sacrificed" .
Following the 2009 World Social Forum, attended by the

In this paper, our goal is two-fold, both empirical and
methodological. First, we present a cross-cultural analysis of
how various indigenous groups perceive risk, using data collected in 2009 as part of a project co-led by Lu and Sorensen
in seven different Amazonian communities of four ethnic
groups, a discussion which includes the theory, methods,
and results of risk mapping interviews and which highlights
inter- and intra-ethnic differences among the Waorani, Cofán,
Kichwa, and Shuar. Second, highlighting the limitations of
this methodology, namely deficit-based analysis—meaning
drawing conclusions based solely on investigation of deficits
in a study population—we suggest a new means of addressing
these shortcomings by describing a 2012 pilot study by Silva
 among Waorani and Cofán youth in four of the abovementioned communities, located at varying distances from
the Ecuadorian oil complex and the market. Results from the
2009 and 2012 studies suggest that risk mapping should be
paired with tenables mapping, a novel method we developed
in order to provide a much more comprehensive and nuanced
understanding of the lived experience of interviewees, one
that considers both their experience of hardship and their
demonstrations of resilience.
Participatory Risk Mapping (PRM)
Smith, Barrett, and Box  define "risk" as
"uncertain circumstances, and in particular exposure to
potentially unfavorable circumstances, or the possibility
of incurring nontrivial loss....Risk is therefore, by this
definition, something undesirable." It is defined as variable
across individuals , implying
its variability at different scales of social analysis, like the
household, community, and international levels. In an admittedly simplified analysis, Smith and colleagues "identify two
broad approaches to questions of risk." The subjectivist approach focuses on individual, subjective expressions of perceived risk. The objectivist approach focuses on quantitative
measurements and commonly defines "risk" as "imperfect
knowledge with known probabilities of observing possible
outcomes" meaning that risk is "distinct from 'uncertainty,"
for which the probabilities are unknown" . Smith and colleagues choose a subjectivist approach because it "allows the subjects to decide
on their own what are the major risks rather than being
told to choose from a list that is biased to begin with by
the researcher" . They
develop a participatory risk mapping (PRM) methodology
to "describe accurately cross-sectional and inter-temporal
variation in subjects' risk assessment" .
We prefer the subjectivist approach because "asking people what worries them, and which worry is greatest, seems both practical and intuitive" , and because risk—exposure to uncertain circumstances and the possibility of incurring nontrivial loss—is heterogeneous and contextually-specific. This is certainly the case in the study region of the Northern Ecuadorian Amazon, where different indigenous groups face diverse challenges: expansion of the market economy and colonization, Amazonian urbanization, extractive industries, and resulting

Barrett, and Box  suggest that "researchers, donors,
policymakers, and development practitioners who want to
understand and assist specific populations must begin by
acknowledging that not everybody is "in the same boat' with
regards to the hazards they face." The nature and subjective
severity of risk "varies considerably even among what appears to many outsiders a relatively homogeneous population
with regard to economy and environment" , as is often the case with Amazonian
indigenous people. In a study of perception of environmental
risk, numerous scholars "suggest that not all ethnic populations perceive, experience, or respond to risks equivalently"
, which reinforces
the heterogeneity of risk and suggests that privileging what
Smith, Barrett, and Box define as the subjectivist approach
aligns both with scholarship on risk and with the lived experience of the people in the communities where the 2009 and
2012 studies were conducted.
tions, identifying some solutions to risk or types of coping
mechanisms. For example, Baird, Leslie, and McCabe 
used PRM in Maasai agro-pastoralist villages living at different proximities to the border of Tarangire National Park
in Northern Tanzania and found wide variation in mitigation
responses to the livelihood threat posed by conservation.
In some villages, households preemptively converted lands
to agriculture rather than face land-use restrictions or evictions, whereas other villages felt that nothing could be done.
In addition to Baird, Leslie, and McCabe , the PRM
technique has been applied to other East African case studies, such as the agro-pastoralists in other parts of semi-arid
Tanzania , northern Kenya , and southern Ethiopia . However, these cases represent the bulk of
the studies using PRM, and to the best of our knowledge, the
Amazonian groups.
The risk mapping technique is a two-stage system of ordinal rankings where interviewees first identify risks as prompted by open-ended and non-leading questions (as many as they would like), and then rank the risks they identified from most to least severe . After ranking risks, interviewees are asked about mitigating and coping strategies: efforts to avoid risks or minimize their impact once experienced, respectively. The risk map "gauge[s] how widely the risk is perceived by residents (its incidence) along with individual perceptions of how significant a risk is perceived to be relative to other risks (its severity)" . The incidence index of a risk is basically the proportion of all interviewees who named that risk in their interview, with

1.0 signifying that all of them did. The severity of risk is
measured using an index that employs uniform intervals
between ranked risks for a given respondent. For each
respondent, the interval is defined as 1/n_i, where n_i is the
number of risks identified by respondent i. Calculating an
individual risk severity value R_{ij} for risk j of rank r among a
group of n risks is: R_{ij}=1-(r_{ij}-1)^{n}/n_{i} . However, this generates a counter-intuitive graph in
which on the y-axis, severity of the risk runs between 1 and
2, with 1 being the most serious and 2 being the least serious. In a revision, Smith, Barrett, and Box  calculate
the severity index as 2-R_{ij}, which allows for the creation
of more intuitive graphs of PRM data. The y-axis shows a
risk severity closer to zero when a particular risk is deemed
unimportant, closer to 1 when it is of primary importance
(Figure 1). It is important to note a weakness in the PRM
methodology: because n_i is non-constant, the interval steps
between identified risks vary across interviewees .
Ecuador's lowland rainforest, or Oriente, is culturally diverse, home to the Shuar, Achuar, lowland Kichwa, Cofán, Siona, Secoya, Záparo, Shiwiar, Andoa, and Waorani peoples. The 2009 study in the northern Oriente covers the lowland Kichwa, Shuar, Cofán, and Waorani, in Sucumbios, Napo, and Orellana provinces. Detailed ethnographic descriptions of the four ethnic groups are in Lu, Bilsborrow, and Oña . Here, we provide only a brief overview, contextualized through exemplary ethnographic vignettes.
Varied histories among these groups of sustained interaction with Western society—for the Shuar and Cofán coming during Spanish colonization and for the Waorani coming in the late 1950s—underlie their dispersal across the

Oriente and contemporary Ecuador as a whole and underlie their differing population sizes and linguistic roots. These histories include decimation via the Spanish conquest and subsequent projects of natural resource extraction: precious metals, quinine, rubber, timber, and oil. Members of all four indigenous groups who participated in the 2009 study reside primarily along roads and/or waterways and are all involved in market activities—horticulture, wage labor, sale of forest products—to varying degrees. All except the Shuar people reside primarily in the northern Ecuadorian Amazon region. And significantly, six out of seven communities in which the 2009 study was conducted are located within oil concessions .
Nine Ecuadorian ethnographers were placed in seven indigenous communities: three Kichwa (given their larger population size), one Cofán, one Shuar, and two Waorani, for a total of 126 households and 1,004 individuals. In total, 187 risk mapping interviews (Table 1) were completed in the study villages with adults, with a focus on heads of households. Asking interviewees what they perceived to be risks in their life seemed too abstract, so people were asked, in Spanish, what they worry about, using terms like "risk," "concern," and "worry" interchangeably .
Both the Cofán and Kichwa interviewees articulated risks that were assigned to 15 categories; for the Waorani there were 13 categories and for the Shuar there were 12 (see Figures 2, 3, 4, 5). In a demonstration of the vast inter- and intra-ethnic variability among the four indigenous groups in which investigation took place, the vast majority of categories are uncommonly felt, that is to say, named by less than half of interviewees. Heterogeneity of perceptions among these groups will be explored in more detail (i.e., disaggregation of data according to age and gender) in future publications. For the Cofán and Shuar, two categories each were listed as common: economics and general health. Waorani interviewees also listed two categories as common: education and general health. Among Kichwa interviewees,

none of the categories were common, likely a reflection of
the larger sample size of this ethnic group.
Nine risk categories were mentioned by all four of the study groups (see Table 2). One-third of these refer to different aspects of physical well-being (general health, access to medical care, and children's health). General health is a broad category that encompasses specific illnesses, symptoms, or simply the expression of concern for the health of the family and/or community. Cofán interviewees identified a tripartite ontology of health risks arguably shared by the other indigenous groups
who participated in the 2009 study: historically-recognized health risks (e.g., snake bite, food-related illness, accidents), health risk from brujeria (witchcraft), and health risks introduced by nonindigenous people (e.g., tuberculosis, sexually-transmitted infections, alcoholism). Kichwa interviewees expressed what are arguably the most common responses to these risks among all four indigenous groups: medicina de la ciudad (Western medicine), remedios de la selva (traditional medicine), and curanderos (shamans). Risk vis-á-vis access to medical care includes unavailable or prohibitively expensive

medications and lack of nearby medical centers and/or personnel. People consistently and specifically mentioned the health of their children, both in terms of actual patterns of illness and potential concerns. Categories related to physical well-being were almost always commonly felt and always near the border between the severe and mild quadrants.

also provide jobs for each male head of household for some

In addition to these cross-culturally represented categories, another nine categories were less uniformly mentioned.

For all groups except the Shuar, interviewees mentioned that they were concerned about the environment (e.g., contamination, lack of subsistence resources, deforestation). The Shuar do not rely on hunting, and the Waorani are only recently experiencing significant defaunation in their territory. All groups except for the Waorani mentioned concern about drinking water and food security (Waorani territory is the most ecologically intact of all the study groups), although a few Waorani mentioned worry about loss of their culture. The Kichwa and Cofán expressed concerns about lack of transportation infrastructure and diminishing game populations.
Participatory Tenables Mapping (PTM)
The participatory tenables mapping (PTM) methodology delineated in this section is intended as a corollary to the existing PRM methodology described above. In a comparable manner to PRM, PTM systematically assesses tenables in a manner that calculates indices and generates maps, facilitating comparisons between and among study populations. For the purposes of this methodology, the term "tenables" is defined as secure material and symbolic resources. In the case of an Amazonian community, both an outboard motor and perceived access to gasoline could be described as tenables, the motor itself as a material resource, and perceived access to gasoline a symbolic one. As PTM
provides a means of analyzing the perceived resonance and importance of socioeconomic, political, cultural, and environmental resources among interviewees, it contributes to a more complete analysis of perceptions of community capacities and deficiencies than PRM alone. In other words, it avoids the flaws in deficit-based analysis, which threatens to reify the notions of instability, insecurity, and/or inadequacy that underlie concepts of risk .
"participatory asset mapping," which has a distinct history,
means of gathering data, and forms of data presentation
. Despite clear and
significant distinctions, the goals of participatory asset
mapping methodologies and the PTM methodology that is
the focus of this paper are very similar. "Asset mapping is
a process of learning what resources are available in your
community" and includes the "mapping of available skills
and work experience," the identification of natural resource

Overall, Johnson's salient points of consideration in
analyzing risk are reflected in Smith and colleagues' descriptions of risk as a "composite expression of exposure,
perception, mitigation, and coping." As mentioned above,
a PTM approach is not intended as a substitution or an alternate to PRM but as a corollary, and as such, will employ
much of the conceptual framework of PRM, including conceptualizing resource tenability as a composite expression
of access, perception, maximization, and management. The
first two composite parts of participatory risk and tenables
mapping protocol provide quantifiable data. Exposure,
defined as an "objective, measurable component related
to space and time, but not to a particular person," refers to
the number of times that "a given hazard" is perceived by
a specific person . In the
tenables mapping corollary, "exposure to risk" translates
to "access to a resource," a measurable quantity reflecting
temporally and spatially specific incidence of perceived
access to a material or symbolic resource. Perception is
"a subjective component unique to individuals and not
directly observable by a researcher." It "reflects an individual's belief that he or she might experience a particular
hazard and how severe its effects might be." Different
histories, preferences, and/or information can cause different subjective perceptions of identical exposure to a given
risk . In the case of the
PTM methodology, different histories, preferences, and/
or information can cause different subjective perceptions
of identical access to a given resource. For instance, one
munity boundaries: "We want to make boundaries so that
each community has a fixed area of land...a protected area
for the monkeys and other animals so that people do not
hunt them." Another Waorani interviewee eschewed the
idea of community boundaries, saying "We cannot make
limits between communities, this could cause problems
between brothers, family, and in-laws....[A]fter a few days,
I complain and you complain. We start having problems.
This is not good" .
The last two components of risk assessment—mitigation
and coping—"relate to the capacity to reduce the adverse
effects of hazards, either ex ante (mitigation) or ex post (coping)" . In PTM, these two
components necessarily differ. Rather than addressing the
manner in which the "adverse effects of hazards" are reduced,
the PTM methodology focuses on identifying how the utility
of tenables is maximized in an ex ante or ex post fashion.
to as maximization while an ex post effort as management.
The greater an individual's ability to maximize observable
resource utility through premeditated behavior, the greater
their maximization capacity. A person who enjoys the utility
of a resource without exhausting that resource can be said
to demonstrate effective management. An example of such
maximization could be that many Native Amazonian communities in Ecuador participate in the Sociobosque or Partner
Forest program, a Ministry of Environment scheme that pays
communities in cash, market-derived goods, and job training
in exchange for not collecting resources from a portion of
forest within their territory.
Following assertions by Smith and colleagues, high exposure to and perception of resources will manifest as an increased number of resources mentioned in a tenables assessment. Tenables assessments vary in direct correlation with perceived subjective ability to manage and maximize the utility of resources. Hence, an individual with effective maximization and management strategies will likely perceive an abundance of tenables, each with relatively high utility. Someone less equipped with maximization and management strategies will likely perceive tenables at low abundance rates and consider these to have low utility, as the beneficial qualities of unmanaged (and therefore, unmaximized) tenables are quickly exhausted or are otherwise not resilient.

Admittedly, the small sample size (n=19) in this pilot PTM study makes broad generalization of conclusions drawn from this data hasty at best. The results, however, point to potentially intriguing processes that complicate existing notions of indigeneity, market integration, conservation, and development discourses and their impact in the Oriente, especially among a demographic (i.e., youth) who represent the majority of indigenous populations. Most readily apparent in our discussion of the PTM methodology is the need for further field-testing with larger sample size among varied groups.
As the PTM methodology is intended to correlate to PRM, ideal field-testing would consist of administering both protocols during a single interview, in order to elucidate socioeconomically, historically, and geographically specific manifestations of the dialectic between perceived risks and resources. Current results from PRM and PTM reflect the transforming economies and ecologies of the Oriente. The
PRM data, collected from Kichwa, Shuar, Waorani, and Cofán adult heads of households in seven communities, reflect the relative lack of basic resources and services available in these communities, which for many decades have been and continue to be plagued by environmental injustice, lacking basic services and resources while their lands are destroyed by extractive activities that enrich elites in urban centers. In 2009, when these data were collected, these communities demonstrated wide variety in MI levels, yet they all expressed concerns about the lack of income opportunities, health care, and education in their villages.
These worries were not only a reflection of their tenuous and inequitable links with external agents but also signs of internal tensions: people struggled with both their economic and sociocultural reproduction. Conflict, divisiveness, weak governance, and lack of unity further undermine these communities' ability to advocate for themselves vis-á-vis powerful, supra-local actors but also perhaps reflect growing inequality and insecurity between families . Less commonly articulated were perceived risks of cultural loss and environmental degradation, a finding that could be interpreted—if the PRM data was solely used—as indicative of the negative effects of "acculturation."

The fact that indigenous youth perceived value in their ancestral material culture—the reproduction of which depends largely on forest-based natural resources—while perceiving relatively lower incidence and utility of resources like infrastructure (and the education and health care it facilitates) suggests the complexity of contemporary identity formation processes among indigenous youth in the Oriente, and by extension, among coming generations of indigenous people. PTM data also reveals an emerging conflict. Reproduction of ancestral material culture—artesanias, ancestral dress, games, foods, and the broad category of ancestral

customs—requires forest-based resources: organic building materials, medicinal plants, and plants used in producing ancestral artwork. However, the Ecuadorian oil industry is expanding , which suggests increasing levels of MI in Amazonian indigenous communities and increasingly degraded ecologies, which inhibit the valuation and reproduction of ancestral culture. In every community visited during this pilot study, multiple interviewees mentioned forest-based resources, and the products derived from them, to be not only a means of creating economic opportunity but as an integral means of sociocultural reproduction.
A significant drawback to this study is the fact that the 2009 PRM and the 2012 PTM datasets were collected at different points in time among distinct populations. Nonetheless, we argue that for a more complete perspective, PTM should complement PRM, in acknowledgement of the dialectical relationship between risks and resources recognized in the literature. That relationship—which demonstrates the potential significance of applying a combined PTM/PRM methodology to analysis of transforming economies and ecologies of the Oriente—is exemplified in a discussion of health-related risks and resources and the way these are embedded in broader political economic and ecological processes.
Good health requires consideration of a number of different risks and resources mentioned in this study: ecological integrity; nutritional security, which depends on access to non-degraded arable land, also rivers and forests, or otherwise depends on a source of monetary income; health care; and medical infrastructure. Secure access to these, or its absence, is effectively determined by the trajectories of economies and ecologies of the Oriente. These, in turn, are determined by the expanding oil complex that provides only limited socioeconomic opportunities, increases ecological degradation, changes consumption patterns, and provides only limited access to health care via understaffed community health centers and itinerant doctors.
As the oil-as-development model spreads throughout the Oriente, the socioeconomic and political marginalization of indigenous peoples is exacerbated; their de facto ability to access the resources critical to assuring long-term good health diminishes. PRM data for the 2009 study reveals that across all ethnic groups when the subject of health was mentioned—categories for General Health, Access to Medical Care, and Children's Health—although these issues were often considered uncommon risks, their severity was consistently considered to be high. Those communities live at various levels of market integration, meaning both near and far from oil development and the economic and ecological change it catalyzes.
PTM data from 2012 reveals that—in comparison to people in low MI communities—those who live in high MI level communities more regularly perceive access to the oil-as-development model spreading throughout the Oriente. Data from the PRM and PTM studies in conjunction suggest that even communities closest to the Amazonian manifestation
of the Revolución Ciudadana—despite relatively higher perceived access to infrastructure—do not perceive health-related risks to be less severe. Similar arguments could be made for access to infrastructure and perception of severe risk related to education and economic opportunity.
Among the Waorani, infrastructural improvements such as teacher housing, schools, medical centers, and job provision fail to evince consistent and tangible gains. Unreliable teacher presence, substandard educational curricula, limited to nonexistent access to quality health care, and itinerant and menial labor suggest the failure of the Revolución Ciudadana. In an exemplary case, sanitation infrastructure provided to a community quickly fell into disrepair due to lack of maintenance; residents dismantled the structure, taking the wood planks back to their homes, leaving two lonely and exposed—effectively useless—toilets at the edge of the school's playground.

in avoidance of meat between households belonging to estab
lished and Evangelical churches. Furthermore, members of all
church groups deviated in terms of dietary restrictions from
indigenous norms as exemplified in dietary advice given by
shamans. We conclude that, despite doctrinal opposition to
shamanistic practices associated with indigenous taboos, there

and, to some degree, Sabbatarian forms of Christianity.

The approximately 48,000 km2 area is bisected east to west
by the Kanuku Mountains, a
largely forested and uninhab
ited range reaching elevations of slightly more than
1,000 m. Human impacts on the forest environment consist
largely of localized hunting pressure and swidden agricul
ture. Savannah ecosystems are also influenced by occasional
fires (mostly anthropogenic) and by low-intensity cattle
grazing in some areas. At the time the study was conducted,
the Rupununi had been spared high-intensity resource ex
ploitation such as the large-scale timber removal, deforesta
tion and mechanized agriculture that have resulted in greatly
altered ecosystems in other areas of Amazonia.
The Wapishana, speakers of an Arawakan language, live
in the South Rupununi (i.e., south of the Kanuku
Mountains) and the Makushi, members of the Carib linguis
tic group, are the dominant ethnic group in the North
Rupununi. Despite this general spatial segregation, few vil
lages are ethnically homogenous, with representatives of at
least one other indigenous group present .
center of Lethem (approximate population 2,000), which

The present study was part of a larger interdisciplinary effort examining the relationships among economic integration, indigenous culture, hunting and wildlife dynamics in the Guyanese Amazon . The research team, which included ecologists, geographers and anthropologists, as well as trained members of local communities, collected data from 2007 to 2010 in 23 predominantly indigenous communities of the
North and South Rupununi (Fig. 1).2 Due to the importance of a landscape-level perspective on hunting and wildlife dynamics in the overall research design, coupled with the desire of Makushi and Wapishana leaders to participate in the research project, the study was

Household heads generally ranged from 18 to 70 years of age.
When possible, male and female household heads were interviewed together, though only one was surveyed when the

Both wild and domestic forms of meat were considered.
Additionally, technicians recorded hunting activity by residents of their communities through hunting return surveys administered on a weekly basis to all households that hunted during that time period , representing 831 hunters. While some hunts went unrecorded for various reasons (e.g., some hunters not wanting to share information with the technicians), one of the authors (Luzar) visited all communities in the study to verify hunting return data with hunters and local leaders and to identify and resolve problems leading to undercounts when they occurred. Technicians also administered surveys to 235 principal hunters (determined based on hunting records for each household) in 23 study sites. These surveys included information regarding the reasons for using or avoiding certain game species.
Finally, technicians conducted "shaman visit surveys" for visits to shamans and prayer people in six focal communities in which shamans were present (each village having one known shaman and multiple prayer people). While no shamans agreed to be surveyed on a regular basis, three did agree to informal interviews with one of the authors (Luzar), providing additional qualitative context to the quantitative data. These six focal communities represented all but three of the communities in the Rupununi in which shamans were reported to reside.3 During interviews technicians recorded data from all households pertaining to visits to shamans that had occurred during the prior week including information on dietary advice given
by the shaman. Over the three and a half year study period, project scientists and community technicians also collected qualitative data, including key informant interviews about shamanism, food taboos and the history of churches in the region.
For the purposes of the analyses, we divided 15 of the 16 church affiliations present among households included in the study into three categories—"established," "Evangelical" and "Sabbatarian." Given their rarity in the region (n=9), we did not include in the analysis Jehovah's Witness households, nor did we consider those households that claimed no church affiliation (n=5). We also did not consider "mixed" households in which members belonged to more than one church type (n=60), due to the internal heterogeneity of this group (e.g., a "mixed" household might include any proportion of the three church types).
In order to test our first hypothesis regarding church affiliation and adherence to one or more food taboos, we used ARE  to conduct a chi-square test of the relationship between church affiliation and adherence to a meat-based dietary taboo by at least one member of a household. To test the second and third hypotheses regarding patterns of meat avoidance, using Excel we derived meat avoidance profiles for each church type by compiling frequencies of species or categories of meats avoided by one or more members of households in each religious group and for all meats proscribed by a shaman (Fig. 2).
