Andréa Zhouri1
Since the 1970s, the ghost of an "international conspiracy" to occupy the Brazilian 
Amazon has emerged against the backdrop of shifting social and political realities. This 
article looks at how Brazilian political elites and military forces have influenced national 
interpretations of environmentalism and indigenous peoples' rights. It considers the 
formation of long-standing images and ideas perpetuated through economic plans 
like Avança Brasil, Growth Accelerated Program (PAC), and the Initiative for the 
Integration of Regional Infrastructure in South America (IIRSA) as well as international 
environmental initiatives such as on reducing emissions from deforestation and forest 
degradation in developing countries (REDD). Environmentalists and indigenous peoples 
have been viewed as opponents to economic growth and barriers to development. 
These images reflect entrenched political positions within Brazilian society, largely 
unrecognized on the international stage but critical to understanding Brazil's ambiguous 
positions in international negotiations.
amazon, development, environmentalism, territory, indigenous peoples, military
They (the military) are worried about losing Brazil. Brazil will not go away. Brazil will 
always be here for years and many years to come . . . 
David Yanomami, Folha de São Paulo, August 30, 1993
Corresponding Author:
Dr. Andréa Zhouri, Federal University of Minas Gerais, GESTA-UFMG, Campus Pampulha, Belo Horizonte, 
Minas Gerais, 31270-901, Brazil
Email: azhouri@gmail.com
Federal University of Minas Gerais, Belo Horizonte, Brazil
Zhouri 253
There is no intelligence at all, they are like animals that are born and reproduce. The 
Indians do not walk around naked because they want, but because they do not have 
clothes. If they receive a good machete, they abandon their arrows.
General Euclydes Figueiredo, Folha de São Paulo, August 21, 1993
The ghost of Amazonian occupation lies latent in the Brazilian national imagination. 
From time to time, it incarnates social and political entities which, as a result of strategic ideological political maneuvers, are ultimately decontextualized and undermined. 
Fears of a "conspiracy to occupy the Amazon" arise in relation to the demarcation of 
indigenous lands as well as in national positions on contested issues such as reducing 
emissions from deforestation and forest degradation in developing countries (REDD).
As an example, the atmosphere in Brazil before the 2009 UN Framework Convention 
on Climate Change in Copenhagen was tense as sectors of Lula's administration remained 
divided on REDD. The Ministry of Environment called for Brazil to play a more proactive 
role in REDD, whereas the Ministry of Foreign Affairs proposed more conservative 
approach, reflecting domestic concerns about Brazilian sovereignty over the rain forest. 
During the preparations for the Conference of Parties (COP), a seminar was held in Belém, 
Pará, titled "Climate and Forest—REDD and Marked-Based Mechanisms as a Solution 
for the Amazon?" Social movements and nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) signed 
the Letter from Belém (discussed in further detail), calling for the Brazilian government to 
reject REDD. The document questioned the market-oriented approach proposed for the 
REDD program and argued instead for comprehensive land reform in the country, including the demarcation of indigenous lands, one of the most controversial issues in Brazil.
Around the same time, the military held a meeting in the capital city of Brasília—
the 110 National Meeting of Officials from the Military Reserve (110 ENOREx)—
where they produced the Declaração de Brasília, a document that outlines the military's 
agenda for development and maintaining Brazilian sovereignty in the Amazon. Among 
other statements, the military called attention to their role as guardians of national 
values and interests and, ultimately, as opinion makers within Brazilian society.
Such tensions have influenced development plans and environmental and indigenous policies, as in the case of the ratification of the Raposa Serra do Sol land in 2008. 
They reveal the ongoing impacts of long-established ideologies (albeit to varying 
degrees between governments and power coalitions) despite advances in the debate on 
sustainability and human and cultural rights over the last 20 years.
Indeed, in 2001, a series of reports published by Folha de São Paulo revealed the 
existence of a Brazilian military information center in Marabá, in the state of Pará. The 
exposure of secret army documents revealed the "survival" of spying practices, 
thought to have been shutdown at the end of the military regime. Social movements, 
union organizations, political parties, and NGOs, especially the movement of landless 
peoples (MST), Central Única dos Trabalhadores [Workers Union](CUT), Partido dos 
Trabalhadores [Workers Party] (PT), and environmental NGOs were classified in these 
documents as "adverse forces." To defeat these "forces," the military assumes possible 
"infringements of rights."1
 Spying operations against the MST, baptized "Operação 
Pescado," and against the NGOs, denominated "Operação Poseidon," reveal a special 
interest in organizations with activities in the Amazon region.
While it may seem surprising, the discovery of authoritarian practices at the 
heart of a social-democratic government in the 21st century is not particularly 
remarkable when you consider recent Brazilian political history. The attitude of 
suspicion in relation to environmentalists dates back to the 1970s but was particularly evident during the preparation for the 1992 United Nations Conference on 
Environment and Development (UNCED-92), held in the early years after the cold 
war as well as the enactment of the 1988 constitution. During this same period, 
these suspicions also extended to territorial policies affecting indigenous peoples 
and the debate that dealt with the demarcation and ratification of the Yanomami 
indigenous reserve.
Faced with the conflict involving the ratification of the Raposa Serra do Sol indigenous land in Roraima, which led to high levels of political and military tension in 
2008, it became pertinent to reflect on the discursive elements that support military 
ideas and those of the elite in general. These ideas relate to issues of territory, and the 
military development agenda of antagonism toward environmentalists, advocates of 
indigenous rights, and the Indians themselves.2
In a broader context, such conflicts must be understood against the current phase of 
flexible accumulation of capital, when the appropriation of natural resources and of 
space is of significant relevance . Symbols, concepts, and 
images that represent security, stability, and control are also important. In addition, 
territorial conflicts must also be considered in face of the growing disjuncture between 
state and nation, something that calls into question the issue of sovereignty and the 
emergence of new territories. As Appadurai  recalled,
The territory can be looked upon as a crucial problem in the contemporary crisis 
of the Nation-state or, more precisely, in the crisis of the relation between State 
and nation . . . the existing State-nations rely on some implicit notion of ethnic 
consistency as the basis of state sovereignty, they certainly tend to turn into a 
minority, degrade, punish, assassinate or expel those who are seen as the ethnic 
minority, p. 44.
The Fallacy of Bipolarity: Us (Brazilians) 
Versus Them (the Developed Nations)
Fears about a possible "internationalization of the Amazon," at times very legitimate 
, have been part of the Brazilian history, thus providing the basis 
for geopolitical positions that have shaped the policies adopted for the occupation of 
Amazonia . With the rise of international environmental concerns in the mid-1970s, and the end of the cold war period in the 1980s, 
the idea of "internationalization" was reinvigorated and assumed new features. For 
instance, during the debates leading up to the 1992 UNCED conference, held in Rio 
de Janeiro.
During this period, the voices questioning the legitimacy of environmental and 
indigenous concerns were primarily within the military forces and among Amazon 
politicians who hold a notion of territory and development fairly ubiquitous among 
Brazilian elites.3
 Military forces and Brazilian elites largely perceive the arguments 
against the predatory model of development as well as the defense of differential 
rights of citizenship (that underline the claims for the demarcation of indigenous territories) as truly representing the economic interests of wealthy nations. However, far 
from addressing the real exploitative national and transnational economic interests in 
the Amazon—logging, mining, agribusiness, and the like—the nationalist arguments 
are mainly in opposition to environmentalists and human rights advocates. In the face 
of current policies aimed at speeding up growth to position Brazil more aggressively 
in economic globalization (Lula's PAC—Growth Acceleration Plan, discussed below), 
the nationalist approach reveals a strategy of simplification and stereotyping of complex social and political issues that threaten the growth agenda. Such a strategy serves 
to delegitimize the claims of indigenous peoples and environmental and human rights 
advocates in Brazilian society. It does so by reducing the complex arguments for sustainable development of Amazonia and recognition of the rights of its people to matters of international conspiracy, national security, and sovereignty. As a result, one 
can observe a shift from the discussion concerning rights of citizenship to a discussion 
about economic interests, in which indigenous territories are transformed into areas of 
capitalist exploitation with the consequent perpetuation of social, ethnic, and environmental injustices.
In geopolitical terms, this discursive strategy is constructed as a case of us, Brazilians, 
against them, the "developed world," whereby us and them assume a homogeneous 
generic meaning under the umbrella concept of nation-states. In this process, there is a 
silencing of us, as a multiethnic and multicultural society, and them is presented as an 
oppositional category encompassing multiple actors—including governments, corporations, environmentalists, and human rights advocates. Hence, in a calculated move, the 
voices of environmentalists are equated with nonnational capitalist interests. The fact 
that governments and international economic interests are ambiguously and vaguely 
mentioned leaves open the actual possibility for particular economic alliances. Meanwhile, the construction of an "external enemy" creates and justifies a governmental role 
for the military within the formal structures of the state, considering that their political 
space has declined with the democratization of Brazilian society and the end of the cold 
war period . In fact, the "us versus 
them" construct perpetuates military mistrust and justifies occupation of Amazonian territories. It transforms these territories into empty spaces without identity, rationalizing 
exploitation of natural resources. It is possible to analyze the formation of these ideas in 
current times by considering the debates that occurred in the period of UNCED-92, 
including the demarcation of the Yanomami reserve, and the discussions surrounding 
the ratification of the Raposa Serra do Sol reserve in 2008 and 2009.
UNCED-92: An "International Conspiracy"
On the eve of the UNCED conference held in Rio de Janeiro, sectors of the armed 
forces and environmentalists exchanged accusations that were widely publicized by 
the Brazilian press. At the time, nationalist groups denounced an "international conspiracy to occupy Amazônia." According to their rationale, such an occupation would 
be advocated and approved with the consent of environmentalists and the Brazilian 
government during UNCED-92. To address this concern, nationalists suggested that 
the conference be supervised by "people capable of defending national interests: the 
Armed Forces" . In response to this charge, environmentalists accused the military dictatorship of causing most of the problems in the 
Amazon region through their relentless promotion of economic growth at any cost 
during the 1970s.
The dispute escalated, with environmentalists being called "internationalists and 
non-patriotic" or "enemies of the nation." Ex-Army Secretary Leônidas Pires even 
publicly declared that he felt for José Lutzenberger, the environmental secretary at the 
time, "the same hate I felt for the communist leader Luis Carlos Prestes" . The comparison between José Lutzenberger—an 
internationally known Brazilian environmentalist who played an important role in 
denouncing and stopping the World Bank's projects in the Amazon in the late 1980s—
and Luiz Carlos Prestes—the head of the Communist Party in Brazil for many 
decades—is illustrative of how environmentalists gradually replaced communists as 
"enemies of the nation" in the eyes of military sectors between the late 1980s and early 
1990s. This perspective has helped create a social climate where land conflicts involving indigenous peoples, settlers, miners, and ranchers that result in human rights abuses 
and even deaths are tolerated.
At a symposium held in October 1991, two national army bodies (Escola de 
Comando e Estado-Maior do Exército [ECEME] and Centro Brasileiro de Estudos 
Estratégicos [CEBRES]) produced a document aimed at mobilizing public opinion 
related to the Rio Conference. The document clearly referenced a geopolitical position 
elaborated in the 1960s  whereby "the 
development of the Amazon" is considered in terms of military strategy. It reveals a 
plan of identifying internal and external enemies concerned with the issue of "the 
environment," dissociated from the issue of "development." The opposition between 
economy/development and environment aligns with the opposition between we
(Brazilians) and they (the "developed" countries, enemies of "Brazilian development"). 
As Gilberto Mestrinho, governor of the state of Amazonas at the time, points out,
There is an industry of mystification making us believe that we cannot cut down 
the trees because . . . of the fragility of the Amazon ecosystem . . . why do they
spread such myths . . . ? Because . . . it comprises the biggest mineral area in the 
world . . . Hence, immediately in the second half of the 60s and strongly in the 
70s, Brazil was invaded by a campaign for the preservation of the Amazon . . . 
Exactly for what purpose? Because the rich mineral resources of the Amazon 
compete with the investments that developed countries have in other parts of the 
world. 
The use of homogenizing categories like us and them erases  any 
possible element of global diversity as well as erases the different and opposing 
groups existing within a single nation-state. The category we, Brazilians, concerning 
the ones who would benefit from the extraction of natural resources, conceals the 
unfair distribution of wealth and power that excludes and sabotages indigenous 
peoples and forest dwellers. However, the category them, foreigners, overlooks 
international economic interests allowed to operate in the region. Thus, the governmentapproved activities of financial agencies such as the World Bank, as well as governments 
and corporations from "developed countries," are able to be cloaked under the 
"threatening" and diversionary category of environmentalists.
While transnational actors such as environmentalists and human rights advocates 
are perceived as representatives of economic interests from other nations, similar 
groups within Brazil are classified as subversives on the one hand, or emotional, 
romantic, and naive political activists on the other. In either case, they are perceived 
as aligned with foreign interests or are even classified as foreigners to the Amazon 
region themselves. As Amazonian Senator Aluisio Bezerra, pointed out,
When we speak about the Amazon we cannot be naive and pretend we do not 
see the greed there is over it: we cannot think naively . . . as Mr Lutzenberger 
does. It is the biggest mineral region of the planet . . . it is an enormous genetic 
bank coveted by those countries which can make good use of this bank to continuously monopolize on biotechnology, not to mention the use of strategic 
minerals . . . We must discuss the modernization of the defense of our northern 
borders. And, when I speak about the northern borders I do not speak like the 
environmentalists who speak from the bars of Ipanema, Leblon or any other in 
São Paulo. 
Although the issue of natural resources raises important concerns in the context of 
the transnational economy and may perhaps shape new concepts of security,4
 such 
attacks on environmental activists and defenses of military occupation of the Amazon 
disregard legitimate political and economic concerns. The portrait of environmentalists 
defending the Amazon while comfortably situated in fashionable bars in the south of 
the country reveals regional divides and is intended to undermine any critical perspective 
from southern Brazilians. It also invokes the image of a rich middle-class youth—
urban, alienated, and "immature"—speaking about "the environment" with little real 
knowledge. This image is drawn, first, from the differences in population size, cultural 
differences and, especially, economic inequalities between the "rich" southern and the 
"poor" northern states of Brazil. Therefore, the "rich" south of Brazil is associated 
with the "rich" countries of the northern hemisphere and thus environmentalists from 
the southern states are considered as aliens, similar to their transnational "partners." 
Second, these political discourses promote a divide between economic and environmental 
issues, whereby environmental issues are reduced to the cultural sphere and are therefore 
made a secondary priority, while economic concerns dominate the main arena. 
Moreover, environmental issues are also associated with immaturity, even conveyed 
by the color green as a symbol of both new (young, youth) and naivete . 
It is worth noting that Ipanema and Leblon are bairros (suburbs) in Rio de Janeiro that 
conjure certain images in most Brazilian's minds. These associations consist basically 
of two different, and at times paradoxical, meanings: On one hand, these suburbs were 
considered to be rich, urban, cool, and fashionable places between the 1960s and 
1970s—as in the Bossa Nova song "The Girl from Ipanema," and, on the other hand, 
these two areas were political meeting points for urban middle-class leftist groups 
consisting of artists, intellectuals, and students during the dictatorship years. More 
recently, these suburbs also served as the launching point for Brazil's Green Party. 
Such a comparison once again situates the environmental groups of the 1990s in the 
same symbolic political space that the intellectual political resistance movement 
occupied in the 1960s and 1970s, when the military regime labeled members of this 
movement "subversives" and "enemies of the nation."
This phenomenon appears widespread. Jonathan Hill  identified a similar 
discourse in his study of an indigenous land dispute in the Venezuelan Amazon. However, although in Venezuela environmentalists are grouped under the category of 
"subversives" and are associated with the practice of "terrorism" and guerrilla warfare, in Brazil environmentalists are portrayed as members of an idealistic, intellectual, rich, and fashionable middle-class disconnected from the Amazon reality and 
thus lacking the authority to speak to it. Conversely, environmentalism may also be 
associated with a list of illegal and antisocial behaviors such as the illegal trade of 
drugs and minerals. In the symposium document referred to earlier, environmental 
and indigenous issues are classified as Amazon problems associated with and having 
the same status as guerrillas, narcotics smugglers, and illegal mining—problems to be 
eliminated with a geopolitical strategy for the Amazon . These sets of associations are identified by Hill  in his analysis of the 
Venezuelan case as a process of "semantic accommodation," whereby the use of radical metaphor, or analogy, opens up the semantic category of "terrorist subversion" 
into an infinitely expandable list of "enemies." From another point of view, as suggested by Laclau , the military and local politicians' discourses create a "chain 
of equivalence" between signifiers that are emptied of their specific meanings. Therefore, communists, environmentalists, terrorists, and drug traders, for instance, can be 
subsumed under the categories of "enemies" or "subversives."
Hill identifies this as a widely adopted military position in Latin America. He outlined how the theory of a conspiracy appears in the documents of the "17th Congress 
of the Armies of the Americas" held in Argentina in November 1987 . His 
analysis reveals the process of stereotyping indigenous peoples and their supporters as 
"subversive organizations" with ties to an international communist movement, thereby 
reducing the complex social problems they represent to a bipolar opposition between 
"national security" and "terrorist subversion." In so doing, an alternative discourse 
about cultural pluralism within the nation-state is suppressed by the imposition of a 
military discourse of sovereignty versus subversion . This is the 
case of the arguments raised against the demarcation of indigenous lands in Brazil, as 
recently stated by military sectors in the Declaração de Brasília:
It is imperative to denounce and stop the attempts to subject the Brazilian 
Amazon to "relative sovereignty" or its internationalization. We urge to hold 
responsible those false experts who attested the existence of indigenous "nations." 
They reject the Brazilian nationality and are charmed by mercenaries, most of 
them NGOs, who claim access to enormous territories where nothing is produced. They are only interested in our immense biodiversity and vast economic 
resources. The Brazilian people need to know the real intentions of national and 
international organizations that act freely in the Brazilian Amazon. The episode 
of Raposa Serra do Sol, an area which can be classified today as having a "relative autonomy" for Brazil because of the action of bad Brazilians, is an example 
of the kind of threats imposed over our Amazonia. 
The impact of such ideas goes beyond the military circles, as can be seen in the 
attacks launched against anthropologists by Veja, a mainstream weekly magazine, in 
May 2010 .
Indigenous Peoples, Development, 
and Environmental Regimes
The indigenous peoples' land rights in Brazil are regulated by Article 231 of the 1988 
Federal Constitution. The caput of the Article reads as follows:
It is recognized to indigenous peoples their social organization, their customs, 
languages, beliefs and traditions and the original rights over lands they traditionally occupy, belonging to the Union to demarcate them, to protect and enforce 
respect for all their assets.5
This article was the result of decades of struggle by organized indigenous groups 
and their supporters. It has been estimated that the population of Indians in Brazil 
numbers between 500,000 to 700,000, and nearly 60% of this population lives in the 
Amazon region. The Yanomamis live in the Amazon and are one of the 232 indigenous 
groups in the country. Their population was estimated at around 10,000 in the late 
1980s, spread between the Brazilian states of Roraima and Amazonas. There were 
also 15,000 Yanomamis living on the Venezuelan side of the border. Since gold was 
first found in Roraima in 1987, there has been a flood of thousands of garimpeiros
(gold diggers) into the Yanomamis' territory. The "contact" with garimpeiros has 
been a complete disaster for the Yanomamis. There has been a severe decline in their 
population due to the establishment of new diseases—such as the flu and malaria as 
a well as a rise in malnutrition and other related diseases. There have also been direct 
conflicts resulting in murder. A massive campaign for the demarcation of their area 
was launched and soon the Yanomamis became a symbol, not only of the struggle for 
indigenous peoples' rights but also of the impasses and dramas of the frontier.6
As an attempt to improve Brazil's image and minimize criticism from the international community during the UNCED-92 Conference, former President Fernando Collor signed a law recognizing the 9.4 million hectares of continuous Yanomami territory 
at the time. In so doing, he surpassed the previous president's decree to create 19 "islands" 
of reserves, a proposal that was more acceptable to the military. Military officials, 
frightened by the fragility of the border and in alliance with local elites who feared 
losing the mineral resources of the region, opposed to such a demarcation. Since the 
Brazilian constitution was established in 1988, there have been several attempts to 
modify the Article related to demarcation of indigenous lands. Most of the proposals 
presented in the House of Representatives have been developed by congressmen from 
Amazonian states.
The rationale behind the rhetoric and actions of the military and Amazon politicians becomes clearer when examining numerous past attempts to prevent the demarcation of indigenous lands.7
 Take for instance this statement in the CEBRES & ECEME 
 document:
The Brazilian Centre for Strategic Studies considers it necessary to prevent any 
isolated indigenous grouping or, at least, to maintain those groups not yet placed 
in areas of environmental protection . . . The Centre is also against the delimitation of reserves in open lands, thus avoiding them forming boundaries with 
neighboring countries. This is a serious security issue, and one in which we can 
simply eliminate possible attempts to discuss Brazilian sovereignty over those 
territories . . . Finally, CEBRES is against the radical preservation of the aboriginal culture and the formation of cysts in areas not proportionally related to the 
number of silvículas, p.204.8
The labeling of indigenous groups as silvículas who would be placed in "areas of 
environmental protection" produces an effect of dehumanization, undermining the 
social, political, and cultural status of indigenous peoples within Brazilian society. The 
reference to areas "not proportionally related to the number of silvículas" is a clear 
reference to the demarcation of the Yanomami lands that was imminent when this 
document was written.
Since the Yanomami became emblematic of the struggle for the demarcation of 
indigenous lands in Brazil in the early 1990s and since Gilberto Mestrinho, then governor of the state of Amazonas, was a leading opponent to this demarcation and to 
environmentalists' priorities in general, it is worthwhile to consider Mestrinho's position regarding the demarcation of the Yanomamis' lands:
[The Amazon region] is 500 million hectares . . . A little piece of the state of 
Amazonas alone is bigger than Portugal: 9.4 million hectares in one area of 
150 million hectares. And it is precisely an area equivalent to the dimensions 
of Portugal that they want to transform into an indigenous reserve for three 
thousand or so Indians [sic] . . . But the whole world stood up demanding the 
preservation and demarcation of the reserve, not for the tribe, but for the Yanomami nation. Precisely because they are occupying that area . . . where there 
are primary natural deposits of Amazon gold . . . 
There are several important issues to consider in the above excerpt. First, this 
statement again lays out the dichotomy of us and them—"underdeveloped" and 
"developed" nations. This discourse portrays them, the White Western "developed" 
nations, as greedy people merely using the Yanomami as a means to achieve their 
own ends. The Yanomamis themselves are seen, therefore, as nonsubjects occupying
an area rich in gold. In other words, in a process of social and historical inversion, 
they are transformed into intruders in a land to be exploited by Western entrepreneurs. 
Second, for geopolitical purposes, the size of the territory is compared to the size of 
the indigenous population, ignoring the established historic relationship the Yanomami 
have with their land. The comparison between the size of the Yanomami reserve and 
a European State (Portugal) establishes a frame of reference common to us and them. 
From this perspective, it is not acceptable that a tribe, not a nation, that does not even 
represent the uniform character of a nation nor have a single language nor a common 
"peaceful" relationship with one another (meanings associated to the idea of "civilization") 
would be given a territory the size of a nation-state:
They (Yanomami) belong to four different groups who speak different languages and do not get along with each other . . . So, these Yanomamis, according 
to data collected by the army, number 3.460 [sic] . . . on the Brazilian side. 
Because there are also those Indians on the Venezuelan side, in bigger numbers 
than the ones on this side here. But the whole world defends them. They invented 
the myth that the Indian needs to walk. Areas for wandering around . . . This is 
an invention of bureaucrats. Thus, they want to give to the Yanomamis an open 
reserve of 9.4 million hectares. However, we are not allowed to give an inch of 
land to a small farmer because he will damage the forest . . . there is a whole 
strategy of emptying the Amazon. To create difficulties in the life of the rural 
man of the Amazon . . . 
As Mestrinho himself states, the Yanomami's area is a small piece of land in 
relation to the whole of the Amazon. It is only perceived as "too big" when compared 
to Portugal, a European state. The fact that the Yanomamis are spread between Brazil 
and Venezuela is another argument used to deny Indians the rights to Brazilian lands. 
They are on the frontier, or on the border; therefore, they are neither Brazilians nor 
Venezuelans. Literally living on the margins of society, they are marginalized. From 
a geopolitical perspective, they assume the relative status of strangers.
The stranger is, according to Zygmunt Baumann , neither a friend nor an 
enemy. The stranger belongs to an uncertain realm, indeterminate and unfamiliar, 
beyond the comfort of cognitive clarity. The stranger is a constant threat to world 
order and is a third element that does not fit into the simple logic of the friends versus 
the enemies. In this sense, a similar reference could also be made here to Douglas' 
 analysis of the categories of purity and danger, order and disorder; Indians do 
not fit into the "order" of the civilized nation-state ideas conceived by the Amazon 
politician. Consequently, Amazon politicians transform Yanomamis into a threat to 
Brazilian sovereignty, not only because they can become a nation-state but also 
because they can be either Venezuelans or Brazilians, thus creating a vulnerable opening along the border with Venezuela. Indigenous peoples would also contribute to 
increasing Brazil's "vulnerability" in relation to other nation-states through their alliances with environmentalists and indigenous rights support groups world-wide.
Finally, another strategy observed in Mestrinho's statement is to emphasize opposition and conflict between groups that share the same social position—excluded subjects 
within Brazilian society. These are the indigenous peoples and the poor, rural "man." 
His arguments lead one to believe that environmental conservation within the Amazon 
forest, and the demarcation of indigenous lands, is actually incompatible with the 
"development" of the region and contributes to the impoverishment of local peoples.
The policy of settling Indians in environmentally protected areas does not acknowledge their cultural connection to particular lands. This policy also provokes conflicts 
between indigenous peoples and conservationists. In the same region as the Yanomami 
reserve, conflicts arose related to the Raposa Serra do Sol Indigenous Reserve and the 
Monte Roraima National Park. The overlap of conservation units (UCs) and indigenous lands (TI) paves the way for the expansion of the agricultural frontier and the 
project of exploiting the Amazon for international economic gain by means of successive programs. This is the case of the Avança Brasil program that began in the late 
1990s, the Initiative for the Integration of South American Regional Infrastructure 
implemented in 2000, and the PAC launched in 2007.
The Avança Brasil program was created during Fernando Henrique Cardoso's 
administration in the late 1990s. It was announced as a response to significant national 
challenges, including reduction of social and regional inequalities, national unification, 
as well as integration into the world market economy. Although written in the language of sustainable development, and emphasizing participation as a guiding principle, the program was actually driven by massive infrastructure investments, such as 
large dams, roads, waterways, and so on These investments were defended as basic 
requirements for the attraction of national and international capital. The economic basis 
of the program, with proposed fiscal adjustment and raising of the gross domestic 
product (GDP), followed in the footsteps of the Plano Real, which addressed conditionalities imposed by World Bank accords. Therefore, the program was based on 
developmental concepts in accordance with economic liberalization: the utilization of 
natural resources for economic gain and long-term social and environmental damage 
for short-term improvements in material productivity; an economy dissociated from 
the natural and cultural features and conditions of regions and places.
Lula's administration has done little to shift this agenda. The PAC, launched in 
2007, is also a national program which consists of a set of economic policies aimed at 
accelerating Brazilian economic growth, with a total investment of R$503.9 billion. 
Similar to Avança Brasil, one of its priorities is investment in the country's infrastructure, especially in the areas of sanitation, housing, transportation, and energy, among 
others. However, it is worth noting that nearly 55% of the PAC budget was directed to 
finance energy infrastructure, mainly large dams. Projects which were criticized in the 
1980s for their environmental and social impacts in the Amazon have actually made a 
comeback under Lula's administration, as in the case of the Belo Monte dam, in the 
Xingu river, and the Jirau and Santo Antônio dams, in the Tocantins river.
In addition, both Avança Brasil and PAC have close connections to a wider economic plan applicable to all of South America—the Initiative for the Integration of 
South American Regional Infrastructure (IIRSA). This program involves the governments of the 12 countries of South America, with the objective of physical integration 
of these countries by means of modernizing the infrastructure of transport, energy, and 
telecommunications through collective action. IIRSA is intended to stimulate South 
American political, social, and economic integration. This proposal originated from an 
initiative presented by Banco Interamericano de Desenvolvimento (BID) and Corporação Andina de Fomento (CAF) in the year 2000, during the South American presidential meeting in Brasília. A central aspect of this program is a BID concept focused on 
economic liberalization, encouraging private initiative and limiting state-level economic initiatives. The idea is to generate competitive market economies in South 
America, which means transforming forests and territories of indigenous peoples into 
commodities . Efforts at modifying regulation are 
underway, such as the proposal to review and amend the forest law as well as the environmental licensing regulation . President 
Lula himself has, on several occasions, identified environmentalists, indigenous peoples' advocates, and environmental licensing regulations as barriers to development 
. Environmental regimes designed to address 
climate change, such as REDD (although intended for a different purpose), can also 
play a part in transforming forests into commodities by putting a price on environmental services. This approach is challenged by many indigenous groups and local communities. The relative impacts of these different initiatives have yet to be analyzed in 
terms of their complex interactions. Local groups and environmentalists are still very 
divided in their opinions on market-oriented environmental projects .
In October 2009, 2 months prior to the Copenhagen meeting, a number of local 
groups and NGOs from the Brazilian Amazon gathered at a seminar in Belém, Pará, 
called "Climate and Forest—REDD and Marked-Based Mechanisms as a Solution for 
the Amazon?" The gathered parties signed on the Letter from Belém in which they 
publicly called on the Brazilian government to reject REDD. As stated in the letter,
We reject the use of marked-based mechanisms as tools to reduce carbon emissions based on the firm conviction that markets cannot be expected to take 
responsibility for life on the planet . . . The REDD proposals do not make any 
distinction between native forests and large-scale tree monoculture, and they 
allow economic actors . . . to resort to standing forest appreciation mechanisms 
to preserve and strengthen their economic and political power to the detriment 
of those populations.
In addition, it further calls for general land reform:
Deforestation—resulting from the advance of monoculture and of policies that 
favor agribusiness and a development model based on the predatory exploitation 
and export of natural resources—can only be avoided if the land issue is appropriately addressed through a Land Reform and sustainable territorial reorganization 
measures, and if territories occupied by traditional peoples and communities and 
by native peoples are legally recognized. 
Similar positions can be found among several indigenous peoples worldwide. The 
final declaration of the "World People's Conference on Climate Change and the 
Rights of Mother Earth," held in Cochabamba, Bolívia, in April 2010, rejects REDD 
for the same reasons.9
In Brazil, REDD has been supported by a diverse range of actors who hold different views and interests, such as NGOs and researchers concerned with ecological 
modernization, governors of the Amazonian states, and segments of agribusiness. The 
governors of the Amazonian states have sought compensation for the preservation of 
environmental services through the prevention of deforestation. In October 2009, they 
signed the Letter of Macapá, putting pressure on the federal government, especially 
the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, particularly sensitive to possible threats to national 
sovereignty . Several researchers interviewed at the time 
described Brazilian policies related to the Amazon rain forest as being grounded in a 
"historical paranoia involving national sovereignty and the control over Amazonia" 
. "This sovereignty protection 
ends up being a protection to deforestation forces" said Instituto Nacional de Pesquisas Espaciais's (INPE) researcher, Myanna Lahsen, in defense of REDD .
Agents of ecological modernization also overlook indigenous people's claims, thus 
making opposition to REDD even more challenging. In failing to acknowledge 
indigenous peoples' positions they, perhaps unintentionally, align themselves with 
the hegemonic social and political discourses of Brazilian elites and military forces. In 
fact, resistance to REDD is sustained by the differing goals of its proponents: On the 
one side, there are those who believe they are acting in favor of self-determination for 
the forests peoples and against market-oriented solutions. On the other, there are sectors of the government who are motivated by suspicion of international forces but who 
do not necessarily oppose market-oriented initiatives, since they fit within the developmental concept . Indeed, economic projects defended by the state, 
advanced in multilateral initiatives, have contributed to the intensification of conflicts 
among several social groups, such as the historical invasions of prospectors in indigenous reserves and the recent tensions between rice producers and indigenous peoples 
in the state of Roraima.
Serra do Sol—Escalating Conflicts
Within market-oriented policies, the infrastructure and energy projects proposed for the 
northern border of the Amazon, aimed at the exploration and trade of minerals, timber, 
and land, follow the path of the Calha Norte Project . The argument used for the implementation of this project was the 
"strengthening of national presence" along the Amazon border, considered a vulnerable segment of national territory. The resistance to demarcation of indigenous lands, 
such as the Yanomami reserves at the end of the 1980s, must be analyzed in this context. Twenty years later, public debate and criticism is once again escalating due to 
conflicts between rice producing landowners and the indigenous peoples of the Raposa 
Serra do Sol reserve, on the border with Venezuela. In April 2005, after a struggle that 
lasted 30 years, the Raposa Serra do Sol indigenous reserve was approved, despite 
protests from landowners and politicians in the region. The reserve is comprised of an 
area of 1,747,464 hectares and is occupied by five groups of indigenous peoples—
Ingarikó, Macuxi, Patanoma, Taurepang, and Wapixana—with an overall population 
of almost 19,000 Indians. The rice producers, who arrived in Roraima from other states 
through colonization programs implemented by the federal government, together with 
the military and other political elites, never accepted this demarcation of continuous 
territory . As substantiated by 
the Mestrinho discourse, in reference to the demarcation of the Yanomami land in the 
early 1990s, the rice producers also blame international economic actors with interests 
in mineral mining in the Amazon for the support and defense of demarcation of indigenous lands. Thus, the argument of an international conspiracy to dominate the 
economic resources of the Amazon is reinvigorated in the context of the disputes 
against the ratification of the Raposa Serra do Sol indigenous reserve.
Throughout the demarcation process, hundreds of protests were held to delay and 
ideally prevent the legal recognition of that indigenous reserve. However, they were 
ultimately shutdown by the Ministry of Justice. The non-Indians who had occupied 
part of the land were compensated or resettled on other nearby lands. But a small

into small areas or in other terms discontinuous territory. This would permit the recognition of their privately owned lands, which they were illegally occupying within

The co-option of people, the use of violence, including kidnapping of missionaries 
and Indians, and the use of car bombs and dynamite were all among the farmers' resistance strategies. In response, the government created a committee to study and resolve 
the conflict by means of negotiation. However, the presidential decision to approve the 
continuous lands in 2005 reinvigorated the violent resistance of the rice producers. In 
April 2008, a federal police operation was organized to remove the farmers, but some 
residents of the area, who had ties to the rice producers, tried to prevent this measure 
with the use of force. There was an attack which resulted in the shooting of nine Indians. During the hostilities, the Roraima State government—together with federal senators and congressmen from the state, who supported the rice producers and saw the 
demarcation of indigenous land as an unreasonable appropriation of territory which 
challenged economic development in Roraima—took the case to the courts and managed to suspend the federal police operation. The case went to trial months later, in 
August of 2008. The Supreme Federal Court assessed whether the land should remain 
as a continuous territory, as previously ratified, or if it should be fragmented into discontinuous territories, in accordance with the rice producers' plea. Following heated 
debates, a decision in favor of maintaining the ratification of the continuous territory 
was made the following year but with several conditions. Some of these conditions 
were already postulated in the constitutional text and others were newly aimed at 
restricting utilization of lands and resources by the indigenous peoples, establishing 
the right of the state to freely exploit the lands and their resources, according to their 
interests, without consulting the communities.
At the height of the crisis in April of 2008, the press systematically circulated 
the arguments of the military and politicians, which had an incredible resemblance to 
the debate that took place two decades earlier in relation to the demarcation of the 
Yanomami reserve and the holding of UNCED-92. The main headline of a magazine 
with a high national circulation stated as follows:
The Amazon: its sovereignty is in question
Proposals for the internationalization of Brazil's greatest green treasure gain strength 
in the global community. An immediate answer is necessary. 
Similar to the statements on conspiracy analyzed above, here again the Amazon is 
reduced to a treasure chest coveted by the generalized "global community." The criticism

The resistance to applying the terminology of "nation" to Indian regions is reproduced 
in the comments of a journalist from the leading television news show in the country:
Are there indigenous nations distinct from the Brazilian nation? There is malicious

The unwillingness to use the term nation when referring to indigenous peoples is 
linked with a suspicion that this will benefit national and international NGOs. Many

and gain control of Amazonian territory, and would use the Indians as a means to reach 
this objective. This discourse reinforces military convictions, such as these statements 
by General Augusto Heleno, Military Commander of the Amazon:
There is an orchestration on a global level to say that Brazil can not take care of 
the Amazon. 
Brazilian indigenous politics is completely disassociated from the historical 
process of colonization of our country. It needs to be revised urgently. I am not 
against the organs that take care of this, I would like to join them so that we can 
revise a political approach that has failed until today, it is just a question of 
going there to see that the situation is sorrowful, or even worse, chaotic .
Besides the indigenous question, General Heleno listed several threats to the 
Amazon, including landowner conflicts, NGOs, and other entities perceived as dangerous, 
consistent with prior discourse. The virulent military concern regarding the ratification 
of continuous indigenous reserves is also evident in the following statement by 
General Mário Matheus Madureira, chief of staff of the Eastern Military Command:
The risk to sovereignty is that some areas could be separated from Brazilian territory. International NGOs and indigenous groups could solicit this political 
division. It could result in the same situation that occurred in Kosovo. It is a 
concern for all. 
The defense of national sovereignty, heavily driven by territorial concerns, is one of 
the most important issues still influencing Brazilian policies related to Amazonia. 
Domestic debates regarding this region are intertwined with concerns about the country's development and the global environmental movement, as witnessed in recent 
national dialogues related to climate change and the role of the Amazon as a carbon 
sink. This controversy about Amazonian governance influences alliances among different political actors in a complex social and political landscape not well understood 
by the international community .
Challenging the discourse of national sovereignty promoted by the military, and 
echoed in public opinion and other sectors of the Brazilian State, indigenous and traditional peoples' movements argue in defense of "food sovereignty" and land reform, 
including the demarcation of indigenous lands. Social movements and indigenous 
groups presented their case at COP 15 in Copenhagen .
The defenders of ecological modernization represent a paradox. Many NGOs and 
researchers who subscribe to the idea of the "forestry vocation" of Amazonia  may perceive the positions of such movements as supporting, albeit unintentionally, deforestation forces in Amazonia. Ecological modernists criticize sectors of 
the state suspicious of market strategies, believing they may contribute to internationalization of the country. Such concerns are raised by diverse organizations and 
projects.
The long-held fear regarding the "international conspiracy to occupy the Amazon" 
prevents consideration of alternative models of development, cultural plurality, and 
indigenous rights. Through logic correlating geographical space, territory, place, and 
nation, the military claims that the demarcation of indigenous lands could lead to recognition by the UN as independent "nations," and therefore threaten Brazilian sovereignty. The nationalist accusations, which select NGOs, social movements, and 
indigenous peoples as their primary targets, shift the focus of the discussion on rights
to a discussion about interests. Meanwhile, transnational investments in the region 
that are perceived as politically and economically beneficial are, in fact, encouraged. 
As a result, development has a singular and ever-evolving meaning.
The military, as defenders of the nation-state, champion fears about any alternate 
reality that does not fit within the homogenizing perspective of the "imagined community" related to the nation . In the 
peripheral regions, the state assumes an ambiguous position—on one hand claiming to 
defend the interests of the local elite, on the other building and maintaining relationships with powerful transnational actors—such as corporations, the credit systems of 
the World Bank, and other bilateral and multilateral agencies as well as environmental 
initiatives. Thus, in addition to local, regional, and national complexities, tensions in 
the Amazon must also be understood in terms of global economic processes. Moreover, conflicts must be considered in the face of the growing disjuncture between state 
and nation, calling into question the relationship between state sovereignty and territoriality. Ultimately, the hostility of the military and political elites toward environmentalists, indigenous peoples, and their allies sustains the struggle for sustainability, 
cultural rights, and environmental justice in the Brazilian Amazon.
